he ntiquities of the ews
by osephus
 ewish ntiquities, in twenty books, was written after osephus's ewish ars
was received with favor. he treatise covers the history of the ews until the
outbreak of the great war. ts sources are unique making its value uneven and,
at times, unverifiable. ntil ehemiah, the account is based on the scriptures
with many legendary additions. he period between  and  ...
parallels  accabees, supplemented from other sources including the lost
work of trabo called istories, icolaus of amascus, and ivy. erod's reign
is detailed after the istory of icolaus and a book entitled he emorabilia of
ing erod, now missing. he story of the high priests was likely derived from
official documents in erusalem. opies of the oman decrees in favor of the
ews included by osephus are quite valuable. he ntiquities were probably
published in  , in the thirteenth year of omitian, the th year of the
author's life.
reface to the work
—rom the reation to the death of saac

—rom the death of leander the reat to that of udas accabeus

—rom the death of ueen leandra to the death of ntigonus

 . hose who undertake to write histories, do not,  perceive, take that trouble
on one and the same account, but for many reasons, and those such as are very
different one from another. or some of them apply themselves to this part of
learning to show their skill in composition, and that they may therein acquire a
reputation for speaking finely others of them there are, who write histories in
order to gratify those that happen to be concerned in them, and on that account
have spared no pains, but rather gone beyond their own abilities in the
performance but others there are, who, of necessity and by force, are driven to
write history, because they are concerned in the facts, and so cannot ecuse
themselves from committing them to writing, for the advantage of posterity
nay, there are not a few who are induced to draw their historical facts out of
darkness into light, and to produce them for the benefit of the public, on
account of the great importance of the facts themselves with which they have
been concerned. ow of these several reasons for writing history,  must
profess the two last were my own reasons also for since  was myself
interested in that war which we ews had with the omans, and knew myself its
particular actions, and what conclusion it had,  was forced to give the history
of it, because  saw that others perverted the truth of those actions in their
writings.
. ow  have undertaken the present work, as thinking it will appear to all
the reeks    worthy of their study for it will contain all our antiquities, and the
constitution of our government, as interpreted out of the ebrew criptures.
nd indeed  did formerly intend, when  wrote of the war,    to eplain who the
ews originally were,—what fortunes they had been subject to,—and by what
legislature they had been instructed in piety, and the eercise of other virtues,—
what wars also they had made in remote ages, till they were unwillingly
engaged in this last with the omans but because this work would take up a
great compass,  separated it into a set treatise by itself, with a beginning of its

own, and its own conclusion but in process of time, as usually happens to such
as undertake great things,  grew weary and went on slowly, it being a large
subject, and a difficult thing to translate our history into a foreign, and to us
unaccustomed language. owever, some persons there were who desired to
know our history, and so ehorted me to go on with it and, above all the rest,
paphroditus,    a man who is a lover of all kind of learning, but is principally
delighted with the knowledge of history, and this on account of his having been
himself concerned in great affairs, and many turns of fortune, and having
shown a wonderful rigor of an ecellent nature, and an immovable virtuous
resolution in them all.  yielded to this man's persuasions, who always ecites
such as have abilities in what is useful and acceptable, to join their endeavors
with his.  was also ashamed myself to permit any laziness of disposition to
have a greater influence upon me, than the delight of taking pains in such
studies as were very useful  thereupon stirred up myself, and went on with my
work more cheerfully. esides the foregoing motives,  had others which 
greatly reflected on and these were, that our forefathers were willing to
communicate such things to others and that some of the reeks took
considerable pains to know the affairs of our nation.
.  found, therefore, that the second of the tolemies was a king who was
etraordinarily diligent in what concerned learning, and the collection of books
that he was also peculiarly ambitious to procure a translation of our law, and of
the constitution of our government therein contained, into the reek tongue.
ow leazar the high priest, one not inferior to any other of that dignity among
us, did not envy the forenamed king the participation of that advantage, which
otherwise he would for certain have denied him, but that he knew the custom of
our nation was, to hinder nothing of what we esteemed ourselves from being
communicated to others. ccordingly,  thought it became me both to imitate
the generosity of our high priest, and to suppose there might even now be many
lovers of learning like the king for he did not obtain all our writings at that
time but those who were sent to leandria as interpreters, gave him only the
books of the law, while there were a vast number of other matters in our sacred
books. hey, indeed, contain in them the history of five thousand years in
which time happened many strange accidents, many chances of war, and great
actions of the commanders, and mutations of the form of our government.
pon the whole, a man that will peruse this history, may principally learn from
it, that all events succeed well, even to an incredible degree, and the reward of
felicity is proposed by od but then it is to those that follow his will, and do
not venture to break his ecellent laws and that so far as men any way
apostatize from the accurate observation of them, what was practical before
becomes impracticable    and whatsoever they set about as a good thing, is
converted into an incurable calamity. nd now  ehort all those that peruse

these books, to apply their minds to od and to eamine the mind of our
legislator, whether he hath not understood his nature in a manner worthy of
him and hath not ever ascribed to him such operations as become his power,
and hath not preserved his writings from those indecent fables which others
have framed, although, by the great distance of time when he lived, he might
have securely forged such lies for he lived two thousand years ago at which
vast distance of ages the poets themselves have not been so hardy as to fi even
the generations of their gods, much less the actions of their men, or their own
laws. s  proceed, therefore,  shall accurately describe what is contained in
our records, in the order of time that belongs to them for  have already
promised so to do throughout this undertaking and this without adding any
thing to what is therein contained, or taking away any thing therefrom.
. ut because almost all our constitution depends on the wisdom of oses,
our legislator,  cannot avoid saying somewhat concerning him beforehand,
though  shall do it briefly  mean, because otherwise those that read my book
may wonder how it comes to pass, that my discourse, which promises an
account of laws and historical facts, contains so much of philosophy. he
reader is therefore to know, that oses deemed it eceeding necessary, that he
who would conduct his own life well, and give laws to others, in the first place
should consider the ivine nature and, upon the contemplation of od's
operations, should thereby imitate the best of all patterns, so far as it is possible
for human nature to do, and to endeavor to follow after it neither could the
legislator himself have a right mind without such a contemplation nor would
any thing he should write tend to the promotion of virtue in his readers  mean,
unless they be taught first of all, that od is the ather and ord of all things,
and sees all things, and that thence he bestows a happy life upon those that
follow him but plunges such as do not walk in the paths of virtue into
inevitable miseries. ow when oses was desirous to teach this lesson to his
countrymen, he did not begin the establishment of his laws after the same
manner that other legislators did  mean, upon contracts and other rights
between one man and another, but by raising their minds upwards to regard
od, and his creation of the world and by persuading them, that we men are
the most ecellent of the creatures of od upon earth. ow when once he had
brought them to submit to religion, he easily persuaded them to submit in all
other things for as to other legislators, they followed fables, and by their
discourses transferred the most reproachful of human vices unto the gods, and
afforded wicked men the most plausible ecuses for their crimes but as for our
legislator, when he had once demonstrated that od was possessed of perfect
virtue, he supposed that men also ought to strive after the participation of it
and on those who did not so think, and so believe, he inflicted the severest
punishments.  ehort, therefore, my readers to eamine this whole undertaking

in that view for thereby it will appear to them, that there is nothing therein
disagreeable either to the majesty of od, or to his love to mankind for all
things have here a reference to the nature of the universe while our legislator
speaks some things wisely, but enigmatically, and others under a decent
allegory, but still eplains such things as required a direct eplication plainly
and epressly. owever, those that have a mind to know the reasons of every
thing, may find here a very curious philosophical theory, which  now indeed
shall wave the eplication of but if od afford me time for it,  will set about
writing it after  have finished the present work.  shall now betake myself to
the history before me, after  have first mentioned what oses says of the
creation of the world, which  find described in the sacred books after the
manner following.
 ____________________
  hat is, all the entiles, both reeks and omans.
  e may seasonably note here, that osephus wrote his even ooks of the ewish ar long before he
wrote these his ntiquities. hose books of the ar were published about .. , and these ntiquities,
.. , about eighteen years later.
  his paphroditus was certainly alive in the third year of rajan, .. . ee the note on the irst
ook gainst pion, sect. . ho he was we do not know for as to paphroditus, the freedman of ero,
and afterwards omitian's secretary, who was put to death by omitian in the th or th year of his
reign, he could not be alive in the third of rajan.
  osephus here plainly alludes to the famous reek proverb, f od be with us, every thing that is
impossible becomes possible.
  "s to this intended work of osephus concerning the reasons of many of the ewish laws, and what
philosophical or allegorical sense they would bear, the loss of which work is by some of the learned not
much regretted,  am inclinable, in part, to abricius's opinion, ap. avercamp, p. , , hat "we need not
doubt but that, among some vain and frigid conjectures derived from ewish imaginations, osephus would
have taught us a greater number of ecellent and useful things, which perhaps nobody, neither among the
ews, nor among the hristians, can now inform us of so that  would give a great deal to find it still
etant."
  
      

 - 
       
  
     ,     

 . n the beginning od created the heaven and the earth. ut when the earth
did not come into sight, but was covered with thick darkness, and a wind

moved upon its surface, od commanded that there should be light and when
that was made, he considered the whole mass, and separated the light and the
darkness and the name he gave to one was ight, and the other he called ay
and he named the beginning of light, and the time of rest, the vening and the
orning, and this was indeed the first day. ut oses said it was one day—the
cause of which  am able to give even now but because  have promised to give
such reasons for all things in a treatise by itself,  shall put off its eposition till
that time. fter this, on the second day, he placed the heaven over the whole
world, and separated it from the other parts, and he determined it should stand
by itself. e also placed a crystalline firmament] round it, and put it together
in a manner agreeable to the earth, and fitted it for giving moisture and rain,
and for affording the advantage of dews. n the third day he appointed the dry
land to appear, with the sea itself round about it and on the very same day he
made the plants and the seeds to spring out of the earth. n the fourth day he
adorned the heaven with the sun, the moon, and the other stars, and appointed
them their motions and courses, that the vicissitudes of the seasons might be
clearly signified. nd on the fifth day he produced the living creatures, both
those that swim, and those that fly the former in the sea, the latter in the air he
also sorted them as to society and miture, for procreation, and that their kinds
might increase and multiply. n the sith day he created the four-footed beasts,
and made them male and female on the same day he also formed man.
ccordingly oses says, hat in just si days the world, and all that is therein,
was made. nd that the seventh day was a rest, and a release from the labor of
such operations—whence it is that we celebrate a rest from our labors on that
day, and call it the abbath, which word denotes rest in the ebrew tongue.
. oreover, oses, after the seventh day was over    begins to talk
philosophically and concerning the formation of man, says thus hat od
took dust from the ground, and formed man, and inserted in him a spirit and a
soul.    his man was called dam, which in the ebrew tongue signifies one
that is red, because he was formed out of red earth, compounded together for
of that kind is virgin and true earth. od also presented the living creatures,
when he had made them, according to their kinds, both male and female, to
dam, who gave them those names by which they are still called. ut when he
saw that dam had no female companion, no society, for there was no such
created, and that he wondered at the other animals which were male and
female, he laid him asleep, and took away one of his ribs, and out of it formed
the woman whereupon dam knew her when she was brought to him, and
acknowledged that she was made out of himself. ow a woman is called in the
ebrew tongue ssa but the name of this woman was ve, which signifies the
mother of all living.
. oses says further, that od planted a paradise in the east, flourishing

with all sorts of trees and that among them was the tree of life, and another of
knowledge, whereby was to be known what was good and evil and that when
he brought dam and his wife into this garden, he commanded them to take
care of the plants. ow the garden was watered by one river,    which ran round
about the whole earth, and was parted into four parts. nd hison, which
denotes a multitude, running into ndia, makes its eit into the sea, and is by the
reeks called anges. uphrates also, as well as igris, goes down into the ed
ea.    ow the name uphrates, or hrath, denotes either a dispersion, or a
flower by iris, or iglath, is signified what is swift, with narrowness and
eon runs through gypt, and denotes what arises from the east, which the
reeks call ile.
. od therefore commanded that dam and his wife should eat of all the
rest of the plants, but to abstain from the tree of knowledge and foretold to
them, that if they touched it, it would prove their destruction. ut while all the
living creatures had one language,    at that time the serpent, which then lived
together with dam and his wife, showed an envious disposition, at his
supposal of their living happily, and in obedience to the commands of od and
imagining, that when they disobeyed them, they would fall into calamities, he
persuaded the woman, out of a malicious intention, to taste of the tree of
knowledge, telling them, that in that tree was the knowledge of good and evil
which knowledge, when they should obtain, they would lead a happy life nay,
a life not inferior to that of a god by which means he overcame the woman,
and persuaded her to despise the command of od. ow when she had tasted
of that tree, and was pleased with its fruit, she persuaded dam to make use of
it also. pon this they perceived that they were become naked to one another
and being ashamed thus to appear abroad, they invented somewhat to cover
them for the tree sharpened their understanding and they covered themselves
with fig-leaves and tying these before them, out of modesty, they thought they
were happier than they were before, as they had discovered what they were in
want of. ut when od came into the garden, dam, who was wont before to
come and converse with him, being conscious of his wicked behavior, went out
of the way. his behavior surprised od and he asked what was the cause of
this his procedure and why he, that before delighted in that conversation, did
now fly from it, and avoid it. hen he made no reply, as conscious to himself
that he had transgressed the command of od, od said, " had before
determined about you both, how you might lead a happy life, without any
affliction, and care, and veation of soul and that all things which might
contribute to your enjoyment and pleasure should grow up by my providence,
of their own accord, without your own labor and pains-taking which state of
labor and pains-taking would soon bring on old age, and death would not be at
any remote distance but now thou hast abused this my good-will, and hast

disobeyed my commands for thy silence is not the sign of thy virtue, but of thy
evil conscience." owever, dam ecused his sin, and entreated od not to be
angry at him, and laid the blame of what was done upon his wife and said that
he was deceived by her, and thence became an offender while she again
accused the serpent. ut od allotted him punishment, because he weakly
submitted to the counsel of his wife and said the ground should not henceforth
yield its fruits of its own accord, but that when it should be harassed by their
labor, it should bring forth some of its fruits, and refuse to bring forth others.
e also made ve liable to the inconveniency of breeding, and the sharp pains
of bringing forth children and this because she persuaded dam with the same
arguments wherewith the serpent had persuaded her, and had thereby brought
him into a calamitous condition. e also deprived the serpent of speech, out of
indignation at his malicious disposition towards dam. esides this, he inserted
poison under his tongue, and made him an enemy to men and suggested to
them, that they should direct their strokes against his head, that being the place
wherein lay his mischievous designs towards men, and it being easiest to take
vengeance on him, that way. nd when he had deprived him of the use of his
feet, he made him to go rolling all along, and dragging himself upon the
ground. nd when od had appointed these penalties for them, he removed
dam and ve out of the garden into another place.
  
     ,   
     
. dam and ve had two sons the elder of them was named ain which
name, when it is interpreted, signifies a possession the younger was bel,
which signifies sorrow. hey had also daughters. ow the two brethren were
pleased with different courses of life for bel, the younger, was a lover of
righteousness and believing that od was present at all his actions, he ecelled
in virtue and his employment was that of a shepherd. ut ain was not only
very wicked in other respects, but was wholly intent upon getting, and he first
contrived to plough the ground. e slew his brother on the occasion following
—hey had resolved to sacrifice to od. ow ain brought the fruits of the
earth, and of his husbandry but bel brought milk, and the first-fruits of his
flocks but od was more delighted with the latter oblation,    when he was
honored with what grew naturally of its own accord, than he was with what was
the invention of a covetous man, and gotten by forcing the ground whence it
was that ain was very angry that bel was preferred by od before him and
he slew his brother, and hid his dead body, thinking to escape discovery. ut
od, knowing what had been done, came to ain, and asked him what was

become of his brother, because he had not seen him of many days whereas he
used to observe them conversing together at other times. ut ain was in doubt
with himself, and knew not what answer to give to od. t first he said that he
was himself at a loss about his brother's disappearing but when he was
provoked by od, who pressed him vehemently, as resolving to know what the
matter was, he replied, he was not his brother's guardian or keeper, nor was he
an observer of what he did. ut, in return, od convicted ain, as having been
the murderer of his brother and said, " wonder at thee, that thou knowest not
what is become of a man whom thou thyself hast destroyed." od therefore did
not inflict the punishment of death] upon him, on account of his offering
sacrifice, and thereby making supplication to him not to be etreme in his wrath
to him but he made him accursed, and threatened his posterity in the seventh
generation. e also cast him, together with his wife, out of that land. nd when
he was afraid that in wandering about he should fall among wild beasts, and by
that means perish, od bid him not to entertain such a melancholy suspicion,
and to go over all the earth without fear of what mischief he might suffer from
wild beasts and setting a mark upon him, that he might be known, he
commanded him to depart.
. nd when ain had traveled over many countries, he, with his wife, built
a city, named od, which is a place so called, and there he settled his abode
where also he had children. owever, he did not accept of his punishment in
order to amendment, but to increase his wickedness for he only aimed to
procure every thing that was for his own bodily pleasure, though it obliged him
to be injurious to his neighbors. e augmented his household substance with
much wealth, by rapine and violence he ecited his acquaintance to procure
pleasures and spoils by robbery, and became a great leader of men into wicked
courses. e also introduced a change in that way of simplicity wherein men
lived before and was the author of measures and weights. nd whereas they
lived innocently and generously while they knew nothing of such arts, he
changed the world into cunning craftiness. e first of all set boundaries about
lands he built a city, and fortified it with walls, and he compelled his family to
come together to it and called that city noch, after the name of his eldest son
noch. ow ared was the son of noch whose son was alaleel whose son
was athusela whose son was amech who had seventy-seven children by
two wives, illa and da. f those children by da, one was abal he erected
tents, and loved the life of a shepherd. ut ubal, who was born of the same
mother with him, eercised himself in music    and invented the psaltery and the
harp. ut ubal, one of his children by the other wife, eceeded all men in
strength, and was very epert and famous in martial performances. e procured
what tended to the pleasures of the body by that method and first of all
invented the art of making brass. amech was also the father of a daughter,

whose name was aamah. nd because he was so skilful in matters of divine
revelation, that he knew he was to be punished for ain's murder of his brother,
he made that known to his wives. ay, even while dam was alive, it came to
pass that the posterity of ain became eceeding wicked, every one
successively dying, one after another, more wicked than the former. hey were
intolerable in war, and vehement in robberies and if any one were slow to
murder people, yet was he bold in his profligate behavior, in acting unjustly,
and doing injuries for gain.
. ow dam, who was the first man, and made out of the earth, (for our
discourse must now be about him), after bel was slain, and ain fled away, on
account of his murder, was solicitous for posterity, and had a vehement desire
of children, he being two hundred and thirty years old after which time he
lived other seven hundred, and then died. e had indeed many other children, 
 but eth in particular. s for the rest, it would be tedious to name them  will
therefore only endeavor to give an account of those that proceeded from eth.
ow this eth, when he was brought up, and came to those years in which he
could discern what was good, became a virtuous man and as he was himself of
an ecellent character, so did he leave children behind him who imitated his
virtues.    ll these proved to be of good dispositions. hey also inhabited the
same country without dissensions, and in a happy condition, without any
misfortunes falling upon them, till they died. hey also were the inventors of
that peculiar sort of wisdom which is concerned with the heavenly bodies, and
their order. nd that their inventions might not be lost before they were
sufficiently known, upon dam's prediction that the world was to be destroyed
at one time by the force of fire, and at another time by the violence and quantity
of water, they made two pillars,    the one of brick, the other of stone they
inscribed their discoveries on them both, that in case the pillar of brick should
be destroyed by the flood, the pillar of stone might remain, and ehibit those
discoveries to mankind and also inform them that there was another pillar of
brick erected by them. ow this remains in the land of iriad to this day.
  
         
   ,   ,   
    
. ow this posterity of eth continued to esteem od as the ord of the
universe, and to have an entire regard to virtue, for seven generations but in
process of time they were perverted, and forsook the practices of their
forefathers and did neither pay those honors to od which were appointed
them, nor had they any concern to do justice towards men. ut for what degree

of zeal they had formerly shown for virtue, they now showed by their actions a
double degree of wickedness, whereby they made od to be their enemy. or
many angels    of od accompanied with women, and begat sons that proved
unjust, and despisers of all that was good, on account of the confidence they
had in their own strength for the tradition is, that these men did what
resembled the acts of those whom the recians call giants. ut oah was very
uneasy at what they did and being displeased at their conduct, persuaded them
to change their dispositions and their acts for the better—but seeing they did
not yield to him, but were slaves to their wicked pleasures, he was afraid they
would kill him, together with his wife and children, and those they had married
so he departed out of that land.
. ow od loved this man for his righteousness yet he not only
condemned those other men for their wickedness, but determined to destroy the
whole race of mankind, and to make another race that should be pure from
wickedness and cutting short their lives, and making their years not so many as
they formerly lived, but one hundred and twenty only,    he turned the dry land
into sea and thus were all these men destroyed but oah alone was saved for
od suggested to him the following contrivance and way of escape—hat he
should make an ark of four stories high, three hundred    cubits long, fifty cubits
broad, and thirty cubits high. ccordingly he entered into that ark, and his wife,
and sons, and their wives, and put into it not only other provisions, to support
their wants there, but also sent in with the rest all sorts of living creatures, the
male and his female, for the preservation of their kinds and others of them by
sevens. ow this ark had firm walls, and a roof, and was braced with cross
beams, so that it could not be any way drowned or overborne by the violence of
the water. nd thus was oah, with his family, preserved. ow he was the tenth
from dam, as being the son of amech, whose father was athusela he was
the son of noch, the son of ared and ared was the son of alaleel, who,
with many of his sisters, were the children of ainan, the son of nos. ow
nos was the son of eth, the son of dam.
. his calamity happened in the si hundredth year of oah's government,
age,] in the second month,    called by the acedonians ius, but by the
ebrews archesuan for so did they order their year in gypt. ut oses
appointed that isan, which is the same with anthicus, should be the first
month for their festivals, because he brought them out of gypt in that month
so that this month began the year as to all the solemnities they observed to the
honor of od, although he preserved the original order of the months as to
selling and buying, and other ordinary affairs. ow he says that this flood
began on the twenty-seventh seventeenth] day of the forementioned month
and this was two thousand si hundred and fifty-si one thousand si hundred
and fifty-si] years from dam, the first man and the time is written down in

our sacred books, those who then lived having noted down,    with great
accuracy, both the births and deaths of illustrious men.
. or indeed eth was born when dam was in his two hundred and
thirtieth year, who lived nine hundred and thirty years. eth begat nos in his
two hundred and fifth year who, when he had lived nine hundred and twelve
years, delivered the government to ainan his son, whom he had in his hundred
and ninetieth year. e lived nine hundred and five years. ainan, when he had
lived nine hundred and ten years, had his son alaleel, who was born in his
hundred and seventieth year. his alaleel, having lived eight hundred and
ninety-five years, died, leaving his son ared, whom he begat when he was in
his hundred and sity-fifth year. e lived nine hundred and sity-two years
and then his son noch succeeded him, who was born when his father was one
hundred and sity-two years old. ow he, when he had lived three hundred and
sity-five years, departed and went to od whence it is that they have not
written down his death. ow athusela, the son of noch, who was born to
him when he was one hundred and sity-five years old, had amech for his son
when he was one hundred and eighty-seven years of age to whom he delivered
the government, when he had retained it nine hundred and sity-nine years.
ow amech, when he had governed seven hundred and seventy-seven years,
appointed oah, his son, to be ruler of the people, who was born to amech
when he was one hundred and eighty-two years old, and retained the
government nine hundred and fifty years. hese years collected together make
up the sum before set down. ut let no one inquire into the deaths of these men

fter this they were dispersed abroad, on account of their languages, and went

. hen, bram heard of their calamity, he was at once afraid for ot his

. hen the ebrews had obtained such a wonderful deliverance, the country
was a great trouble to them, for it was entirely a desert, and without sustenance
for them and also had eceeding little water, so that it not only was not at all
sufficient for the men, but not enough to feed any of the cattle, for it was
parched up, and had no moisture that might afford nutriment to the vegetables
so they were forced to travel over this country, as having no other country but
this to travel in. hey had indeed carried water along with them from the land
over which they had traveled before, as their conductor had bidden them but
when that was spent, they were obliged to draw water out of wells, with pain,
by reason of the hardness of the soil. oreover, what water they found was
bitter, and not fit for drinking, and this in small quantities also and as they thus
traveled, they came late in the evening to a place called arah,    which had that
name from the badness of its water, for ar denotes bitterness. hither they
came afflicted both by the tediousness of their journey, and by their want of
food, for it entirely failed them at that time. ow here was a well, which made
them choose to stay in the place, which, although it were not sufficient to
satisfy so great an army, did yet afford them some comfort, as found in such
desert places for they heard from those who had been to search, that there was
nothing to be found, if they traveled on farther. et was this water bitter, and
not fit for men to drink and not only so, but it was intolerable even to the cattle
themselves.
. hen oses saw how much the people were cast down, and that the
occasion of it could not be contradicted, for the people were not in the nature of
a complete army of men, who might oppose a manly fortitude to the necessity
that distressed them the multitude of the children, and of the women also,
being of too weak capacities to be persuaded by reason, blunted the courage of
the men themselves,-he was therefore in great difficulties, and made
everybody's calamity his own for they ran all of them to him, and begged of
him the women begged for their infants, and the men for the women, that he
would not overlook them, but procure some way or other for their deliverance.
e therefore betook himself to prayer to od, that he would change the water
from its present badness, and make it fit for drinking. nd when od had
granted him that favor, he took the top of a stick that lay down at his feet, and

divided it in the middle, and made the section lengthways. e then let it down
into the well, and persuaded the ebrews that od had hearkened to his
prayers, and had promised to render the water such as they desired it to be, in
case they would be subservient to him in what he should enjoin them to do, and
this not after a remiss or negligent manner. nd when they asked what they
were to do in order to have the water changed for the better, he bid the strongest
men among them that stood there, to draw up water    and told them, that when
the greatest part was drawn up, the remainder would be fit to drink. o they
labored at it till the water was so agitated and purged as to be fit to drink.
. nd now removing from thence they came to lim which place looked
well at a distance, for there was a grove of palm-trees but when they came near
to it, it appeared to be a bad place, for the palm-trees were no more than
seventy and they were ill-grown and creeping trees, by the want of water, for
the country about was all parched, and no moisture sufficient to water them,
and make them hopeful and useful, was derived to them from the fountains,
which were in number twelve they were rather a few moist places than springs,
which not breaking out of the ground, nor running over, could not sufficiently
water the trees. nd when they dug into the sand, they met with no water and
if they took a few drops of it into their hands, they found it to be useless, on
account of its mud. he trees were too weak to bear fruit, for want of being
sufficiently cherished and enlivened by the water. o they laid the blame on
their conductor, and made heavy complaints against him and said that this their
miserable state, and the eperience they had of adversity, were owing to him
for that they had then journeyed an entire thirty days, and had spent all the
provisions they had brought with them and meeting with no relief, they were in
a very desponding condition. nd by fiing their attention upon nothing but
their present misfortunes, they were hindered from remembering what
deliverances they had received from od, and those by the virtue and wisdom
of oses also so they were very angry at their conductor, and were zealous in
their attempt to stone him, as the direct occasion of their present miseries.
. ut as for oses himself, while the multitude were irritated and bitterly
set against him, he cheerfully relied upon od, and upon his consciousness of
the care he had taken of these his own people and he came into the midst of
them, even while they clamored against him, and had stones in their hands in
order to despatch him. ow he was of an agreeable presence, and very able to
persuade the people by his speeches accordingly he began to mitigate their
anger, and ehorted them not to be over-mindful of their present adversities,
lest they should thereby suffer the benefits that had formerly been bestowed on
them to slip out of their memories and he desired them by no means, on
account of their present uneasiness, to cast those great and wonderful favors
and gifts, which they had obtained of od, out of their minds, but to epect

deliverance out of those their present troubles which they could not free
themselves from, and this by the means of that ivine rovidence which
watched over them. eeing it is probable that od tries their virtue, and
eercises their patience by these adversities, that it may appear what fortitude
they have, and what memory they retain of his former wonderful works in their
favor, and whether they will not think of them upon occasion of the miseries
they now feel. e told them, it appeared they were not really good men, either
in patience, or in remembering what had been successfully done for them,
sometimes by contemning od and his commands, when by those commands
they left the land of gypt and sometimes by behaving themselves ill towards
him who was the servant of od, and this when he had never deceived them,
either in what he said, or had ordered them to do by od's command. e also
put them in mind of all that had passed how the gyptians were destroyed
when they attempted to detain them, contrary to the command of od and after
what manner the very same river was to the others bloody, and not fit for
drinking, but was to them sweet, and fit for drinking and how they went a new
road through the sea, which fled a long way from them, by which very means
they were themselves preserved, but saw their enemies destroyed and that
when they were in want of weapons, od gave them plenty of them-and so he
recounted all the particular instances, how when they were, in appearance, just
going to be destroyed, od had saved them in a surprising manner and that he
had still the same power and that they ought not even now to despair of his
providence over them and accordingly he ehorted them to continue quiet, and
to consider that help would not come too late, though it come not immediately,
if it be present with them before they suffer any great misfortune that they
ought to reason thus that od delays to assist them, not because he has no
regard to them, but because he will first try their fortitude, and the pleasure they
take in their freedom, that he may learn whether you have souls great enough to
bear want of food, and scarcity of water, on its account or whether you rather
love to be slaves, as cattle are slaves to such as own them, and feed them
liberally, but only in order to make them more useful in their service. hat as
for himself, he shall not be so much concerned for his own preservation for if
he die unjustly, he shall not reckon it any affliction, but that he is concerned for
them, lest, by casting stones at him, they should be thought to condemn od
himself.
. y this means oses pacified the people, and restrained them from
stoning him, and brought them to repent of what they were going to do. nd
because he thought the necessity they were under made their passion less
unjustifiable, he thought he ought to apply himself to od by prayer and
supplication and going up to an eminence, he requested of od for some
succor for the people, and some way of deliverance from the want they were in,

because in him, and in him alone, was their hope of salvation and he desired
that he would forgive what necessity had forced the people to do, since such
was the nature of mankind, hard to please, and very complaining under
adversities. ccordingly od promised he would take care of them, and afford
them the succor they were desirous of. ow when oses had heard this from
od, he came down to the multitude. ut as soon as they saw him joyful at the
promises he had received from od, they changed their sad countenances into
gladness. o he placed himself in the midst of them, and told them he came to
bring them from od a deliverance from their present distresses. ccordingly a

. oses took out the tribe of evi from communicating with the rest of the

. e also determined concerning animals which of them might be used for

. e also ordered that those whose bodies were afflicted with leprosy, and
that had a gonorrhea, should not come into the city    nay, he removed the

permits those also who have taken care of funerals to come in after the same

had the gonorrhea. ut he that sheds his seed in his sleep, if he go down into

with several sorts of sacrifices concerning which we will speak hereafter.
. hence one cannot but smile at those who say that oses was himself
afflicted with the leprosy when he fled out of gypt, and that he became the
conductor of those who on that account left that country, and led them into the
land of anaan for had this been true, oses would not have made these laws
to his own dishonor, which indeed it was more likely he would have opposed, if
others had endeavored to introduce them and this the rather, because there are
lepers in many nations, who yet are in honor, and not only free from reproach
and avoidance, but who have been great captains of armies, and been intrusted
with high offices in the commonwealth, and have had the privilege of entering
into holy places and temples so that nothing hindered, but if either oses
himself, or the multitude that was with him, had been liable to such a
misfortune in the color of his skin, he might have made laws about them for
their credit and advantage, and have laid no manner of difficulty upon them.
ccordingly, it is a plain case, that it is out of violent prejudice only that they
report these things about us. ut oses was pure from any such distemper, and
lived with countrymen who were pure of it also, and thence made the laws
which concerned others that had the distemper. e did this for the honor of

pleases.
. s to the women, when they have born a child, oses forbade them to

admitted before twice that number of days be over. nd when after the before-

. ut if any one suspect that his wife has been guilty of adultery, he was to
bring a tenth deal of barley flour they then cast one handful to od and gave
the rest of it to the priests for food. ne of the priests set the woman at the
gates that are turned towards the temple, and took the veil from her head, and

wrote the name of od on parchment, and enjoined her to swear that she had
not at all injured her husband and to wish that, if she had violated her chastity,
her right thigh might be put out of joint that her belly might swell and that she
might die thus but that if her husband, by the violence of his affection, and of

she might bear a male child in the tenth month. ow when these oaths were
over, the priest wiped the name of od out of the parchment, and wrung the

the woman, if she were unjustly accused, conceived with child, and brought it
to perfection in her womb but if she had broken her faith of wedlock to her
husband, and had sworn falsely before od, she died in a reproachful manner

the ceremonies about sacrifices, and about the purifications thereto belonging,

laws to them—
  
  
. s for adultery, oses forbade it entirely, as esteeming it a happy thing that
men should be wise in the affairs of wedlock and that it was profitable both to
cities and families that children should be known to be genuine. e also
abhorred men's lying with their mothers, as one of the greatest crimes and the
like for lying with the father's wife, and with aunts, and sisters, and sons' wives,
as all instances of abominable wickedness. e also forbade a man to lie with
his wife when she was defiled by her natural purgation and not to come near
brute beasts nor to approve of the lying with a male, which was to hunt after
unlawful pleasures on account of beauty. o those who were guilty of such
insolent behavior, he ordained death for their punishment.
. s for the priests, he prescribed to them a double degree of purity    for
he restrained them in the instances above, and moreover forbade them to marry
harlots. e also forbade them to marry a slave, or a captive, and such as got
their living by cheating trades, and by keeping inns as also a woman parted
from her husband, on any account whatsoever. ay, he did not think it proper
for the high priest to marry even the widow of one that was dead, though he
allowed that to the priests but he permitted him only to marry a virgin, and to
retain her. hence it is that the high priest is not to come near to one that is
dead, although the rest are not prohibited from coming near to their brethren, or

parents, or children, when they are dead but they are to be unblemished in all
respects. e ordered that the priest who had any blemish, should have his
portion indeed among the priests, but he forbade him to ascend the altar, or to
enter into the holy house. e also enjoined them, not only to observe purity in
their sacred ministrations, but in their daily conversation, that it might be
unblamable also. nd on this account it is that those who wear the sacerdotal
garments are without spot, and eminent for their purity and sobriety nor are
they permitted to drink wine so long as they wear those garments.    oreover,
they offer sacrifices that are entire, and have no defect whatsoever.
. nd truly oses gave them all these precepts, being such as were
observed during his own lifetime but though he lived now in the wilderness,
yet did he make provision how they might observe the same laws when they
should have taken the land of anaan. e gave them rest to the land from

accord out of the earth should in common belong to all that pleased to use it,
making no distinction in that respect between their own countrymen and
foreigners and he ordained, that they should do the same after seven times
seven years, which in all are fifty years and that fiftieth year is called by the
ebrews he ubilee, wherein debtors are freed from their debts, and slaves are
set at liberty which slaves became such, though they were of the same stock,
by transgressing some of those laws the punishment of which was not capital,
but they were punished by this method of slavery. his year also restores the
land to its former possessors in the manner following—hen the ubilee is
come, which name denotes liberty, he that sold the land, and he that bought it,
meet together, and make an estimate, on one hand, of the fruits gathered and,
on the other hand, of the epenses laid out upon it. f the fruits gathered come
to more than the epenses laid out, he that sold it takes the land again but if the
epenses prove more than the fruits, the present possessor receives of the
former owner the difference that was wanting, and leaves the land to him and
if the fruits received, and the epenses laid out, prove equal to one another, the
present possessor relinquishes it to the former owners. oses would have the
same law obtain as to those houses also which were sold in villages but he
made a different law for such as were sold in a city for if he that sold it
tendered the purchaser his money again within a year, he was forced to restore
it but in case a whole year had intervened, the purchaser was to enjoy what he
had bought. his was the constitution of the laws which oses learned of od
when the camp lay under ount inai, and this he delivered in writing to the
ebrews.

. hen oses had led the ebrews away from thence to a place called aran,

. oses came now boldly to the multitude, and informed them that od was
moved at their abuse of him, and would inflict punishment upon them, not
indeed such as they deserved for their sins, but such as parents inflict on their
children, in order to their correction. or, he said, that when he was in the
tabernacle, and was bewailing with ears that destruction which was coming
upon them od put him in mind what things he had done for them, and what
benefits they had received from him, and yet how ungrateful they had been to
him that just now they had been induced, through the timorousness of the spies,
to think that their words were truer than his own promise to them and that on
this account, though he would not indeed destroy them all, nor utterly
eterminate their nation, which he had honored more than any other part of
mankind, yet he would not permit them to take possession of the land of

anaan, nor enjoy its happiness but would make them wander in the
wilderness, and live without a fied habitation, and without a city, for forty
years together, as a punishment for this their transgression but that he had
promised to give that land to our children, and that he would make them the
possessors of those good things which, by your ungoverned passions, you have
deprived yourselves of.
. hen oses had discoursed thus to them according to the direction of
od, the multitude, grieved, and were in affliction and entreated ost to
procure their reconciliation to od, and to permit them no longer to wander in
the wilderness, but bestow cities upon them. ut he replied, that od would not
admit of any such trial, for that od was not moved to this determination from
any human levity or anger, but that he had judicially condemned them to that
punishment. ow we are not to disbelieve that oses, who was but a single
person, pacified so many ten thousands when they were in anger, and converted
them to a mildness temper for od was with him, and prepared the way to his
persuasions of the multitude and as they had often been disobedient, they were
now sensible that such disobedience was disadvantageous to them and that they
had still thereby fallen into calamities.
. ut this man was admirable for his virtue, and powerful in making men
give credit to what he delivered, not only during the time of his natural life, but
even there is still no one of the ebrews who does not act even now as if
oses were present, and ready to punish him if he should do any thing that is
indecent nay, there is no one but is obedient to what laws he ordained,
although they might be concealed in their transgressions. here are also many
other demonstrations that his power was more than human, for still some there
have been, who have come from the parts beyond uphrates, a journey of four
months, through many dangers, and at great epenses, in honor of our temple
and yet, when they had offered their oblations, could not partake of their own
sacrifices, because oses had forbidden it, by somewhat in the law that did not
permit them, or somewhat that had befallen them, which our ancient customs
made inconsistent therewith some of these did not sacrifice at all, and others
left their sacrifices in an imperfect condition many were not able, even at first,
so much as to enter the temple, but went their ways in this as preferring a
submission to the laws of oses before the fulfilling of their own inclinations,
they had no fear upon them that anybody could convict them, but only out of a
reverence to their own conscience. hus this legislation, which appeared to be
divine, made this man to be esteemed as one superior to his own nature. ay,
further, a little before the beginning of this war, when laudius was emperor of
the omans, and smael was our high priest, and when so great a famine    was
come upon us, that one tenth deal of wheat] was sold for four drachmae, and
when no less than seventy cori of flour were brought into the temple, at the

feast of unleavened bread, (these cori are thirty-one icilian, but forty-one
thenian medimni,) not one of the priests was so hardy as to eat one crumb of
it, even while so great a distress was upon the land and this out of a dread of
the law, and of that wrath which od retains against acts of wickedness, even
when no one can accuse the actors. hence we are not to wonder at what was
then done, while to this very day the writings left by oses have so great a
force, that even those that hate us do confess, that he who established this
settlement was od, and that it was by the means of oses, and of his virtue
but as to these matters, let every one take them as he thinks fit.

. ow this life of the ebrews in the wilderness was so disagreeable and
troublesome to them, and they were so uneasy at it, that although od had

forbidden them to meddle with the anaanites, yet could they not be persuaded
to be obedient to the words of oses, and to be quiet but supposing they
should be able to beat their enemies, without his approbation, they accused
him, and suspected that he made it his business to keep in a distressed
condition, that they might always stand in need of his assistance. ccordingly
they resolved to fight with the anaanites, and said that od gave them his
assistance, not out of regard to oses's intercessions, but because he took care
of their entire nation, on account of their forefathers, whose affairs he took
under his own conduct as also, that it was on account of their own virtue that
he had formerly procured them their liberty, and would be assisting to them,
now they were willing to take pains for it. hey also said that they were
possessed of abilities sufficient for the conquest of their enemies, although
oses should have a mind to alienate od from them that, however, it was for
their advantage to be their own masters, and not so far to rejoice in their
deliverance from the indignities they endured under the gyptians, as to bear
the tyranny of oses over them, and to suffer themselves to be deluded, and
live according to his pleasure, as though od did only foretell what concerns us
out of his kindness to him, as if they were not all the posterity of braham that
od made him alone the author of all the knowledge we have, and we must still
learn it from him that it would be a piece of prudence to oppose his arrogant
pretences, and to put their confidence in od, and to resolve to take possession
of that land which he had promised them, and not to give ear to him, who on
this account, and under the pretence of ivine authority, forbade them so to do.
onsidering, therefore, the distressed state they were in at present, and that in

they resolved to fight with the anaanites, as submitting only to od, their
supreme ommander, and not waiting for any assistance from their legislator.
. hen, therefore, they had come to this resolution, as being best for them,
they went against their enemies but those enemies were not dismayed either at
the attack itself, or at the great multitude that made it, and received them with
great courage. any of the ebrews were slain and the remainder of the army,
upon the disorder of their troops, were pursued, and fled, after a shameful
manner, to their camp. hereupon this unepected misfortune made them quite
despond and they hoped for nothing that was good as gathering from it, that
this affliction came from the wrath of od, because they rashly went out to war
without his approbation.
. ut when oses saw how deeply they were affected with this defeat, and

. he people mourned for aron thirty days, and when this mourning was

oses therefore sent messengers to ihon, the king of this country, desiring

. hen oses saw that the morite king was disposed to enter upon

ereupon the ebrews took possession of their land, which is a country situate

into ordan loses its own name, and takes the other while ordan itself runs

. hen matters were come to this state, g, the king of ilead and

 have been oftener in danger of death from you than from our enemies. hat

ordan, and are in possession of the land of anaan. ince, when you shall have

nd in order to prevent your ignorance of virtue, and the degeneracy of your

. hen he had spoken thus, he gave them the laws and the constitution of

oses left concerning our common conversation and intercourse one with

. hen you have possessed yourselves of the land of anaan, and have

. et there be taken out of your fruits a tenth, besides that which you have

. ou are not to offer sacrifices out of the hire of a woman who is a

. et not any one of you wear a garment made of woolen and linen, for

. et every one commemorate before od the benefits which he

 . et there be seven men to judge in every city,    and these such as have

. ut let not a single witness be credited, but three, or two at the least,

. ristocracy, and the way of living under it, is the best constitution and

. et it not be esteemed lawful to remove boundaries, neither our own,

. e that plants a piece of land, the trees of which produce fruits before

. ou are not to sow with seed a piece of land which is planted with

 whatsoever, (and many such causes happen among men,) let him in writing

. s to those young men that despise their parents, and do not pay them
honor, but offer them affronts, either because they are ashamed of them or think
themselves wiser than they,—in the first place, let their parents admonish them
in words, (for they are by nature of authority sufficient for becoming their

the sake of pleasure, nor for the augmentation of their riches, by joining both
their stocks together, but that they might have children to take care of them in
their old age, and might by them have what they then should want. nd say

children. nd on such the law inflicts ineorable punishment of which
punishment mayst thou never have the eperience." ow if the insolence of

multitude following him, and there let him be stoned and when he has
continued there for one whole day, that all the people may see him, let him be
buried in the night. nd thus it is that we bury all whom the laws condemn to
die, upon any account whatsoever. et our enemies that fall in battle be also
buried nor let any one dead body lie above the ground, or suffer a punishment
beyond what justice requires.
. et no one lend to any one of the ebrews upon usury, neither usury of
what is eaten or what is drunken, for it is not just to make advantage of the
misfortunes of one of thy own countrymen but when thou hast been assistant

him.

reasonable that those tribes that came along with them from beyond ordan, and
had partaken of the dangers they had undergone, being their own kindred,
should now be dismissed and sent home, and should have thanks for the pains
they had taken together with them. s also, he thought it reasonable that they

etraordinary virtue, who should measure the land faithfully, and without any
fallacy or deceit should inform them of its real magnitude.
. ow oshua, when he had thus spoken to them, found that the multitude
approved of his proposal. o he sent men to measure their country, and sent

the tabernacle.
. o oshua took both leazar and the senate, and with them the heads of
the tribes, and distributed the land to the nine tribes, and to the half-tribe of

udea, reaching as far as erusalem, and its breadth etended to the ake of
odom. ow in the lot of this tribe there were the cities of skelon and aza.
he lot of imeon, which was the second, included that part of dumea which
bordered upon gypt and rabia. s to the enjamites, their lot fell so, that its
length reached from the river ordan to the sea, but in breadth it was bounded

of phraim had by lot the land that etended in length from the river ordan to
ezer but in breadth as far as from ethel, till it ended at the reat lain. he

half-tribe of anasseh had the land from ordan to the city of ora but its

ssachar, which had its limits in length, ount armel and the river, but its
limit in breadth was ount abor. he tribe of ebulon's lot included the land

such it was, and all that part which lay over-against idon. he city rce
belonged to their share, which is also named ctipus. he aphtalites received
the eastern parts, as far as the city of amascus and the pper alilee, unto
ount ibanus, and the ountains of ordan, which rise out of that mountain
that is, out of that part of it whose limits belong to the neighboring city of rce.
he anites' lot included all that part of the valley which respects the sun-
setting, and were bounded by zotus and ora as also they had all amnia and
ath, from kron to that mountain where the tribe of udah begins.
. fter this manner did oshua divide the si nations that bear the name of
the sons of anaan, with their land, to be possessed by the nine tribes and a
half for oses had prevented him, and had already distributed the land of the
morites, which itself was so called also from one of the sons of anaan, to the
two tribes and a half, as we have shown already. ut the parts about idon, as

were not yet regularly disposed of.
. ut now was oshua hindered by his age from eecuting what he
intended to do (as did those that succeeded him in the government, take little
care of what was for the advantage of the public) so he gave it in charge to
every tribe to leave no remainder of the race of the anaanites in the land that
had been divided to them by lot that oses had assured them beforehand, and
they might rest fully satisfied about it, that their own security and their
observation of their own laws depended wholly upon it. oreover, he enjoined

in the country of the morites and three of these he assigned to those that fled
from the man-slayers, who were to inhabit there for he was very solicitous that
nothing should be neglected which oses had ordained. hese cities were, of
the tribe of udah, ebron of that of phraim, hechem and of that of
aphtali, adesh, which is a place of the pper alilee. e also distributed
among them the rest of the prey not yet distributed, which was very great
whereby they had an affluence of great riches, both all in general, and every
one in particular and this of gold and of vestments, and of other furniture,
besides a multitude of cattle, whose number could not be told.
. fter this was over, he gathered the army together to a congregation,
and spake thus to those tribes that had their settlement in the land of the
morites beyond ordan,—for fifty thousand of them had armed themselves,

and had gone to the war along with them—"ince that od, who is the ather
and ord of the ebrew nation, has now given us this land for a possession,
and promised to preserve us in the enjoyment of it as our own for ever and
since you have with alacrity offered yourselves to assist us when we wanted
that assistance on all occasions, according to his command it is but just, now
all our difficulties are over, that you should be permitted to enjoy rest, and that
we should trespass on your alacrity to help us no longer that so, if we should
again stand in need of it, we may readily have it on any future emergency, and
not tire you out so much now as may make you slower in assisting us another
thee. e, therefore, return you our thanks for the dangers you have undergone
with us, and we do it not at this thee only, but we shall always be thus disposed
and be so good as to remember our friends, and to preserve in mind what
advantages we have had from them and how you have put off the enjoyments
of your own happiness for our sakes, and have labored for what we have now,
by the good-will of od, obtained, and resolved not to enjoy your own
prosperity till you had afforded us that assistance. owever, you have, by
joining your labor with ours, gotten great plenty of riches, and will carry home
with you much prey, with gold and silver, and, what is more than all these, our
good-will towards you, and a mind willingly disposed to make a requital of
your kindness to us, in what case soever you shall desire it, for you have not
omitted any thing which oses beforehand required of you, nor have you
despised him because he was dead and gone from you, so that there is nothing
to diminish that gratitude which we owe to you. e therefore dismiss you
joyful to your own inheritances and we entreat you to suppose, that there is no
limit to be set to the intimate relation that is between us and that you will not
imagine, because this river is interposed between us, that you are of a different
race from us, and not ebrews for we are all the posterity of braham, both
we that inhabit here, and you that inhabit there and it is the same od that
brought our forefathers and yours into the world, whose worship and form of
government we are to take care of, which he has ordained, and are most
carefully to observe because while you continue in those laws, od will also
show himself merciful and assisting to you but if you imitate the other nations,
and forsake those laws, he will reject your nation." hen oshua had spoken
thus, and saluted them all, both those in authority one by one, and the whole
multitude in common, he himself staid where he was but the people conducted

they hardly knew how to part one from the other.
. ow when the tribe of euben, and that of ad, and as many of the
anassites as followed them, were passed over the river, they built an altar on
the banks of ordan, as a monument to posterity, and a sign of their relation to
those that should inhabit on the other side. ut when those on the other side

heard that those who had been dismissed had built an altar, but did not hear
with what intention they built it, but supposed it to be by way of innovation,

but thinking this defamatory report, as if it were built for divine worship, was
credible, they appeared in arms, as though they would avenge themselves on

punish them for their subversion of the laws of their country for they did not

had given the occasion, but to regard the will of od, and the manner wherein
he desired to be worshipped so these men put themselves in array for war. ut
oshua, and leazar the high priest, and the senate, restrained them and

we have learned the true reasons by which you have been moved to build this
altar, we may neither seem to have been too rash in assaulting you by our

may then justly punish you if the accusation prove true for we can hardly
suppose that you, who have been acquainted with the will of od and have
been hearers of those laws which he himself hath given us, now you are
separated from us, and gone to that patrimony of yours, which you, through the
grace of od, and that providence which he eercises over you, have obtained

us, and can introduce strange gods, and imitate the wicked practices of the
anaanites. ow this will appear to have been a small crime if you repent now,
and proceed no further in your madness, but pay a due reverence to, and keep

got out of the reach of od's power you are every where in places that belong

ake therefore such measures in this assembly, as supposing that your own
safety, and the safety of those that are dearest to you, is therein concerned, and
believe that it is better for you to be conquered by words, than to continue in
your purpose, and to eperience deeds and war therefore."
. hen hineas had discoursed thus, the governors of the assembly, and
the whole multitude, began to make an apology for themselves, concerning

that they owned one and the same common od with all the ebrews, and that

monument of our relation to you for ever, and a necessary caution to us to act

this was the occasion of our building this altar whence we beg you will have a

of the posterity of braham well worthy of perdition, in case they attempt to

. hen they had made this answer, and hineas had commended them for

as many of the common people as could be present and when they were come,

ehortation to them.
. o oshua, when he had thus discoursed to them, died, having lived a
hundred and ten years forty of which he lived with oses, in order to learn
what might be for his advantage afterwards. e also became their commander
after his death for twenty-five years. e was a man that wanted not wisdom nor
eloquence to declare his intentions to the people, but very eminent on both
accounts. e was of great courage and magnanimity in action and in dangers,
and very sagacious in procuring the peace of the people, and of great virtue at
all proper seasons. e was buried in the city of imnah, of the tribe of
phraim.    bout the same time died leazar the high priest, leaving the high
priesthood to his son hineas. is monument also, and sepulchre, are in the city
of abatha.

. fter the death of oshua and leazar, hineas prophesied,    that according
to od's will they should commit the government to the tribe of udah, and that
this tribe should destroy the race of the anaanites for then the people were
concerned to learn what was the will of od. hey also took to their assistance
the tribe of imeon but upon this condition, that when those that had been
tributary to the tribe of udah should be slain, they should do the like for the
tribe of imeon.
. ut the affairs of the anaanites were at this time in a flourishing
condition, and they epected the sraelites with a great army at the city ezek,
having put the government into the hands of donibezek, which name denotes
the ord of ezek, for doni in the ebrew tongue signifies ord. ow they
hoped to have been too hard for the sraelites, because oshua was dead but
when the sraelites had joined battle with them,  mean the two tribes before
mentioned, they fought gloriously, and slew above ten thousand of them, and
put the rest to flight and in the pursuit they took donibezek, who, when his
fingers and toes were cut off by them, said, "ay, indeed,  was not always to
lie concealed from od, as  find by what  now endure, while  have not been

ashamed to do the same to seventy-two kings."    o they carried him alive as
far as erusalem and when he was dead, they buried him in the earth, and went
on still in taking the cities and when they had taken the greatest part of them,
they besieged erusalem and when they had taken the lower city, which was
not under a considerable time, they slew all the inhabitants but the upper city
was not to be taken without great difficulty, through the strength of its walls,
and the nature of the place.
. or which reason they removed their camp to ebron and when they
had taken it, they slew all the inhabitants. here were till then left the race of
giants, who had bodies so large, and countenances so entirely different from

bones of these men are still shown to this very day, unlike to any credible
relations of other men. ow they gave this city to the evites as an
etraordinary reward, with the suburbs of two thousand cities but the land
