of oses. his aleb was one of the spies which oses sent into the land of
anaan. hey also gave land for habitation to the posterity of ethro, the
idianite, who was the father-in-law to oses for they had left their own
country, and followed them, and accompanied them in the wilderness.
. ow the tribes of udah and imeon took the cities which were in the
mountainous part of anaan, as also skelon and shdod, of those that lay near
the sea but aza and kron escaped them, for they, lying in a flat country, and
having a great number of chariots, sorely galled those that attacked them. o
these tribes, when they were grown very rich by this war, retired to their own
cities, and laid aside their weapons of war.
. ut the enjamites, to whom belonged erusalem, permitted its
inhabitants to pay tribute. o they all left off, the one to kill, and the other to
epose themselves to danger, and had time to cultivate the ground. he rest of
the tribes imitated that of enjamin, and did the same and, contenting
themselves with the tributes that were paid them, permitted the anaanites to
live in peace.
. owever, the tribe of phraim, when they besieged ethel, made no
advance, nor performed any thing worthy of the time they spent, and of the
pains they took about that siege yet did they persist in it, still sitting down
before the city, though they endured great trouble thereby but, after some time,
they caught one of the citizens that came to them to get necessaries, and they
gave him some assurances that, if he would deliver up the city to them, they
would preserve him and his kindred so he aware that, upon those terms, he
would put the city into their hands. ccordingly, he that, thus betrayed the city
was preserved with his family and the sraelites slew all the inhabitants, and
retained the city for themselves.

. fter this, the sraelites grew effeminate as to fighting any more against
their enemies, but applied themselves to the cultivation of the land, which
producing them great plenty and riches, they neglected the regular disposition
of their settlement, and indulged themselves in luury and pleasures nor were
they any longer careful to hear the laws that belonged to their political
government whereupon od was provoked to anger, and put them in mind,
first, how, contrary to his directions, they had spared the anaanites and, after
that, how those anaanites, as opportunity served, used them very barbarously.
ut the sraelites, though they were in heaviness at these admonitions from
od, yet were they still very unwilling to go to war and since they got large
tributes from the anaanites, and were indisposed for taking pains by their
luury, they suffered their aristocracy to be corrupted also, and did not ordain
themselves a senate, nor any other such magistrates as their laws had formerly
required, but they were very much given to cultivating their fields, in order to
get wealth which great indolence of theirs brought a terrible sedition upon
them, and they proceeded so far as to fight one against another, from the
following occasion—
. here was a evite    a man of a vulgar family, that belonged to the tribe
of phraim, and dwelt therein this man married a wife from ethlehem, which
is a place belonging to the tribe of udah. ow he was very fond of his wife,
and overcome with her beauty but he was unhappy in this, that he did not meet
with the like return of affection from her, for she was averse to him, which did
more inflame his passion for her, so that they quarreled one with another
perpetually and at last the woman was so disgusted at these quarrels, that she
left her husband, and went to her parents in the fourth month. he husband
being very uneasy at this her departure, and that out of his fondness for her,
came to his father and mother-in-law, and made up their quarrels, and was
reconciled to her, and lived with them there four days, as being kindly treated
by her parents. n the fifth day he resolved to go home, and went away in the
evening for his wife's parents were loath to part with their daughter, and
delayed the time till the day was gone. ow they had one servant that followed
them, and an ass on which the woman rode and when they were near
erusalem, having gone already thirty furlongs, the servant advised them to take
up their lodgings somewhere, lest some misfortune should befall them if they
traveled in the night, especially since they were not far off enemies, that season
often giving reason for suspicion of dangers from even such as are friends but
the husband was not pleased with this advice, nor was he willing to take up his
lodging among strangers, for the city belonged to the anaanites, but desired
rather to go twenty furlongs farther, and so to take their lodgings in some
sraelite city. ccordingly, he obtained his purpose, and came to ibeah, a city
of the tribe of enjamin, when it was just dark and while no one that lived in

the market-place invited him to lodge with him, there came an old man out of
the field, one that was indeed of the tribe of phraim, but resided in ibeah,
and met him, and asked him who he was, and for what reason he came thither
so late, and why he was looking out for provisions for supper when it was dark
o which he replied, that he was a evite, and was bringing his wife from her
parents, and was going home but he told him his habitation was in the tribe of
phraim so the old man, as well because of their kindred as because they lived
in the same tribe, and also because they had thus accidentally met together,
took him in to lodge with him. ow certain young men of the inhabitants of
ibeah, having seen the woman in the market-place, and admiring her beauty,
when they understood that she lodged with the old man, came to the doors, as
contemning the weakness and fewness of the old man's family and when the
old man desired them to go away, and not to offer any violence or abuse there,
they desired him to yield them up the strange woman, and then he should have
no harm done to him and when the old man alleged that the evite was of his
kindred, and that they would be guilty of horrid wickedness if they suffered
themselves to be overcome by their pleasures, and so offend against their laws,
they despised his righteous admonition, and laughed him to scorn. hey also
threatened to kill him if he became an obstacle to their inclinations whereupon,
when he found himself in great distress, and yet was not willing to overlook his
guests, and see them abused, he produced his own daughter to them and told
them that it was a smaller breach of the law to satisfy their lust upon her, than
to abuse his guests, supposing that he himself should by this means prevent any
injury to be done to those guests. hen they no way abated of their earnestness
for the strange woman, but insisted absolutely on their desires to have her, he
entreated them not to perpetrate any such act of injustice but they proceeded to
take her away by force, and indulging still more the violence of their
inclinations, they took the woman away to their house, and when they had
satisfied their lust upon her the whole night, they let her go about daybreak. o
she came to the place where she had been entertained, under great affliction at
what had happened and was very sorrowful upon occasion of what she had
suffered, and durst not look her husband in the face for shame, for she
concluded that he would never forgive her for what she had done so she fell
down, and gave up the ghost but her husband supposed that his wife was only
fast asleep, and, thinking nothing of a more melancholy nature had happened,
endeavored to raise her up, resolving to speak comfortably to her, since she did
not voluntarily epose herself to these men's lust, but was forced away to their
house but as soon as he perceived she was dead, he acted as prudently as the
greatness of his misfortunes would admit, and laid his dead wife upon the
beast, and carried her home and cutting her, limb by limb, into twelve pieces,
he sent them to every tribe, and gave it in charge to those that carried them, to

inform the tribes of those that were the causes of his wife's death, and of the
violence they had offered to her.
. pon this the people were greatly disturbed at what they saw, and at what
they heard, as never having had the eperience of such a thing before so they
gathered themselves to hiloh, out of a prodigious and a just anger, and
assembling in a great congregation before the tabernacle, they immediately
resolved to take arms, and to treat the inhabitants of ibeah as enemies but the
senate restrained them from doing so, and persuaded them, that they ought not
so hastily to make war upon people of the same nation with them, before they
discoursed them by words concerning the accusation laid against them it being
part of their law, that they should not bring an army against foreigners
themselves, when they appear to have been injurious, without sending an
ambassage first, and trying thereby whether they will repent or not and
accordingly they ehorted them to do what they ought to do in obedience to
their laws, that is, to send to the inhabitants of ibeah, to know whether they
would deliver up the offenders to them, and if they deliver them up, to rest
satisfied with the punishment of those offenders but if they despised the
message that was sent them, to punish them by taking up arms against them.
ccordingly they sent to the inhabitants of ibeah, and accused the young men
of the crimes committed in the affair of the evite's wife, and required of them
those that had done what was contrary to the law, that they might be punished,
as having justly deserved to die for what they had done but the inhabitants of
ibeah would not deliver up the young men, and thought it too reproachful to
them, out of fear of war, to submit to other men's demands upon them vaunting
themselves to be no way inferior to any in war, neither in their number nor in
courage. he rest of their tribe were also making great preparation for war, for
they were so insolently mad as also to resolve to repel force by force.
. hen it was related to the sraelites what the inhabitants of ibeah had
resolved upon, they took their oath that no one of them would give his daughter
in marriage to a enjamite, but make war with greater fury against them than
we have learned our forefathers made war against the anaanites and sent out
presently an army of four hundred thousand against them, while the
enjamites' army-was twenty-five thousand and si hundred five hundred of
whom were ecellent at slinging stones with their left hands, insomuch that
when the battle was joined at ibeah the enjamites beat the sraelites, and of
them there fell two thousand men and probably more had been destroyed had
not the night came on and prevented it, and broken off the fight so the
enjamites returned to the city with joy, and the sraelites returned to their
camp in a great fright at what had happened. n the net day, when they fought
again, the enjamites beat them and eighteen thousand of the sraelites were
slain, and the rest deserted their camp out of fear of a greater slaughter. o they

came to ethel,    a city that was near their camp, and fasted on the net day
and besought od, by hineas the high priest, that his wrath against them might
cease, and that he would be satisfied with these two defeats, and give them the
victory and power over their enemies. ccordingly od promised them so to
do, by the prophesying of hineas.
. hen therefore they had divided the army into two parts, they laid the
one half of them in ambush about the city ibeah by night, while the other half
attacked the enjamites, who retiring upon the assault, the enjamites pursued
them, while the ebrews retired by slow degrees, as very desirous to draw
them entirely from the city and the other followed them as they retired, till
both the old men and the young men that were left in the city, as too weak to
fight, came running out together with them, as willing to bring their enemies
under. owever, when they were a great way from the city the ebrews ran
away no longer, but turned back to fight them, and lifted up the signal they had
agreed on to those that lay in ambush, who rose up, and with a great noise fell
upon the enemy. ow, as soon as ever they perceived themselves to be
deceived, they knew not what to do and when they were driven into a certain
hollow place which was in a valley, they were shot at by those that
encompassed them, till they were all destroyed, ecepting si hundred, which
formed themselves into a close body of men, and forced their passage through
the midst of their enemies, and fled to the neighboring mountains, and, seizing
upon them, remained there but the rest of them, being about twenty-five
thousand, were slain. hen did the sraelites burn ibeah, and slew the women,
and the males that were under age and did the same also to the other cities of
the enjamites and, indeed, they were enraged to that degree, that they sent
twelve thousand men out of the army, and gave them orders to destroy abesh
ilead, because it did not join with them in fighting against the enjamites.
ccordingly, those that were sent slew the men of war, with their children and
wives, ecepting four hundred virgins. o such a degree had they proceeded in
their anger, because they not only had the suffering of the evite's wife to
avenge, but the slaughter of their own soldiers.
. owever, they afterward were sorry for the calamity they had brought
upon the enjamites, and appointed a fast on that account, although they
supposed those men had suffered justly for their offense against the laws so
they recalled by their ambassadors those si hundred which had escaped. hese
had seated themselves on a certain rock called immon, which was in the
wilderness. o the ambassadors lamented not only the disaster that had befallen
the enjamites, but themselves also, by this destruction of their kindred and
persuaded them to take it patiently and to come and unite with them, and not,
so far as in them lay, to give their suffrage to the utter destruction of the tribe of
enjamin and said to them, "e give you leave to take the whole land of

enjamin to yourselves, and as much prey as you are able to carry away with
you." o these men with sorrow confessed, that what had been done was
according to the decree of od, and had happened for their own wickedness
and assented to those that invited them, and came down to their own tribe. he
sraelites also gave them the four hundred virgins of abesh ilead for wives
but as to the remaining two hundred, they deliberated about it how they might
compass wives enough for them, and that they might have children by them
and whereas they had, before the war began, taken an oath, that no one would
give his daughter to wife to a enjamite, some advised them to have no regard
to what they had sworn, because the oath had not been taken advisedly and
judiciously, but in a passion, and thought that they should do nothing against
od, if they were able to save a whole tribe which was in danger of perishing
and that perjury was then a sad and dangerous thing, not when it is done out of
necessity, but when it is done with a wicked intention. ut when the senate
were affrighted at the very name of perjury, a certain person told them that he
could show them a way whereby they might procure the enjamites wives
enough, and yet keep their oath. hey asked him what his proposal was. e
said, "hat three times in a year, when we meet in hiloh, our wives and our
daughters accompany us let then the enjamites be allowed to steal away, and
marry such women as they can catch, while we will neither incite them nor
forbid them and when their parents take it ill, and desire us to inflict
punishment upon them, we will tell them, that they were themselves the cause
of what had happened, by neglecting to guard their daughters, and that they
ought not to be over angry at the enjamites, since that anger was permitted to
rise too high already." o the sraelites were persuaded to follow this advice,
and decreed, that the enjamites should be allowed thus to steal themselves
wives. o when the festival was coming on, these two hundred enjamites lay
in ambush before the city, by two and three together, and waited for the coming
of the virgins, in the vineyards and other places where they could lie concealed.
ccordingly the virgins came along playing, and suspected nothing of what
was coming upon them, and walked after an unguarded manner, so those that
laid scattered in the road, rose up, and caught hold of them by this means these
enjamites got them wives, and fell to agriculture, and took good care to
recover their former happy state. nd thus was this tribe of the enjamites,
after they had been in danger of entirely perishing, saved in the manner
forementioned, by the wisdom of the sraelites and accordingly it presently
flourished, and soon increased to be a multitude, and came to enjoy all other
degrees of happiness. nd such was the conclusion of this war.

,      
. ow it happened that the tribe of an suffered in like manner with the tribe
of enjamin and it came to do so on the occasion following—hen the
sraelites had already left off the eercise of their arms for war, and were intent
upon their husbandry, the anaanites despised them, and brought together an
army, not because they epected to suffer by them, but because they had a mind
to have a sure prospect of treating the ebrews ill when they pleased, and
might thereby for the time to come dwell in their own cities the more securely
they prepared therefore their chariots, and gathered their soldiery together, their
cities also combined together, and drew over to them skelon and kron,
which were within the tribe of udah, and many more of those that lay in the
plain. hey also forced the anites to fly into the mountainous country, and left
them not the least portion of the plain country to set their foot on. ince then
these anites were not able to fight them, and had not land enough to sustain
them, they sent five of their men into the midland country, to seek for a land to
which they might remove their habitation. o these men went as far as the
neighborhood of ount ibanus, and the fountains of the esser ordan, at the
great plain of idon, a day's journey from the city and when they had taken a
view of the land, and found it to be good and eceeding fruitful, they
acquainted their tribe with it, whereupon they made an epedition with the
army, and built there the city an, of the same name with the son of acob, and
of the same name with their own tribe.
. he sraelites grew so indolent, and unready of taking pains, that
misfortunes came heavier upon them, which also proceeded in part from their
contempt of the ivine worship for when they had once fallen off from the
regularity of their political government, they indulged themselves further in
living according to their own pleasure, and according to their own will, till they
were full of the evil doings that were common among the anaanites. od
therefore was angry with them, and they lost that their happy state which they
had obtained by innumerable labors, by their luury for when husan, king of
the ssyrians, had made war against them, they lost many of their soldiers in
the battle, and when they were besieged, they were taken by force nay, there
were some who, out of fear, voluntarily submitted to him, and though the
tribute laid upon them was more than they could bear, yet did they pay it, and
underwent all sort of oppression for eight years after which time they were
freed from them in the following manner—
. here was one whose name was thniel, the son of enaz, of the tribe of
udah, an active man and of great courage. e had an admonition from od not

to overlook the sraelites in such a distress as they were now in, but to endeavor
boldly to gain them their liberty so when he had procured some to assist him in
this dangerous undertaking, (and few they were, who, either out of shame at
their present circumstances, or out of a desire of changing them, could be
prevailed on to assist him,) he first of all destroyed that garrison which husan
had set over them but when it was perceived that he had not failed in his first
attempt, more of the people came to his assistance so they joined battle with
the ssyrians, and drove them entirely before them, and compelled them to
pass over uphrates. ereupon thniel, who had given such proofs of his valor,
received from the multitude authority to judge the people and when he had
ruled over them forty years, he died.
  
        , 
        
    
. . hen thniel was dead, the affairs of the sraelites fell again into
disorder and while they neither paid to od the honor due to him, nor were
obedient to the laws, their afflictions increased, till glon, king of the oabites,
did so greatly despise them on account of the disorders of their political
government, that he made war upon them, and overcame them in several
battles, and made the most courageous to submit, and entirely subdued their
army, and ordered them to pay him tribute. nd when he had built him a royal
palace at ericho,    he omitted no method whereby he might distress them and
indeed he reduced them to poverty for eighteen years. ut when od had once
taken pity of the sraelites, on account of their afflictions, and was moved to
compassion by their supplications put up to him, he freed them from the hard
usage they had met with under the oabites. his liberty he procured for them
in the following manner—
. here was a young man of the tribe of enjamin, whose name was hud,
the son of era, a man of very great courage in bold undertakings, and of a
very strong body, fit for hard labor, but best skilled in using his left hand, in
which was his whole strength and he also dwelt at ericho. ow this man
became familiar with glon, and that by means of presents, with which he
obtained his favor, and insinuated himself into his good opinion whereby he
was also beloved of those that were about the king. ow, when on a time, he
was bringing presents to the king, and had two servants with him, he put a
dagger on his right thigh secretly, and went in to him it was then summer time,

and the middle of the day, when the guards were not strictly on their watch,
both because of the heat, and because they were gone to dinner. o the young
man, when he had offered his presents to the king, who then resided in a small
parlor that stood conveniently to avoid the heat, fell into discourse with him,
for they were now alone, the king having bid his servants that attended him to
go their ways, because he had a mind to talk with hud. e was now sitting on
his throne and fear seized upon hud lest he should miss his stroke, and not
give him a deadly wound so he raised himself up, and said he had a dream to
impart to him by the command of od upon which the king leaped out of his
throne for joy of the dream so hud smote him to the heart, and leaving his
dagger in his body, he went out and shut the door after him. ow the king's
servants were very still, as supposing that the king had composed himself to
sleep.
. ereupon hud informed the people of ericho privately of what he had
done, and ehorted them to recover their liberty who heard him gladly, and
went to their arms, and sent messengers over the country, that should sound
trumpets of rams' horns for it was our custom to call the people together by
them. ow the attendants of glon were ignorant of what misfortune had
befallen him for a great while but, towards the evening, fearing some
uncommon accident had happened, they entered into his parlor, and when they
found him dead, they were in great disorder, and knew not what to do and
before the guards could be got together, the multitude of the sraelites came
upon them, so that some of them were slain immediately, and some were put to
flight, and ran away toward the country of oab, in order to save themselves.
heir number was above ten thousand. he sraelites seized upon the ford of
ordan, and pursued them, and slew them, and many of them they killed at the
ford, nor did one of them escape out of their hands and by this means it was
that the ebrews freed themselves from slavery under the oabites. hud also
was on this account dignified with the government over all the multitude, and
died after he had held the government eighty years.    e was a man worthy of
commendation, even besides what he deserved for the forementioned act of his.
fter him hamgar, the son of nath, was elected for their governor, but died in
the first year of his government.
  
       
       
    ,

. nd now it was that the sraelites, taking no warning by their former
misfortunes to amend their manners, and neither worshipping od nor
submitting to the laws, were brought under slavery by abin, the king of the
anaanites, and that before they had a short breathing time after the slavery
under the oabites for this abin out of azor, a city that was situate over the
emechonitis, and had in pay three hundred footmen, and ten thousand
horsemen, with fewer than three thousand chariots. isera was commander of
all his army, and was the principal person in the king's favor. e so sorely beat
the sraelites when they fought with him, that he ordered them to pay tribute.
. o they continued to that hardship for twenty years, as not good enough
of themselves to grow wise by their misfortunes. od was willing also hereby
the more to subdue their obstinacy and ingratitude towards himself so when at
length they were become penitent, and were so wise as to learn that their
calamities arose from their contempt of the laws, they besought eborah, a
certain prophetess among them, (which name in the ebrew tongue signifies a
ee,) to pray to od to take pity on them, and not to overlook them, now they
were ruined by the anaanites. o od granted them deliverance, and chose
them a general, arak, one that was of the tribe of aphtali. ow arak, in the
ebrew tongue, signifies ightning.
. o eborah sent for arak, and bade him choose out ten thousand young
men to go against the enemy, because od had said that that number was
sufficient, and promised them victory. ut when arak said that he would not
be the general unless she would also go as a general with him, she had
indignation at what he said "hou,  arak, deliverest up meanly that authority
which od hath given thee into the hand of a woman, and  do not reject it!" o
they collected ten thousand men, and pitched their camp at ount abor,
where, at the king's command, isera met them, and pitched his camp not far
from the enemy whereupon the sraelites, and arak himself, were so
affrighted at the multitude of those enemies, that they were resolved to march
off, had not eborah retained them, and commanded them to fight the enemy
that very day, for that they should conquer them, and od would be their
assistance.
. o the battle began and when they were come to a close fight, there
came down from heaven a great storm, with a vast quantity of rain and hail, and
the wind blew the rain in the face of the anaanites, and so darkened their eyes,
that their arrows and slings were of no advantage to them, nor would the
coldness of the air permit the soldiers to make use of their swords while this
storm did not so much incommode the sraelites, because it came in their backs.
hey also took such courage, upon the apprehension that od was assisting
them, that they fell upon the very midst of their enemies, and slew a great

number of them so that some of them fell by the sraelites, some fell by their
own horses, which were put into disorder, and not a few were killed by their
own chariots. t last isera, as soon as he saw himself beaten, fled away, and
came to a woman whose name was ael, a enite, who received him, when he
desired to be concealed and when he asked for somewhat to drink, she gave
him sour milk, of which he drank so unmeasurably that he fell asleep but when
he was asleep, ael took an iron nail, and with a hammer drove it through his
temples into the floor and when arak came a little afterward, she showed
isera nailed to the ground and thus was this victory gained by a woman, as
eborah had foretold. arak also fought with abin at azor and when he met
with him, he slew him and when the general was fallen, arak overthrew the
city to the foundation, and was the commander of the sraelites for forty years.
  
        
    ,   
        
,        
. ow when arak and eborah were dead, whose deaths happened about the
same time, afterwards the idianites called the malekites and rabians to
their assistance, and made war against the sraelites, and were too hard for
those that fought against them and when they had burnt the fruits of the earth,
they carried off the prey. ow when they had done this for three years, the
multitude of the sraelites retired to the mountains, and forsook the plain
country. hey also made themselves hollows under ground, and caverns, and
preserved therein whatsoever had escaped their enemies for the idianites
made epeditions in harvest-time, but permitted them to plough the land in
winter, that so, when the others had taken the pains, they might have fruits for
them to carry away. ndeed, there ensued a famine and a scarcity of food upon
which they betook themselves to their supplications to od, and besought him
to save them.
. ideon also, the son of oash, one of the principal persons of the tribe of
anasseh, brought his sheaves of corn privately, and thrashed them at the
wine-press for he was too fearful of their enemies to thrash them openly in the
thrashing-floor. t this time somewhat appeared to him in the shape of a young
man, and told him that he was a happy man, and beloved of od. o which he
immediately replied, " mighty indication of od's favor to me, that  am
forced to use this wine-press instead of a thrashing-floor!" ut the appearance

ehorted him to be of good courage, and to make an attempt for the recovery of
their liberty. e answered, that it was impossible for him to recover it, because
the tribe to which he belonged was by no means numerous and because he was
but young himself, and too inconsiderable to think of such great actions. ut
the other promised him, that od would supply what he was defective in, and
would afford the sraelites victory under his conduct.
. ow, therefore, as ideon was relating this to some young men, they
believed him, and immediately there was an army of ten thousand men got
ready for fighting. ut od stood by ideon in his sleep, and told him that
mankind were too fond of themselves, and were enemies to such as ecelled in
virtue. ow that they might not pass od over, but ascribe the victory to him,
and might not fancy it obtained by their own power, because they were a great
many, and able of themselves to fight their enemies, but might confess that it
was owing to his assistance, he advised him to bring his army about noon, in
the violence of the heat, to the river, and to esteem those that bent down on
their knees, and so drank, to be men of courage but for all those that drank
tumultuously, that he should esteem them to do it out of fear, and as in dread of
their enemies. nd when ideon had done as od had suggested to him, there
were found three hundred men that took water with their hands tumultuously
so od bid him take these men, and attack the enemy. ccordingly they pitched
their camp at the river ordan, as ready the net day to pass over it.
. ut ideon was in great fear, for od had told him beforehand that he
should set upon his enemies in the night-time but od, being willing to free
him from his fear, bid him take one of his soldiers, and go near to the
idianites' tents, for that he should from that very place have his courage
raised, and grow bold. o he obeyed, and went and took his servant hurah
with him and as he came near to one of the tents, he discovered that those that
were in it were awake, and that one of them was telling to his fellow soldier a
dream of his own, and that so plainly that ideon could hear him. he dream
was this—e thought he saw a barley-cake, such a one as could hardly be
eaten by men, it was so vile, rolling through the camp, and overthrowing the
royal tent, and the tents of all the soldiers. ow the other soldier eplained this
vision to mean the destruction of the army and told them what his reason was
which made him so conjecture, viz. hat the seed called barley was all of it
allowed to be of the vilest sort of seed, and that the sraelites were known to be
the vilest of all the people of sia, agreeably to the seed of barley, and that
what seemed to look big among the sraelites was this ideon and the army that
was with him "and since thou sayest thou didst see the cake overturning our
tents,  am afraid lest od hath granted the victory over us to ideon."
. hen ideon had heard this dream, good hope and courage came upon
him and he commanded his soldiers to arm themselves, and told them of this

vision of their enemies. hey also took courage at what was told them, and
were ready to perform what he should enjoin them. o ideon divided his army
into three parts, and brought it out about the fourth watch of the night, each part
containing a hundred men they all bare empty pitchers and lighted lamps in
their hands, that their onset might not be discovered by their enemies. hey had
also each of them a ram's horn in his right hand, which he used instead of a
trumpet. he enemy's camp took up a large space of ground, for it happened
that they had a great many camels and as they were divided into different
nations, so they were all contained in one circle. ow when the ebrews did as
they were ordered beforehand, upon their approach to their enemies, and, on
the signal given, sounded with their rams' horns, and brake their pitchers, and
set upon their enemies with their lamps, and a great shout, and cried, "ictory
to ideon, by od's assistance," a disorder and a fright seized upon the other
men while they were half asleep, for it was night-time, as od would have it
so that a few of them were slain by their enemies, but the greatest part by their
own soldiers, on account of the diversity of their language and when they were
once put into disorder, they killed all that they met with, as thinking them to be
enemies also. hus there was a great slaughter made. nd as the report of
ideon's victory came to the sraelites, they took their weapons and pursued
their enemies, and overtook them in a certain valley encompassed with torrents,
a place which these could not get over so they encompassed them, and slew
them all, with their kings, reb and eeb. ut the remaining captains led those
soldiers that were left, which were about eighteen thousand, and pitched their
camp a great way off the sraelites. owever, ideon did not grudge his pains,
but pursued them with all his army, and joining battle with them, cut off the
whole enemies' army, and took the other leaders, eba and almuna, and made
them captives. ow there were slain in this battle of the idianites, and of their
auiliaries the rabians, about a hundred and twenty thousand and the
ebrews took a great prey, gold, and silver, and garments, and camels, and
asses. nd when ideon was come to his own country of phrah, he slew the
kings of the idianites.
. owever, the tribe of phraim was so displeased at the good success of
ideon, that they resolved to make war against him, accusing him because he
did not tell them of his epedition against their enemies. ut ideon, as a man
of temper, and that ecelled in every virtue, pleaded, that it was not the result of
his own authority or reasoning, that made him attack the enemy without them
but that it was the command of od, and still the victory belonged to them as
well as those in the army. nd by this method of cooling their passions, he
brought more advantage to the ebrews, than by the success he had against
these enemies, for he thereby delivered them from a sedition which was arising
among them yet did this tribe afterwards suffer the punishment of this their

injurious treatment of ideon, of which we will give an account in due time.
. ereupon ideon would have laid down the government, but was over-
persuaded to take it, which he enjoyed forty years, and distributed justice to
them, as the people came to him in their differences and what he determined
was esteemed valid by all. nd when he died, he was buried in his own country
of phrah.
  
         
      
. ow ideon had seventy sons that were legitimate, for he had many wives
but he had also one that was spurious, by his concubine rumah, whose name
was bimelech, who, after his father's death, retired to hechem to his mother's
relations, for they were of that place and when he had got money of such of
them as were eminent for many instances of injustice, he came with them to his
father's house, and slew all his brethren, ecept otham, for he had the good
fortune to escape and be preserved but bimelech made the government
tyrannical, and constituted himself a lord, to do what he pleased, instead of
obeying the laws and he acted most rigidly against those that were the patrons
of justice.
. ow when, on a certain time, there was a public festival at hechem, and
all the multitude was there gathered together, otham his brother, whose escape
we before related, went up to ount erizzim, which hangs over the city
hechem, and cried out so as to be heard by the multitude, who were attentive
to him. e desired they would consider what he was going to say to them so
when silence was made, he said, that when the trees had a human voice, and
there was an assembly of them gathered together, they desired that the fig-tree
would rule over them but when that tree refused so to do, because it was
contented to enjoy that honor which belonged peculiarly to the fruit it bare, and
not that which should be derived to it from abroad, the trees did not leave off
their intentions to have a ruler, so they thought proper to make the offer of that
honor to the vine but when the vine was chosen, it made use of the same words
which the fig-tree had used before, and ecused itself from accepting the
government and when the olive-tree had done the same, the brier, whom the
trees had desired to take the kingdom, (it is a sort of wood good for firing,) it
promised to take the government, and to be zealous in the eercise of it but
that then they must sit down under its shadow, and if they should plot against it
to destroy it, the principle of fire that was in it should destroy them. e told

them, that what he had said was no laughing matter for that when they had
eperienced many blessings from ideon, they overlooked bimelech, when
he overruled all, and had joined with him in slaying his brethren and that he
was no better than a fire himself. o when he had said this, he went away, and
lived privately in the mountains for three years, out of fear of bimelech.
.  little while after this festival, the hechemites, who had now repented
themselves of having slain the sons of ideon, drove bimelech away, both
from their city and their tribe whereupon he contrived how he might distress
their city. ow at the season of vintage, the people were afraid to go out and
gather their fruits, for fear bimelech should do them some mischief. ow it
happened that there had come to them a man of authority, one aal, that
sojourned with them, having his armed men and his kinsmen with him so the
hechemites desired that he would allow them a guard during their vintage
whereupon he accepted of their desires, and so the people went out, and aal
with them at the head of his soldiery. o they gathered their fruit with safety
and when they were at supper in several companies, they then ventured to curse
bimelech openly and the magistrates laid ambushes in places about the city,
and caught many of bimelech's followers, and destroyed them.
. ow there was one ebul, a magistrate of the hechemites, that had
entertained bimelech. e sent messengers, and informed him how much aal
had irritated the people against him, and ecited him to lay ambushes before the
city, for that he would persuade aal to go out against him, which would leave
it in his power to be revenged on him and when that was once done, he would
bring him to be reconciled to the city. o bimelech laid ambushes, and
himself lay with them. ow aal abode in the suburbs, taking little care of
himself and ebul was with him. ow as aal saw the armed men coming on,
he said to ebul, hat some armed men were coming but the other replied,
hey were only shadows of huge stones and when they were come nearer,
aal perceived what was the reality, and said, they were not shadows, but men
lying in ambush. hen said ebul, "idst not thou reproach bimelech for
cowardice why dost thou not then show how very courageous thou art thyself,
and go and fight him" o aal, being in disorder, joined battle with
bimelech, and some of his men fell whereupon he fled into the city, and took
his men with him. ut ebul managed his matters so in the city, that he
procured them to epel aal out of the city, and this by accusing him of
cowardice in this action with the soldiers of bimelech. ut bimelech, when
he had learned that the hechemites were again coming out to gather their
grapes, placed ambushes before the city, and when they were coming out, the
third part of his army took possession of the gates, to hinder the citizens from
returning in again, while the rest pursued those that were scattered abroad, and
so there was slaughter every where and when he had overthrown the city to the

very foundations, for it was not able to bear a siege, and had sown its ruins with
salt, he proceeded on with his army till all the hechemites were slain. s for
those that were scattered about the country, and so escaped the danger, they
were gathered together unto a certain strong rock, and settled themselves upon
it, and prepared to build a wall about it and when bimelech knew their
intentions, he prevented them, and came upon them with his forces, and laid
faggots of dry wood round the place, he himself bringing some of them, and by
his eample encouraging the soldiers to do the same. nd when the rock was
encompassed round about with these faggots, they set them on fire, and threw
in whatsoever by nature caught fire the most easily so a mighty flame was
raised, and nobody could fly away from the rock, but every man perished, with
their wives and children, in all about fifteen hundred men, and the rest were a
great number also. nd such was the calamity which fell upon the hechemites
and men's grief on their account had been greater than it was, had they not
brought so much mischief on a person who had so well deserved of them, and
had they not themselves esteemed this as a punishment for the same.
. ow bimelech, when he had affrighted the sraelites with the miseries
he had brought upon the hechemites, seemed openly to affect greater authority
than he now had, and appeared to set no bounds to his violence, unless it were
with the destruction of all. ccordingly he marched to hebes, and took the city
on the sudden and there being a great tower therein, whereunto the whole
multitude fled, he made preparation to besiege it. ow as he was rushing with
violence near the gates, a woman threw a piece of a millstone upon his head,
upon which bimelech fell down, and desired his armor-bearer to kill him lest
his death should be thought to be the work of a woman—who did what he was
bid to do. o he underwent this death as a punishment for the wickedness he
had perpetrated against his brethren, and his insolent barbarity to the
hechemites. ow the calamity that happened to those hechemites was
according to the prediction of otham, owever, the army that was with
bimelech, upon his fall, was scattered abroad, and went to their own homes.
. ow it was that air the ileadite,    of the tribe of anasseh, took the
government. e was a man happy in other respects also, but particularly in his
children, who were of a good character. hey were thirty in number, and very
skilful in riding on horses, and were intrusted with the government of the cities
of ilead. e kept the government twenty-two years, and died an old man and
he was buried in amon, a city of ilead.

. fter bdon was dead, the hilistines overcame the sraelites, and received

. here was one anoah, a person of such great virtue, that he had few

. o the woman proved with child, and was careful to observe the

. owever, he at length    transgressed the laws of his country, and altered

. ut in process of time amson's hair grew again. nd there was a public

. ow after the death of amson, li the high priest was governor of the

. hen uth was come with her mother-in-law to ethlehem, ooz, who

. nd now upon the ill state of the affairs of the ebrews, they made war

.  will first despatch what  have to say about the prophet amuel, and

. ut amuel the prophet, when he had ordered the affairs of the people after
a convenient manner, and had appointed a city for every district of them, he

one with another determined in them, he himself going over those cities twice

order for a long time.
. ut afterwards he found himself oppressed with old age, and not able to
do what he used to do, so he committed the government and the care of the

younger was biah. e also enjoined them to reside and judge the people, the
one at the city of ethel, and the other at eersheba, and divided the people

men afford us an evident eample and demonstration how some children are
not of the like dispositions with their parents but sometimes perhaps good and
moderate, though born of wicked parents and sometimes showing themselves
to be wicked, though born of good parents for these men turning aside from
their father's good courses, and taking a course that was contrary to them,

determinations not according to truth, but according to bribery, and turned aside

contrary to the will of the prophet their father, who had taken a great deal of
care, and made a very careful provision that the multitude should be righteous.
. ut the people, upon these injuries offered to their former constitution

notions in his mind.
. hile he was thus disposed, od appeared to him, and comforted him,

. hen amuel had heard this, he called the ews early in the morning,

. ut the multitude was still so foolish as to be deaf to these predictions of

. here was one of the tribe of enjamin, a man of a good family, and of a
virtuous disposition his name was ish. e had a son, a young man of a

. ow as soon as it was day, amuel raised up aul out of his bed, and

hese three men will salute thee, and speak kindly to thee, and will give thee

of happiness nd when these signs have happened to thee, know that od is

when  send for thee to ilgal, that we may offer thank-offerings to od for
these blessings." hen amuel had said this, and foretold these things, he sent

of amuel.
. ut as soon as aul came into the house of his kinsman bner, whom

natured and envious at those that are in eminent stations.
. hen amuel called the people together to the city izpeh, and spake to

. hen the ebrews had so done, the lot fell upon the tribe of enjamin

. hen said the prophet, "od gives you this man to be your king see how

. fter one month, the war which aul had with ahash, the king of the

left eyes were covered by their shields, they might be wholly useless in war.

. o ahash, contemning the multitude of the ileadites and the answer

. nd when amuel had told them that he ought to confirm the kingdom to

. ow aul chose out of the multitude about three thousand men, and he took

and laid waste the country of the ebrews, while king aul and his son

more than si hundred men with them. ut as he, and his son, and biah the

. ow aul's watchmen told the king that the camp of the hilistines was

. o when aul was desirous of leading his men to the enemy's camp

. o aul, having slain about sity thousand of the enemy, returned home

. ow amuel came unto aul, and said to him, that he was sent by od to
put him in mind that od had preferred him before all others, and ordained him
king that he therefore ought to be obedient to him, and to submit to his
authority, as considering, that though he had the dominion over the other tribes,
yet that od had the dominion over him, and over all things. hat accordingly

gypt, they were making their way to that country which is now their own, 
enjoin thee to punish the malekites, by making war upon them and when thou

require this as a punishment to be inflicted upon them for the mischief they did

reserve any of them for your own advantage and possession, but to devote them
universally to od, and, in obedience to the commands of oses, to blot out
the name of malek entirely." 
 . o aul promised to do what he was commanded and supposing that his
obedience to od would be shown, not only in making war against the

malekites, but more fully in the readiness and quickness of his proceedings,
he made no delay, but immediately gathered together all his forces and when

chiefly compassionate but aul preserved their king and governor from the

. ut when aul had conquered all these malekites that reached from
elusium of gypt to the ed ea, he laid waste all the rest of the enemy's
country but for the nation of the hechemites, he did not touch them, although

. ereupon aul returned home with joy, for the glorious things he had
done, and for the conquest of his enemies, as though he had not neglected any

since injuries do not otherwise grow so great as by the easy tempers of those

hen the king saw him, he ran to him, and embraced him, and said, " return

herefore take notice, that thou art under the wrath of od, for thou hast

. ut then aul was so desirous to retain amuel, that he took hold of his

. ow aul being sensible of the miserable condition he had brought himself

. o amuel, when he had given him these admonitions, went away. ut

. ow the hilistines gathered themselves together again no very long time
afterward and having gotten together a great army, they made war against the

sraelites and having seized a place between hochoh and zekah, they there
pitched their camp. aul also drew out his army to oppose them and by

former camp, and to encamp themselves upon such another hill, over-against

on which they lay, divided their camps asunder. ow there came down a man

a man of vast bulk, for he was of four cubits and a span in tallness, and had
about him weapons suitable to the largeness of his body, for he had a
breastplate on that weighed five thousand shekels he had also a helmet and

of si hundred shekels and many followed him to carry his armor. herefore

net day he came again, and used the same words, and did not leave off for

. ow while this war between the ebrews and the hilistines was going

. ow aul wondered at the boldness and alacrity of avid, but durst not

eperience already of his assistance for  once pursued after and caught a lion

 took him by the tail, and dashed him against the ground. n the same manner

. owever, aul prayed that the end might be, by od's assistance, not

the fight." o he put about him his breastplate, and girded on his sword, and

comest to me with a sword, and with a spear, and with a breastplate but  have
od for my armor in coming against thee, who will destroy thee and all thy

. ow the women were an occasion of aul's envy and hatred to avid for

. owever, aul was not disposed to persevere long in the state wherein he

.    ow this king aul, by perpetrating so barbarous a crime, and
murdering the whole family of the high-priestly dignity, by having no pity of
the infants, nor reverence for the aged, and by overthrowing the city which od
had chosen for the property, and for the support of the priests and prophets
which were there, and had ordained as the only city allotted for the education of
such men, gives all to understand and consider the disposition of men, that
while they are private persons, and in a low condition, because it is not in their
power to indulge nature, nor to venture upon what they wish for, they are
equitable and moderate, and pursue nothing but what is just, and bend their
whole minds and labors that way then it is that they have this belief about od,
that he is present to all the actions of their lives, and that he does not only see
the actions that are done, but clearly knows those their thoughts also, whence
those actions do arise. ut when once they are advanced into power and
authority, then they put off all such notions, and, as if they were no other than
actors upon a theatre, they lay aside their disguised parts and manners, and take
up boldness, insolence, and a contempt of both human and ivine laws, and
this at a time when they especially stand in need of piety and righteousness,
because they are then most of all eposed to envy, and all they think, and all
they say, are in the view of all men then it is that they become so insolent in
their actions, as though od saw them no longer, or were afraid of them
because of their power and whatsoever it is that they either are afraid of by the
rumors they hear, or they hate by inclination, or they love without reason, these

. hen aul heard this, he could not speak for grief, and fell down on the

. ow it is but just to recommend the generosity of this woman,    because
when the king had forbidden her to use that art whence her circumstances were

serve those from whom they may receive some advantage. t would be well

me all opportunity of discoursing on what is for the advantage of cities, and

them all in the prosecution of virtue and is capable of showing them the of
acquiring glory, and an everlasting fame and of imprinting in the kings of
nations, and the rulers of cities, great inclination and diligence of doing well as

of instructing them how to despise all the most terrible adversities and  have a
fair occasion offered me to enter on such a discourse by aul the king of the
ebrews for although he knew what was coming upon him, and that he was to
die immediately, by the prediction of the prophet, he did not resolve to fly from
death, nor so far to indulge the love of life as to betray his own people to the
enemy, or to bring a disgrace on his royal dignity but eposing himself, as well
as all his family and children, to dangers, he thought it a brave thing to fall
together with them, as he was fighting for his subjects, and that it was better his
sons should die thus, showing their courage, than to leave them to their
uncertain conduct afterward, while, instead of succession and posterity, they
gained commendation and a lasting name. uch a one alone seems to me to be a
just, a courageous, and a prudent man and when any one has arrived at these
dispositions, or shall hereafter arrive at them, he is the man that ought to be by
all honored with the testimony of a virtuous or courageous man for as to those
that go out to war with hopes of success, and that they shall return safe,
supposing they should have performed some glorious action,  think those do
not do well who call these valiant men, as so many historians and other writers
who treat of them are wont to do, although  confess those do justly deserve
some commendation also but those only may be styled courageous and bold in
great undertakings, and despisers of adversities, who imitate aul for as for
those that do not know what the event of war will be as to themselves, and
though they do not faint in it, but deliver themselves up to uncertain futurity,
and are tossed this way and that way, this is not so very eminent an instance of
a generous mind, although they happen to perform many great eploits but
when men's minds epect no good event, but they know beforehand they must
die, and that they must undergo that death in the battle also, after this neither to
be affrighted, nor to be astonished at the terrible fate that is coming, but to go
directly upon it, when they know it beforehand, this it is that  esteem the
character of a man truly courageous. ccordingly this aul did, and thereby

daughter of aul, who was our first king, saw him so doing, she laughed at him.
ut when they had brought in the ark, they placed it under the tabernacle which
avid had pitched for it, and he offered costly sacrifices and peace-offerings,
and treated the whole multitude, and dealt both to the women, and the men, and
the infants a loaf of bread and a cake, and another cake baked in a pan, with the
portion of the sacrifice. o when he had thus feasted the people, he sent them
away, and he himself returned to his own house.
. ut when ichal his wife, the daughter of aul, came and stood by him,
she wished him all other happiness, and entreated that whatsoever he should

might be favorable to him yet did she blame him, that so great a king as he was

ashamed to do what was acceptable to od, who had preferred him before her

any regard to what the handmaidens and she herself thought of it. o this
ichal, who was avid's wife, had no children however, when she was

. ow when the king saw that his affairs grew better almost every day, by

he would not permit him to build him that temple, because he had made many

the ebrews in procuring them the liberty they enjoyed. nd when he had said
thus, and had sung a hymn of praise to od, he went his way.

.  little while after this, he considered that he ought to make war against the
hilistines, and not to see any idleness or laziness permitted in his management,
that so it might prove, as od had foretold to him, that when he had overthrown
his enemies, he should leave his posterity to reign in peace afterward so he
called together his army again, and when he had charged them to be ready and
prepared for war, and when he thought that all things in his army were in a
good state, he removed from erusalem, and came against the hilistines and
when he had overcome them in battle, and had cut off a great part of their
country, and adjoined it to the country of the ebrews, he transferred the war to
the oabites and when he had overcome two parts of their army in battle, he
took the remaining part captive, and imposed tribute upon them, to be paid
annually. e then made war against adadezer, the son of ehob, king of
ophene and when he had joined battle with him at the river uphrates, he
destroyed twenty thousand of his footmen, and about seven thousand of his
horsemen. e also took a thousand of his chariots, and destroyed the greatest

part of them, and ordered that no more than one hundred should be kept. 
 . ow when adad,    king of amascus and of yria, heard that avid
fought against adadezer, who was his friend, he came to his assistance with a
powerful army, in hopes to rescue him and when he had joined battle with
avid at the river uphrates, he failed of his purpose, and lost in the battle a

this king in the fourth book of his histories where he speaks thus " great
while after these things had happened, there was one of that country whose

. ow when avid had made an epedition against amascus, and the

which was called he razen] ea, and those most curious lavers, when he
built the temple for od.
. ut when the king of amath was informed of the ill success of
adadezer, and had heard of the ruin of his army, he was afraid on his own
account, and resolved to make a league of friendship and fidelity with avid

before he should come against him so he sent to him his son oram, and

for bishai destroyed eighteen thousand of them in the battle whereupon the

elder sons were near his body, and had the care of it also.
. e also called to mind the covenants and the oaths he had made with
onathan, the son of aul, and the friendship and affection onathan had for
him for besides all the rest of his ecellent qualities with which he was
endowed, he was also eceeding mindful of such as had at other times
bestowed benefits upon him. e therefore gave order that inquiry should be

return of that familiar acquaintance which onathan had had with him, and for
which he was still debtor. nd when one of aul's freed men was brought to
him, who was acquainted with those of his family that were still living, he

son of onathan brought up, and sent for him to come to him. o when
ephibosheth came to the king, he fell on his face and worshipped him but

avid encouraged him, bade him be of good cheer, and epect better times. o
he gave him his father's house, and all the estate which his grandfather aul

and never to be absent one day from that table. nd when the youth had

iba, and told him that he had given the youth his father's house, and all aul's
estate. e also ordered that iba should cultivate his land, and take care of it,
and bring him the profits of all to erusalem. ccordingly, avid brought him
to his table every day, and bestowed upon the youth, iba and his sons, who
were in number fifteen, and his servants, who were in number twenty. hen
the king had made these appointments, and iba had worshipped him, and

onathan dwelt at erusalem, and dieted at the king's table, and had the same
care that a son could claim taken of him. e also had himself a son, whom he
named icha.
  
         
 
. his were the honors that such as were left of aul's and onathan's lineage
received from avid. bout this time died ahash, the king of the mmonites,
who was a friend of avid's and when his son had succeeded his father in the
kingdom, avid sent ambassadors to him to comfort him and ehorted him to
take his father's death patiently, and to epect that he would continue the same
kindness to himself which he had shown to his father. ut the princes of the
mmonites took this message in evil part, and not as avid's kind dispositions
gave reason to take it and they ecited the king to resent it and said that avid
had sent men to spy out the country, and what strength it had, under the
pretence of humanity and kindness. hey further advised him to have a care,
and not to give heed to avid's words, lest he should be deluded by him, and so
fall into an inconsolable calamity. ccordingly ahash's son], the king of the
mmonites, thought these princes spake what was more probable than the truth
would admit, and so abused the ambassadors after a very harsh manner for he
shaved the one half of their beards, and cut off one half of their garments, and
sent his answer, not in words, but in deeds. hen the king of srael saw this, he
had indignation at it, and showed openly that he would not overlook this
injurious and contumelious treatment, but would make war with the
mmonites, and would avenge this wicked treatment of his ambassadors on

their king. o that king's intimate friends and commanders, understanding that
they had violated their league, and were liable to be punished for the same,
made preparations for war they also sent a thousand talents to the yrian king
of esopotamia, and endeavored to prevail with him to assist them for that pay,
and hobach. ow these kings had twenty thousand footmen. hey also hired
the king of the country called aacah, and a fourth king, by name shtob
which last had twelve thousand armed men.
. ut avid was under no consternation at this confederacy, nor at the
forces of the mmonites and putting his trust in od, because he was going to
war in a just cause, on account of the injurious treatment he had met with, he
immediately sent oab, the captain of his host, against them, and gave him the
flower of his army, who pitched his camp by abbah, the metropolis of the
mmonites whereupon the enemy came out, and set themselves in array, not
all of them together, but in two bodies for the auiliaries were set in array in
the plain by themselves, but the army of the mmonites at the gates over
against the ebrews. hen oab saw this, he opposed one stratagem against
another, and chose out the most hardy part of his men, and set them in
opposition to the king of yria, and the kings that were with him, and gave the
other part to his brother bishai, and bid him set them in opposition to the
mmonites and said to him, that in case he should see that the yrians
distressed him, and were too hard for him, he should order his troops to turn
about and assist him and he said that he himself would do the same to him, if
he saw him in the like distress from the mmonites. o he sent his brother
before, and encouraged him to do every thing courageously and with alacrity,
which would teach them to be afraid of disgrace, and to fight manfully and so
he dismissed him to fight with the mmonites, while he fell upon the yrians.
nd though they made a strong opposition for a while, oab slew many of
them, but compelled the rest to betake themselves to flight which, when the
mmonites saw, and were withal afraid of bishai and his army, they staid no
longer, but imitated their auiliaries, and fled to the city. o oab, when he had
thus overcome the enemy, returned with great joy to erusalem to the king.
. his defeat did not still induce the mmonites to be quiet, nor to own
those that were superior to them to be so, and be still, but they sent to
halaman, the king of the yrians, beyond uphrates, and hired him for an
auiliary. e had hobach for the captain of his host, with eighty thousand
footmen, and ten thousand horsemen. ow when the king of the ebrews
understood that the mmonites had again gathered so great an army together,

over the river ordan himself with all his army and when he met them he
joined battle with them, and overcame them, and slew forty thousand of their
footmen, and seven thousand of their horsemen. e also wounded hobach, the

general of halaman's forces, who died of that stroke but the people of

the mmonites, who overran all their country, and laid it waste, and shut them
up in their metropolis abbah, and besieged them therein.

 ,       
. ut avid fell now into a very grievous sin, though he were otherwise
naturally a righteous and a religious man, and one that firmly observed the laws
of our fathers for when late in an evening he took a view round him from the
roof of his royal palace, where he used to walk at that hour, he saw a woman
washing herself in her own house she was one of etraordinary beauty, and
therein surpassed all other women her name was athsheba. o he was
overcome by that woman's beauty, and was not able to restrain his desires, but
sent for her, and lay with her. ereupon she conceived with child, and sent to
the king, that he should contrive some way for concealing her sin (for,
according to the laws of their fathers, she who had been guilty of adultery
ought to be put to death). o the king sent for oab's armor-bearer from the
siege, who was the woman's husband, and his name was riah. nd when he
was come, the king inquired of him about the army, and about the siege and
when he had made answer that all their affairs went according to their wishes,
the king took some portions of meat from his supper, and gave them to him,
and bade him go home to his wife, and take his rest with her. riah did not do
so, but slept near the king with the rest of his armor-bearers. hen the king was
informed of this, he asked him why he did not go home to his house, and to his
wife, after so long an absence which is the natural custom of all men, when
they come from a long journey. e replied, that it was not right, while his
fellow soldiers, and the general of the army, slept upon the ground, in the camp,
and in an enemy's country, that he should go and take his rest, and solace
himself with his wife. o when he had thus replied, the king ordered him to
stay there that night, that he might dismiss him the net day to the general. o
the king invited riah to supper, and after a cunning and deterous manner
plied him with drink at supper, till he was thereby disordered yet did he
nevertheless sleep at the king's gates without any inclination to go to his wife.
pon this the king was very angry at him and wrote to oab, and commanded

him to punish riah, for he told him that he had offended him and he
suggested to him the manner in which he would have him punished, that it
might not be discovered that he was himself the author of this his punishment
for he charged him to set him over against that part of the enemy's army where
the attack would be most hazardous, and where he might be deserted, and be in
the greatest jeopardy, for he bade him order his fellow soldiers to retire out of
the fight. hen he had written thus to him, and sealed the letter with his own
seal, he gave it to riah to carry to oab. hen oab had received it, and upon
reading it understood the king's purpose, he set riah in that place where he
knew the enemy would be most troublesome to them and gave him for his
partners some of the best soldiers in the army and said that he would also come
to their assistance with the whole army, that if possible they might break down
some part of the wall, and enter the city. nd he desired him to be glad of the
opportunity of eposing himself to such great pains, and not to be displeased at
it, since he was a valiant soldier, and had a great reputation for his valor, both
with the king and with his countrymen. nd when riah undertook the work he
was set upon with alacrity, he gave private orders to those who were to be his
companions, that when they saw the enemy make a sally, they should leave
him. hen, therefore, the ebrews made an attack upon the city, the
mmonites were afraid that the enemy might prevent them, and get up into the
city, and this at the very place whither riah was ordered so they eposed their
best soldiers to be in the forefront, and opened their gates suddenly, and fell
upon the enemy with great vehemence, and ran violently upon them. hen
those that were with riah saw this, they all retreated backward, as oab had
directed them beforehand but riah, as ashamed to run away and leave his
post, sustained the enemy, and receiving the violence of their onset, he slew
many of them but being encompassed round, and caught in the midst of them,
he was slain, and some other of his companions were slain with him.
. hen this was done, oab sent messengers to the king, and ordered them
to tell him that he did what he could to take the city soon but that, as they
made an assault on the wall, they had been forced to retire with great loss and
bade them, if they saw the king was angry at it, to add this, that riah was slain
also. hen the king had heard this of the messengers, he took it heinously, and
said that they did wrong when they assaulted the wall, whereas they ought, by
undermining and other stratagems of war, to endeavor the taking of the city,
especially when they had before their eyes the eample of bimelech, the son
of ideon, who would needs take the tower in hebes by force, and was killed
by a large stone thrown at him by an old woman and although he was a man of
great prowess, he died ignominiously by the dangerous manner of his assault
that they should remember this accident, and not come near the enemy's wall,
for that the best method of making war with success was to call to mind the

accidents of former wars, and what good or bad success had attended them in
the like dangerous cases, that so they might imitate the one, and avoid the other.
ut when the king was in this disposition, the messenger told him that riah
was slain also whereupon he was pacified. o he bade the messenger go back
to oab and tell him that this misfortune is no other than what is common
among mankind, and that such is the nature, and such the accidents of war,
insomuch that sometimes the enemy will have success therein, and sometimes
others but that he ordered him to go on still in his care about the siege, that no
ill accident might befall him in it hereafter that they should raise bulwarks and
use machines in besieging the city and when they have gotten it, to overturn its
very foundations, and to destroy all those that are in it. ccordingly the
messenger carried the king's message with which he was charged, and made
haste to oab. ut athsheba, the wife of riah, when she was informed of the
death of her husband, mourned for his death many days and when her
mourning was over, and the tears which she shed for riah were dried up, the
king took her to wife presently and a son was born to him by her.
. ith this marriage od was not well pleased, but was thereupon angry at
avid and he appeared to athan the prophet in his sleep, and complained of
the king. ow athan was a fair and prudent man and considering that kings,
when they fall into a passion, are guided more by that passion than they are by
justice, he resolved to conceal the threatenings that proceeded from od, and
made a good-natured discourse to him, and this after the manner following—
e desired that the king would give him his opinion in the following case
—"here were," said he, "two men inhabiting the same city, the one of them
was rich, and the other poor]. he rich man had a great many flocks of cattle,
of sheep, and of kine but the poor man had but one ewe lamb. his he brought
up with his children, and let her eat her food with them and he had the same
natural affection for her which any one might have for a daughter. ow upon
the coming of a stranger to the rich man, he would not vouchsafe to kill any of
his own flocks, and thence feast his friend but he sent for the poor man's lamb,
and took her away from him, and made her ready for food, and thence feasted
the stranger." his discourse troubled the king eceedingly and he denounced
to athan, that "this man was a wicked man who could dare to do such a thing
and that it was but just that he should restore the lamb fourfold, and be
punished with death for it also." pon this athan immediately said that he was
himself the man who ought to suffer those punishments, and that by his own
sentence and that it was he who had perpetrated this great and horrid crime. e
also revealed to him, and laid before him, the anger of od against him, who
had made him king over the army of the ebrews, and lord of all the nations,
and those many and great nations round about him who had formerly delivered
him out of the hands of aul, and had given him such wives as he had justly

and legally married and now this od was despised by him, and affronted by
his impiety, when he had married, and now had, another man's wife and by
eposing her husband to the enemy, had really slain him that od would inflict
punishments upon him on account of those instances of wickedness that his
own wives should be forced by one of his sons and that he should be
treacherously supplanted by the same son and that although he had perpetrated
his wickedness secretly, yet should that punishment which he was to undergo
be inflicted publicly upon him "that, moreover," said he, "the child which was
born to thee of her shall soon die." hen the king was troubled at these
messages, and sufficiently confounded, and said with tears and sorrow that he
had sinned, (for he was without controversy a pious man, and guilty of no sin at
all in his whole life, ecepting those in the matter of riah,) od had
compassion on him, and was reconciled to him, and promised that he would
preserve to him both his life and his kingdom for he said that, seeing he
repented of the things he had done, he was no longer displeased with him. o
athan, when he had delivered this prophecy to the king, returned home.
. owever, od sent a dangerous distemper upon the child that was born
to avid of the wife of riah, at which the king was troubled, and did not take
any food for seven days, although his servants almost forced him to take it but
he clothed himself in a black garment, and fell down, and lay upon the ground
in sackcloth, entrusting od for the recovery of the child, for he vehemently
loved the child's mother but when, on the seventh day, the child was dead, the
king's servants durst not tell him of it, as supposing that when he knew it, he
would still less admit of food, and other care of himself, by reason of his grief
at the death of his son, since when the child was only sick, he so greatly
afflicted himself, and grieved for him but when the king perceived that his
servants were in disorder, and seemed to be affected, as those who are very
desirous to conceal something, he understood that the child was dead and
when he had called one of his servants to him, and discovered that so it was, he
arose up and washed himself, and took a white garment, and came into the
tabernacle of od. e also commanded them to set supper before him, and
thereby greatly surprised his kindred and servants, while he did nothing of this
when the child was sick, but did it all when he was dead. hereupon having
first begged leave to ask him a question, they besought him to tell them the
reason of this his conduct he then called them unskilful people, and instructed
them how he had hopes of the recovery of the child while it was alive, and
accordingly did all that was proper for him to do, as thinking by such means to
render od propitious to him but that when the child was dead, there was no
longer any occasion for grief, which was then to no purpose. hen he had said
this, they commended the king's wisdom and understanding. e then went in
unto athsheba his wife, and she conceived and bare a son and by the

command of athan the prophet called his name olomon.
. ut oab sorely distressed the mmonites in the siege, by cutting off
their waters, and depriving them of other means of subsistence, till they were in
the greatest want of meat and drink, for they depended only on one small well
of water, and this they durst not drink of too freely, lest the fountain should
entirely fail them. o he wrote to the king, and informed him thereof and
persuaded him to come himself to take the city, that he might have the honor of
the victory. pon this letter of oab's, the king accepted of his good-will and
fidelity, and took with him his army, and came to the destruction of abbah
and when he had taken it by force, he gave it to his soldiers to plunder it but he
himself took the king of the mmonites' crown, whose weight was a talent of
gold    and it had in its middle a precious stone called a sardony which crown
avid ever after wore on his own head. e also found many other vessels in the
city, and those both splendid and of great price but as for the men, he
tormented them,    and then destroyed them and when he had taken the other
cities of the mmonites by force, he treated them after the same manner.
  
    ,     
       
  
. hen the king was returned to erusalem, a sad misfortune befell his house,
on the occasion following e had a daughter, who was yet a virgin, and very
handsome, insomuch that she surpassed all the most beautiful women her
name was amar she had the same mother with bsalom. ow mnon,
avid's eldest son, fell in love with her, and being not able to obtain his desires,
on account of her virginity, and the custody she was under, was so much out of
order, nay, his grief so ate up his body, that he grew lean, and his color was
changed. ow there was one onad, a kinsman and friend of his, who
discovered this his passion, for he was an etraordinary wise man, and of great
sagacity of mind. hen, therefore, he saw that every morning mnon was not
in body as he ought to be, he came to him, and desired him to tell him what was
the cause of it however, he said that he guessed that it arose from the passion
of love. mnon confessed his passion, that he was in love with a sister of his,
who had the same father with himself. o onad suggested to him by what
method and contrivance he might obtain his desires for he persuaded him to
pretend sickness, and bade him, when his father should come to him, to beg of
him that his sister might come and minister to him for if that were done, he

. hen avid his father knew this, he was grieved at the actions of

ow the second year after this wicked affair about his sister was over, and

. ow avid had a design to send to bsalom, not that he should come to

. owever, the king sent a message to his son beforehand, as he was
