. ow bsalom, upon this his success with the king, procured to himself a

authority committed to him, he would distribute justice to them in a most

. mong them came hithophel the ilonite, a counsellor of avid's, and

. hen avid was gone a little farther, there met him iba, the servant of

. hen avid was at ahurim, a place so called, there came out a kinsman

nor is it any wonder that  am obliged to undergo these abuses from him, while
 eperience the like from an impious son of my own but perhaps od will

o he went on his way without troubling himself with himei, who ran along

ut when avid was come to ordan, he allowed those that were with him to

. ut when bsalom, and hithophel his counselor, were come to

ow this came to pass according to the prediction of athan, when he

. nd when bsalom had done what he was advised to by hithophel, he

. o ushai made haste to the high priests, adok and biathar, and told

. ut hithophel, on rejection of his advice, got upon his ass, and rode

that avid would overcome him, and return to his kingdom again so he said it

was better that he should take his own life away with freedom and

 ,    ,      
     
. nd this was the state of avid and his followers but bsalom got together
a vast army of the ebrews to oppose his father, and passed therewith over the

appointed masa to be captain of all his host, instead of oab his kinsman his
father was thra and his mother bigail now she and eruiah, the mother of

companion and friend, but one that came from the city ath and when he was

refusal of theirs was founded upon very wise reasons "or," said they, "if we

will naturally suppose that he hath another army with him." o avid was

pleased with this their advice, and resolved himself to tarry at ahanaim and

. hen did oab put his army in battle-array over against the enemy in the

. ow bsalom had erected for himself a marble pillar in the king's dale,

. ut now himaaz, the son of adok the high priest, went to oab, and

ut when himaaz again desired him to let him go as a messenger, and assured

. ow those ebrews that had been with bsalom, and had retired out of the

. ephibosheth also, aul's grandson, met avid, clothed in a sordid

hen he had said this, avid resolved neither to punish ephibosheth, nor to

. ut avid desired arzillai the ileadite, that great and good man, and

. ow the principal men of the country came to ilgal to him with a great

. hile these rulers were thus disputing one with another, a certain wicked

. o oab resolved to make no delay, but taking with him his brother, and

. fter this, when the country was greatly afflicted with a famine, avid

little afterward the king made war against the hilistines and when he had

. hen the king heard that the hilistines were gathered together at the

avid's most courageous men, behaved himself so as to deserve great
commendation, for he slew many of those that bragged they were the posterity

war again and when avid had sent an army against them, ephan his

ow a little while after this, the hilistines pitched their camp at a city which

finger than men naturally have. ow the person who was sent against them by

. nd now avid being freed from wars and dangers, and enjoying for the
future a profound peace,    composed songs and hymns to od of several sorts
of metre some of those which he made were trimeters, and some were
pentameters. e also made instruments of music, and taught the evites to sing

hymns to od, both on that called the abbath day, and on other festivals. ow
the construction of the instruments was thus he viol was an instrument of ten

and was played upon by the fingers the cymbals were broad and large
instruments, and were made of brass. nd so much shall suffice to be spoken

. ow all the men that were about avid were men of courage. hose that
were most illustrious and famous of them for their actions were thirty-eight of

manifest the virtues of the others also for these were powerful enough to

of chimaas, who frequently leaped upon the troops of the enemy, and did not

and till the sraelites, seeing the hilistines retire by his means, came down

when, in the wars against the hilistines, they pitched their camp at a place

he said that he would rather have it than a great deal of money. hen these

small number. ut when the water was brought to the king, he would not drink

gave him thanks for the salvation of the men. et to these was bishai, oab's
brother for he in one day slew si hundred. he fifth of these was enaiah, by
lineage a priest for being challenged by two] eminent men in the country of
oab, he overcame them by his valor. oreover, there was a man, by nation an
gyptian, who was of a vast bulk, and challenged him, yet did he, when he was
unarmed, kill him with his own spear, which he threw at him for he caught him

him with his own weapons. ne may also add this to the forementioned actions
of the same man, either as the principal of them in alacrity, or as resembling the
rest. hen od sent a snow, there was a lion who slipped and fell into a certain
pit, and because the pit's mouth was narrow it was evident he would perish,
being enclosed with the snow so when he saw no way to get out and save
himself, he roared. hen enaiah heard the wild beast, he went towards him,

. ow king avid was desirous to know how many ten thousands there were
of the people, but forgot the commands of oses,    who told them beforehand,
that if the multitude were numbered, they should pay half a shekel to od for
every head. ccordingly the king commanded oab, the captain of his host, to
go and number the whole multitude but when he said there was no necessity
for such a numeration, he was not persuaded to countermand it], but he
enjoined him to make no delay, but to go about the numbering of the ebrews
immediately. o oab took with him the heads of the tribes, and the scribes, and
went over the country of the sraelites, and took notice how numerous the
multitude were, and returned to erusalem to the king, after nine months and
twenty days and he gave in to the king the number of the people, without the
tribe of enjamin, for he had not yet numbered that tribe, no more than the
tribe of evi, for the king repented of his having sinned against od. ow the
number of the rest of the sraelites was nine hundred thousand men, who were
able to bear arms and go to war but the tribe of udah, by itself, was four

hundred thousand men.
. ow when the prophets had signified to avid that od was angry at
him, he began to entreat him, and to desire he would be merciful to him, and
forgive his sin. ut od sent athan the prophet to him, to propose to him the
election of three things, that he might choose which he liked best hether he
would have famine come upon the country for seven years, or would have a
war, and be subdued three months by his enemies or, whether od should send
a pestilence and a distemper upon the ebrews for three days ut as he was
fallen to a fatal choice of great miseries, he was in trouble, and sorely
confounded and when the prophet had said that he must of necessity make his
choice, and had ordered him to answer quickly, that he might declare what he
had chosen to od, the king reasoned with himself, that in case he should ask
for famine, he would appear to do it for others, and without danger to himself,
since he had a great deal of corn hoarded up, but to the harm of others that in
case he should choose to be overcome by his enemies] for three months, he
would appear to have chosen war, because he had valiant men about him, and
strong holds, and that therefore he feared nothing therefrom so he chose that
affliction which is common to kings and to their subjects, and in which the fear
was equal on all sides and said this beforehand, that it was much better to fall
into the hands of od, than into those of his enemies.
. hen the prophet had heard this, he declared it to od who thereupon
sent a pestilence and a mortality upon the ebrews nor did they die after one
and the same manner, nor so that it was easy to know what the distemper was.
ow the miserable disease was one indeed, but it carried them off by ten
thousand causes and occasions, which those that were afflicted could not
understand for one died upon the neck of another, and the terrible malady
seized them before they were aware, and brought them to their end suddenly,
some giving up the ghost immediately with very great pains and bitter grief,
and some were worn away by their distempers, and had nothing remaining to
be buried, but as soon as ever they fell were entirely macerated some were
choked, and greatly lamented their case, as being also stricken with a sudden
darkness some there were who, as they were burying a relation, fell down
dead,    without finishing the rites of the funeral. ow there perished of this
disease, which began with the morning, and lasted till the hour of dinner,
seventy thousand. ay, the angel stretched out his hand over erusalem, as
sending this terrible judgment upon it. ut avid had put on sackcloth, and lay
upon the ground, entreating od, and begging that the distemper might now
cease, and that he would be satisfied with those that had already perished. nd

make floats of them, and to sail to what place soever of thy country thou shalt
desire, and leave them there, after which thy subjects may carry them to
erusalem. ut do thou take care to procure us corn for this timber, which we
stand in need of, because we inhabit in an island  ."
. he copies of these epistles remain at this day, and are preserved not only
in our books, but among the yrians also insomuch that if any one would know
the certainty about them, he may desire of the keepers of the public records of
yre to show him them, and he will find what is there set down to agree with
what we have said.  have said so much out of a desire that my readers may
know that we speak nothing but the truth, and do not compose a history out of
some plausible relations, which deceive men and please them at the same time,

nor attempt to avoid eamination, nor desire men to believe us immediately
nor are we at liberty to depart from speaking truth, which is the proper
commendation of an historian, and yet be blameless but we insist upon no
admission of what we say, unless we be able to manifest its truth by
demonstration, and the strongest vouchers.
. ow king olomon, as soon as this epistle of the king of yre was

ount ebanon for one month and then to come home, and rest two months,
until the time when the other twenty thousand had finished their task at the

. olomon began to build the temple in the fourth year of his reign, on the

five hundred and ninety-two years after the odus out of gypt but one

. ow, therefore, the king laid the foundations of the temple very deep in

it before the temple its length was twenty cubits, and it was so ordered that it

and its height was raised as high as a hundred and twenty cubits. e also built
round about the temple thirty small rooms, which might include the whole

 and the same in length, but in height twenty. bove these there were other
rooms, and others above them, equal, both in their measures and number so

and truly every one of these rooms had a roof of their own, that was not

roof common to them all, and built with very long beams, that passed through

. ow when the king had divided the temple into two parts, he made the

wherefore olomon set them up not far from each other, that with one wing

. ow olomon sent for an artificer out of yre, whose name was iram

. olomon also cast a brazen sea, whose figure was that of a hemisphere.

here stood round about it twelve oen, that looked to the four winds of

. e also made ten brazen bases for so many quadrangular lavers the

here were four small quadrangular pillars that stood one at each corner these

he whole work was elevated, and stood upon four wheels, which were also

ny one who saw the spokes of the wheels, how eactly they were turned, and

way he also set the sea. ow he appointed the sea to be for washing the hands

. e also made a brazen altar, whose length was twenty cubits, and its

number of tables, but one that was large and made of gold, upon which they set

stood between them. ll these vessels were contained in that part of the holy

. he king also made pouring vessels, in number eighty thousand, and a

f the measures like those which oses called the in and the ssaron, (a

he golden censers, in which they carried the incense to the altar, were twenty

. olomon made all these things for the honor of od, with great variety
and magnificence, sparing no cost, but using all possible liberality in adorning
the temple and these things he dedicated to the treasures of od. e also
placed a partition round about the temple, which in our tongue we call ison,
but it is called hrigcos by the reeks, and he raised it up to the height of three
cubits and it was for the eclusion of the multitude from coming into the

and were shut by golden doors. nto this temple all the people entered that were

. hen king olomon had finished these works, these large and beautiful
buildings, and had laid up his donations in the temple, and all this in the

alacrity therein, insomuch that any one who saw it would have thought it must
have been an immense time ere it could have been finished and would be
surprised that so much should be finished in so short a time short,  mean, if
compared with the greatness of the work he also wrote to the rulers and elders
of the ebrews, and ordered all the people to gather themselves together to

od into it and when this invitation of the whole body of the people to come to
erusalem was every where carried abroad, it was the seventh month before
they came together which month is by our countrymen called hisri, but by the
acedonians yperberetoeus. he feast of tabernacles happened to fall at the
same time, which was celebrated by the ebrews as a most holy and most
eminent feast. o they carried the ark and the tabernacle which oses had
pitched, and all the vessels that were for ministration, to the sacrifices of od,
and removed them to the temple.    he king himself, and all the people and the
evites, went before, rendering the ground moist with sacrifices, and drink-
offerings, and the blood of a great number of oblations, and burning an

about was so full of these odors, that it met, in a most agreeable manner,

persons at a great distance, and was an indication of od's presence and, as
men's opinion was, of his habitation with them in this newly built and
consecrated place, for they did not grow weary, either of singing hymns or of
dancing, until they came to the temple and in this manner did they carry the
ark. ut when they should transfer it into the most secret place, the rest of the

contained nothing else but those two tables of stone that preserved the ten
commandments, which od spake to oses in ount inai, and which were
engraved upon them but they set the candlestick, and the table, and the golden
altar in the temple, before the most secret place, in the very same places

sacrifices but for the brazen altar, olomon set it before the temple, over

. ow as soon as the priests had put all things in order about the ark, and
were gone out, there came down a thick cloud, and stood there, and spread
itself, after a gentle manner, into the temple such a cloud it was as was diffused
and temperate, not such a rough one as we see full of rain in the winter season.
his cloud so darkened the place, that one priest could not discern another, but
it afforded to the minds of all a visible image and glorious appearance of od's
having descended into this temple, and of his having gladly pitched his
tabernacle therein. o these men were intent upon this thought. ut olomon
rose up, (for he was sitting before,) and used such words to od as he thought
agreeable to the ivine nature to receive, and fit for him to give for he said,
"hou hast an eternal house,  ord, and such a one as thou hast created for
thyself out of thine own works we know it to be the heaven, and the air, and
the earth, and the sea, which thou pervadest, nor art thou contained within their
limits.  have indeed built this temple to thee, and thy name, that from thence,
when we sacrifice, and perform sacred operations, we may send our prayers up
into the air, and may constantly believe that thou art present, and art not remote
from what is thine own for neither when thou seest all things, and hearest all
things, nor now, when it pleases thee to dwell here, dost thou leave the care of
all men, but rather thou art very near to them all, but especially thou art present
to those that address themselves to thee, whether by night or by day." hen he
had thus solemnly addressed himself to od, he converted his discourse to the
multitude, and strongly represented the power and providence of od to them
—how he had shown all things that were come to pass to avid his father, as

many of those things had already come to pass, and the rest would certainly
come to pass hereafter and how he had given him his name, and told to avid
what he should be called before he was born and foretold, that when he should
be king after his father's death, he should build him a temple, which since they
saw accomplished, according to his prediction, he required them to bless od,
and by believing him, from the sight of what they had seen accomplished,
never to despair of any thing that he had promised for the future, in order to
their happiness, or suspect that it would not come to pass.
. hen the king had thus discoursed to the multitude, he looked again
towards the temple, and lifting up his right hand to the multitude, he said, "t is
not possible by what men can do to return sufficient thanks to od for his
benefits bestowed upon them, for the eity stands in need of nothing, and is
above any such requital but so far as we have been made superior,  ord, to
other animals by thee, it becomes us to bless thy ajesty, and it is necessary for
us to return thee thanks for what thou hast bestowed upon our house, and on the
ebrew people for with what other instrument can we better appease thee
when thou art angry at us, or more properly preserve thy favor, than with our
voice which, as we have it from the air, so do we know that by that air it
ascends upwards towards thee].  therefore ought myself to return thee thanks
thereby, in the first place, concerning my father, whom thou hast raised from
obscurity unto so great joy and, in the net place, concerning myself, since
thou hast performed all that thou hast promised unto this very day. nd 
beseech thee for the time to come to afford us whatsoever thou,  od, hast
power to bestow on such as thou dost esteem and to augment our house for all
ages, as thou hast promised to avid my father to do, both in his lifetime and at
his death, that our kingdom shall continue, and that his posterity should
successively receive it to ten thousand generations. o not thou therefore fail to
give us these blessings, and to bestow on my children that virtue in which thou
delightest. nd besides all this,  humbly beseech thee that thou wilt let some
portion of thy pirit come down and inhabit in this temple, that thou mayst
appear to be with us upon earth. s to thyself, the entire heavens, and the
immensity of the things that are therein, are but a small habitation for thee,
much more is this poor temple so but  entreat thee to keep it as thine own
house, from being destroyed by our enemies for ever, and to take care of it as
thine own possession but if this people be found to have sinned, and be
thereupon afflicted by thee with any plague, because of their sin, as with death
or pestilence, or any other affliction which thou usest to inflict on those that
transgress any of thy holy laws, and if they fly all of them to this temple,
beseeching thee, and begging of time to deliver them, then do thou hear their
prayers, as being within thine house, and have mercy upon them, and deliver
them from their afflictions. ay, moreover, this help is what  implore of thee,

not for the ebrews only, when they are in distress, but when any shall come
hither from any ends of the world whatsoever, and shall return from their sins
and implore thy pardon, do thou then pardon them, and hear their prayer. or
hereby all shall learn that thou thyself wast pleased with the building of this
house for thee and that we are not ourselves of an unsociable nature, nor
behave ourselves like enemies to such as are not of our own people but are
willing that thy assistance should be communicated by thee to all men in
common, and that they may have the enjoyment of thy benefits bestowed upon
them."
. hen olomon had said this, and had cast himself upon the ground, and
worshipped a long time, he rose up, and brought sacrifices to the altar and
when he had filled it with unblemished victims, he most evidently discovered
that od had with pleasure accepted of all that he had sacrificed to him, for
there came a fire running out of the air, and rushed with violence upon the altar,
in the sight of all, and caught hold of and consumed the sacrifices. ow when
this ivine appearance was seen, the people supposed it to be a demonstration
of od's abode in the temple, and were pleased with it, and fell down upon the
ground and worshipped. pon which the king began to bless od, and ehorted
the multitude to do the same, as now having sufficient indications of od's
favorable disposition to them and to pray that they might always have the like
indications from him, and that he would preserve in them a mind pure from all
wickedness, in righteousness and religious worship, and that they might
continue in the observation of those precepts which od had given them by
oses, because by that means the ebrew nation would be happy, and indeed
the most blessed of all nations among all mankind. e ehorted them also to be
mindful, that by what methods they had attained their present good things, by
the same they must preserve them sure to themselves, and make them greater
and more than they were at present for that it was not sufficient for them to
suppose they had received them on account of their piety and righteousness, but
that they had no other way of preserving them for the time to come for that it is
not so great a thing for men to acquire somewhat which they want, as to
preserve what they have acquired, and to be guilty of no sin whereby it may be
hurt.
. o when the king had spoken thus to the multitude, he dissolved the
congregation, but not till he had completed his oblations, both for himself and
for the ebrews, insomuch that he sacrificed twenty and two thousand oen,
and a hundred and twenty thousand sheep for then it was that the temple did
first of all taste of the victims, and all the ebrews, with their wives and
children, feasted therein nay, besides this, the king then observed splendidly

. hen all these solemnities were abundantly satisfied, and nothing was

every one went to their own homes, giving thanks to the king for the care he
had taken of them, and the works he had done for them and praying to od to
preserve olomon to be their king for a long time. hey also took their journey
home with rejoicing, and making merry, and singing hymns to od. nd indeed
the pleasure they enjoyed took away the sense of the pains they all underwent
in their journey home. o when they had brought the ark into the temple, and
had seen its greatness, and how fine it was, and had been partakers of the many
sacrifices that had been offered, and of the festivals that had been solemnized,
they every one returned to their own cities. ut a dream that appeared to the
king in his sleep informed him that od had heard his prayers and that he
would not only preserve the temple, but would always abide in it that is, in
case his posterity and the whole multitude would be righteous. nd for himself,
it said, that if he continued according to the admonitions of his father, he would
advance him to an immense degree of dignity and happiness, and that then his
posterity should be kings of that country, of the tribe of udah, for ever but that
still, if he should be found a betrayer of the ordinances of the law, and forget
them, and turn away to the worship of strange gods, he would cut him off by
the roots, and would neither suffer any remainder of his family to continue, nor
would overlook the people of srael, or preserve them any longer from
afflictions, but would utterly destroy them with ten thousand wars and
misfortunes would cast them out of the land which he had given their fathers,
and make them sojourners in strange lands and deliver that temple which was
now built to be burnt and spoiled by their enemies, and that city to be utterly
overthrown by the hands of their enemies and make their miseries deserve to
be a proverb, and such as should very hardly be credited for their stupendous
magnitude, till their neighbors, when they should hear of them, should wonder
at their calamities, and very earnestly inquire for the occasion, why the
ebrews, who had been so far advanced by od to such glory and wealth,
should be then so hated by him and that the answer that should be made by the
remainder of the people should be, by confessing their sins, and their
transgression of the laws of their country. ccordingly we have it transmitted to
us in writing, that thus did od speak to olomon in his sleep.

. fter the building of the temple, which, as we have before said, was finished
in seven years, the king laid the foundation of his palace, which be did not
finish under thirteen years, for he was not equally zealous in the building of this
palace as he had been about the temple for as to that, though it was a great
work, and required wonderful and surprising application, yet od, for whom it
was made, so far co-operated therewith, that it was finished in the
forementioned number of years but the palace, which was a building much
inferior in dignity to the temple, both on account that its materials had not been
so long beforehand gotten ready, nor had been so zealously prepared, and on
account that this was only a habitation for kings, and not for od, it was longer
in finishing. owever, this building was raised so magnificently, as suited the
happy state of the ebrews, and of the king thereof. ut it is necessary that 
describe the entire structure and disposition of the parts, that so those that light
upon this book may thereby make a conjecture, and, as it were, have a prospect
of its magnitude.
. his house was a large and curious building, and was supported by many
pillars, which olomon built to contain a multitude for hearing causes, and
taking cognizance of suits. t was sufficiently capacious to contain a great body
of men, who would come together to have their causes determined. t was a
hundred cubits long, and fifty broad, and thirty high, supported by quadrangular
pillars, which were all of cedar but its roof was according to the orinthian
order,    with folding doors, and their adjoining pillars of equal magnitude, each
fluted with three cavities which building as at once firm, and very ornamental.
here was also another house so ordered, that its entire breadth was placed in
the middle it was quadrangular, and its breadth was thirty cubits, having a
temple over against it, raised upon massy pillars in which temple there was a
large and very glorious room, wherein the king sat in judgment. o this was
joined another house that was built for his queen. here were other smaller

to make fine prospects in royal palaces, and which make the mines whence they
are dug famous. ow the conteture of the curious workmanship of these
stones was in three rows, but the fourth row would make one admire its
sculptures, whereby were represented trees, and all sorts of plants with the
shades that arose from their branches, and leaves that hung down from them.
hose trees and plants covered the stone that was beneath them, and their
leaves were wrought so prodigious thin and subtle, that you would think they

invisible the curiosity of those that enjoyed the fresh air and the groves for the
most delightful prospect, for the avoiding the heat, and covering of their bodies.
nd, to say all in brief, olomon made the whole building entirely of white

and having si steps to it on every one of which stood, on each end of the step

. hen olomon had completed all this in twenty years' time, because

—"hen bibalus was dead, his son iram received the kingdom from him,

e also went and cut down materials of timber out of the mountain called

. ow when the king saw that the walls of erusalem stood in need of being
better secured, and made stronger, (for he thought the walls that encompassed
erusalem ought to correspond to the dignity of the city,) he both repaired them,
and made them higher, with great towers upon them he also built cities which
might be counted among the strongest, azor and egiddo, and the third

gypt, had made an epedition against it, and besieged it, and taken it by force
and when he had slain all its inhabitants, he utterly overthrew it, and gave it as
a present to his daughter, who had been married to olomon for which reason
the king rebuilt it, as a city that was naturally strong, and might be useful in

wars, and the mutations of affairs that sometimes happen. oreover, he built
two other cities not far from it, eth-horon was the name of one of them, and
aalath of the other. e also built other cities that lay conveniently for these, in
order to the enjoyment of pleasures and delicacies in them, such as were

name it almyra.
. ow olomon the king was at this time engaged in building these cities.
ut if any inquire why all the kings of gypt from enes, who built emphis,

from their childhood but when they were made kings, they changed them into

her name icaule, as thereby declaring, that while the kings were of the male

name and that it was after that time when the forenamed queen of gypt and
thiopia came to olomon, concerning whom we shall inform the reader

. ut king olomon subdued to himself the remnant of the anaanites that
had not before submitted to him those  mean that dwelt in ount ebanon,
and as far as the city amath and ordered them to pay tribute. e also chose
out of them every year such as were to serve him in the meanest offices, and to
do his domestic works, and to follow husbandry for none of the ebrews were
servants in such low employments] nor was it reasonable, that when od had

appointed also five hundred and fifty rulers over those anaanites who were

. oreover, the king built many ships in the gyptian ay of the ed ea,

from the city loth. his country belonged formerly to the ews, and became

navigation, to whom olomon gave this command that they should go along

urea hersonesus, which belongs to ndia, to fetch him gold. nd when they

. here was then a woman queen of gypt and thiopia    she was
inquisitive into philosophy, and one that on other accounts also was to be
admired. hen this queen heard of the virtue and prudence of olomon, she
had a great mind to see him and the reports that went every day abroad
induced her to come to him, she being desirous to be satisfied by her own
eperience, and not by a bare hearing (for reports thus heard are likely enough

relators) so she resolved to come to him, and that especially in order to have a
trial of his wisdom, while she proposed questions of very great difficulty, and
entreated that he would solve their hidden meaning. ccordingly she came to
erusalem with great splendor and rich furniture for she brought with her
camels laden with gold, with several sorts of sweet spices, and with precious
stones. ow, upon the king's kind reception of her, he both showed a great
desire to please her, and easily comprehending in his mind the meaning of the
curious questions she propounded to him, he resolved them sooner than any
body could have epected. o she was amazed at the wisdom of olomon, and

discovered that it was more ecellent upon trial than what she had heard by
report beforehand and especially she was surprised at the fineness and
largeness of his royal palace, and not less so at the good order of the
apartments, for she observed that the king had therein shown great wisdom but

ebanon, as also at the magnificence of his daily table, and the circumstances

and the skilful and decent management of their attendance nor was she less

management which the priests and evites used about them. hen she saw this
done every day, she was in the greatest admiration imaginable, insomuch that
she was not able to contain the surprise she was in, but openly confessed how
wonderfully she was affected for she proceeded to discourse with the king, and
thereby owned that she was overcome with admiration at the things before
related and said, "ll things indeed,  king, that came to our knowledge by
report, came with uncertainty as to our belief of them but as to those good
things that to thee appertain, both such as thou thyself possessest,  mean
wisdom and prudence, and the happiness thou hast from thy kingdom, certainly
the same that came to us was no falsity it was not only a true report, but it
related thy happiness after a much lower manner than  now see it to be before
my eyes. or as for the report, it only attempted to persuade our hearing, but
did not so make known the dignity of the things themselves as does the sight of
them, and being present among them.  indeed, who did not believe what was
reported, by reason of the multitude and grandeur of the things  inquired about,
do see them to be much more numerous than they were reported to be.
ccordingly  esteem the ebrew people, as well as thy servants and friends, to
be happy, who enjoy thy presence and hear thy wisdom every day continually.
ne would therefore bless od, who hath so loved this country, and those that
inhabit therein, as to make thee king over them."
. ow when the queen had thus demonstrated in words how deeply the
king had affected her, her disposition was known by certain presents, for she
gave him twenty talents of gold, and an immense quantity of spices and
precious stones. (hey say also that we possess the root of that balsam which
our country still bears by this woman's gift).    olomon also repaid her with
many good things, and principally by bestowing upon her what she chose of her
own inclination, for there was nothing that she desired which he denied her
and as he was very generous and liberal in his own temper, so did he show the
greatness of his soul in bestowing on her what she herself desired of him. o
when this queen of thiopia had obtained what we have already given an
account of, and had again communicated to the king what she brought with her,
she returned to her own kingdom.

. bout the same time there were brought to the king from the urea

now so named, and which take that their denomination from the merchants,

tree, but were whiter, and more shining. ow we have said thus much, that
nobody may be ignorant of the difference between these sorts of wood, nor

seasonable and humane thing, when we mentioned it, and the uses the king
made of it, to eplain this difference so far as we have done.
. ow the weight of gold that was brought him was si hundred and sity-

ebanon. e also made cups of gold, and of precious] stones, for the

. ccordingly there went a great fame all around the neighboring
countries, which proclaimed the virtue and wisdom of olomon, insomuch that

had a thousand before, and augmented the number of his horses by two

were at once the most beautiful of all others, and their swiftness was
incomparable also. heir riders also were a further ornament to them, being, in

eminent for their largeness, and far taller than other men. hey had also very
long heads of hair hanging down, and were clothed in garments of yrian

to take his progress out of the city in the morning. here was a certain place
about fifty furlongs distant from erusalem, which is called tham, very
pleasant it is in fine gardens, and abounding in rivulets of water    thither did he
use to go out in the morning, sitting on high in his chariot].
. ow olomon had a divine sagacity in all things, and was very diligent
and studious to have things done after an elegant manner so he did not neglect
the care of the ways, but he laid a causeway of black stone along the roads that
led to erusalem, which was the royal city, both to render them easy for
travelers, and to manifest the grandeur of his riches and government. e also
parted his chariots, and set them in a regular order, that a certain number of
them should be in every city, still keeping a few about him and those cities he
called the cities of his chariots. nd the king made silver as plentiful in
erusalem as stones in the street and so multiplied cedar trees in the plains of
udea, which did not grow there before, that they were like the multitude of
common sycamore trees. e also ordained the gyptian merchants that brought
him their merchandise to sell him a chariot, with a pair of horses, for si
hundred drachmae of silver, and he sent them to the kings of yria, and to those
kings that were beyond uphrates.
. ut although olomon was become the most glorious of kings, and the
best beloved by od, and had eceeded in wisdom and riches those that had
been rulers of the ebrews before him, yet did not he persevere in this happy
state till he died. ay, he forsook the observation of the laws of his fathers, and

came to an end no way suitable to our foregoing history of him. e grew mad
in his love of women, and laid no restraint on himself in his lusts nor was he
satisfied with the women of his country alone, but he married many wives out
of foreign nations idonians, and yrians, and mmonites, and domites and
he transgressed the laws of oses, which forbade ews to marry any but those
that were of their own people. e also began to worship their gods, which he

his very thing our legislator suspected, and so admonished us beforehand, that
we should not marry women of other countries, lest we should be entangled
with foreign customs, and apostatize from our own lest we should leave off to
honor our own od, and should worship their gods. ut olomon was fallen
headlong into unreasonable pleasures, and regarded not those admonitions for
when he had married seven hundred wives,    the daughters of princes and of
eminent persons, and three hundred concubines, and those besides the king of
gypt's daughter, he soon was governed by them, till he came to imitate their
practices. e was forced to give them this demonstration of his kindness and

oen that supported the brazen sea,    and the images of lions about his own
throne for these he made, although it was not agreeable to piety so to do and
this he did, notwithstanding that he had his father as a most ecellent and
domestic pattern of virtue, and knew what a glorious character he had left
behind him, because of his piety towards od. or did he imitate avid,
although od had twice appeared to him in his sleep, and ehorted him to
imitate his father. o he died ingloriously. here came therefore a prophet to

. hen olomon heard this he was grieved, and greatly confounded, upon

. o eroboam was elevated by these words of the prophet and being a

. ow when olomon was dead, and his son ehoboam (who was born of an
mmonite wife whose name was aamah) had succeeded him in the kingdom,

eroboam and when he was come to them, to the city hechem, ehoboam
came to it also, for he had resolved to declare himself king to the sraelites

to be gentler than his father, in the servitude he had imposed on them, because
they had borne a heavy yoke, and that then they should be better affected to
him, and be well contented to serve him under his moderate government, and
should do it more out of love than fear. ut ehoboam told them they should

come to him again in three days' time, when he would give an answer to their
request. his delay gave occasion to a present suspicion, since he had not given
them a favorable answer to their mind immediately for they thought that he
should have given them a humane answer off-hand, especially since he was but
young. owever, they thought that this consultation about it, and that he did not
presently give them a denial, afforded them some good hope of success.
. ehoboam now called his father's friends, and advised with them what
sort of answer he ought to give to the multitude upon which they gave him the
advice which became friends, and those that knew the temper of such a
multitude. hey advised him to speak in a way more popular than suited the
grandeur of a king, because he would thereby oblige them to submit to him
with goodwill, it being most agreeable to subjects that their kings should be
almost upon the level with them. ut ehoboam rejected this so good, and in
general so profitable, advice, (it was such, at least, at that time when he was to
be made king,) od himself,  suppose, causing what was most advantageous to
be condemned by him. o he called for the young men who were brought up
with him, and told them what advice the elders had given him, and bade them
speak what they thought he ought to do. hey advised him to give the
following answer to the people (for neither their youth nor od himself
suffered them to discern what was best)—that his little finger should be thicker
than his father's loins and if they had met with hard usage from his father, they
should eperience much rougher treatment from him and if his father had
chastised them with whips, they must epect that he would do it with
scorpions.    he king was pleased with this advice, and thought it agreeable to
the dignity of his government to give them such an answer. ccordingly, when

. ow  cannot but think that the greatness of a kingdom, and its change
into prosperity, often become the occasion of mischief and of transgression to
men for when ehoboam saw that his kingdom was so much increased, he
went out of the right way unto unrighteous and irreligious practices, and he
despised the worship of od, till the people themselves imitated his wicked
actions for so it usually happens, that the manners of subjects are corrupted at
the same time with those of their governors, which subjects then lay aside their
own sober way of living, as a reproof of their governors' intemperate courses,
and follow their wickedness as if it were virtue for it is not possible to show

with them. greeable whereto it now happened to the subjects of ehoboam

udea] with many ten thousand men for he had one thousand two hundred

good, and not to deprive themselves of the favor of od. hen the king and the

took great care to behave themselves righteously. he king also sent some to
take care that those in the country should observe the laws also.
. nd this was the state of sa, king of the two tribes.  now return to
aasha, the king of the multitude of the sraelites, who slew adab, the son of
eroboam, and retained the government. e dwelt in the city irzah, having
made that his habitation, and reigned twenty-four years. e became more
wicked and impious than eroboam or his son. e did a great deal of mischief
to the multitude, and was injurious to od, who sent the prophet ehu, and told
him beforehand that his whole family should be destroyed, and that he would
bring the same miseries on his house which had brought that of eroboam to
ruin because when he had been made king by him, he had not requited his
kindness, by governing the multitude righteously and religiously which things,
in the first place, tended to their own happiness, and, in the net place, were
pleasing to od that he had imitated this very wicked king eroboam and
although that man's soul had perished, yet did he epress to the life his
wickedness and he said that he should therefore justly eperience the like
calamity with him, since he had been guilty of the like wickedness. ut aasha,
though he heard beforehand what miseries would befall him and his whole
family for their insolent behavior, yet did not he leave off his wicked practices

for the time to come, nor did he care to appear other than worse and worse till
he died nor did he then repent of his past actions, nor endeavor to obtain
pardon of od for them, but did as those do who have rewards proposed to

their labors for thus did aasha, when the prophet foretold to him what would
come to pass, grow worse, as if what were threatened, the perdition of his
family, and the destruction of his house, (which are really among the greatest of
evils,) were good things and, as if he were a combatant for wickedness, he
every day took more and more pains for it and at last he took his army and
assaulted a certain considerable city called amah, which was forty furlongs
distant from erusalem and when he had taken it, he fortified it, having
determined beforehand to leave a garrison in it, that they might thence make
ecursions, and do mischief to the kingdom of sa.
. hereupon sa was afraid of the attempts the enemy might make upon
him and considering with himself how many mischiefs this army that was left
in amah might do to the country over which he reigned, he sent ambassadors
to the king of the amascens, with gold and silver, desiring his assistance, and
putting him in mind that we have had a friendship together from the times of
our forefathers. o he gladly received that sum of money, and made a league
with him, and broke the friendship he had with aasha, and sent the
commanders of his own forces unto the cities that were under aasha's
dominion, and ordered them to do them mischief. o they went and burnt some
of them, and spoiled others jon, and an, and belmain,    and many others.
ow when the king of srael heard this, he left off building and fortifying
amah, and returned presently to assist his own people under the distresses
they were in but sa made use of the materials that were prepared for building
that city, for building in the same place two strong cities, the one of which was
called eba, and the other izpah so that after this aasha had no leisure to
make epeditions against sa, for he was prevented by death, and was buried in

two years, died, being treacherously slain by imri, the captain of half his
army for when he was at rza, his steward's house, he persuaded some of the
horsemen that were under him to assault lah, and by that means he slew him

in the siege of ibbethon, a city of the hilistines.
. hen imri, the captain of the army, had killed lah, he took the
kingdom himself, and, according to ehu's prophecy, slew all the house of
aasha for it came to pass that aasha's house utterly perished, on account of
his impiety, in the same manner as we have already described the destruction of
the house of eroboam. ut the army that was besieging ibbethon, when they
heard what had befallen the king, and that when imri had killed him, he had

gained the kingdom, they made mri their general king, who drew off his army
from ibbethon, and came to irzah, where the royal palace was, and assaulted
the city, and took it by force. ut when imri saw that the city had none to
defend it, he fled into the inmost part of the palace, and set it on fire, and burnt
himself with it, when he had reigned only seven days. pon which the people
of srael were presently divided, and part of them would have ibni to be king,
and part mri but when those that were for mri's ruling had beaten ibni,
mri reigned over all the multitude. ow it was in the thirtieth year of the reign
of sa that mri reigned for twelve years si of these years he reigned in the
city irzah, and the rest in the city called emareon, but named by the reeks
amaria but he himself called it emareon, from emer, who sold him the
mountain whereon he built it. ow mri was no way different from those kings
that reigned before him, but that he grew worse than they, for they all sought
how they might turn the people away from od by their daily wicked practices
and on that account it was that od made one of them to be slain by another,
and that no one person of their families should remain. his mri also died in
amaria and hab his son succeeded him.
. ow by these events we may learn what concern od hath for the affairs
of mankind, and how he loves good men, and hates the wicked, and destroys
them root and branch for many of these kings of srael, they and their families,
were miserably destroyed, and taken away one by another, in a short time, for
their transgression and wickedness but sa, who was king of erusalem, and of
the two tribes, attained, by od's blessing, a long and a blessed old age, for his
piety and righteousness, and died happily, when he had reigned forty and one
years and when he was dead, his son ehoshaphat succeeded him in the
government. e was born of sa's wife zubah. nd all men allowed that he
followed the works of avid his forefather, and this both in courage and piety
but we are not obliged now to speak any more of the affairs of this king.

. ow hab the king of srael dwelt in amaria, and held the government for
twenty-two years and made no alteration in the conduct of the kings that were
his predecessors, but only in such things as were of his own invention for the
worse, and in his most gross wickedness. e imitated them in their wicked

courses, and in their injurious behavior towards od, and more especially he
imitated the transgression of eroboam for he worshipped the heifers that he
had made and he contrived other absurd objects of worship besides those
heifers he also took to wife the daughter of thbaal, king of the yrians and
idonians, whose name was ezebel, of whom he learned to worship her own
gods. his woman was active and bold, and fell into so great a degree of
impurity and madness, that she built a temple to the god of the yrians, which
they call elus, and planted a grove of all sorts of trees she also appointed
priests and false prophets to this god. he king also himself had many such
about him, and so eceeded in madness and wickedness all the kings] that
went before him.
. here was now a prophet of od lmighty, of hesbon, a country in
ilead, that came to hab, and said to him, that od foretold he would not send
rain nor dew in those years upon the country but when he should appear. nd
when he had confirmed this by an oath, he departed into the southern parts, and
made his abode by a brook, out of which he had water to drink for as for his
food, ravens brought it to him every day but when that river was dried up for
want of rain, he came to arephath, a city not far from idon and yre, for it
lay between them, and this at the command of od, for od told him] that he
should there find a woman who was a widow that should give him sustenance.
o when he was not far off the city, he saw a woman that labored with her own
hands, gathering of sticks so od informed him that this was the woman who
was to give him sustenance. o he came and saluted her, and desired her to
bring him some water to drink but as she was going so to do, he called to her,
and would have her to bring him a loaf of bread also whereupon she affirmed
upon oath that she had at home nothing more than one handful of meal, and a
little oil, and that she was going to gather some sticks, that she might knead it,
and make bread for herself and her son after which, she said, they must perish,
and be consumed by the famine, for they had nothing for themselves any
longer. ereupon he said, "o on with good courage, and hope for better
things and first of all make me a little cake, and bring it to me, for  foretell to
thee that this vessel of meal and this cruse of oil shall not fail until od send
rain." hen the prophet had said this, she came to him, and made him the
before-named cake of which she had part for herself, and gave the rest to her
son, and to the prophet also nor did any thing of this fall until the drought
ceased. ow enander mentions this drought in his account of the acts of
thbaal, king of the yrians where he says thus "nder him there was a want
of rain from the month yperberetaeus till the month yperberetaeus of the
year following but when he made supplications, there came great thunders.
his thbaal built the city otrys in hoenicia, and the city uza in ibya." y
these words he designed the want of rain that was in the days of hab, for at

that time it was that thbaal also reigned over the yrians, as enander
informs us.
. ow this woman, of whom we spake before, that sustained the prophet,
when her son was fallen into a distemper till he gave up the ghost, and appeared
to be dead, came to the prophet weeping, and beating her breasts with her
hands, and sending out such epressions as her passions dictated to her, and
complained to him that he had come to her to reproach her for her sins, and that
on this account it was that her son was dead. ut he bid her be of good cheer,
and deliver her son to him, for that he would deliver him again to her alive. o
when she had delivered her son up to him, he carried him into an upper room,
where he himself lodged, and laid him down upon the bed, and cried unto od,
and said, that od had not done well, in rewarding the woman who had
entertained him and sustained him, by taking away her son and he prayed that
he would send again the soul of the child into him, and bring him to life again.
ccordingly od took pity on the mother, and was willing to gratify the
prophet, that he might not seem to have come to her to do her a mischief, and
the child, beyond all epectation, came to life again. o the mother returned the
prophet thanks, and said she was then clearly satisfied that od did converse
with him.
. fter a little while lijah came to king hab, according to od's will, to
inform him that rain was coming.    ow the famine had seized upon the whole
country, and there was a great want of what was necessary for sustenance,
insomuch that it was not only men that wanted it, but the earth itself also,
which did not produce enough for the horses and the other beasts of what was
useful for them to feed on, by reason of the drought. o the king called for
badiah, who was steward over his cattle, and said to him, that he would have
him go to the fountains of water, and to the brooks, that if any herbs could be
found for them, they might mow it down, and reserve it for the beasts. nd
when he had sent persons all over the habitable earth,    to discover the prophet
lijah, and they could not find him, he bade badiah accompany him. o it was
resolved they should make a progress, and divide the ways between them and
badiah took one road, and the king another. ow it happened that the same
time when queen ezebel slew the prophets, that this badiah had hidden a
hundred prophets, and had fed them with nothing but bread and water. ut
when badiah was alone, and absent from the king, the prophet lijah met him
and badiah asked him who he was and when he had learned it from him, he
worshipped him. lijah then bid him go to the king, and tell him that  am here
ready to wait on him. ut badiah replied, "hat evil have  done to thee, that
thou sendest me to one who seeketh to kill thee, and hath sought over all the
earth for thee r was he so ignorant as not to know that the king had left no
place untouched unto which he had not sent persons to bring him back, in

order, if they could take him, to have him put to death" or he told him he was
afraid lest od should appear to him again, and he should go away into another
place and that when the king should send him for lijah, and he should miss of
him, and not be able to find him any where upon earth, he should be put to
death. e desired him therefore to take care of his preservation and told him
how diligently he had provided for those of his own profession, and had saved
a hundred prophets, when ezebel slew the rest of them, and had kept them
concealed, and that they had been sustained by him. ut lijah bade him fear
nothing, but go to the king and he assured him upon oath that he would
certainly show himself to hab that very day.
. o when badiah had informed the king that lijah was there, hab met
him, and asked him, in anger, if he were the man that afflicted the people of the
ebrews, and was the occasion of the drought they lay under ut lijah,
without any flattery, said that he was himself the man, he and his house, which
brought such sad afflictions upon them, and that by introducing strange gods
into their country, and worshipping them, and by leaving their own, who was
the only true od, and having no manner of regard to him. owever, he bade
him go his way, and gather together all the people to him to ount armel,
with his own prophets, and those of his wife, telling him how many there were
of them, as also the prophets of the groves, about four hundred in number. nd
as all the men whom hab sent for ran away to the forenamed mountain, the
prophet lijah stood in the midst of them, and said, "ow long will you live
thus in uncertainty of mind and opinion" e also ehorted them, that in case
they esteemed their own country od to be the true and the only od, they
would follow him and his commandments but in case they esteemed him to be
nothing, but had an opinion of the strange gods, and that they ought to worship
them, his counsel was, that they should follow them. nd when the multitude

the strange gods, and of their own od, he, who was his only prophet, while
they had four hundred, might take a heifer and kill it as a sacrifice, and lay it
upon pieces of wood, and not kindle any fire, and that they should do the same
things, and call upon their own gods to set the wood on fire for if that were
done, they would thence learn the nature of the true od. his proposal pleased
the people. o lijah bade the prophets to choose out a heifer first, and kill it,
and to call on their gods. ut when there appeared no effect of the prayer or
invocation of the prophets upon their sacrifice, lijah derided them, and bade
them call upon their gods with a loud voice, for they might either be on a
journey, or asleep and when these prophets had done so from morning till
noon, and cut themselves with swords and lances,    according to the customs of
their country, and he was about to offer his sacrifice, he bade the prophets] go
away, but bade the people] come near and observe what he did, lest he should

privately hide fire among the pieces of wood. o, upon the approach of the
multitude, he took twelve stones, one for each tribe of the people of the
ebrews, and built an altar with them, and dug a very deep trench and when he
had laid the pieces of wood upon the altar, and upon them had laid the pieces of
the sacrifices, he ordered them to fill four barrels with the water of the fountain,
and to pour it upon the altar, till it ran over it, and till the trench was filled with
the water poured into it. hen he had done this, he began to pray to od, and
to invocate him to make manifest his power to a people that had already been in
an error a long time upon which words a fire came on a sudden from heaven in
the sight of the multitude, and fell upon the altar, and consumed the sacrifice,
till the very water was set on fire, and the place was become dry.
. ow when the sraelites saw this, they fell down upon the ground, and
worshipped one od, and called him the great and the only true od but they
called the others mere names, framed by the evil and vile opinions of men. o
they caught their prophets, and, at the command of lijah, slew them. lijah
also said to the king, that he should go to dinner without any further concern,
for that in a little time he would see od send them rain. ccordingly hab
went his way. ut lijah went up to the highest top of ount armel, and sat
down upon the ground, and leaned his head upon his knees, and bade his
servant go up to a certain elevated place, and look towards the sea, and when he
should see a cloud rising any where, he should give him notice of it, for till that
time the air had been clear. hen the servant had gone up, and had said many
times that he saw nothing, at the seventh time of his going up, he said that he
saw a small black thing in the sky, not larger than a man's foot. hen lijah
heard that, he sent to hab, and desired him to go away to the city before the
rain came down. o he came to the city ezreel and in a little time the air was
all obscured, and covered with clouds, and a vehement storm of wind came
upon the earth, and with it a great deal of rain and the prophet was under a
ivine fury, and ran along with the king's chariot unto ezreel a city of zar 
 ssachar].
. hen ezebel, the wife of hab, understood what signs lijah had
wrought, and how he had slain her prophets, she was angry, and sent
messengers to him, and by them threatened to kill him, as he had destroyed her
prophets. t this lijah was affrighted, and fled to the city called eersheba,
which is situate at the utmost limits of the country belonging to the tribe of
udah, towards the land of dom and there he left his servant, and went away
into the desert. e prayed also that he might die, for that he was not better than
his fathers, nor need he be very desirous to live, when they were dead and he
lay and slept under a certain tree and when somebody awakened him, and he
was risen up, he found food set by him and water so when he had eaten, and
recovered his strength by that his food, he came to that mountain which is

called inai, where it is related that oses received his laws from od and
finding there a certain hollow cave, he entered into it, and continued to make
his abode in it. ut when a certain voice came to him, but from whence he
knew not, and asked him, why he was come thither, and had left the city he
said, that because he had slain the prophets of the foreign gods, and had
persuaded the people that he alone whom they had worshipped from the
beginning was od, he was sought for by the king's wife to be punished for so
doing. nd when he had heard another voice, telling him that he should come
out the net day into the open air, and should thereby know what he was to do,
he came out of the cave the net day accordingly, when he both heard an
earthquake, and saw the bright splendor of a fire and after a silence made, a
ivine voice ehorted him not to be disturbed with the circumstances he was
in, for that none of his enemies should have power over him. he voice also
commanded him to return home, and to ordain ehu, the son of imshi, to be
king over their own multitude and azael, of amascus, to be over the
yrians and lisha, of the city bel, to be a prophet in his stead and that of the
impious multitude, some should be slain by azael, and others by ehu. o
lijah, upon hearing this charge, returned into the land of the ebrews. nd
when he found lisha, the son of haphat, ploughing, and certain others with
him, driving twelve yoke of oen, he came to him, and cast his own garment
upon him upon which lisha began to prophesy presently, and leaving his
oen, he followed lijah. nd when he desired leave to salute his parents,
lijah gave him leave so to do and when he had taken his leave of them, he
followed him, and became the disciple and the servant of lijah all the days of
his life. nd thus have  despatched the affairs in which this prophet was
concerned.
. ow there was one aboth, of the city zar, ezreel,] who had a field
adjoining to that of the king the king would have persuaded him to sell him
that his field, which lay so near to his own lands, at what price he pleased, that
he might join them together, and make them one farm and if he would not
accept of money for it, he gave him leave to choose any of his other fields in its
stead. ut aboth said he would not do so, but would keep the possession of
that land of his own, which he had by inheritance from his father. pon this the
king was grieved, as if he had received an injury, when he could not get another
man's possession, and he would neither wash himself, nor take any food and
when ezebel asked him what it was that troubled him, and why he would
neither wash himself, nor eat either dinner or supper, he related to her the
perverseness of aboth, and how, when he had made use of gentle words to
him, and such as were beneath the royal authority, he had been affronted, and
had not obtained what he desired. owever, she persuaded him not to be cast
down at this accident, but to leave off his grief, and return to the usual care of

his body, for that she would take care to have aboth punished and she
immediately sent letters to the rulers of the sraelites ezreelites] in hab's
name, and commanded them to fast and to assemble a congregation, and to set
aboth at the head of them, because he was of an illustrious family, and to have
three bold men ready to bear witness that he had blasphemed od and the king,
and then to stone him, and slay him in that manner. ccordingly, when aboth
had been thus testified against, as the queen had written to them, that he had
blasphemed against od and hab the king, she desired him to take possession
of aboth's vineyard on free cost. o hab was glad at what had been done,
and rose up immediately from the bed whereon he lay to go to see aboth's
vineyard but od had great indignation at it, and sent lijah the prophet to the

owner of that field unjustly. nd as soon as he came to him, and the king had

dead body of aboth was eaten by dogs, both his own blood and that of his
wife's should be shed, and that all his family should perish, because he had

laws of his country. ereupon hab began to be sorry for the things he had
done, and to repent of them and he put on sackcloth, and went barefoot,    and
would not touch any food he also confessed his sins, and endeavored thus to
appease od. ut od said to the prophet, that while hab was living he would
put off the punishment of his family, because he repented of those insolent

hab's son which message the prophet delivered to the king.

. hen the affairs of hab were thus, at that very time the son of adad,
enhadad,] who was king of the yrians and of amascus, got together an
army out of all his country, and procured thirty-two kings beyond uphrates to
be his auiliaries so he made an epedition against hab but because hab's
army was not like that of enhadad, he did not set it in array to fight him, but
having shut up every thing that was in the country in the strongest cities he had,
he abode in amaria himself, for the walls about it were very strong, and it
appeared to be not easily to be taken in other respects also. o the king of yria
took his army with him, and came to amaria, and placed his army round about

the city, and besieged it. e also sent a herald to hab, and desired he would
admit the ambassadors he would send him, by whom he would let him know
his pleasure. o, upon the king of srael's permission for him to send, those
ambassador's came, and by their king's command spake thus that hab's
riches, and his children, and his wives were enhadad's, and if he would make
an agreement, and give him leave to take as much of what he had as he pleased,
he would withdraw his army, and leave off the siege. pon this hab bade the
ambassadors to go back, and tell their king, that both he himself and all that he
hath are his possessions. nd when these ambassadors had told this to
enhadad, he sent to him again, and desired, since he confessed that all he had
was his, that he would admit those servants of his which he should send the
net day and he commanded him to deliver to those whom he should send
whatsoever, upon their searching his palace, and the houses of his friends and
kindred, they should find to be ecellent in its kind, but that what did not please
them they should leave to him. t this second embassage of the king of yria,
hab was surprised, and gathered together the multitude to a congregation, and
told them that, for himself, he was ready, for their safety and peace, to give up
his own wives and children to the enemy, and to yield to him all his own
possessions, for that was what the yrian king required at his first embassage
but that now he desires to send his servants to search all their houses, and in
them to leave nothing that is ecellent in its kind, seeking an occasion of
fighting against him, "as knowing that  would not spare what is mine own for
your sakes, but taking a handle from the disagreeable terms he offers
concerning you to bring a war upon us however,  will do what you shall
resolve is fit to be done." ut the multitude advised him to hearken to none of
his proposals, but to despise him, and be in readiness to fight him. ccordingly,
when he had given the ambassadors this answer to be reported, that he still
continued in the mind to comply with what terms he at first desired, for the
safety of the citizens but as for his second desires, he cannot submit to them,—
he dismissed them.
. ow when enhadad heard this, he had indignation, and sent
ambassadors to hab the third time, and threatened that his army would raise a
bank higher than those walls, in confidence of whose strength he despised him,
and that by only each man of his army taking a handful of earth hereby making
a show of the great number of his army, and aiming to affright him. hab
answered, that he ought not to vaunt himself when he had only put on his
armor, but when he should have conquered his enemies in the battle. o the
ambassadors came back, and found the king at supper with his thirty-two kings,
and informed him of hab's answer who then immediately gave order for
proceeding thus to make lines round the city, and raise a bulwark, and to
prosecute the siege all manner of ways. ow, as this was doing, hab was in a

great agony, and all his people with him but he took courage, and was freed
from his fears, upon a certain prophet coming to him, and saying to him, that
od had promised to subdue so many ten thousands of his enemies under him.
nd when he inquired by whose means the victory was to be obtained, be said,

their unskilfulness in war]." pon which he called for the sons of the princes,
and found them to be two hundred and thirty-two persons. o when he was
informed that the king of yria had betaken himself to feasting and repose, he
opened the gates, and sent out the princes' sons. ow when the sentinels told
enhadad of it, he sent some to meet them, and commanded them, that if these

and that if they came out peaceably, they should do the same. ow hab had
another army ready within the walls, but the sons of the princes fell upon the

they did not think they would have come out on which account it was that they
assaulted them when they were naked    and drunk, insomuch that they left all
their armor behind them when they fled out of the camp, and the king himself
escaped with difficulty, by fleeing away on horseback. ut hab went a great
way in pursuit of the yrians and when he had spoiled their camp, which
contained a great deal of wealth, and moreover a large quantity of gold and
silver, he took enhadad's chariots and horses, and returned to the city but as
the prophet told him he ought to have his army ready, because the yrian king
would make another epedition against him the net year, hab was busy in
making provision for it accordingly.
. ow enhadad, when he had saved himself, and as much of his army as
he could, out of the battle, he consulted with his friends how he might make
another epedition against the sraelites. ow those friends advised him not to
fight with them on the hills, because their od was potent in such places, and
thence it had come to pass that they had very lately been beaten but they said,
that if they joined battle with them in the plain, they should beat them. hey
also gave him this further advice, to send home those kings whom he had
brought as his auiliaries, but to retain their army, and to set captains over it
instead of the kings, and to raise an army out of their country, and let them be
in the place of the former who perished in the battle, together with horses and

human souls, and flatters them with pleasing hopes, till it leads them about to
the place where it will be too hard for them. ccordingly hab appears to have

    hat is most remarkable in this history, and in many histories on other occasions in the ld
estament, is this, that during the ewish theocracy od acted entirely as the supreme ing of srael, and
the supreme eneral of their armies, and always epected that the sraelites should be in such absolute
subjection to him, their supreme and heavenly ing, and eneral of their armies, as subjects and soldiers

    his reading of osephus, that ehoshaphat put on not his own, but hab's robes, in order to appear to
be hab, while hab was without any robes at all, and hoped thereby to escape his own evil fate, and
disprove icaiah's prophecy against him, is eceeding probable. t gives great light also to this whole
history and shows, that although hab hoped ehoshaphat would he mistaken for him, and run the only
risk of being slain in the battle, yet he was entirely disappointed, while still the escape of the good man
ehoshaphat, and the slaughter of the bad man hab, demonstrated the great distinction that ivine
providence made betwit them.
    e have here a very wise reflection of osephus about ivine rovidence, and what is derived from it,
prophecy, and the inevitable certainty of its accomplishment and that when wicked men think they take
proper methods to elude what is denounced against them, and to escape the ivine judgments thereby
threatened them, without repentance, they are ever by rovidence infatuated to bring about their own
destruction, and thereby withal to demonstrate the perfect veracity of that od whose predictions they in
vain endeavored to elude.

. hen ehoshaphat the king was come to erusalem, from the assistance he
had afforded hab, the king of srael, when he fought with enhadad, king of
yria, the prophet ehu met him, and accused him for assisting hab, a man
both impious and wicked and said to him, that od was displeased with him
for so doing, but that he delivered him from the enemy, notwithstanding he had
sinned, because of his own proper disposition, which was good. hereupon the

king betook himself to thanksgivings and sacrifices to od after which he
presently went over all that country which he ruled round about, and taught the
people, as well the laws which od gave them by oses, as that religious
worship that was due to him. e also constituted judges in every one of the
cities of his kingdom and charged them to have regard to nothing so much in
judging the multitude as to do justice, and not to be moved by bribes, nor by the
dignity of men eminent for either their riches or their high birth, but to
distribute justice equally to all, as knowing that od is conscious of every
secret action of theirs. hen he had himself instructed them thus, and gone
over every city of the two tribes, he returned to erusalem. e there also
constituted judges out of the priests and the evites, and principal persons of
the multitude, and admonished them to pass all their sentences with care and
justice.    nd that if any of the people of his country had differences of great
consequence, they should send them out of the other cities to these judges, who
would be obliged to give righteous sentences concerning such causes and this
with the greater care, because it is proper that the sentences which are given in
that city wherein the temple of od is, and wherein the king dwells, be given
with great care and the utmost justice. ow he set over them mariah the
priest, and ebediah, both] of the tribe of udah and after this manner it was
that the king ordered these affairs.

. hen the prophet had said this, the net day, before the sun-rising, a

of for the maintenance of her children. nd thus did lisha discharge the

. lisha also sent a hasty message to oram,    and ehorted him to take care
of that place, for that therein were some yrians lying in ambush to kill him. o
the king did as the prophet ehorted him, and avoided his going a hunting. nd
when enhadad missed of the success of his lying in ambush, he was wroth

lisha had brought them to amaria, he ordered oram the king to shut the

. ow when these men were come back, and had showed enhadad how
strange an accident had befallen them, and what an appearance and power they

with whom od was so evidently present so he determined to make no more

in fear lest somebody should betray the city to the enemy, by reason of the

we lived upon my son yesterday but this other woman will not do the same

had sent one to take away his head "but," said he, "when he that is commanded

come to me, having altered his mind." ccordingly, they did as they were

. ow what lisha had thus foretold came to pass in the manner following
there was a law at amaria,    that those that had the leprosy, and whose bodies
were not cleansed from it, should abide without the city and there were four

men that on this account abode before the gates, while nobody gave them any

they staid where they were, they should suffer in the same manner,—they
resolved to deliver themselves up to the enemy, that in case they should spare

o when they had confirmed this their resolution, they came by night to the

bring the noise of chariots and armor to their ears, as though an army were
coming upon them, and had made them suspect that it was coming nearer and
nearer to them. n short, they were in such a dread of this army, that they left
their tents, and ran together to enhadad, and said that oram the king of srael
had hired for auiliaries both the king of gypt and the king of the slands, and
led them against them for they heard the noise of them as they were coming.
nd enhadad believed what they said (for there came the same noise to his

betook themselves to flight. nd those lepers who had departed from amaria,
and were gone to the camp of the yrians, of whom we made mention a little

and when they saw nobody there, they ate and drank, and carried garments, and

despise your enemies, as though they were really gone away." nd when a
certain person said that he did very well and wisely to admit such a suspicion,

they might be a security to your army, that they may not go out as if they

hen the king heard this, he sent out the multitude to take the spoils of the

captain of the third band was the only man that received no benefit by this

plenty of provisions which they should soon have.
. ereupon, when enhadad, the king of yria, had escaped to amascus,
and understood that it was od himself that cast all his army into this fear and

cast down at his having od so greatly for his enemy, and fell into a distemper.
ow it happened that lisha the prophet, at that time, was gone out of his own
country to amascus, of which enhadad was informed he sent azael, the
most faithful of all his servants, to meet him, and to carry him presents, and

azael asked him what was the occasion of this confusion he was in, he said

of them, and wilt burn their strongest cities, and wilt destroy their children, and
dash them against the stones, and wilt rip up their women with child." nd
when azael said, "ow can it be that  should have power enough to do such

of yria. o when azael was come to enhadad, he told him good news
concerning his distemper    but on the net day he spread a wet cloth, in the
nature of a net, over him, and strangled him, and took his dominion. e was an
active man, and had the good-will of the yrians, and of the people of

who ruled after him, are honored to this day as gods, by reason of their
