these kings,    and value themselves upon their antiquity nor do they know that
these kings are much later than they imagine, and that they are not yet eleven
hundred years old. ow when oram, the king of srael, heard that enhadad
was dead, he recovered out of the terror and dread he had been in on his
account, and was very glad to live in peace.
  
     ,  
    
. ow ehoram the king of erusalem, for we have said before that he had the
same name with the king of srael, as soon as he had taken the government
upon him, betook himself to the slaughter of his brethren, and his father's
friends, who were governors under him, and thence made a beginning and a
demonstration of his wickedness nor was he at all better than those kings of
srael who at first transgressed against the laws of their country, and of the
ebrews, and against od's worship. nd it was thaliah, the daughter of
hab, whom he had married, who taught him to be a bad man in other respects,
and also to worship foreign gods. ow od would not quite root out this
family, because of the promise he had made to avid. owever, ehoram did
not leave off the introduction of new sorts of customs to the propagation of
impiety, and to the ruin of the customs of his own country. nd when the
domites about that time had revolted from him, and slain their former king,
who was in subjection to his father, and had set up one of their own choosing,
ehoram fell upon the land of dom, with the horsemen that were about him,

and the chariots, by night, and destroyed those that lay near to his own
kingdom, but did not proceed further. owever, this epedition did him no

ibnah. e was indeed so mad as to compel the people to go up to the high
places of the mountains, and worship foreign gods.
. s he was doing this, and had entirely cast his own country laws out of
his mind, there was brought him an epistle from lijah the prophet,    which
declared that od would eecute great judgments upon him, because he had not

srael and had compelled the tribe of udah, and the citizens of erusalem, to

compelled the sraelites to do, and because he had slain his brethren, and the
men that were good and righteous. nd the prophet gave him notice in this
epistle what punishment he should undergo for these crimes, namely, the
destruction of his people, with the corruption of the king's own wives and

torments, those his bowels falling out by the violence of the inward rottenness

at all to help himself, but shall die in that manner. his it was which lijah
denounced to him in that epistle.
. t was not long after this that an army of those rabians that lived near to
thiopia, and of the hilistines, fell upon the kingdom of ehoram, and spoiled
the country and the king's house. oreover, they slew his sons and his wives
one only of his sons was left him, who escaped the enemy his name was
haziah after which calamity, he himself fell into that disease which was
foretold by the prophet, and lasted a great while, (for od inflicted this
punishment upon him in his belly, out of his wrath against him,) and so he died
miserably, and saw his own bowels fall out. he people also abused his dead
body  suppose it was because they thought that such his death came upon him
by the wrath of od, and that therefore he was not worthy to partake of such a
funeral as became kings. ccordingly, they neither buried him in the sepulchres
of his fathers, nor vouchsafed him any honors, but buried him like a private
man, and this when he had lived forty years, and reigned eight. nd the people
of erusalem delivered the government to his son haziah.
  
     ,

. ow oram, the king of srael, after the death of enhadad, hoped that he
might now take amoth, a city of ilead, from the yrians. ccordingly he
made an epedition against it, with a great army but as he was besieging it, an
arrow was shot at him by one of the yrians, but the wound was not mortal. o
he returned to have his wound healed in ezreel, but left his whole army in
amoth, and ehu, the son of imshi, for their general for he had already taken
the city by force and he proposed, after he was healed, to make war with the
yrians but lisha the prophet sent one of his disciples to amoth, and gave
him holy oil to anoint ehu, and to tell him that od had chosen him to be their
king. e also sent him to say other things to him, and bid him to take his
journey as if he fled, that when he came away he might escape the knowledge
of all men. o when he was come to the city, he found ehu sitting in the midst
of the captains of the army, as lisha had foretold he should find him. o he
came up to him, and said that he desired to speak with him about certain
matters and when he was arisen, and had followed him into an inward
chamber, the young man took the oil, and poured it on his head, and said that
od ordained him to be king, in order to his destroying the house of hab, and
that he might revenge the blood of the prophets that were unjustly slain by
ezebel, that so their house might utterly perish, as those of eroboam the son of
ebat, and of aasha, had perished for their wickedness, and no seed might
remain of hab's family. o when he had said this, he went away hastily out of
the chamber, and endeavored not to be seen by any of the army.
. ut ehu came out, and went to the place where he before sat with the
captains and when they asked him, and desired him to tell them, wherefore it
was that this young man came to him, and added withal that he was mad, he
replied,—"ou guess right, for the words he spake were the words of a
madman" and when they were eager about the matter, and desired he would
tell them, he answered, that od had said he had chosen him to be king over the
multitude. hen he had said this, every one of them put off his garment,    and
strewed it under him, and blew with trumpets, and gave notice that ehu was
king. o when he had gotten the army together, he was preparing to set out
immediately against oram, at the city ezreel, in which city, as we said before,
he was healing of the wound which he had received in the siege of amoth. t
happened also that haziah, king of erusalem, was now come to oram, for he
was his sister's son, as we have said already, to see how he did after his wound,
and this upon account of their kindred but as ehu was desirous to fall upon
oram, and those with him, on the sudden, he desired that none of the soldiers
might run away and tell to oram what had happened, for that this would be an
evident demonstration of their kindness to him, and would show that their real
inclinations were to make him king.

. o they were pleased with what he did, and guarded the roads, lest
somebody should privately tell the thing to those that were at ezreel. ow ehu
took his choice horsemen, and sat upon his chariot, and went on for ezreel and
when he was come near, the watchman whom oram had set there to spy out
such as came to the city, saw ehu marching on, and told oram that he saw a
troop of horsemen marching on. pon which he immediately gave orders, that
one of his horsemen should be sent out to meet them, and to know who it was
that was coming. o when the horseman came up to ehu, he asked him in what
condition the army was, for that the king wanted to know it but ehu bid him
not at all to meddle with such matters, but to follow him. hen the watchman
saw this, he told oram that the horseman had mingled himself among the
company, and came along with them. nd when the king had sent a second
messenger, ehu commanded him to do as the former did and as soon as the
watchman told this also to oram, he at last got upon his chariot himself,
together with haziah, the king of erusalem for, as we said before, he was
there to see how oram did, after he had been wounded, as being his relation.
o he went out to meet ehu, who marched slowly,    and in good order and
when oram met him in the field of aboth, he asked him if all things were
well in the camp but ehu reproached him bitterly, and ventured to call his
mother a witch and a harlot. pon this the king, fearing what he intended, and
suspecting he had no good meaning, turned his chariot about as soon as he
could, and said to haziah, "e are fought against by deceit and treachery."
ut ehu drew his bow, and smote him, the arrow going through his heart so
oram fell down immediately on his knee, and gave up the ghost. ehu also
gave orders to idkar, the captain of the third part of his army, to cast the dead
body of oram into the field of aboth, putting him in mind of the prophecy
which lijah prophesied to hab his father, when he had slain aboth, that both
he and his family should perish in that place for that as they sat behind hab's
chariot, they heard the prophet say so, and that it was now come to pass
according to his prophecy. pon the fall of oram, haziah was afraid of his
own life, and turned his chariot into another road, supposing he should not be
seen by ehu but he followed after him, and overtook him at a certain acclivity,
and drew his bow, and wounded him so he left his chariot, and got upon his

time he died of that wound, and was carried to erusalem, and buried there,
after he had reigned one year, and had proved a wicked man, and worse than
his father.
. ow when ehu was come to ezreel, ezebel adorned herself and stood
upon a tower, and said, he was a fine servant that had killed his master! nd
when he looked up to her, he asked who she was, and commanded her to come
down to him. t last he ordered the eunuchs to throw her down from the tower

and being thrown down, she be-sprinkled the wall with her blood, and was
trodden upon by the horses, and so died. hen this was done, ehu came to the
palace with his friends, and took some refreshment after his journey, both with

and bury her, because of the nobility of her blood, for she was descended from
kings but those that were appointed to bury her found nothing else remaining
but the etreme parts of her body, for all the rest were eaten by dogs. hen
ehu heard this, he admired the prophecy of lijah, for he foretold that she
should perish in this manner at ezreel.
. ow hab had seventy sons brought up in amaria. o ehu sent two
epistles, the one to them that brought up the children, the other to the rulers of
amaria, which said, that they should set up the most valiant of hab's sons for
king, for that they had abundance of chariots, and horses, and armor, and a
great army, and fenced cities, and that by so doing they might avenge the
murder of hab. his he wrote to try the intentions of those of amaria. ow
when the rulers, and those that had brought up the children, had read the letter,
they were afraid and considering that they were not at all able to oppose him,
who had already subdued two very great kings, they returned him this answer
hat they owned him for their lord, and would do whatsoever he bade them. o
he wrote back to them such a reply as enjoined them to obey what he gave
order for, and to cut off the heads of hab's sons, and send them to him.
ccordingly the rulers sent for those that brought up the sons of hab, and
commanded them to slay them, to cut off their heads, and send them to ehu. o
they did whatsoever they were commanded, without omitting any thing at all,
and put them up in wicker baskets, and sent them to ezreel. nd when ehu, as
he was at supper with his friends, was informed that the heads of hab's sons
were brought, he ordered them to make two heaps of them, one before each of
the gates and in the morning he went out to take a view of them, and when he
saw them, he began to say to the people that were present, that he did himself
make an epedition against his master oram], and slew him, but that it was
not he that slew all these and he desired them to take notice, that as to hab's
family, all things had come to pass according to od's prophecy, and his house
was perished, according as lijah had foretold. nd when he had further
destroyed all the kindred of hab that were found in ezreel, he went to
amaria and as he was upon the road, he met the relations of haziah king of
erusalem, and asked them whither they were going hey replied, that they
came to salute oram, and their own king haziah, for they knew not that he
had slain them both. o ehu gave orders that they should catch these, and kill
them, being in number forty-two persons.
. fter these, there met him a good and a righteous man, whose name was
ehonadab, and who had been his friend of old. e saluted ehu, and began to

commend him, because he had done every thing according to the will of od,
in etirpating the house of hab. o ehu desired him to come up into his
chariot, and make his entry with him into amaria and told him that he would
not spare one wicked man, but would punish the false prophets, and false
priests, and those that deceived the multitude, and persuaded them to leave the
worship of od lmighty, and to worship foreign gods and that it was a most
ecellent and most pleasing sight to a good and a righteous man to see the
wicked punished. o ehonadab was persuaded by these arguments, and came
up into ehu's chariot, and came to amaria. nd ehu sought out for all hab's
kindred, and slew them. nd being desirous that none of the false prophets, nor
the priests of hab's god, might escape punishment, he caught them deceitfully
by this wile for he gathered all the people together, and said that he would
worship twice as many gods as hab worshipped, and desired that his priests,
and prophets, and servants might be present, because he would offer costly and
great sacrifices to hab's god and that if any of his priests were wanting, they
should be punished with death. ow hab's god was called aal and when he
had appointed a day on which he would offer those sacrifices, he sent
messengers through all the country of the sraelites, that they might bring the
priests of aal to him. o ehu commanded to give all the priests vestments
and when they had received them, he went into the house of aal], with his
friend ehonadab, and gave orders to make search whether there were not any
foreigner or stranger among them, for he would have no one of a different
religion to mi among their sacred offices. nd when they said that there was

. ow when thaliah, the daughter of hab, heard of the death of her brother

. ow, on the seventh year, ehoiada communicated the matter to certain

o they brought the child into the midst of them, and put on him the royal

. hen thaliah unepectedly heard the tumult and the acclamations, she

. ow as soon as what concerned thaliah was by this stratagem, after this

. ow azael, king of yria, fought against the sraelites and their king ehu,
and spoiled the eastern parts of the country beyond ordan, which belonged to

. ow ehoash, king of erusalem, had an inclination to repair the temple

. ut as soon as he was dead (which was when he had lived one hundred

. owever, it was not long before the king suffered punishment for his

. n the one and twentieth year of the reign of ehoash, ehoahaz, the son

. ow after the death of ehoahaz, his son oash took the kingdom, in the

took the kingdom of srael in amaria, for he had the same name with the king

. ow, upon the death of azael, the king of yria, that kingdom came to

. ow, in the second year of the reign of oash over srael, maziah reigned
over the tribe of udah in erusalem. is mother's name was ehoaddan, who
was born at erusalem. e was eceeding careful of doing what was right, and

ehoash, and to punish those his friends that had laid violent hands upon him

severity on their children, but acted therein according to the laws of oses,

. ow upon the victory which maziah had gotten, and the great acts he

oash, the king of srael, and commanded that he and all his people should be

what he commanded him, he must fight for his dominion. o which message
oash returned this answer in writing "ing oash to king maziah. here was
a vastly tall cypress tree in ount ebanon, as also a thistle this thistle sent to
the cypress tree to give the cypress tree's daughter in marriage to the thistle's
son but as the thistle was saying this, there came a wild beast, and trod down
the thistle and this may be a lesson to thee, not to be so ambitious, and to have
a care, lest upon thy good success in the fight against the malekites thou
growest so proud, as to bring dangers upon thyself and upon thy kingdom."
. hen maziah had read this letter, he was more eager upon this

oash, and they were going to join battle with him, there came such a fear and

it happened, that as they were scattered about by the terror that was upon them,

innovations in religion, and his contempt of od, when he had lived fifty-four

. n the fifteenth year of the reign of maziah, eroboam the son of oash
reigned over srael in amaria forty years. his king was guilty of contumely
against od,    and became very wicked in worshipping of idols, and in many
undertakings that were absurd and foreign. e was also the cause of ten
thousand misfortunes to the people of srael. ow one onah, a prophet,
foretold to him that he should make war with the yrians, and conquer their

amath, and on the southern to the lake sphaltitis for the bounds of the

o eroboam made an epedition against the yrians, and overran all their
country, as onah had foretold.
. ow  cannot but think it necessary for me, who have promised to give

commanded by od to go to the kingdom of ineveh and when he was there,
to publish it in that city, how it should lose the dominion it had over the nations.

ship,] without imitating any thing that the others did but as the waves grew

hen they had cast lots,    the lot fell upon the prophet and when they asked

by nation, and a prophet of lmighty od and he persuaded them to cast him

and that when he had been there three days, and as many nights, he was

very little time they should lose the dominion of sia. nd when he had

it written in our books.]
. hen eroboam the king had passed his life in great happiness, and had
ruled forty years, he died, and was buried in amaria, and his son echariah
took the kingdom. fter the same manner did zziah, the son of maziah,

erusalem. e was a good man, and by nature righteous and magnanimous, and
very laborious in taking care of the affairs of his kingdom. e made an
epedition also against the hilistines, and overcame them in battle, and took
the cities of ath and abneh, and brake down their walls after which
epedition he assaulted those rabs that adjoined to gypt. e also built a city
upon the ed ea, and put a garrison into it. e, after this, overthrew the
mmonites, and appointed that they should pay tribute. e also overcame all
the countries as far as the bounds of gypt, and then began to take care of
erusalem itself for the rest of his life for he rebuilt and repaired all those parts

been thrown down by the king of srael, when he took his father maziah

all sorts of seeds. e had also about him an army composed of chosen men, in

with grapplers, and other instruments of that sort.
. hile zziah was in this state, and making preparation for futurity], he
was corrupted in his mind by pride, and became insolent, and this on account of
that abundance which he had of things that will soon perish, and despised that

power which is of eternal duration (which consisted in piety towards od, and
in the observation of the laws) so he fell by occasion of the good success of his
affairs, and was carried headlong into those sins of his father, which the

ccordingly, when a remarkable day was come, and a general festival was to

the temple, and not transgress against od, he was wroth at them, and

. t was now the fourteenth year of the government of ezekiah, king of the

from their impious practices, and to leave off the honors they paid to strange

also searched the houses, and the villages, and the cities, out of a suspicion that
somebody might have one idol or other in private nay, indeed, he took away
the chariots of the sun] that were set up in his royal palace,    which his
predecessors had framed, and what thing soever there was besides which they
worshipped as a god. nd when he had thus purged all the country, he called

gave to the evites five thousand lambs, and five hundred oen, by which
means there was great plenty of sacrifices and they offered those sacrifices
according to the laws of oses, while every priest eplained the matter, and

celebrated by the ebrews from the times of amuel the prophet and the
plenty of sacrifices now was the occasion that all things were performed
according to the laws, and according to the custom of their forefathers. o
when osiah had after this lived in peace, nay, in riches and reputation also,

. ow eco, king of gypt, raised an army, and marched to the river
uphrates, in order to fight with the edes and abylonians, who had
overthrown the dominion of the ssyrians,    for he had a desire to reign over
sia. ow when he was come to the city endes, which belonged to the
kingdom of osiah, he brought an army to hinder him from passing through his
own country, in his epedition against the edes. ow eco sent a herald to
osiah, and told him that he did not make this epedition against him, but was
making haste to uphrates and desired that he would not provoke him to fight
against him, because he obstructed his march to the place whither he had
resolved to go. ut osiah did not admit of this advice of eco, but put himself
into a posture to hinder him from his intended march.  suppose it was fate that
pushed him on this conduct, that it might take an occasion against him for as
he was setting his army in array,    and rode about in his chariot, from one wing
of his army to another, one of the gyptians shot an arrow at him, and put an
end to his eagerness of fighting for being sorely wounded, he commanded a
retreat to be sounded for his army, and returned to erusalem, and died of that
wound and was magnificently buried in the sepulchre of his fathers, when he
had lived thirty-nine years, and of them had reigned thirty-one. ut all the
people mourned greatly for him, lamenting and grieving on his account many
days and eremiah the prophet composed an elegy to lament him,    which is
etant till this time also. oreover, this prophet denounced beforehand the sad
calamities that were coming upon the city. e also left behind him in writing a
description of that destruction of our nation which has lately happened in our
days, and the taking of abylon nor was he the only prophet who delivered
such predictions beforehand to the multitude, but so did zekiel also, who was
the first person that wrote, and left behind him in writing two books concerning
these events. ow these two prophets were priests by birth, but of them
eremiah dwelt in erusalem, from the thirteenth year of the reign of osiah,
until the city and temple were utterly destroyed. owever, as to what befell this
prophet, we will relate it in its proper place.
. pon the death of osiah, which we have already mentioned, his son,
ehoahaz by name, took the kingdom, being about twenty-three years old. e
reigned in erusalem and his mother was amutal, of the city ibnah. e was
an impious man, and impure in his course of life but as the king of gypt
returned from the battle, he sent for ehoahaz to come to him, to the city called
amath,    which belongs to yria and when he was come, he put him in bands,
and delivered the kingdom to a brother of his, by the father's side, whose name
was liakim, and changed his name to ehoiakim and laid a tribute upon the

land of a hundred talents of silver, and a talent of gold and this sum of money

where he died when he had reigned three months and ten days. ow
ehoiakim's mother was called ebudah, of the city umah. e was of a
wicked disposition, and ready to do mischief nor was he either religious
towards od, or good-natured towards men.

. ow in the fourth year of the reign of ehoiakim, one whose name was
ebuchadnezzar took the government over the abylonians, who at the same
time went up with a great army to the city archemish, which was at uphrates,
upon a resolution he had taken to fight with eco king of gypt, under whom
all yria then was. nd when eco understood the intention of the king of
abylon, and that this epedition was made against him, he did not despise his
attempt, but made haste with a great band of men to uphrates to defend
himself from ebuchadnezzar and when they had joined battle, he was beaten,
and lost many ten thousands of his soldiers] in the battle. o the king of
abylon passed over uphrates, and took all yria, as far as elusium,
ecepting udea. ut when ebuchadnezzar had already reigned four years,

abylon made an epedition with mighty forces against the ews, and required

e was affrighted at his threatening, and bought his peace with money, and
brought the tribute he was ordered to bring for three years.
. ut on the third year, upon hearing that the king of the abylonians made
an epedition against the gyptians, he did not pay his tribute yet was he
disappointed of his hope, for the gyptians durst not fight at this time. nd
indeed the prophet eremiah foretold every day, how vainly they relied on their
hopes from gypt, and how the city would be overthrown by the king of
abylon, and ehoiakim the king would be subdued by him. ut what he thus

bringing him before the court, they required that a sentence and a punishment

might be given against him. ow all the rest gave their votes for his
condemnation, but the elders refused, who prudently sent away the prophet
from the court of the prison], and persuaded the rest to do eremiah no harm
for they said that he was not the only person who foretold what would come to
the city, but that icah signified the same before him, as well as many others,
none of which suffered any thing of the kings that then reigned, but were
honored as the prophets of od. o they mollified the multitude with these

the scribe, and bring them to him, that they might be punished. owever, they

. ow, a little time afterwards, the king of abylon made an epedition

days.

. ut a terror seized on the king of abylon, who had given the kingdom to
ehoiachin, and that immediately he was afraid that he should bear him a
grudge, because of his killing his father, and thereupon should make the
country revolt from him wherefore he sent an army, and besieged ehoiachin in
erusalem but because he was of a gentle and just disposition, he did not desire
to see the city endangered on his account, but he took his mother and kindred,
and delivered them to the commanders sent by the king of abylon, and
accepted of their oaths, that neither should they suffer any harm, nor the city
which agreement they did not observe for a single year, for the king of abylon
did not keep it, but gave orders to his generals to take all that were in the city
captives, both the youth and the handicraftsmen, and bring them bound to him
their number was ten thousand eight hundred and thirty-two as also ehoiachin,
and his mother and friends. nd when these were brought to him, he kept them
in custody, and appointed ehoiachin's uncle, edekiah, to be king and made
him take an oath, that he would certainly keep the kingdom for him, and make
no innovation, nor have any league of friendship with the gyptians.
. ow edekiah was twenty and one year's old when he took the
government and had the same mother with his brother ehoiakim, but was a
despiser of justice and of his duty, for truly those of the same age with him
were wicked about him, and the whole multitude did what unjust and insolent
things they pleased for which reason the prophet eremiah came often to him,
and protested to him, and insisted, that he must leave off his impieties and
transgressions, and take care of what was right, and neither give ear to the

who deluded them, as if the king of abylon would make no more war against

since what they said was not true, and the events would not prove such as they
epected]. ow as to edekiah himself, while he heard the prophet speak, he
believed him, and agreed to every thing as true, and supposed it was for his

in abylon what calamities were coming upon the people, which when he
heard, he sent accounts of them unto erusalem. ut edekiah did not believe
their prophecies, for the reason following it happened that the two prophets
agreed with one another in what they said as in all other things, that the city
should be taken, and edekiah himself should be taken captive but zekiel

disagreed with him, and said that edekiah should not see abylon, while
eremiah said to him, that the king of abylon should carry him away thither in
bonds. nd because they did not both say the same thing as to this
circumstance, he disbelieved what they both appeared to agree in, and
condemned them as not speaking truth therein, although all the things foretold
him did come to pass according to their prophecies, as we shall show upon a
fitter opportunity.
. ow when edekiah had preserved the league of mutual assistance he
had made with the abylonians for eight years, he brake it, and revolted to the
gyptians, in hopes, by their assistance, of overcoming the abylonians. hen
the king of abylon knew this, he made war against him he laid his country

ally was in, he took a great army with him, and came into udea, as if he would
raise the siege upon which the king of abylon departed from erusalem, and
met the gyptians, and joined battle with them, and beat them and when he

as soon as the king of abylon was departed from erusalem, the false prophets

servitude, and overthrow the abylonians "and that we shall be dismissed, and
return to this land, and rebuild the temple, and restore erusalem."    hen
eremiah said this, the greater part believed him but the rulers, and those that

resolved to go elsewhere, to his own country, which was called nathoth, and
was twenty furlongs distant from erusalem and as he was going, one of the
rulers met him, and seized upon him, and accused him falsely, as though he

would not believe him, but seized upon him, and led him away to the rulers,

and laid an accusation against him, under whom he endured all sorts of
torments and tortures, and was reserved to be punished and this was the
condition he was in for some time, while he suffered what  have already
described unjustly.
. ow in the ninth year of the reign of edekiah, on the tenth day of the
tenth month, the king of abylon made a second epedition against erusalem,
and lay before it eighteen months, and besieged it with the utmost application.
here came upon them also two of the greatest calamities at the same time that

but cried out, and proclaimed aloud, and ehorted the multitude to open their

preserved, and their whole families but if they did not so, they should be
destroyed and he foretold, that if any one staid in the city, he should certainly
perish by one of these ways,—either be consumed by the famine, or slain by
the enemy's sword but that if he would flee to the enemy, he should escape
death. et did not these rulers who heard believe him, even when they were in
the midst of their sore calamities but they came to the king, and in their anger
informed him what eremiah had said, and accused him, and complained of the
prophet as of a madman, and one that disheartened their minds, and by the

otherwise ready to epose themselves to dangers for him, and for their country,

them that the city should certainly be taken, and be utterly destroyed.
. ut for the king himself, he was not at all irritated against eremiah, such
was his gentle and righteous disposition yet, that he might not be engaged in a
quarrel with those rulers at such a time, by opposing what they intended, he let
them do with the prophet whatsoever they would whereupon, when the king
had granted them such a permission, they presently came into the prison, and
took him, and let him down with a cord into a pit full of mire, that he might be
suffocated, and die of himself. o he stood up to the neck in the mire which
was all about him, and so continued but there was one of the king's servants,

whatsoever else they understood to be necessary for the prophet's preservation,

to take, and drew up the prophet out of the mire, and left him at liberty in the

prison].
. ut when the king had sent to call him privately, and inquired what he
could say to him from od, which might be suitable to his present
circumstances, and desired him to inform him of it, eremiah replied, that he
had somewhat to say but he said withal, he should not be believed, nor, if he
admonished them, should be hearkened to "for," said he, "thy friends have
determined to destroy me, as though  had been guilty of some wickedness and
where are now those men who deceived us, and said that the king of abylon
would not come and fight against us any more but  am afraid now to speak

abylonians and he said that it was od who prophesied this by him, that he
must do so] if he would be preserved, and escape out of the danger he was in,
and that then neither should the city fall to the ground, nor should the temple be
burned but that if he disobeyed] he would be the cause of these miseries
coming upon the citizens, and of the calamity that would befall his whole
house. hen the king heard this, he said that he would willingly do what he
persuaded him to, and what he declared would be to his advantage, but that he
was afraid of those of his own country that had fallen away to the abylonians,
lest he should be accused by them to the king of abylon, and be punished. ut
the prophet encouraged him, and said he had no cause to fear such punishment,
for that he should not have the eperience of any misfortune, if he would
deliver all up to the abylonians, neither himself, nor his children, nor his
wives, and that the temple should then continue unhurt. o when eremiah had
said this, the king let him go, and charged him to betray what they had resolved

they should have learned that he had been sent for, and should inquire of him
what it was that he was sent for, and what he had said to him but to pretend to
them that he besought him that he might not be kept in bonds and in prison.
nd indeed he said so to them for they came to the prophet, and asked him
what advice it was that he came to give the king relating to them. nd thus 
have finished what concerns this matter.

. ow the king of abylon was very intent and earnest upon the siege of
erusalem and he erected towers upon great banks of earth, and from them
repelled those that stood upon the walls he also made a great number of such
banks round about the whole city, whose height was equal to those walls.
owever, those that were within bore the siege with courage and alacrity, for
they were not discouraged, either by the famine, or by the pestilential
distemper, but were of cheerful minds in the prosecution of the war, although
those miseries within oppressed them also, and they did not suffer themselves
to be terrified, either by the contrivances of the enemy, or by their engines of
war, but contrived still different engines to oppose all the other withal, till
indeed there seemed to be an entire struggle between the abylonians and the
people of erusalem, which had the greater sagacity and skill the former party
supposing they should be thereby too hard for the other, for the destruction of
the city the latter placing their hopes of deliverance in nothing else but in
persevering in such inventions in opposition to the other, as might demonstrate
the enemy's engines were useless to them. nd this siege they endured for
eighteen months, until they were destroyed by the famine, and by the darts
which the enemy threw at them from the towers.
. ow the city was taken on the ninth day of the fourth month, in the
eleventh year of the reign of edekiah. hey were indeed only generals of the
king of abylon, to whom ebuchadnezzar committed the care of the siege, for
he abode himself in the city of iblah. he names of these generals who
ravaged and subdued erusalem, if any one desire to know them, were these
ergal harezer, amgar ebo, absaris, orsechim, and abmag. nd when
the city was taken about midnight, and the enemy's generals were entered into
the temple, and when edekiah was sensible of it, he took his wives, and his
children, and his captains, and his friends, and with them fled out of the city,
through the fortified ditch, and through the desert and when certain of the
deserters had informed the abylonians of this, at break of day, they made
haste to pursue after edekiah, and overtook him not far from ericho, and
encompassed him about. ut for those friends and captains of edekiah who
had fled out of the city with him, when they saw their enemies near them, they
left him, and dispersed themselves, some one way, and some another, and every
one resolved to save himself so the enemy took edekiah alive, when he was
deserted by all but a few, with his children and his wives, and brought him to
the king. hen he was come, ebuchadnezzar began to call him a wicked
wretch, and a covenant-breaker, and one that had forgotten his former words,
when he promised to keep the country for him. e also reproached him for his
ingratitude, that when he had received the kingdom from him, who had taken it
from ehoiachin, and given it to him, he had made use of the power he gave

him against him that gave it "but," said he, "od is great, who hated that
conduct of thine, and hath brought thee under us." nd when he had used these
words to edekiah, he commanded his sons and his friends to be slain, while
edekiah and the rest of the captains looked on after which he put out the eyes
of edekiah, and bound him, and carried him to abylon. nd these things
happened to him,    as eremiah and zekiel had foretold to him, that he should
be caught, and brought before the king of abylon, and should speak to him
face to face, and should see his eyes with his own eyes and thus far did
eremiah prophesy. ut he was also made blind, and brought to abylon, but
did not see it, according to the prediction of zekiel.
. e have said thus much, because it was sufficient to show the nature of
od to such as are ignorant of it, that it is various, and acts many different
ways, and that all events happen after a regular manner, in their proper season,
and that it foretells what must come to pass. t is also sufficient to show the
ignorance and incredulity of men, whereby they are not permitted to foresee
any thing that is future, and are, without any guard, eposed to calamities, so
that it is impossible for them to avoid the eperience of those calamities.
. nd after this manner have the kings of avid's race ended their lives,
being in number twenty-one, until the last king, who all together reigned five
hundred and fourteen years, and si months, and ten days of whom aul, who
was their first king, retained the government twenty years, though he was not of
the same tribe with the rest.
. nd now it was that the king of abylon sent ebuzaradan, the general
of his army, to erusalem, to pillage the temple, who had it also in command to
burn it and the royal palace, and to lay the city even with the ground, and to
transplant the people into abylon. ccordingly, he came to erusalem in the
eleventh year of king edekiah, and pillaged the temple, and carried out the
vessels of od, both gold and silver, and particularly that large laver which
olomon dedicated, as also the pillars of brass, and their chapiters, with the
golden tables and the candlesticks and when he had carried these off, he set
fire to the temple in the fifth month, the first day of the month, in the eleventh
year of the reign of edekiah, and in the eighteenth year of ebuchadnezzar he
also burnt the palace, and overthrew the city. ow the temple was burnt four
hundred and seventy years, si months, and ten days after it was built. t was
then one thousand and sity-two years, si months, and ten days from the
departure out of gypt and from the deluge to the destruction of the temple,
the whole interval was one thousand nine hundred and fifty-seven years, si
months, and ten days but from the generation of dam, until this befell the
temple, there were three thousand five hundred and thirteen years, si months,
and ten days so great was the number of years hereto belonging. nd what
actions were done during these years we have particularly related. ut the

general of the abylonian king now overthrew the city to the very foundations,
and removed all the people, and took for prisoners the high priest eraiah, and
ephaniah the priest that was net to him, and the rulers that guarded the
temple, who were three in number, and the eunuch who was over the armed
men, and seven friends of edekiah, and his scribe, and sity other rulers all
which, together with the vessels which they had pillaged, he carried to the king
of abylon to iblah, a city of yria. o the king commanded the heads of the
high priest and of the rulers to be cut off there but he himself led all the
captives and edekiah to abylon. e also led osedek the high priest away
bound. e was the son of eraiah the high priest, whom the king of abylon
had slain in iblah, a city of yria, as we just now related.
. nd now, because we have enumerated the succession of the kings, and
who they were, and how long they reigned,  think it necessary to set down the
names of the high priests, and who they were that succeeded one another in the
high priesthood under the kings. he first high priest then at the temple which
olomon built was adok after him his son chimas received that dignity
after chimas was zarias his son was oram, and oram's son was sus after
him was ioramus his son was hideas, and hideas' son was udeas, and
udeas' son was uelus, and uelus' son was otham, and otham's son was
rias, and rias' son was erias, and erias' son was deas, and his son was
allumus, and allumus' son was lcias, and his son was zarias, and his son]
was areas,    and his son was osedek, who was carried captive to abylon. ll
these received the high priesthood by succession, the sons from their father.
. hen the king was come to abylon, he kept edekiah in prison until he
died, and buried him magnificently, and dedicated the vessels he had pillaged
out of the temple of erusalem to his own gods, and planted the people in the
country of abylon, but freed the high priest from his bonds.

. ow the general of the army, ebuzaradan, when he had carried the people
of the ews into captivity, left the poor, and those that had deserted, in the

country, and made one, whose name was edaliah, the son of hikam, a person
of a noble family, their governor which edaliah was of a gentle and righteous
disposition. e also commanded them that they should cultivate the ground,
and pay an appointed tribute to the king. e also took eremiah the prophet out
of prison, and would have persuaded him to go along with him to abylon, for
that he had been enjoined by the king to supply him with whatsoever he
wanted and if he did not like to do so, he desired him to inform him where he
resolved to dwell, that he might signify the same to the king. ut the prophet
had no mind to follow him, nor to dwell any where else, but would gladly live
in the ruins of his country, and in the miserable remains of it. hen the general
understood what his purpose was, he enjoined edaliah, whom he left behind,
to take all possible care of him, and to supply him with whatsoever he wanted.
o when he had given him rich presents, he dismissed him. ccordingly,
eremiah abode in a city of that country, which was called ispah and desired
of ebuzaradan that he would set at liberty his disciple aruch,    the son of
eriah, one of a very eminent family, and eceeding skilful in the language of
his country.
. hen ebuzaradan had done thus, he made haste to abylon. ut as to
those that fled away during the siege of erusalem, and had been scattered over
the country, when they heard that the abylonians were gone away, and had left
a remnant in the land of erusalem, and those such as were to cultivate the

. ut now ebuchadnezzar, king of abylon, took some of the most noble of

. ow two years after the destruction of gypt, king ebuchadnezzar saw

. hen aniel was come in to the king, he ecused himself first, that he

king that shall come from the west, armed with brass, shall destroy that

or things present, but not things that are future yet if any one be so very
desirous of knowing truth, as not to wave such points of curiosity, and cannot
curb his inclination for understanding the uncertainties of futurity, and whether
they will happen or not, let him be diligent in reading the book of aniel,
which he will find among the sacred writings.
. hen ebuchadnezzar heard this, and recollected his dream, he was

.  little after this the king saw in his sleep again another vision how he

assured those that think me defective in any such point, or complain of my

. ow when king ebuchadnezzar had reigned forty-three years,    he ended

before him. ow erosus makes mention of his actions in the third book of his

. ut now, after the death of ebuchadnezzar, vil-erodach his son

. hen altasar heard this, he called for aniel and when he had

. hen aniel had told the king that the writing upon the wall signified

misfortunes to him, but bestowed it all upon him as reasoning thus, that what
he was to reward was peculiar to himself, and to fate, and did not belong to the
prophet, but that it was the part of a good and a just man to give what he had
promised, although the events were of a melancholy nature. ccordingly, the
king determined so to do. ow, after a little while, both himself and the city
were taken by yrus, the king of ersia, who fought against him for it was
altasar, under whom abylon was taken, when he had reigned seventeen
years. nd this is the end of the posterity of king ebuchadnezzar, as history
informs us but when abylon was taken by arius, and when he, with his
kinsman yrus, had put an end to the dominion of the abylonians, he was
sity-two years old. e was the son of styages, and had another name among
the reeks. oreover, he took aniel the prophet, and carried him with him
into edia, and honored him very greatly, and kept him with him for he was
one of the three presidents whom he set over his three hundred and sity
provinces, for into so many did arius part them.
. owever, while aniel was in so great dignity, and in so great favor with
arius, and was alone intrusted with every thing by him, a having somewhat
divine in him, he was envied by the rest for those that see others in greater
honor than themselves with kings envy them and when those that were grieved
at the great favor aniel was in with arius sought for an occasion against him,
he afforded them no occasion at all, for he was above all the temptations of
money, and despised bribery, and esteemed it a very base thing to take any
thing by way of reward, even when it might be justly given him he afforded
those that envied him not the least handle for an accusation. o when they
could find nothing for which they might calumniate him to the king, nothing
that was shameful or reproachful, and thereby deprive him of the honor he was
in with him, they sought for some other method whereby they might destroy
him. hen therefore they saw that aniel prayed to od three times a day, they
thought they had gotten an occasion by which they might ruin him so they
came to arius and told him that the princes and governors had thought proper
to allow the multitude a relaation for thirty days, that no one might offer a
petition or prayer either to himself or to the gods, but that he who shall
transgress this decree shall be cast into the den of lions, and there perish."
. hereupon the king, not being acquainted with their wicked design, nor
suspecting that it was a contrivance of theirs against aniel, said he was
pleased with this decree of theirs, and he promised to confirm what they
desired he also published an edict to promulgate to the people that decree
which the princes had made. ccordingly, all the rest took care not to transgress
those injunctions, and rested in quiet but aniel had no regard to them, but, as

he was wont, he stood and prayed to od in the sight of them all but the
princes having met with the occasion they so earnestly sought to find against
aniel, came presently to the king, and accused him, that aniel was the only
person that transgressed the decree, while not one of the rest durst pray to their
gods. his discovery they made, not because of his impiety, but because they
had watched him, and observed him out of envy for supposing that arius did
thus out of a greater kindness to him than they epected, and that he was ready
to grant him pardon for this contempt of his injunctions, and envying this very
pardon to aniel, they did not become more honorable to him, but desired he
might be cast into the den of lions according to the law. o arius, hoping that
od would deliver him, and that he would undergo nothing that was terrible by
the wild beasts, bid him bear this accident cheerfully. nd when he was cast
into the den, he put his seal to the stone that lay upon the mouth of the den, and
went his way, but he passed all the night without food and without sleep, being
in great distress for aniel but when it was day, he got up, and came to the
den, and found the seal entire, which he had left the stone sealed withal he also
opened the seal, and cried out, and called to aniel, and asked him if he were
alive. nd as soon as he heard the king's voice, and said that he had suffered no
harm, the king gave order that he should be drawn up out of the den. ow when
his enemies saw that aniel had suffered nothing which was terrible, they
would not own that he was preserved by od, and by his providence but they
said that the lions had been filled full with food, and on that account it was, as
they supposed, that the lions would not touch aniel, nor come to him and this
they alleged to the king. ut the king, out of an abhorrence of their wickedness,
gave order that they should throw in a great deal of flesh to the lions and when
they had filled themselves, he gave further order that aniel's enemies should
be cast into the den, that he might learn whether the lions, now they were full,
would touch them or not. nd it appeared plain to arius, after the princes had
been cast to the wild beasts, that it was od who preserved aniel,    for the
lions spared none of them, but tore them all to pieces, as if they had been very
hungry, and wanted food.  suppose therefore it was not their hunger, which had
been a little before satisfied with abundance of flesh, but the wickedness of
these men, that provoked them to destroy the princes] for if it so please od,
that wickedness might, by even those irrational creatures, be esteemed a plain
foundation for their punishment.
. hen therefore those that had intended thus to destroy aniel by
treachery were themselves destroyed, king arius sent letters] over all the
country, and praised that od whom aniel worshipped, and said that he was
the only true od, and had all power. e had also aniel in very great esteem,
and made him the principal of his friends. ow when aniel was become so
illustrious and famous, on account of the opinion men had that he was beloved

of od, he built a tower at cbatana, in edia it was a most elegant building,
and wonderfully made, and it is still remaining, and preserved to this day and
to such as see it, it appears to have been lately built, and to have been no older
than that very day when any one looks upon it, it is so fresh,    flourishing, and
beautiful, and no way grown old in so long time for buildings suffer the same
as men do, they grow old as well as they, and by numbers of years their
strength is dissolved, and their beauty withered. ow they bury the kings of
edia, of ersia, and arthia in this tower to this day, and he who was
entrusted with the care of it was a ewish priest which thing is also observed to
this day. ut it is fit to give an account of what this man did, which is most
admirable to hear, for he was so happy as to have strange revelations made to
him, and those as to one of the greatest of the prophets, insomuch, that while he
was alive he had the esteem and applause both of the kings and of the
multitude and now he is dead, he retains a remembrance that will never fail,
for the several books that he wrote and left behind him are still read by us till
this time and from them we believe that aniel conversed with od for he did
not only prophesy of future events, as did the other prophets, but he also
determined the time of their accomplishment. nd while prophets used to
foretell misfortunes, and on that account were disagreeable both to the kings
and to the multitude, aniel was to them a prophet of good things, and this to
such a degree, that by the agreeable nature of his predictions, he procured the
goodwill of all men and by the accomplishment of them, he procured the belief
of their truth, and the opinion of a sort of] divinity for himself, among the
multitude. e also wrote and left behind him what made manifest the accuracy
and undeniable veracity of his predictions for he saith, that when he was in
usa, the metropolis of ersia, and went out into the field with his companions,
there was, on the sudden, a motion and concussion of the earth, and that he was
left alone by himself, his friends fleeing away from him, and that he was
disturbed, and fell on his face, and on his two hands, and that a certain person
touched him, and, at the same time, bid him rise, and see what would befall his
countrymen after many generations. e also related, that when he stood up, he
was shown a great rain, with many horns growing out of his head, and that the
last was higher than the rest that after this he looked to the west, and saw a he-
goat carried through the air from that quarter that he rushed upon the ram with
violence, and smote him twice with his horns, and overthrew him to the ground,
and trampled upon him that afterward he saw a very great horn growing out of
the head of the he-goat, and that when it was broken off, four horns grew up
that were eposed to each of the four winds, and he wrote that out of them
arose another lesser horn, which, as he said, waed great and that od showed
to him that it should fight against his nation, and take their city by force, and
bring the temple worship to confusion, and forbid the sacrifices to be offered

for one thousand two hundred and ninety-si days. aniel wrote that he saw
these visions in the lain of usa and he hath informed us that od interpreted
the appearance of this vision after the following manner e said that the ram
signified the kingdoms of the edes and ersians, and the horns those kings
that were to reign in them and that the last horn signified the last king, and that
he should eceed all the kings in riches and glory that the he-goat signified that
one should come and reign from the reeks, who should twice fight with the
ersian, and overcome him in battle, and should receive his entire dominion
that by the great horn which sprang out of the forehead of the he-goat was
meant the first king and that the springing up of four horns upon its falling off,
and the conversion of every one of them to the four quarters of the earth,
signified the successors that should arise after the death of the first king, and
the partition of the kingdom among them, and that they should be neither his
children, nor of his kindred, that should reign over the habitable earth for many
years and that from among them there should arise a certain king that should
overcome our nation and their laws, and should take away their political
government, and should spoil the temple, and forbid the sacrifices to be offered
for three years' time. nd indeed it so came to pass, that our nation suffered
these things under ntiochus piphanes, according to aniel's vision, and what
he wrote many years before they came to pass. n the very same manner aniel
also wrote concerning the oman government, and that our country should be
made desolate by them. ll these things did this man leave in writing, as od
had showed them to him, insomuch that such as read his prophecies, and see
how they have been fulfilled, would wonder at the honor wherewith od
honored aniel and may thence discover how the picureans are in an error,
who cast rovidence out of human life, and do not believe that od takes care
of the affairs of the world, nor that the universe is governed and continued in
being by that blessed and immortal nature, but say that the world is carried
along of its own accord, without a ruler and a curator which, were it destitute
of a guide to conduct it, as they imagine, it would be like ships without pilots,
which we see drowned by the winds, or like chariots without drivers, which are
overturned so would the world be dashed to pieces by its being carried without
a rovidence, and so perish, and come to nought. o that, by the forementioned
predictions of aniel, those men seem to me very much to err from the truth,
who determine that od eercises no providence over human affairs for if that
were the case, that the world went on by mechanical necessity, we should not
see that all things would come to pass according to his prophecy. ow as to
myself,  have so described these matters as  have found them and read them
but if any one is inclined to another opinion about them, let him enjoy his
different sentiments without any blame from me.

. hen the foundations of the temple were laying, and when the ews were
very zealous about building it, the neighboring nations, and especially the

edia, and had planted in amaria, when he carried the people of srael
captives, besought the governors, and those that had the care of such affairs,
that they would interrupt the ews, both in the rebuilding of their city, and in
the building of their temple. ow as these men were corrupted by them with
money, they sold the utheans their interest for rendering this building a slow
and a careless work, for yrus, who was busy about other wars, knew nothing
of all this and it so happened, that when he had led his army against the
assagetae, he ended his life.    ut when ambyses, the son of yrus, had
taken the kingdom, the governors in yria, and hoenicia, and in the countries
of mmon, and oab, and amaria, wrote an epistle to ambyses whose
contents were as follow "o our lord ambyses. e thy servants, athumus
the historiographer, and emellius the scribe, and the rest that are thy judges in
yria and hoenicia, send greeting. t is fit,  king, that thou shouldst know
that those ews which were carried to abylon are come into our country, and
are building that rebellious and wicked city, and its market-places, and setting
up its walls, and raising up the temple know therefore, that when these things
are finished, they will not be willing to pay tribute, nor will they submit to thy
commands, but will resist kings, and will choose rather to rule over others than
be ruled over themselves. e therefore thought it proper to write to thee, 
king, while the works about the temple are going on so fast, and not to overlook
this matter, that thou mayst search into the books of thy fathers, for thou wilt
find in them that the ews have been rebels, and enemies to kings, as hath their
city been also, which, for that reason, hath been till now laid waste. e thought
proper also to inform thee of this matter, because thou mayst otherwise perhaps
be ignorant of it, that if this city be once inhabited and be entirely encompassed

with walls, thou wilt be ecluded from thy passage to oelesyria and
hoenicia."
. hen ambyses had read the epistle, being naturally wicked, he was
irritated at what they told him, and wrote back to them as follows "ambyses
the king, to athumus the historiographer, to eeltethemus, to emellius the
scribe, and the rest that are in commission, and dwelling in amaria and
hoenicia, after this manner  have read the epistle that was sent from you and
 gave order that the books of my forefathers should be searched into, and it is
there found that this city hath always been an enemy to kings, and its
inhabitants have raised seditions and wars. e also are sensible that their kings
have been powerful and tyrannical, and have eacted tribute of oelesyria and
hoenicia. herefore  gave order, that the ews shall not be permitted to build
that city, lest such mischief as they used to bring upon kings be greatly
augmented." hen this epistle was read, athumus, and emellius the scribe,
and their associates, got suddenly on horseback, and made haste to erusalem

city and the temple. ccordingly, these works were hindered from going on till
the second year of the reign of arius, for nine years more for ambyses
reigned si years, and within that time overthrew gypt, and when he was
come back, he died at amascus.

. fter the slaughter of the magi, who, upon the death of ambyses, attained
the government of the ersians for a year, those families which were called the
seven families of the ersians appointed arius, the son of ystaspes, to be
their king. ow he, while he was a private man, had made a vow to od, that if
he came to be king, he would send all the vessels of od that were in abylon
to the temple at erusalem. ow it so fell out, that about this time orobabel,
who had been made governor of the ews that had been in captivity, came to
arius, from erusalem for there had been an old friendship between him and
the king. e was also, with two others, thought worthy to be guard of the king's
body and obtained that honor which he hoped for.
. ow, in the first year of the king's reign, arius feasted those that were

about him, and those born in his house, with the rulers of the edes, and
princes of the ersians, and the toparchs of ndia and thiopia, and the generals
of the armies of his hundred and twenty-seven provinces. ut when they had

at their own houses, and arius the king went to bed but after he had rested a
little part of the night, he awaked, and not being able to sleep any more, he fell
into conversation with the three guards of his body, and promised, that to him
who should make an oration about points that he should inquire of, such as
should be most agreeable to truth, and to the dictates of wisdom, he would
grant it as a reward of his victory, to put on a purple garment, and to drink in
cups of gold, and to sleep upon gold, and to have a chariot with bridles of gold,
and a head tire of fine linen, and a chain of gold about his neck, and to sit net
to himself, on account of his wisdom "and," says he, "he shall be called my
cousin." ow when he had promised to give them these gifts, he asked the first
of them, "hether wine was not the strongest"—the second, "hether kings
were not such"—and the third, "hether women were not such or whether
truth was not the strongest of all" hen he had proposed that they should
make their inquiries about these problems, he went to rest but in the morning
he sent for his great men, his princes, and toparchs of ersia and edia, and set
himself down in the place where he used to give audience, and bid each of the
guards of his body to declare what they thought proper concerning the proposed
questions, in the hearing of them all.
. ccordingly, the first of them began to speak of the strength of wine, and
demonstrated it thus "hen," said he, " am to give my opinion of wine,  you
men,  find that it eceeds every thing, by the following indications it deceives
the mind of those that drink it, and reduces that of the king to the same state
with that of the orphan, and he who stands in need of a tutor and erects that of
the slave to the boldness of him that is free and that of the needy becomes like
that of the rich man, for it changes and renews the souls of men when it gets
into them and it quenches the sorrow of those that are under calamities, and
makes men forget the debts they owe to others, and makes them think
themselves to be of all men the richest it makes them talk of no small things,
but of talents, and such other names as become wealthy men only nay more, it
makes them insensible of their commanders, and of their kings, and takes away
the remembrance of their friends and companions, for it arms men even against
those that are dearest to them, and makes them appear the greatest strangers to
them and when they are become sober, and they have slept out their wine in
the night, they arise without knowing any thing they have done in their cups. 
take these for signs of power, and by them discover that wine is the strongest
and most insuperable of all things."
. s soon as the first had given the forementioned demonstrations of the

strength of wine, he left off and the net to him began to speak about the
strength of a king, and demonstrated that it was the strongest of all, and more
powerful than any thing else that appears to have any force or wisdom. e
began his demonstration after the following manner and said, "hey are men
who govern all things they force the earth and the sea to become profitable to
them in what they desire, and over these men do kings rule, and over them they
have authority. ow those who rule over that animal which is of all the
strongest and most powerful, must needs deserve to be esteemed insuperable in
power and force. or eample, when these kings command their subjects to
make wars, and undergo dangers, they are hearkened to and when they send
them against their enemies, their power is so great that they are obeyed. hey
command men to level mountains, and to pull down walls and towers nay,
when they are commanded to be killed and to kill, they submit to it, that they
may not appear to transgress the king's commands and when they have
conquered, they bring what they have gained in the war to the king. hose also
who are not soldiers, but cultivate the ground, and plough it, and when, after
they have endured the labor and all the inconveniences of such works of
husbandry, they have reaped and gathered in their fruits, they bring tributes to
the king and whatsoever it is which the king says or commands, it is done of
necessity, and that without any delay, while he in the mean time is satiated with
all sorts of food and pleasures, and sleeps in quiet. e is guarded by such as
watch, and such as are, as it were, fied down to the place through fear for no
one dares leave him, even when he is asleep, nor does any one go away and
take care of his own affairs but he esteems this one thing the only work of
necessity, to guard the king, and accordingly to this he wholly addicts himself.
ow then can it be otherwise, but that it must appear that the king eceeds all
in strength, while so great a multitude obeys his injunctions"
. ow when this man had held his peace, the third of them, who was
orobabel, began to instruct them about women, and about truth, who said
thus "ine is strong, as is the king also, whom all men obey, but women are
superior to them in power for it was a woman that brought the king into the
world and for those that plant the vines and make the wine, they are women
who bear them, and bring them up nor indeed is there any thing which we do
not receive from them for these women weave garments for us, and our
household affairs are by their means taken care of, and preserved in safety nor
can we live separate from women. nd when we have gotten a great deal of
gold and silver, and any other thing that is of great value, and deserving regard,
and see a beautiful woman, we leave all these things, and with open mouth fi
our eyes upon her countenance, and are willing to forsake what we have, that
we may enjoy her beauty, and procure it to ourselves. e also leave father, and
mother, and the earth that nourishes us, and frequently forget our dearest

friends, for the sake of women nay, we are so hardy as to lay down our lives
for them. ut what will chiefly make you take notice of the strength of women
is this that follows do not we take pains, and endure a great deal of trouble, and
that both by land and sea, and when we have procured somewhat as the fruit of
our labors, do not we bring them to the women, as to our mistresses, and
bestow them upon them ay,  once saw the king, who is lord of so many
people, smitten on the face by pame, the daughter of abases hemasius, his
concubine, and his diadem taken away from him, and put upon her own head,
while he bore it patiently and when she smiled he smiled, and when she was
angry he was sad and according to the change of her passions, he flattered his
wife, and drew her to reconciliation by the great humiliation of himself to her,
if at any time he saw her displeased at him."
. nd when the princes and rulers looked one upon another, he began to
speak about truth and he said, " have already demonstrated how powerful
women are but both these women themselves, and the king himself, are weaker
than truth for although the earth be large, and the heaven high, and the course
of the sun swift, yet are all these moved according to the will of od, who is
true and righteous, for which cause we also ought to esteem truth to be the
strongest of all things, and that what is unrighteous is of no force against it.
oreover, all things else that have any strength are mortal and short-lived, but
truth is a thing that is immortal and eternal. t affords us not indeed such a
beauty as will wither away by time, nor such riches as may be taken away by
fortune, but righteous rules and laws. t distinguishes them from injustice, and
puts what is unrighteous to rebuke." 
 . o when orobabel had left off his discourse about truth, and the

the king commanded that he should ask for somewhat over and above what he

understanding."
. o the king was pleased with what he had said, and arose and kissed him
and wrote to the toparchs and governors, and enjoined them to conduct

. ow when orobabel had obtained these grants from the king, he went

he came to abylon, and brought the good news to his countrymen of what

. nd thus did these men go, a certain and determinate number out of

two myriads and eight thousand    the evites were seventy-four the number of
the women and children mied together was forty thousand seven hundred and

. ow in the seventh month after they were departed out of abylon, both
eshua the high priest, and orobabel the governor, sent messengers every way
round about, and gathered those that were in the country together to erusalem
universally, who came very gladly thither. e then built the altar on the same
place it had formerly been built, that they might offer the appointed sacrifices

did not please the neighboring nations, who all of them bare an ill-will to them.
hey also celebrated the feast of tabernacles at that time, as the legislator had
ordained concerning it and after they offered sacrifices, and what were called
the daily sacrifices, and the oblations proper for the abbaths, and for all the
holy festivals. hose also that had made vows performed them, and offered
their sacrifices from the first day of the seventh month. hey also began to

build the temple, and gave a great deal of money to the masons and to the
carpenters, and what was necessary for the maintenance of the workmen. he
idonians also were very willing and ready to bring the cedar trees from
ibanus, to bind them together, and to make a united float of them, and to bring

what was now done at the command of arius.
. n the second year of their coming to erusalem, as the ews were there in
the second month, the building of the temple went on apace and when they had
laid its foundations on the first day of the second month of that second year,
they set, as overseers of the work, such evites as were full twenty years old
and eshua and his sons and brethren, and odmiel the brother of udas, the son
of minadab, with his sons and the temple, by the great diligence of those that
had the care of it, was finished sooner than any one would have epected. nd
when the temple was finished, the priests, adorned with their accustomed

bless od. ow the priests and evites, and the elder part of the families,
recollecting with themselves how much greater and more sumptuous the old

their temple. ereupon they were disconsolate, and not able to contain their
grief, and proceeded so far as to lament and shed tears on those accounts but
the people in general were contented with their present condition and because
they were allowed to build them a temple, they desired no more, and neither
regarded nor remembered, nor indeed at all tormented themselves with the
comparison of that and the former temple, as if this were below their
epectations but the wailing of the old men and of the priests, on account of

. ut when the amaritans, who were still enemies to the tribes of udah
and enjamin, heard the sound of the trumpets, they came running together,
and desired to know what was the occasion of this tumult and when they
perceived that it was from the ews, who had been carried captive to abylon,

the heads of the families, and desired that they would give them leave to build

"e worship their od, and especially pray to him, and are desirous of their
religious settlement, and this ever since halmanezer, the king of ssyria,
transplanted us out of uthah and edia to this place." hen they said thus,

orobabel and eshua the high priest, and the heads of the families of the

. hen the utheans heard this, for the amaritans have that appellation,

ow at this time isinnes, the governor of yria and hoenicia, and

of od lmighty that this temple was built for him by a king of theirs, that
lived in great prosperity, and one that eceeded all men in virtue and that it
continued a long time, but that because of their fathers' impiety towards od,
ebuchadnezzar, king of the abylonians and of the haldeans, took their city

transplanted the people whom he had made captives, and removed them to

ithridates the treasurer and gave order to have them carried to erusalem, and

finished, by reason of the malignity of our enemies. f therefore you have a

. hen orobabel and the high priest had made this answer, isinnes, and
those that were with him, did not resolve to hinder the building, until they had

discouragement from the ersians, for that od foretold this to them. o, in
dependence on those prophets, they applied themselves earnestly to building,
and did not intermit one day.
. ow arius, when the amaritans had written to him, and in their epistle

he gave order that what concerned these matters should be sought for among

. hen arius had found this book among the records of yrus, he wrote
an answer to isinnes and his associates, whose contents were these "ing
arius to isinnes the governor, and to athrabuzanes, sendeth greeting.
aving found a copy of this epistle among the records of yrus,  have sent it
you and  will that all things be done as is therein written.—arewell." o

when isinnes, and those that were with him, understood the intention of the
king, they resolved to follow his directions entirely for the time to come. o
they forwarded the sacred works, and assisted the elders of the ews, and the
princes of the anhedrim and the structure of the temple was with great
diligence brought to a conclusion, by the prophecies of aggai and echariah,

the reign of arius, on the twenty-third day of the twelfth month, which is by
us called dar, but by the acedonians ystrus, the priests, and evites, and
the other multitude of the sraelites, offered sacrifices, as the renovation of their
former prosperity after their captivity, and because they had now the temple
rebuilt, a hundred bulls, two hundred rams, four hundred lambs, and twelve
kids of the goats, according to the number of their tribes, (for so many are the
tribes of the sraelites,) and this last for the sins of every tribe. he priests also
and the evites set the porters at every gate, according to the laws of oses.

. nd as the feast of unleavened bread was at hand, in the first month,

.      ut the amaritans, being evil and enviously disposed to the ews,

. pon the death of arius, eres his son took the kingdom, who, as he

worship, and he was eceeding friendly to the ews. ow about this time a son

hou shalt also dedicate those holy vessels which have been given thee, and as

. hen sdras had received this epistle, he was very joyful, and began to

nd when these ews had understood what piety the king had towards od, and

. ow these things were truly done under the conduct of sdras and he

. fter sdras had said this, he left off praying and when all those that

. ow when they kept the feast of tabernacles in the seventh month    and

. ow there was one of those ews that had been carried captive who was
cup-bearer to king eres his name was ehemiah. s this man was walking

. ow when he was come to abylon, and had taken with him many of his

we have this especial opportunity for it." hen he had said this, he gave order

people, according to their villages and cities, as every one's ability should

require. nd when he had added this promise, that he himself, with his

places, and thence both they and the country gained that appellation.
. ut now when the mmonites, and oabites, and amaritans, and all

hey also slew many of the ews, and sought how they might destroy

and he built them houses at his own epenses and he commanded that part of

be fuller of people than it was before. o when ehemiah had done many other

. fter the death of eres, the kingdom came to be transferred to his son
yrus, whom the reeks called rtaeres. hen this man had obtained the
government over the ersians, the whole nation of the ews, with    their wives
and children, were in danger of perishing the occasion whereof we shall
declare in a little time for it is proper, in the first place, to eplain somewhat
relating to this king, and how he came to marry a ewish wife, who was herself
of the royal family also, and who is related to have saved our nation for when
rtaeres had taken the kingdom, and had set governors over the hundred
twenty and seven provinces, from ndia even unto thiopia, in the third year of
his reign, he made a costly feast for his friends, and for the nations of ersia,
and for their governors, such a one as was proper for a king to make, when he
had a mind to make a public demonstration of his riches, and this for a hundred
and fourscore days after which he made a feast for other nations, and for their
ambassadors, at hushan, for seven days. ow this feast was ordered after the
manner following he caused a tent to be pitched, which was supported by
pillars of gold and silver, with curtains of linen and purple spread over them,
that it might afford room for many ten thousands to sit down. he cups with
which the waiters ministered were of gold, and adorned with precious stones,
for pleasure and for sight. e also gave order to the servants that they should
not force them to drink, by bringing them wine continually, as is the practice of
the ersians, but to permit every one of the guests to enjoy himself according to
his own inclination. oreover, he sent messengers through the country, and

festival many days, on account of his kingdom. n like manner did ashti the
queen gather her guests together, and made them a feast in the palace. ow the
king was desirous to show her, who eceeded all other women in beauty, to
those that feasted with him, and he sent some to command her to come to his

feast. ut she, out of regard to the laws of the ersians, which forbid the wives
to be seen by strangers, did not go to the king    and though he oftentimes sent
the eunuchs to her, she did nevertheless stay away, and refused to come, till the
king was so much irritated, that he brake up the entertainment, and rose up, and
called for those seven who had the interpretation of the laws committed to
