that when she was frequently called by him to his feast, she did not obey him
once. e therefore gave order that they should inform him what could be done
by the law against her. o one of them, whose name was emucan, said that
this affront was offered not to him alone, but to all the ersians, who were in

their husbands, if they had "such an eample of arrogance in the queen towards
thee, who rulest over all." ccordingly, he ehorted him to punish her, who had
been guilty of so great an affront to him, after a severe manner and when he
had so done, to publish to the nations what had been decreed about the queen.
o the resolution was to put ashti away, and to give her dignity to another
woman.
. ut the king having been fond of her, did not well bear a separation, and
yet by the law he could not admit of a reconciliation so he was under trouble,

in number four hundred. nd when the eunuch thought the virgins had been

king's bed, he sent one to be with the king every day. o when he had
accompanied with her, he sent her back to the eunuch and when sther had
come to him, he was pleased with her, and fell in love with the damsel, and

also sent angari, as they are called, or messengers, unto every nation, and gave

month, on account of this his marriage. ccordingly, sther came to his royal
palace, and he set a diadem on her head. nd thus was sther married, without

been his own daughter.
. ow the king had made a law,    that none of his own people should
approach him unless he were called, when he sat upon his throne and men, with
aes in their hands, stood round about his throne, in order to punish such as
approached to him without being called. owever, the king sat with a golden
sceptre in his hand, which he held out when he had a mind to save any one of
those that approached to him without being called, and he who touched it was
free from danger. ut of this matter we have discoursed sufficiently.
. ome time after this two eunuchs], igthan and eresh, plotted against
the king and arnabazus, the servant of one of the eunuchs, being by birth a
ew, was acquainted with their conspiracy, and discovered it to the queen's
uncle and ordecai, by the means of sther, made the conspirators known to

had been the occasion of his preservation. e only bid the scribes to set down

the king.
. ow there was one aman, the son of medatha, by birth an malekite,

king and queen. hey saw him with pleasure, and feasted him in an obliging
manner, out of the respect they bare to his father. o he came to the merchants
privately, and bought a hundred boys, that had learning, and were in the flower
of their ages, each at a talent apiece as also he bought a hundred maidens, each
at the same price as the other. nd when he was invited to feast with the king
among the principal men in the country, he sat down the lowest of them all,
because he was little regarded, as a child in age still and this by those who
placed every one according to their dignity. ow when all those that sat with
him had laid the bones of the several parts on a heap before yrcanus, (for they
had themselves taken away the flesh belonging to them,) till the table where he
sat was filled full with them, rypho, who was the king's jester, and was

appointed for jokes and laughter at festivals, was now asked by the guests that

these bones." nd the king laughing at what rypho said, and asking of
yrcanus, how he came to have so many bones before him he replied, "ery
rightfully, my lord for they are dogs that eat the flesh and the bones together,
as these thy guests have done, (looking in the mean time at those guests,) for
there is nothing before them but they are men that eat the flesh, and cast away
the bones, as , who am also a man, have now done." pon which the king
admired at his answer, which was so wisely made and bid them all make an
acclamation, as a mark of their approbation of his jest, which was truly a
facetious one. n the net day yrcanus went to every one of the king's
friends, and of the men powerful at court, and saluted them but still inquired of
the servants what present they would make the king on his son's birthday and
when some said that they would give twelve talents, and that others of greater
dignity would every one give according to the quantity of their riches, he
pretended to every one of them to be grieved that he was not able to bring so
large a present for that he had no more than five talents. nd when the servants
heard what he said, they told their masters and they rejoiced in the prospect
that oseph would be disapproved, and would make the king angry, by the
smallness of his present. hen the day came, the others, even those that
brought the most, offered the king not above twenty talents but yrcanus gave

talent a-piece, for them to carry, and introduced them, the boys to the king, and
the maidens to leopatra every body wondering at the unepected richness of

attended about the king with gifts to the value of a great number of talents, that
he might escape the danger he was in from them for to these it was that
yrcanus's brethren had written to destroy him. ow tolemy admired at the
young man's magnanimity, and commanded him to ask what gift he pleased.
ut he desired nothing else to be done for him by the king than to write to his
father and brethren about him. o when the king had paid him very great
respects, and had given him very large gifts, and had written to his father and
his brethren, and all his commanders and officers, about him, he sent him away.
ut when his brethren heard that yrcanus had received such favors from the
king, and was returning home with great honor, they went out to meet him, and
to destroy him, and that with the privity of their father for he was angry at him
for the large] sum of money that he bestowed for presents, and so had no
concern for his preservation. owever, oseph concealed the anger he had at his
son, out of fear of the king. nd when yrcanus's brethren came to fight him,

he slew many others of those that were with them, as also two of his brethren
themselves but the rest of them escaped to erusalem to their father. ut when
yrcanus came to the city, where nobody would receive him, he was afraid for
himself, and retired beyond the river ordan, and there abode, but obliging the
barbarians to pay their taes.
. t this time, eleucus, who was called oter, reigned over sia, being
the son of ntiochus the reat. nd now] yrcanus's father, oseph, died. e
was a good man, and of great magnanimity and brought the ews out of a state
of poverty and meanness, to one that was more splendid. e retained the farm

also, nias, died about this time], and left the high priesthood to his son
imeon. nd when he was dead, nias his son succeeded him in that dignity.
o him it was that reus, king of the acedemonians, sent an embassage, with
an epistle the copy whereof here follows—
",    ,  , 
.
"e have met with a certain writing, whereby we have discovered that both
the ews and the acedemonians are of one stock, and are derived from the
kindred of braham.    t is but just therefore that you, who are our brethren,
should send to us about any of your concerns as you please. e will also do the

concerns as in common with yours. emotoles, who brings you this letter, will

with a dragon in his claws."
. s for yrcanus, when he saw that ntiochus had a great army, and
feared lest he should be caught by him, and brought to punishment for what he

. bout this time, upon the death of nias the high priest, they gave the high
priesthood to esus his brother for that son which nias left or nias .] was
yet but an infant and, in its proper place, we will inform the reader of all the
circumstances that befell this child. ut this esus, who was the brother of
nias, was deprived of the high priesthood by the king, who was angry with
him, and gave it to his younger brother, whose name also was nias for imon
had these three sons, to each of which the priesthood came, as we have already
informed the reader.    his esus changed his name to ason, but nias was
called enelaus. ow as the former high priest, esus, raised a sedition against
enelaus, who was ordained after him, the multitude were divided between
them both. nd the sons of obias took the part of enelaus, but the greater
part of the people assisted ason and by that means enelaus and the sons of
obias were distressed, and retired to ntiochus, and informed him that they
were desirous to leave the laws of their country, and the ewish way of living
according to them, and to follow the king's laws, and the recian way of living.
herefore they desired his permission to build them a gymnasium at
erusalem.    nd when he had given them leave, they also hid the circumcision
of their genitals, that even when they were naked they might appear to be
reeks. ccordingly, they left off all the customs that belonged to their own
country, and imitated the practices of the other nations.
. ow ntiochus, upon the agreeable situation of the affairs of his
kingdom, resolved to make an epedition against gypt, both because he had a
desire to gain it, and because he contemned the son of tolemy, as now weak,
and not yet of abilities to manage affairs of such consequence so he came with
great forces to elusium, and circumvented tolemy hilometer by treachery,
and seized upon gypt. e then came to the places about emphis and when
he had taken them, he made haste to leandria, in hopes of taking it by siege,
and of subduing tolemy, who reigned there. ut he was driven not only from
leandria, but out of all gypt, by the declaration of the omans, who
charged him to let that country alone according as  have elsewhere formerly
declared.  will now give a particular account of what concerns this king, how
he subdued udea and the temple for in my former work  mentioned those
things very briefly, and have therefore now thought it necessary to go over that
history again, and that with great accuracy.
. ing ntiochus returning out of gypt,    for fear of the omans, made an
epedition against the city erusalem and when he was there, in the hundred
and forty-third year of the kingdom of the eleucidae, he took the city without
fighting, those of his own party opening the gates to him. nd when he had
gotten possession of erusalem, he slew many of the opposite party and when

he had plundered it of a great deal of money, he returned to ntioch.
. ow it came to pass, after two years, in the hundred forty and fifth year,
on the twenty-fifth day of that month which is by us called hasleu, and by the
acedonians pelleus, in the hundred and fifty-third olympiad, that the king
came up to erusalem, and, pretending peace, he got possession of the city by
treachery at which time he spared not so much as those that admitted him into
it, on account of the riches that lay in the temple but, led by his covetous
inclination, (for he saw there was in it a great deal of gold, and many ornaments
that had been dedicated to it of very great value,) and in order to plunder its
wealth, he ventured to break the league he had made. o he left the temple bare,
and took away the golden candlesticks, and the golden altar of incense], and
table of shew-bread], and the altar of burnt-offering] and did not abstain from
even the veils, which were made of fine linen and scarlet. e also emptied it of
its secret treasures, and left nothing at all remaining and by this means cast the
ews into great lamentation, for he forbade them to offer those daily sacrifices
which they used to offer to od, according to the law. nd when he had
pillaged the whole city, some of the inhabitants he slew, and some he carried
captive, together with their wives and children, so that the multitude of those
captives that were taken alive amounted to about ten thousand. e also burnt
down the finest buildings and when he had overthrown the city walls, he built
a citadel in the lower part of the city,    for the place was high, and overlooked
the temple on which account he fortified it with high walls and towers, and put
into it a garrison of acedonians. owever, in that citadel dwelt the impious
and wicked part of the ewish] multitude, from whom it proved that the
citizens suffered many and sore calamities. nd when the king had built an idol
altar upon od's altar, he slew swine upon it, and so offered a sacrifice neither
according to the law, nor the ewish religious worship in that country. e also
compelled them to forsake the worship which they paid their own od, and to
adore those whom he took to be gods and made them build temples, and raise
idol altars in every city and village, and offer swine upon them every day. e
also commanded them not to circumcise their sons, and threatened to punish
any that should be found to have transgressed his injunction. e also appointed
overseers, who should compel them to do what he commanded. nd indeed
many ews there were who complied with the king's commands, either
voluntarily, or out of fear of the penalty that was denounced. ut the best men,
and those of the noblest souls, did not regard him, but did pay a greater respect
to the customs of their country than concern as to the punishment which he
threatened to the disobedient on which account they every day underwent great
miseries and bitter torments for they were whipped with rods, and their bodies
were torn to pieces, and were crucified, while they were still alive, and
breathed. hey also strangled those women and their sons whom they had

circumcised, as the king had appointed, hanging their sons about their necks as
they were upon the crosses. nd if there were any sacred book of the law
found, it was destroyed, and those with whom they were found miserably
perished also.
. hen the amaritans saw the ews under these sufferings, they no longer
confessed that they were of their kindred, nor that the temple on ount
erizzim belonged to lmighty od. his was according to their nature, as we
have already shown. nd they now said that they were a colony of edes and
ersians and indeed they were a colony of theirs. o they sent ambassadors to
ntiochus, and an epistle, whose contents are these "o king ntiochus the
god piphanes, a memorial from the idonians, who live at hechem. ur
forefathers, upon certain frequent plagues, and as following a certain ancient
superstition, had a custom of observing that day which by the ews is called the
abbath.    nd when they had erected a temple at the mountain called
erizzim, though without a name, they offered upon it the proper sacrifices.
ow, upon the just treatment of these wicked ews, those that manage their
affairs, supposing that we were of kin to them, and practiced as they do, make
us liable to the same accusations, although we be originally idonians, as is
evident from the public records. e therefore beseech thee, our benefactor and
savior, to give order to pollonius, the governor of this part of the country, and
to icanor, the procurator of thy affairs, to give us no disturbance, nor to lay to
our charge what the ews are accused for, since we are aliens from their nation,
and from their customs but let our temple, which at present hath no name at all
be named the emple of upiter ellenius. f this were once done, we should be
no longer disturbed, but should be more intent on our own occupation with
quietness, and so bring in a greater revenue to thee." hen the amaritans had
petitioned for this, the king sent them back the following answer, in an epistle
"ing ntiochus to icanor. he idonians, who live at hechem, have sent me
the memorial enclosed. hen therefore we were advising with our friends
about it, the messengers sent by them represented to us that they are no way
concerned with accusations which belong to the ews, but choose to live after
the customs of the reeks. ccordingly, we declare them free from such
accusations, and order that, agreeable to their petition, their temple be named
the emple of upiter ellenius." e also sent the like epistle to pollonius, the
governor of that part of the country, in the forty-sith year, and the eighteenth
day of the month ecatombeon.

. ow at this time there was one whose name was attathias, who dwelt at
odin, the son of ohn, the son of imeon, the son of samoneus, a priest of
the order of oarib, and a citizen of erusalem. e had five sons ohn, who was
called addis, and imeon, who was called atthes, and udas, who was called
accabeus,    and leazar, who was called uran, and onathan, who was called
pphus. ow this attathias lamented to his children the sad state of their
affairs, and the ravage made in the city, and the plundering of the temple, and
the calamities the multitude were under and he told them that it was better for
them to die for the laws of their country, than to live so ingloriously as they
then did.
. ut when those that were appointed by the king were come to odin,
that they might compel the ews to do what they were commanded, and to
enjoin those that were there to offer sacrifice, as the king had commanded, they
desired that attathias, a person of the greatest character among them, both on
other accounts, and particularly on account of such a numerous and so
deserving a family of children, would begin the sacrifice, because his fellow
citizens would follow his eample, and because such a procedure would make
him honored by the king. ut attathias said he would not do it and that if all
the other nations would obey the commands of ntiochus, either out of fear, or
to please him, yet would not he nor his sons leave the religious worship of their
country. ut as soon as he had ended his speech, there came one of the ews
into the midst of them, and sacrificed, as ntiochus had commanded. t which
attathias had great indignation, and ran upon him violently, with his sons,
who had swords with them, and slew both the man himself that sacrificed, and
pelles the king's general, who compelled them to sacrifice, with a few of his
soldiers. e also overthrew the idol altar, and cried out, "f," said he, "any one
be zealous for the laws of his country, and for the worship of od, let him
follow me." nd when he had said this, he made haste into the desert with his
sons, and left all his substance in the village. any others did the same also,
and fled with their children and wives into the desert, and dwelt in caves. ut
when the king's generals heard this, they took all the forces they then had in the
citadel at erusalem, and pursued the ews into the desert and when they had
overtaken them, they in the first place endeavored to persuade them to repent,
and to choose what was most for their advantage, and not put them to the
necessity of using them according to the law of war. ut when they would not
comply with their persuasions, but continued to be of a different mind, they
fought against them on the abbath day, and they burnt them as they were in

the caves, without resistance, and without so much as stopping up the entrances
of the caves. nd they avoided to defend themselves on that day, because they
were not willing to break in upon the honor they owed the abbath, even in
such distresses for our law requires that we rest upon that day. here were
about a thousand, with their wives and children, who were smothered and died
in these caves but many of those that escaped joined themselves to attathias,
and appointed him to be their ruler, who taught them to fight, even on the
abbath day and told them that unless they would do so, they would become
their own enemies, by observing the law so rigorously], while their adversaries
would still assault them on this day, and they would not then defend
themselves, and that nothing could then hinder but they must all perish without
fighting. his speech persuaded them. nd this rule continues among us to this
day, that if there be a necessity, we may fight on abbath days. o attathias
got a great army about him, and overthrew their idol altars, and slew those that
broke the laws, even all that he could get under his power for many of them
were dispersed among the nations round about them for fear of him. e also
commanded that those boys which were not yet circumcised should be
circumcised now and he drove those away that were appointed to hinder such
their circumcision.
. ut when he had ruled one year, and was fallen into a distemper, he
called for his sons, and set them round about him, and said, " my sons,  am
going the way of all the earth and  recommend to you my resolution, and
beseech you not to be negligent in keeping it, but to be mindful of the desires of
him who begat you, and brought you up, and to preserve the customs of your
country, and to recover your ancient form of government, which is in danger of
being overturned, and not to be carried away with those that, either by their
own inclination, or out of necessity, betray it, but to become such sons as are
worthy of me to be above all force and necessity, and so to dispose your souls,
as to be ready, when it shall be necessary, to die for your laws as sensible of
this, by just reasoning, that if od see that you are so disposed he will not
overlook you, but will have a great value for your virtue, and will restore to you
again what you have lost, and will return to you that freedom in which you
shall live quietly, and enjoy your own customs. our bodies are mortal, and
subject to fate but they receive a sort of immortality, by the remembrance of
what actions they have done. nd  would have you so in love with this
immortality, that you may pursue after glory, and that, when you have
undergone the greatest difficulties, you may not scruple, for such things, to lose
your lives.  ehort you, especially, to agree one with another and in what
ecellency any one of you eceeds another, to yield to him so far, and by that
means to reap the advantage of every one's own virtues. o you then esteem
imon as your father, because he is a man of etraordinary prudence, and be

governed by him in what counsels be gives you. ake accabeus for the
general of your army, because of his courage and strength, for he will avenge
your nation, and will bring vengeance on your enemies. dmit among you the
righteous and religious, and augment their power."
. hen attathias had thus discoursed to his sons, and had prayed to od
to be their assistant, and to recover to the people their former constitution, he
died a little afterward, and was buried at odin all the people making great
lamentation for him. hereupon his son udas took upon him the
administration of public affairs, in the hundred and forty-sith year and thus,
by the ready assistance of his brethren, and of others, udas cast their enemies
out of the country, and put those of their own country to death who had
transgressed its laws, and purified the land of all the pollutions that were in it.

. hen pollonius, the general of the amaritan forces, heard this, he took
his army, and made haste to go against udas, who met him, and joined battle
with him, and beat him, and slew many of his men, and among them
pollonius himself, their general, whose sword being that which he happened
then to wear, he seized upon, and kept for himself but he wounded more than
he slew, and took a great deal of prey from the enemy's camp, and went his
way. ut when eron, who was general of the army of oelesyria, heard that
many had joined themselves to udas, and that he had about him an army
sufficient for fighting, and for making war, he determined to make an
epedition against him, as thinking it became him to endeavor to punish those
that transgressed the king's injunctions. e then got together an army, as large
as he was able, and joined to it the runagate and wicked ews, and came against
udas. e came as far as ethhoron, a village of udea, and there pitched his
camp upon which udas met him and when he intended to give him battle, he
saw that his soldiers were backward to fight, because their number was small,
and because they wanted food, for they were fasting, he encouraged them, and
said to them, that victory and conquest of enemies are not derived from the
multitude in armies, but in the eercise of piety towards od and that they had
the plainest instances in their forefathers, who, by their righteousness, eerting

themselves on behalf of their own laws, and their own children, had frequently
conquered many ten thousands,—for innocence is the strongest army. y this
speech he induced his men to contemn the multitude of the enemy, and to fall
upon eron. nd upon joining battle with him, he beat the yrians and when
their general fell among the rest, they all ran away with speed, as thinking that
to be their best way of escaping. o he pursued them unto the plain, and slew
about eight hundred of the enemy but the rest escaped to the region which lay
near to the sea.
. hen king ntiochus heard of these things, he was very angry at what
had happened so he got together all his own army, with many mercenaries,
whom he had hired from the islands, and took them with him, and prepared to
break into udea about the beginning of the spring. ut when, upon his
mustering his soldiers, he perceived that his treasures were deficient, and there
was a want of money in them, for all the taes were not paid, by reason of the
seditions there had been among the nations he having been so magnanimous
and so liberal, that what he had was not sufficient for him, he therefore resolved
first to go into ersia, and collect the taes of that country. ereupon he left
one whose name was ysias, who was in great repute with him governor of the
kingdom, as far as the bounds of gypt, and of the ower sia, and reaching
from the river uphrates, and committed to him a certain part of his forces, and
of his elephants, and charged him to bring up his son ntiochus with all
possible care, until he came back and that he should conquer udea, and take
its inhabitants for slaves, and utterly destroy erusalem, and abolish the whole
nation. nd when king ntiochus had given these things in charge to ysias, he
went into ersia and in the hundred and forty-seventh year he passed over
uphrates, and went to the superior provinces.
. pon this ysias chose tolemy, the son of orimenes, and icanor, and
orgias, very potent men among the king's friends, and delivered to them forty
thousand foot soldiers, and seven thousand horsemen, and sent them against
udea, who came as far as the city mmaus, and pitched their camp in the plain
country. here came also to them auiliaries out of yria, and the country round
about as also many of the runagate ews. nd besides these came some
merchants to buy those that should be carried captives, (having bonds with
them to bind those that should be made prisoners,) with that silver and gold
which they were to pay for their price. nd when udas saw their camp, and
how numerous their enemies were, he persuaded his own soldiers to be of good
courage, and ehorted them to place their hopes of victory in od, and to make
supplication to him, according to the custom of their country, clothed in
sackcloth and to show what was their usual habit of supplication in the greatest
dangers, and thereby to prevail with od to grant them the victory over their
enemies. o he set them in their ancient order of battle used by their

forefathers, under their captains of thousands, and other officers, and dismissed
such as were newly married, as well as those that had newly gained
possessions, that they might not fight in a cowardly manner, out of an
inordinate love of life, in order to enjoy those blessings. hen he had thus
disposed his soldiers, he encouraged them to fight by the following speech,
which he made to them " my fellow soldiers, no other time remains more
opportune than the present for courage and contempt of dangers for if you now
fight manfully, you may recover your liberty, which, as it is a thing of itself
agreeable to all men, so it proves to be to us much more desirable, by its
affording us the liberty of worshipping od. ince therefore you are in such
circumstances at present, you must either recover that liberty, and so regain a
happy and blessed way of living, which is that according to our laws, and the
customs of our country, or to submit to the most opprobrious sufferings nor
will any seed of your nation remain if you be beat in this battle. ight therefore
manfully and suppose that you must die, though you do not fight but believe,
that besides such glorious rewards as those of the liberty of your country, of
your laws, of your religion, you shall then obtain everlasting glory. repare
yourselves, therefore, and put yourselves into such an agreeable posture, that
you may be ready to fight with the enemy as soon as it is day tomorrow
morning."
. nd this was the speech which udas made to encourage them. ut when
the enemy sent orgias, with five thousand foot and one thousand horse, that
he might fall upon udas by night, and had for that purpose certain of the
runagate ews as guides, the son of attathias perceived it, and resolved to fall
upon those enemies that were in their camp, now their forces were divided.
hen they had therefore supped in good time, and had left many fires in their
camp, he marched all night to those enemies that were at mmaus. o that
when orgias found no enemy in their camp, but suspected that they were
retired, and had hidden themselves among the mountains, he resolved to go and
seek them wheresoever they were. ut about break of day udas appeared to
those enemies that were at mmaus, with only three thousand men, and those
ill armed, by reason of their poverty and when he saw the enemy very well and
skilfully fortified in their camp, he encouraged the ews, and told them that
they ought to fight, though it were with their naked bodies, for that od had
sometimes of old given such men strength, and that against such as were more
in number, and were armed also, out of regard to their great courage. o he
commanded the trumpeters to sound for the battle and by thus falling upon the
enemies when they did not epect it, and thereby astonishing and disturbing
their minds, he slew many of those that resisted him, and went on pursuing the
rest as far as adara, and the plains of dumea, and shdod, and amnia and of
these there fell about three thousand. et did udas ehort his soldiers not to be

too desirous of the spoils, for that still they must have a contest and battle with
orgias, and the forces that were with him but that when they had once
overcome them, then they might securely plunder the camp, because they were
the only enemies remaining, and they epected no others. nd just as he was
speaking to his soldiers, orgias's men looked down into that army which they
left in their camp, and saw that it was overthrown, and the camp burnt for the
smoke that arose from it showed them, even when they were a great way off,
what had happened. hen therefore those that were with orgias understood
that things were in this posture, and perceived that those that were with udas
were ready to fight them, they also were affrighted, and put to flight but then
udas, as though he had already beaten orgias's soldiers without fighting,
returned and seized on the spoils. e took a great quantity of gold, and silver,
and purple, and blue, and then returned home with joy, and singing hymns to
od for their good success for this victory greatly contributed to the recovery
of their liberty.
. ereupon ysias was confounded at the defeat of the army which he had
sent, and the net year he got together sity thousand chosen men. e also took
five thousand horsemen, and fell upon udea and he went up to the hill country
of ethsur, a village of udea, and pitched his camp there, where udas met him
with ten thousand men and when he saw the great number of his enemies, he
prayed to od that he would assist him, and joined battle with the first of the
enemy that appeared, and beat them, and slew about five thousand of them, and
thereby became terrible to the rest of them. ay, indeed, ysias observing the
great spirit of the ews, how they were prepared to die rather than lose their
liberty, and being afraid of their desperate way of fighting, as if it were real
strength, he took the rest of the army back with him, and returned to ntioch,
where he listed foreigners into the service, and prepared to fall upon udea with
a greater army.
. hen therefore the generals of ntiochus's armies had been beaten so
often, udas assembled the people together, and told them, that after these many
victories which od had given them, they ought to go up to erusalem, and
purify the temple, and offer the appointed sacrifices. ut as soon as he, with the
whole multitude, was come to erusalem, and found the temple deserted, and its
gates burnt down, and plants growing in the temple of their own accord, on
account of its desertion, he and those that were with him began to lament, and
were quite confounded at the sight of the temple so he chose out some of his
soldiers, and gave them order to fight against those guards that were in the
citadel, until he should have purified the temple. hen therefore he had
carefully purged it, and had brought in new vessels, the candlestick, the table
of shew-bread], and the altar of incense], which were made of gold, he hung
up the veils at the gates, and added doors to them. e also took down the altar

of burnt-offering], and built a new one of stones that he gathered together, and
not of such as were hewn with iron tools. o on the five and twentieth day of
the month asleu, which the acedonians call ppelleus, they lighted the
lamps that were on the candlestick, and offered incense upon the altar of
incense], and laid the loaves upon the table of shew-bread], and offered burnt-
offerings upon the new altar of burnt-offering]. ow it so fell out, that these
things were done on the very same day on which their ivine worship had
fallen off, and was reduced to a profane and common use, after three years'
time for so it was, that the temple was made desolate by ntiochus, and so
continued for three years. his desolation happened to the temple in the
hundred forty and fifth year, on the twenty-fifth day of the month ppelleus,
and on the hundred fifty and third olympiad but it was dedicated anew, on the
same day, the twenty-fifth of the month ppelleus, on the hundred and forty-
eighth year, and on the hundred and fifty-fourth olympiad. nd this desolation
came to pass according to the prophecy of aniel, which was given four
hundred and eight years before for he declared that the acedonians would
dissolve that worship for some time].
. ow udas celebrated the festival of the restoration of the sacrifices of
the temple for eight days, and omitted no sort of pleasures thereon but he
feasted them upon very rich and splendid sacrifices and he honored od, and
delighted them by hymns and psalms. ay, they were so very glad at the revival
of their customs, when, after a long time of intermission, they unepectedly had
regained the freedom of their worship, that they made it a law for their
posterity, that they should keep a festival, on account of the restoration of their
temple worship, for eight days. nd from that time to this we celebrate this
festival, and call it ights.  suppose the reason was, because this liberty
beyond our hopes appeared to us and that thence was the name given to that
festival. udas also rebuilt the walls round about the city, and reared towers of
great height against the incursions of enemies, and set guards therein. e also
fortified the city ethsura, that it might serve as a citadel against any distresses
that might come from our enemies.

. hen these things were over, the nations round about the ews were very
uneasy at the revival of their power, and rose up together, and destroyed many
of them, as gaining advantage over them by laying snares for them, and making
secret conspiracies against them. udas made perpetual epeditions against
these men, and endeavored to restrain them from those incursions, and to
prevent the mischiefs they did to the ews. o he fell upon the dumeans, the
posterity of sau, at crabattene, and slew a great many of them, and took their
spoils. e also shut up the sons of ean, that laid wait for the ews and he sat
down about them, and besieged them, and burnt their towers, and destroyed the
men that were in them]. fter this he went thence in haste against the
mmonites, who had a great and a numerous army, of which imotheus was
the commander. nd when he had subdued them, he seized on the city azer,
and took their wives and their children captives, and burnt the city, and then
returned into udea. ut when the neighboring nations understood that he was
returned, they got together in great numbers in the land of ilead, and came
against those ews that were at their borders, who then fled to the garrison of
athema and sent to udas, to inform him that imotheus was endeavoring to
take the place whither they were fled. nd as these epistles were reading, there
came other messengers out of alilee, who informed him that the inhabitants of
tolemais, and of yre and idon, and strangers of alilee, were gotten
together.
. ccordingly udas, upon considering what was fit to be done, with
relation to the necessity both these cases required, gave order that imon his
brother should take three thousand chosen men, and go to the assistance of the
ews in alilee, while he and another of his brothers, onathan, made haste into
the land of ilead, with eight thousand soldiers. nd he left oseph, the son of
acharias, and zarias, to be over the rest of the forces and charged them to
keep udea very carefully, and to fight no battles with any persons whomsoever
until his return. ccordingly, imon went into alilee, and fought the enemy,
and put them to flight, and pursued them to the very gates of tolemais, and
slew about three thousand of them, and took the spoils of those that were slain,
and those ews whom they had made captives, with their baggage, and then
returned home.
. ow as for udas accabeus, and his brother onathan, they passed over
the river ordan and when they had gone three days journey, they lighted upon
the abateans, who came to meet them peaceably, and who told them how the
affairs of those in the land of ilead stood and how many of them were in
distress, and driven into garrisons, and into the cities of alilee and ehorted
him to make haste to go against the foreigners, and to endeavor to save his own
countrymen out of their hands. o this ehortation udas hearkened, and
returned to the wilderness and in the first place fell upon the inhabitants of

osor, and took the city, and beat the inhabitants, and destroyed all the males,
and all that were able to fight, and burnt the city. or did he stop even when
night came on, but he journeyed in it to the garrison where the ews happened
to be then shut up, and where imotheus lay round the place with his army. nd
udas came upon the city in the morning and when he found that the enemy
were making an assault upon the walls, and that some of them brought ladders,
on which they might get upon those walls, and that others brought engines to
batter them], he bid the trumpeter to sound his trumpet, and he encouraged his
soldiers cheerfully to undergo dangers for the sake of their brethren and
kindred he also parted his army into three bodies, and fell upon the backs of
their enemies. ut when imotheus's men perceived that it was accabeus that
was upon them, of both whose courage and good success in war they had
formerly had sufficient eperience, they were put to flight but udas followed
them with his army, and slew about eight thousand of them. e then turned
aside to a city of the foreigners called alle, and took it, and slew all the males,
and burnt the city itself. e then removed from thence, and overthrew asphom
and osor, and many other cities of the land of ilead.
. ut not long after this, imotheus prepared a great army, and took many
others as auiliaries and induced some of the rabians, by the promise of
rewards, to go with him in this epedition, and came with his army beyond the
brook, over against the city aphon and he encouraged his soldiers, if it came
to a battle with the ews, to fight courageously, and to hinder their passing over
the brook for he said to them beforehand, that "if they come over it, we shall
be beaten." nd when udas heard that imotheus prepared himself to fight, he
took all his own army, and went in haste against imotheus his enemy and
when he had passed over the brook, he fell upon his enemies, and some of them
met him, whom he slew, and others of them he so terrified, that he compelled
them to throw down their arms and fly and some of them escaped, but some of
them fled to what was called the emple of arnaim, and hoped thereby to
preserve themselves but udas took the city, and slew them, and burnt the
temple, and so used several ways of destroying his enemies.
. hen he had done this, he gathered the ews together, with their children
and wives, and the substance that belonged to them, and was going to bring
them back into udea but as soon as he was come to a certain city, whose name
was phron, that lay upon the road, (and it was not possible for him to go any
other way, so he was not willing to go back again,) he then sent to the
inhabitants, and desired that they would open their gates, and permit them to go
on their way through the city for they had stopped up the gates with stones,
and cut off their passage through it. nd when the inhabitants of phron would
not agree to this proposal, he encouraged those that were with him, and
encompassed the city round, and besieged it, and, lying round it by day and

night, took the city, and slew every male in it, and burnt it all down, and so
obtained a way through it and the multitude of those that were slain was so
great, that they went over the dead bodies. o they came over ordan, and
arrived at the great plain, over against which is situate the city ethshan, which
is called by the reeks cythopolis.    nd going away hastily from thence, they
came into udea, singing psalms and hymns as they went, and indulging such
tokens of mirth as are usual in triumphs upon victory. hey also offered thank-
offerings, both for their good success, and for the preservation of their army, for
not one of the ews was slain in these battles. 
 . ut as to oseph, the son of acharias, and zarias, whom udas left
generals of the rest of his forces] at the same time when imon was in alilee,
fighting against the people of tolemais, and udas himself, and his brother
onathan, were in the land of ilead, did these men also affect the glory of
being courageous generals in war, in order whereto they took the army that was
under their command, and came to amnia. here orgias, the general of the
forces of amnia, met them and upon joining battle with him, they lost two
thousand of their army,    and fled away, and were pursued to the very borders of
udea. nd this misfortune befell them by their disobedience to what
injunctions udas had given them, not to fight with any one before his return.
or besides the rest of udas's sagacious counsels, one may well wonder at this
concerning the misfortune that befell the forces commanded by oseph and
zarias, which he understood would happen, if they broke any of the
injunctions he had given them. ut udas and his brethren did not leave off
fighting with the dumeans, but pressed upon them on all sides, and took from
them the city of ebron, and demolished all its fortifications, and set all its
towers on fire, and burnt the country of the foreigners, and the city arissa.
hey came also to shdod, and took it, and laid it waste, and took away a great
deal of the spoils and prey that were in it, and returned to udea.

. bout this time it was that king ntiochus, as he was going over the upper
countries, heard that there was a very rich city in ersia, called lymais and
therein a very rich temple of iana, and that it was full of all sorts of donations
dedicated to it as also weapons and breastplates, which, upon inquiry, he found

had been left there by leander, the son of hilip, king of acedonia. nd
being incited by these motives, he went in haste to lymais, and assaulted it,
and besieged it. ut as those that were in it were not terrified at his assault, nor
at his siege, but opposed him very courageously, he was beaten off his hopes
for they drove him away from the city, and went out and pursued after him,
insomuch that he fled away as far as abylon, and lost a great many of his
army. nd when he was grieving for this disappointment, some persons told
him of the defeat of his commanders whom he had left behind him to fight
against udea, and what strength the ews had already gotten. hen this
concern about these affairs was added to the former, he was confounded, and
by the aniety he was in fell into a distemper, which, as it lasted a great while,
and as his pains increased upon him, so he at length perceived he should die in
a little time so he called his friends to him, and told them that his distemper
was severe upon him and confessed withal, that this calamity was sent upon
him for the miseries he had brought upon the ewish nation, while he plundered
their temple, and contemned their od and when he had said this, he gave up
the ghost. hence one may wonder at olybius of egalopolis, who, though
otherwise a good man, yet saith that "ntiochus died because he had a purpose
to plunder the temple of iana in ersia" for the purposing to do a thing,    but
not actually doing it, is not worthy of punishment. ut if olybius could think
that ntiochus thus lost his life on that account, it is much more probable that
this king died on account of his sacrilegious plundering of the temple at
erusalem. ut we will not contend about this matter with those who may think
that the cause assigned by this olybius of egalopolis is nearer the truth than
that assigned by us.
. owever, ntiochus, before he died, called for hilip, who was one of
his companions, and made him the guardian of his kingdom and gave him his
diadem, and his garment, and his ring, and charged him to carry them, and
deliver them to his son ntiochus and desired him to take care of his
education, and to preserve the kingdom for him.    his ntiochus died in the
hundred forty and ninth year but it was ysias that declared his death to the
multitude, and appointed his son ntiochus to be king, (of whom at present he
had the care,) and called him upator.
. t this time it was that the garrison in the citadel of erusalem, with the
ewish runagates, did a great deal of harm to the ews for the soldiers that were
in that garrison rushed out upon the sudden, and destroyed such as were going
up to the temple in order to offer their sacrifices, for this citadel adjoined to and
overlooked the temple. hen these misfortunes had often happened to them,
udas resolved to destroy that garrison whereupon he got all the people
together, and vigorously besieged those that were in the citadel. his was in the
hundred and fiftieth year of the dominion of the eleucidae. o he made

engines of war, and erected bulwarks, and very zealously pressed on to take the
citadel. ut there were not a few of the runagates who were in the place that
went out by night into the country, and got together some other wicked men
like themselves, and went to ntiochus the king, and desired of him that he
would not suffer them to be neglected, under the great hardships that lay upon
them from those of their own nation and this because their sufferings were
occasioned on his father's account, while they left the religious worship of their
fathers, and preferred that which he had commanded them to follow that there
was danger lest the citadel, and those appointed to garrison it by the king,
should be taken by udas, and those that were with him, unless he would send
them succors. hen ntiochus, who was but a child, heard this, he was angry,
and sent for his captains and his friends, and gave order that they should get an
army of mercenaries together, with such men also of his own kingdom as were
of an age fit for war. ccordingly, an army was collected of about a hundred
thousand footmen, and twenty thousand horsemen, and thirty-two elephants.
. o the king took this army, and marched hastily out of ntioch, with
ysias, who had the command of the whole, and came to dumea, and thence
went up to the city ethsura, a city that was strong, and not to be taken without
great difficulty. e set about this city, and besieged it. nd while the
inhabitants of ethsura courageously opposed him, and sallied out upon him,
and burnt his engines of war, a great deal of time was spent in the siege. ut
when udas heard of the king's coming, he raised the siege of the citadel, and
met the king, and pitched his camp in certain straits, at a place called
ethzachariah, at the distance of seventy furlongs from the enemy but the king
soon drew his forces from ethsura, and brought them to those straits. nd as
soon as it was day, he put his men in battle-array, and made his elephants
follow one another through the narrow passes, because they could not be set
sideways by one another. ow round about every elephant there were a
thousand footmen, and five hundred horsemen. he elephants also had high
towers upon their backs], and archers in them]. nd he also made the rest of
his army to go up the mountains, and put his friends before the rest and gave
orders for the army to shout aloud, and so he attacked the enemy. e also
eposed to sight their golden and brazen shields, so that a glorious splendor
was sent from them and when they shouted the mountains echoed again. hen
udas saw this, he was not terrified, but received the enemy with great courage,
and slew about si hundred of the first ranks. ut when his brother leazar,
whom they called uran, saw the tallest of all the elephants armed with royal
breastplates, and supposed that the king was upon him, he attacked him with
great quickness and bravery. e also slew many of those that were about the
elephant, and scattered the rest, and then went under the belly of the elephant,
and smote him, and slew him so the elephant fell upon leazar, and by his

weight crushed him to death. nd thus did this man come to his end, when he
had first courageously destroyed many of his enemies.
. ut udas, seeing the strength of the enemy, retired to erusalem, and
prepared to endure a siege. s for ntiochus, he sent part of his army to
ethsura, to besiege it, and with the rest of his army he came against erusalem
but the inhabitants of ethsura were terrified at his strength and seeing that
their provisions grew scarce, they delivered themselves up on the security of
oaths that they should suffer no hard treatment from the king. nd when
ntiochus had thus taken the city, he did them no other harm than sending them
out naked. e also placed a garrison of his own in the city. ut as for the
temple of erusalem, he lay at its siege a long time, while they within bravely
defended it for what engines soever the king set against them, they set other
engines again to oppose them. ut then their provisions failed them what fruits
of the ground they had laid up were spent and the land being not ploughed that
year, continued unsowed, because it was the seventh year, on which, by our
laws, we are obliged to let it lay uncultivated. nd withal, so many of the
besieged ran away for want of necessaries, that but a few only were left in the
temple.
. nd these happened to be the circumstances of such as were besieged in
the temple. ut then, because ysias, the general of the army, and ntiochus
the king, were informed that hilip was coming upon them out of ersia, and
was endeavoring to get the management of public affairs to himself, they came
into these sentiments, to leave the siege, and to make haste to go against hilip
yet did they resolve not to let this be known to the soldiers or to the officers
but the king commanded ysias to speak openly to the soldiers and the officers,
without saying a word about the business of hilip and to intimate to them that
the siege would be very long that the place was very strong that they were
already in want of provisions that many affairs of the kingdom wanted
regulation and that it was much better to make a league with the besieged, and
to become friends to their whole nation, by permitting them to observe the laws
of their fathers, while they broke out into this war only because they were
deprived of them, and so to depart home. hen ysias had discoursed thus to
them, both the army and the officers were pleased with this resolution.
. ccordingly the king sent to udas, and to those that were besieged with
them, and promised to give them peace, and to permit them to make use of, and
live according to, the laws of their fathers and they gladly received his
proposals and when they had gained security upon oath for their performance,
they went out of the temple. ut when ntiochus came into it, and saw how
strong the place was, he broke his oaths, and ordered his army that was there to
pluck down the walls to the ground and when he had so done, he returned to
ntioch. e also carried with him nias the high priest, who was also called

enelaus for ysias advised the king to slay enelaus, if he would have the
ews be quiet, and cause him no further disturbance, for that this man was the
origin of all the mischief the ews had done them, by persuading his father to
compel the ews to leave the religion of their fathers. o the king sent
enelaus to erea, a city of yria, and there had him put to death, when he had
been high priest ten years. e had been a wicked and an impious man and, in
order to get the government to himself, had compelled his nation to transgress
their own laws. fter the death of enelaus, lcimus, who was also called
acimus, was made high priest. ut when king ntiochus found that hilip had
already possessed himself of the government, he made war against him, and
subdued him, and took him, and slew him. ow as to nias, the son of the high
priest, who, as we before informed you, was left a child when his father died,
when he saw that the king had slain his uncle enelaus, and given the high
priesthood to lcimus, who was not of the high priest stock, but was induced
by ysias to translate that dignity from his family to another house, he fled to
tolemy, king of gypt and when he found he was in great esteem with him,
and with his wife leopatra, he desired and obtained a place in the omus of
eliopolis, wherein he built a temple like to that at erusalem of which
therefore we shall hereafter give an account, in a place more proper for it.

. bout the same time emetrius, the son of eleucus, fled away from ome,
and took ripoli, a city of yria, and set the diadem on his own head. e also
gathered certain mercenary soldiers together, and entered into his kingdom, and
was joyfully received by all, who delivered themselves up to him. nd when
they had taken ntiochus the king, and ysias, they brought them to him alive
both which were immediately put to death by the command of emetrius, when
ntiochus had reigned two years, as we have already elsewhere related. ut
there were now many of the wicked ewish runagates that came together to
him, and with them lcimus the high priest, who accused the whole nation, and
particularly udas and his brethren and said that they had slain all his friends,
and that those in his kingdom that were of his party, and waited for his return,

were by them put to death that these men had ejected them out of their own
country, and caused them to be sojourners in a foreign land and they desired
that he would send some one of his own friends, and know from him what
mischief udas's party had done.
. t this emetrius was very angry, and sent acchides, a friend of
ntiochus piphanes,    a good man, and one that had been intrusted with all
esopotamia, and gave him an army, and committed lcimus the high priest to
his care and gave him charge to slay udas, and those that were with him. o
acchides made haste, and went out of ntioch with his army and when he
was come into udea, he sent to udas and his brethren, to discourse with them
about a league of friendship and peace, for he had a mind to take him by
treachery. ut udas did not give credit to him, for he saw that he came with so
great an army as men do not bring when they come to make peace, but to make
war. owever, some of the people acquiesced in what acchides caused to be
proclaimed and supposing they should undergo no considerable harm from
lcimus, who was their countryman, they went over to them and when they
had received oaths from both of them, that neither they themselves, nor those of
the same sentiments, should come to any harm, they intrusted themselves with
them. ut acchides troubled not himself about the oaths he had taken, but
slew threescore of them, although, by not keeping his faith with those that first
went over, he deterred all the rest, who had intentions to go over to him, from
doing it. ut as he was gone out of erusalem, and was at the village called
ethzetho, he sent out, and caught many of the deserters, and some of the
people also, and slew them all and enjoined all that lived in the country to
submit to lcimus. o he left him there, with some part of the army, that he
might have wherewith to keep the country in obedience and returned to ntioch
to king emetrius.
. ut lcimus was desirous to have the dominion more firmly assured to
him and understanding that, if he could bring it about that the multitude should
be his friends, he should govern with greater security, he spake kind words to
them all, and discoursed to each of them after an agreeable and pleasant
manner by which means he quickly had a great body of men and an army
about him, although the greater part of them were of the wicked, and the
deserters. ith these, whom he used as his servants and soldiers, he went all
over the country, and slew all that he could find of udas's party. ut when
udas saw that lcimus was already become great, and had destroyed many of
the good and holy men of the country, he also went all over the country, and
destroyed those that were of the other party. ut when lcimus saw that he was
not able to oppose udas, nor was equal to him in strength, he resolved to apply
himself to king emetrius for his assistance so he came to ntioch, and
irritated him against udas, and accused him, alleging that he had undergone a

great many miseries by his means, and that he would do more mischief unless
he were prevented, and brought to punishment, which must be done by sending
a powerful force against him.
. o emetrius, being already of opinion that it would be a thing
pernicious to his own affairs to overlook udas, now he was becoming so great,
sent against him icanor, the most kind and most faithful of all his friends for
he it was who fled away with him from the city of ome. e also gave him as
many forces as he thought sufficient for him to conquer udas withal, and bid
him not to spare the nation at all. hen icanor was come to erusalem, he did
not resolve to fight udas immediately, but judged it better to get him into his
power by treachery so he sent him a message of peace, and said there was no
manner of necessity for them to fight and hazard themselves and that he would
give him his oath that he would do him no harm, for that he only came with
some friends, in order to let him know what king emetrius's intentions were,
and what opinion he had of their nation. hen icanor had delivered this
message, udas and his brethren complied with him, and suspecting no deceit,
they gave him assurances of friendship, and received icanor and his army but
while he was saluting udas, and they were talking together, he gave a certain
signal to his own soldiers, upon which they were to seize upon udas but he
perceived the treachery, and ran back to his own soldiers, and fled away with
them. o upon this discovery of his purpose, and of the snares laid for udas,
icanor determined to make open war with him, and gathered his army
together, and prepared for fighting him and upon joining battle with him at a
certain village called apharsalama, he beat udas,    and forced him to fly to
that citadel which was at erusalem.
. nd when icanor came down from the citadel unto the temple, some of
the priests and elders met him, and saluted him and showed him the sacrifices
which they offered to od for the king upon which he blasphemed, and
threatened them, that unless the people would deliver up udas to him, upon his
return he would pull down the temple. nd when he had thus threatened them,
he departed from erusalem. ut the priests fell into tears out of grief at what he
had said, and besought od to deliver them from their enemies. ut now for
icanor, when he was gone out of erusalem, and was at a certain village called
ethoron, he there pitched his camp, another army out of yria having joined
him. nd udas pitched his camp at dasa, another village, which was thirty
furlongs distant from ethoron, having no more than one thousand soldiers.
nd when he had encouraged them not to be dismayed at the multitude of their
enemies, nor to regard how many they were against whom they were going to
fight, but to consider who they themselves were, and for what great rewards
they hazarded themselves, and to attack the enemy courageously, he led them
out to fight, and joining battle with icanor, which proved to be a severe one,

he overcame the enemy, and slew many of them and at last icanor himself, as
he was fighting gloriously, fell—upon whose fall the army did not stay but
when they had lost their general, they were put to flight, and threw down their
arms. udas also pursued them and slew them, and gave notice by the sound of
the trumpets to the neighboring villages that he had conquered the enemy
which, when the inhabitants heard, they put on their armor hastily, and met
their enemies in the face as they were running away, and slew them, insomuch
that not one of them escaped out of this battle, who were in number nine
thousand his victory happened to fall on the thirteenth day of that month
which by the ews is called dar and by the acedonians ystrus and the
ews thereon celebrate this victory every year, and esteem it as a festival day.
fter which the ewish nation were, for a while, free from wars, and enjoyed
peace but afterward they returned into their former state of wars and hazards.
. ut now as the high priest lcimus, was resolving to pull down the wall
of the sanctuary, which had been there of old time, and had been built by the
holy prophets,    he was smitten suddenly by od, and fell down. his stroke
made him fall down speechless upon the ground and undergoing torments for
many days, he at length died, when he had been high priest four years. nd
when he was dead, the people bestowed the high priesthood on udas who
hearing of the power of the omans,    and that they had conquered in war

sent to ome some of his friends, upolemus the son of ohn, and ason the

engraven in brass. he decree itself was this "he decree of the senate
concerning a league of assistance and friendship with the nation of the ews. t
shall not be lawful for any that are subject to the omans to make war with the
nation of the ews, nor to assist those that do so, either by sending them corn,
or ships, or money and if any attack be made upon the ews, the omans shall
assist them, as far as they are able and again, if any attack be made upon the
omans, the ews shall assist them. nd if the ews have a mind to add to, or to

common consent of the omans. nd whatsoever addition shall thus be made,
it shall be of force." his decree was written by upolemus the son of ohn, and

and two thousand horsemen. ow udas had no more soldiers than one
thousand.    hen these saw the multitude of acchides's men, they were afraid,
and left their camp, and fled all away, ecepting eight hundred. ow when
udas was deserted by his own soldiers, and the enemy pressed upon him, and
gave him no time to gather his army together, he was disposed to fight with
acchides's army, though he had but eight hundred men with him so he
ehorted these men to undergo the danger courageously, and encouraged them
to attack the enemy. nd when they said they were not a body sufficient to fight
so great an army, and advised that they should retire now, and save themselves
and that when he had gathered his own men together, then he should fall upon
the enemy afterwards, his answer was this "et not the sun ever see such a
thing, that  should show my back to the enemy and although this be the time
that will bring me to my end, and  must die in this battle,  will rather stand to
it courageously, and bear whatsoever comes upon me, than by now running
away bring reproach upon my former great actions, or tarnish their glory." his
was the speech he made to those that remained with him, whereby he
encouraged them to attack the enemy.
. ut acchides drew his army out of their camp, and put them in array for
the battle. e set the horsemen on both the wings, and the light soldiers and the
archers he placed before the whole army, but he was himself on the right wing.
nd when he had thus put his army in order of battle, and was going to join
battle with the enemy, he commanded the trumpeter to give a signal of battle,
and the army to make a shout, and to fall on the enemy. nd when udas had
done the same, he joined battle with them and as both sides fought valiantly,

and the battle continued till sun-set, udas saw that acchides and the strongest
part of the army was in the right wing, and thereupon took the most courageous
men with him, and ran upon that part of the army, and fell upon those that were
there, and broke their ranks, and drove them into the middle, and forced them
to run away, and pursued them as far as to a mountain called za but when
those of the left wing saw that the right wing was put to flight, they
encompassed udas, and pursued him, and came behind him, and took him into
the middle of their army so being not able to fly, but encompassed round about
with enemies, he stood still, and he and those that were with him fought and
when he had slain a great many of those that came against him, he at last was
himself wounded, and fell and gave up the ghost, and died in a way like to his
former famous actions. hen udas was dead, those that were with him had no
one whom they could regard as their commander] but when they saw
themselves deprived of such a general, they fled. ut imon and onathan,
udas's brethren, received his dead body by a treaty from the enemy, and
carried it to the village of odin, where their father had been buried, and there
buried him while the multitude lamented him many days, and performed the
usual solemn rites of a funeral to him. nd this was the end that udas came to.
e had been a man of valor and a great warrior, and mindful of the commands
of their father attathias and had undergone all difficulties, both in doing and
suffering, for the liberty of his countrymen. nd when his character was so
ecellent while he was alive], he left behind him a glorious reputation and
memorial, by gaining freedom for his nation, and delivering them from slavery
under the acedonians. nd when he had retained the high priesthood three
years, he died.

. y what means the nation of the ews recovered their freedom when they
had been brought into slavery by the acedonians, and what struggles, and
how great battles, udas, the general of their army, ran through till he was slain
as he was fighting for them, hath been related in the foregoing book but after
he was dead, all the wicked, and those that transgressed the laws of their
forefathers, sprang up again in udea, and grew upon them, and distressed them
on every side.  famine also assisted their wickedness, and afflicted the

country, till not a few, who by reason of their want of necessaries, and because

acedonians. nd now acchides gathered those ews together who had

manner, came to his brother onathan, and desired him that he would imitate his

. ut when acchides knew that onathan had pitched his camp among the
