within having betrayed it to him for they were at enmity with emetrius, on
account of his insolence and difficulty of access for he shut himself up in a
palace of his that had four towers which he had built himself, not far from
ntioch and admitted nobody. e was withal slothful and negligent about the
public affairs, whereby the hatred of his subjects was the more kindled against
him, as we have elsewhere already related. hen therefore emetrius heard
that leander was in tolemais, he took his whole army, and led it against
him he also sent ambassadors to onathan about a league of mutual assistance
and friendship, for he resolved to be beforehand with leander, lest the other
should treat with him first, and gain assistance from him and this he did out of
the fear he had lest onathan should remember how ill emetrius had formerly
treated him, and should join with him in this war against him. e therefore
gave orders that onathan should be allowed to raise an army, and should get
armor made, and should receive back those hostages of the ewish nation
whom acchides had shut up in the citadel of erusalem. hen this good
fortune had befallen onathan, by the concession of emetrius, he came to
erusalem, and read the king's letter in the audience of the people, and of those
that kept the citadel. hen these were read, these wicked men and deserters,
who were in the citadel, were greatly afraid, upon the king's permission to
onathan to raise an army, and to receive back the hostages. o he delivered
every one of them to his own parents. nd thus did onathan make his abode at
erusalem, renewing the city to a better state, and reforming the buildings as he
pleased for he gave orders that the walls of the city should be rebuilt with
square stones, that it might be more secure from their enemies. nd when those
that kept the garrisons that were in udea saw this, they all left them, and fled to
ntioch, ecepting those that were in the city ethsura, and those that were in
the citadel of erusalem, for the greater part of these was of the wicked ews
and deserters, and on that account these did not deliver up their garrisons.
. hen leander knew what promises emetrius had made onathan, and
withal knew his courage, and what great things he had done when he fought the
acedonians, and besides what hardships he had undergone by the means of
emetrius, and of acchides, the general of emetrius's army, he told his
friends that he could not at present find any one else that might afford him
better assistance than onathan, who was both courageous against his enemies,
and had a particular hatred against emetrius, as having both suffered many
hard things from him, and acted many hard things against him. f therefore they
were of opinion that they should make him their friend against emetrius, it

was more for their advantage to invite him to assist them now than at another
time. t being therefore determined by him and his friends to send to onathan,
he wrote to him this epistle "ing leander to his brother onathan, sendeth
greeting. e have long ago heard of thy courage and thy fidelity, and for that
reason have sent to thee, to make with thee a league of friendship and mutual
assistance. e therefore do ordain thee this day the high priest of the ews, and
that thou beest called my friend.  have also sent thee, as presents, a purple robe
and a golden crown, and desire that, now thou art by us honored, thou wilt in
like manner respect us also."
. hen onathan had received this letter, he put on the pontifical robe at
the time of the feast of tabernacles,    four years after the death of his brother
udas, for at that time no high priest had been made. o he raised great forces,
and had abundance of armor got ready. his greatly grieved emetrius when he
heard of it, and made him blame himself for his slowness, that he had not
prevented leander, and got the good will of onathan, but had given him time
so to do. owever, he also himself wrote a letter to onathan, and to the people,
the contents whereof are these "ing emetrius to onathan, and to the nation
of the ews, sendeth greeting. ince you have preserved your friendship for us,
and when you have been tempted by our enemies, you have not joined
yourselves to them,  both commend you for this your fidelity, and ehort you
to continue in the same disposition, for which you shall be repaid, and receive
rewards from us for  will free you from the greatest part of the tributes and
taes which you formerly paid to the kings my predecessors, and to myself and
 do now set you free from those tributes which you have ever paid and
besides,  forgive you the ta upon salt, and the value of the crowns which you
used to offer to me    and instead of the third part of the fruits of the field], and
the half of the fruits of the trees,  relinquish my part of them from this day and
as to the poll money, which ought to be given me for every head of the
inhabitants of udea, and of the three toparchies that adjoin to udea, amaria,
and alilee, and erea, that  relinquish to you for this time, and for all time to
come.  will also that the city of erusalem be holy and inviolable, and free
from the tithe, and from the taes, unto its utmost bounds. nd  so far recede
from my title to the citadel, as to permit onathan your high priest to possess it,
that he may place such a garrison in it as he approves of for fidelity and good
will to himself, that they may keep it for us.  also make free all those ews who
have been made captives and slaves in my kingdom.  also give order that the
beasts of the ews be not pressed for our service and let their abbaths, and all
their festivals, and three days before each of them, be free from any imposition.
n the same manner,  set free the ews that are inhabitants of my kingdom, and
order that no injury be done them.  also give leave to such of them as are
willing to list themselves in my army, that they may do it, and those as far as

thirty thousand which ewish soldiers, wheresoever they go, shall have the
same pay that my own army hath and some of them  will place in my
garrisons, and some as guards about mine own body, and as rulers over those
that are in my court.  give them leave also to use the laws of their forefathers,
and to observe them and  will that they have power over the three toparchies
that are added to udea and it shall be in the power of the high priest to take
care that no one ew shall have any other temple for worship but only that at
erusalem.  bequeath also, out of my own revenues, yearly, for the epenses
about the sacrifices, one hundred and fifty thousand drachmae] and what
money is to spare,  will that it shall be your own.  also release to you those ten
thousand drachmae which the kings received from the temple, because they
appertain to the priests that minister in that temple. nd whosoever shall fly to
the temple at erusalem, or to the places thereto belonging, or who owe the king
money, or are there on any other account, let them be set free, and let their
goods be in safety.  also give you leave to repair and rebuild your temple, and
that all be done at my epenses.  also allow you to build the walls of your city,
and to erect high towers, and that they be erected at my charge. nd if there be
any fortified town that would be convenient for the ewish country to have very
strong, let it be so built at my epenses."
. his was what emetrius promised and granted to the ews by this letter.
ut king leander raised a great army of mercenary soldiers, and of those that
deserted to him out of yria, and made an epedition against emetrius. nd
when it was come to a battle, the left wing of emetrius put those who opposed
them to flight, and pursued them a great way, and slew many of them, and
spoiled their camp but the right wing, where emetrius happened to be, was
beaten and as for all the rest, they ran away. ut emetrius fought
courageously, and slew a great many of the enemy but as he was in the pursuit
of the rest, his horse carried him into a deep bog, where it was hard to get out,
and there it happened, that upon his horse's falling down, he could not escape
being killed for when his enemies saw what had befallen him, they returned
back, and encompassed emetrius round, and they all threw their darts at him
but he, being now on foot, fought bravely. ut at length he received so many
wounds, that he was not able to bear up any longer, but fell. nd this is the end
that emetrius came to, when he had reigned eleven years,    as we have
elsewhere related.

. ut then the son of nias the high priest, who was of the same name with
his father, and who fled to king tolemy, who was called hilometor, lived now
at leandria, as we have said already. hen this nias saw that udea was
oppressed by the acedonians and their kings, out of a desire to purchase to
himself a memorial and eternal fame he resolved to send to king tolemy and
queen leopatra, to ask leave of them that he might build a temple in gypt like
to that at erusalem, and might ordain evites and priests out of their own
stock. he chief reason why he was desirous so to do, was, that he relied upon
the prophet saiah, who lived above si hundred years before, and foretold that
there certainly was to be a temple built to lmighty od in gypt by a man that
was a ew. nias was elevated with this prediction, and wrote the following
epistle to tolemy and leopatra "aving done many and great things for you
in the affairs of the war, by the assistance of od, and that in oelesyria and
hoenicia,  came at length with the ews to eontopolis, and to other places of
your nation, where  found that the greatest part of your people had temples in
an improper manner, and that on this account they bare ill will one against
another, which happens to the gyptians by reason of the multitude of their
temples, and the difference of opinions about ivine worship. ow  found a
very fit place in a castle that hath its name from the country iana this place is
full of materials of several sorts, and replenished with sacred animals  desire
therefore that you will grant me leave to purge this holy place, which belongs to
no master, and is fallen down, and to build there a temple to lmighty od,
after the pattern of that in erusalem, and of the same dimensions, that may be
for the benefit of thyself, and thy wife and children, that those ews which
dwell in gypt may have a place whither they may come and meet together in
mutual harmony one with another, and be subservient to thy advantages for the
prophet saiah foretold that 'there should be an altar in gypt to the ord od' 
 and many other such things did he prophesy relating to that place."
. nd this was what nias wrote to king tolemy. ow any one may
observe his piety, and that of his sister and wife leopatra, by that epistle which
they wrote in answer to it for they laid the blame and the transgression of the
law upon the head of nias. nd this was their reply "ing tolemy and queen
leopatra to nias, send greeting. e have read thy petition, wherein thou
desirest leave to be given thee to purge that temple which is fallen down at
eontopolis, in the omus of eliopolis, and which is named from the country
ubastis on which account we cannot but wonder that it should be pleasing to
od to have a temple erected in a place so unclean, and so full of sacred
animals. ut since thou sayest that saiah the prophet foretold this long ago, we
give thee leave to do it, if it may be done according to your law, and so that we
may not appear to have at all offended od herein."

. o nias took the place, and built a temple, and an altar to od, like
indeed to that in erusalem, but smaller and poorer.  do not think it proper for
me now to describe its dimensions or its vessels, which have been already
described in my seventh book of the ars of the ews. owever, nias found
other ews like to himself, together with priests and evites, that there
performed ivine service. ut we have said enough about this temple.
. ow it came to pass that the leandrian ews, and those amaritans
who paid their worship to the temple that was built in the days of leander at
ount erizzim, did now make a sedition one against another, and disputed
about their temples before tolemy himself the ews saying that, according to
the laws of oses, the temple was to be built at erusalem and the amaritans
saying that it was to be built at erizzim. hey desired therefore the king to sit
with his friends, and hear the debates about these matters, and punish those
with death who were baffled. ow abbeus and heodosius managed the
argument for the amaritans, and ndronicus, the son of essalamus, for the
people of erusalem and they took an oath by od and the king to make their
demonstrations according to the law and they desired of tolemy, that
whomsoever he should find that transgressed what they had sworn to, he would
put him to death. ccordingly, the king took several of his friends into the
council, and sat down, in order to hear what the pleaders said. ow the ews
that were at leandria were in great concern for those men, whose lot it was to
contend for the temple at erusalem for they took it very ill that any should
take away the reputation of that temple, which was so ancient and so celebrated
all over the habitable earth. ow when abbeus and heodosius had given
leave to ndronicus to speak first, he began to demonstrate out of the law, and
out of the successions of the high priests, how they every one in succession
from his father had received that dignity, and ruled over the temple and how all
the kings of sia had honored that temple with their donations, and with the
most splendid gifts dedicated thereto. ut as for that at erizzim, he made no
account of it, and regarded it as if it had never had a being. y this speech, and
other arguments, ndronicus persuaded the king to determine that the temple at
erusalem was built according to the laws of oses,    and to put abbeus and
heodosius to death. nd these were the events that befell the ews at
leandria in the days of tolemy hilometor.
  
      
    ,   
,  ,

. emetrius being thus slain in battle, as we have above related, leander
took the kingdom of yria and wrote to tolemy hilometor, and desired his
daughter in marriage and said it was but just that he should be joined an
affinity to one that had now received the principality of his forefathers, and had
been promoted to it by od's providence, and had conquered emetrius, and
that was on other accounts not unworthy of being related to him. tolemy
received this proposal of marriage gladly and wrote him an answer, saluting
him on account of his having received the principality of his forefathers and
promising him that he would give him his daughter in marriage and assured
him that he was coming to meet him at tolemais, and desired that he would
there meet him, for that he would accompany her from gypt so far, and would
there marry his child to him. hen tolemy had written thus, he came suddenly
to tolemais, and brought his daughter leopatra along with him and as he
found leander there before him, as he desired him to come, he gave him his
child in marriage, and for her portion gave her as much silver and gold as
became such a king to give.
. hen the wedding was over, leander wrote to onathan the high
priest, and desired him to come to tolemais. o when he came to these kings,
and had made them magnificent presents, he was honored by them both.
leander compelled him also to put off his own garment, and to take a purple
garment, and made him sit with him on his throne and commanded his captains
that they should go with him into the middle of the city, and proclaim, that it
was not permitted to any one to speak against him, or to give him any
disturbance. nd when the captains had thus done, those that were prepared to
accuse onathan, and who bore him ill-will, when they saw the honor that was
done him by proclamation, and that by the king's order, ran away, and were
afraid lest some mischief should befall them. ay, king leander was so very
kind to onathan, that he set him down as the principal of his friends.
. ut then, upon the hundred and sity-fifth year, emetrius, the son of
emetrius, came from rete with a great number of mercenary soldiers, which
asthenes, the retan, brought him, and sailed to ilicia. his thing cast
leander into great concern and disorder when he heard it so he made haste
immediately out of hoenicia, and came to ntioch, that he might put matters
in a safe posture there before emetrius should come. e also left pollonius
aus    governor of oelesyria, who coming to amnia with a great army, sent to
onathan the high priest, and told him that it was not right that he alone should
live at rest, and with authority, and not be subject to the king that this thing had
made him a reproach among all men, that he had not yet made him subject to
the king. "o not thou therefore deceive thyself, and sit still among the
mountains, and pretend to have forces with thee but if thou hast any

dependence on thy strength, come down into the plain, and let our armies be
compared together, and the event of the battle will demonstrate which of us is
the most courageous. owever, take notice, that the most valiant men of every
city are in my army, and that these are the very men who have always beaten
thy progenitors but let us have the battle in such a place of the country where
we may fight with weapons, and not with stones, and where there may be no
place whither those that are beaten may fly."
. ith this onathan was irritated and choosing himself out ten thousand
of his soldiers, he went out of erusalem in haste, with his brother imon, and
came to oppa, and pitched his camp on the outside of the city, because the
people of oppa had shut their gates against him, for they had a garrison in the
city put there by pollonius. ut when onathan was preparing to besiege them,
they were afraid he would take them by force, and so they opened the gates to
him. ut pollonius, when he heard that oppa was taken by onathan, took
three thousand horsemen, and eight thousand footmen and came to shdod and
removing thence, he made his journey silently and slowly, and going up to
oppa, he made as if he was retiring from the place, and so drew onathan into
the plain, as valuing himself highly upon his horsemen, and having his hopes of
victory principally in them. owever, onathan sallied out, and pursued
pollonius to shdod but as soon as pollonius perceived that his enemy was
in the plain, he came back and gave him battle. ut pollonius had laid a
thousand horsemen in ambush in a valley, that they might be seen by their
enemies as behind them which when onathan perceived, he was under no
consternation, but ordering his army to stand in a square battle-array, he gave
them a charge to fall on the enemy on both sides, and set them to face those that
attacked them both before and behind and while the fight lasted till the
evening, he gave part of his forces to his brother imon, and ordered him to
attack the enemies but for himself, he charged those that were with him to
cover themselves with their armor, and receive the darts of the horsemen, who
did as they were commanded so that the enemy's horsemen, while they threw
their darts till they had no more left, did them no harm, for the darts that were
thrown did not enter into their bodies, being thrown upon the shields that were
united and conjoined together, the closeness of which easily overcame the force
of the darts, and they flew about without any effect. ut when the enemy grew
remiss in throwing their darts from morning till late at night, imon perceived
their weariness, and fell upon the body of men before him and because his
soldiers showed great alacrity, he put the enemy to flight. nd when the
horsemen saw that the footmen ran away, neither did they stay themselves, but
they being very weary, by the duration of the fight till the evening, and their
hope from the footmen being quite gone, they basely ran away, and in great
confusion also, till they were separated one from another, and scattered over all

the plain. pon which onathan pursued them as far as shdod, and slew a
great many of them, and compelled the rest, in despair of escaping, to fly to the
temple of agon, which was at shdod but onathan took the city on the first
onset, and burnt it, and the villages about it nor did he abstain from the temple
of agon itself, but burnt it also, and destroyed those that had fled to it. ow
the entire multitude of the enemies that fell in the battle, and were consumed in
the temple, were eight thousand. hen onathan therefore had overcome so
great an army, he removed from shdod, and came to skelon and when he
had pitched his camp without the city, the people of skelon came out and met
him, bringing him hospitable presents, and honoring him so he accepted of
their kind intentions, and returned thence to erusalem with a great deal of prey,
which he brought thence when he conquered his enemies. ut when leander
heard that pollonius, the general of his army, was beaten, he pretended to be
glad of it, because he had fought with onathan his friend and ally against his
directions. ccordingly, he sent to onathan, and gave testimony to his worth
and gave him honorary rewards, as a golden button,    which it is the custom to
give the king's kinsmen, and allowed him kron and its toparchy for his own
inheritance.
. bout this time it was that king tolemy, who was called hilometor, led
an army, part by the sea, and part by land, and came to yria, to the assistance
of leander, who was his son-in-law and accordingly all the cities received
him willingly, as leander had commanded them to do, and conducted him as
far as shdod where they all made loud complaints about the temple of agon,
which was burnt, and accused onathan of having laid it waste, and destroyed
the country adjoining with fire, and slain a great number of them. tolemy
heard these accusations, but said nothing. onathan also went to meet tolemy
as far as oppa, and obtained from him hospitable presents, and those glorious
in their kinds, with all the marks of honor and when he had conducted him as
far as the river called leutherus, he returned again to erusalem.
. ut as tolemy was at tolemais, he was very near to a most unepected
destruction for a treacherous design was laid for his life by leander, by the
means of mmonius, who was his friend and as the treachery was very plain,
tolemy wrote to leander, and required of him that he should bring
mmonius to condign punishment, informing him what snares had been laid
for him by mmonius, and desiring that he might be accordingly punished for
it. ut when leander did not comply with his demands, he perceived that it
was he himself who laid the design, and was very angry at him. leander had
also formerly been on very ill terms with the people of ntioch, for they had
suffered very much by his means yet did mmonius at length undergo the
punishment his insolent crimes had deserved, for he was killed in an
opprobrious manner, like a woman, while he endeavored to conceal himself in

a feminine habit, as we have elsewhere related.
. ereupon tolemy blamed himself for having given his daughter in
marriage to leander, and for the league he had made with him to assist him
against emetrius so he dissolved his relation to him, and took his daughter
away from him, and immediately sent to emetrius, and offered to make a
league of mutual assistance and friendship with him, and agreed with him to
give him his daughter in marriage, and to restore him to the principality of his
fathers. emetrius was well pleased with this embassage, and accepted of his
assistance, and of the marriage of his daughter. ut tolemy had still one more
hard task to do, and that was to persuade the people of ntioch to receive
emetrius, because they were greatly displeased at him, on account of the
injuries his father emetrius had done them yet did he bring this about for as
the people of ntioch hated leander on mmonius's account, as we have
shown already, they were easily prevailed with to cast him out of ntioch who,
thus epelled out of ntioch, came into ilicia. tolemy came then to ntioch,
and was made king by its inhabitants, and by the army so that he was forced to
put on two diadems, the one of sia, the other of gypt but being naturally a
good and a righteous man, and not desirous of what belonged to others, and
besides these dispositions, being also a wise man in reasoning about futurities,
he determined to avoid the envy of the omans so he called the people of
ntioch together to an assembly, and persuaded them to receive emetrius and
assured them that he would not be mindful of what they did to his father in case
he should be now obliged by them and he undertook that he would himself be
a good monitor and governor to him, and promised that he would not permit
him to attempt any bad actions but that, for his own part, he was contented
with the kingdom of gypt. y which discourse he persuaded the people of
ntioch to receive emetrius.
. ut now leander made haste with a numerous and great army, and
came out of ilicia into yria, and burnt the country belonging to ntioch, and
pillaged it whereupon tolemy, and his son-in-law emetrius, brought their
army against him, (for he had already given him his daughter in marriage,) and
beat leander, and put him to flight and accordingly he fled into rabia. ow
it happened in the time of the battle that tolemy's horse, upon hearing the
noise of an elephant, cast him off his back, and threw him on the ground upon
the sight of which accident, his enemies fell upon him, and gave him many
wounds upon his head, and brought him into danger of death for when his
guards caught him up, he was so very ill, that for four days' time he was not
able either to understand or to speak. owever, abdiel, a prince among the
rabians, cut off leander's head, and sent it to tolemy, who recovering of
his wounds, and returning to his understanding, on the fifth day, heard at once a
most agreeable hearing, and saw a most agreeable sight, which were the death

and the head of leander yet a little after this his joy for the death of
leander, with which he was so greatly satisfied, he also departed this life.
ow leander, who was called alas, reigned over sia five years, as we
have elsewhere related.
. ut when emetrius, who was styled icator,    had taken the kingdom,
he was so wicked as to treat tolemy's soldiers very hardly, neither
remembering the league of mutual assistance that was between them, nor that
he was his son-in-law and kinsman, by leopatra's marriage to him so the
soldiers fled from his wicked treatment to leandria but emetrius kept his
elephants. ut onathan the high priest levied an army out of all udea, and
attacked the citadel at erusalem, and besieged it. t was held by a garrison of
acedonians, and by some of those wicked men who had deserted the customs
of their forefathers. hese men at first despised the attempts of onathan for
taking the place, as depending on its strength but some of those wicked men
went out by night, and came to emetrius, and informed him that the citadel
was besieged who was irritated with what he heard, and took his army, and
came from ntioch, against onathan. nd when he was at ntioch, he wrote to
him, and commanded him to come to him quickly to tolemais upon which
onathan did not intermit the siege of the citadel, but took with him the elders
of the people, and the priests, and carried with him gold, and silver, and
garments, and a great number of presents of friendship, and came to emetrius,
and presented him with them, and thereby pacified the king's anger. o he was
honored by him, and received from him the confirmation of his high
priesthood, as he had possessed it by the grants of the kings his predecessors.
nd when the ewish deserters accused him, emetrius was so far from giving
credit to them, that when he petitioned him that he would demand no more than
three hundred talents for the tribute of all udea, and the three toparchies of
amaria, and erea, and alilee, he complied with the proposal, and gave him a
letter confirming all those grants whose contents were as follows "ing
emetrius to onathan his brother, and to the nation of the ews, sendeth
greeting. e have sent you a copy of that epistle which we have written to
asthenes our kinsman, that you may know its contents. 'ing emetrius to
asthenes our father, sendeth greeting.  have determined to return thanks, and
to show favor to the nation of the ews, which hath observed the rules of justice
in our concerns. ccordingly,  remit to them the three prefectures, pherima,
and ydda, and amatha, which have been added to udea out of amaria, with
their appurtenances as also what the kings my predecessors received from
those that offered sacrifices in erusalem, and what are due from the fruits of
the earth, and of the trees, and what else belongs to us with the salt-pits, and
the crowns that used to be presented to us. or shall they be compelled to pay
any of those taes from this time to all futurity. ake care therefore that a copy

of this epistle be taken, and given to onathan, and be set up in an eminent
place of their holy temple.'" nd these were the contents of this writing. nd
now when emetrius saw that there was peace every where, and that there was
no danger, nor fear of war, he disbanded the greatest part of his army, and
diminished their pay, and even retained in pay no others than such foreigners as
came up with him from rete, and from the other islands. owever, this
procured him ill-will and hatred from the soldiers on whom he bestowed
nothing from this time, while the kings before him used to pay them in time of
peace as they did before, that they might have their good-will, and that they
might be very ready to undergo the difficulties of war, if any occasion should
require it.

   ,    , 
       
    
. ow there was a certain commander of leander's forces, an panemian
by birth, whose name was iodotus, and was also called rypho, took notice
the ill-will of the soldiers bare to emetrius, and went to alchus the rabian,
who brought up ntiochus, the son of leander, and told him what ill-will the
army bare emetrius, and persuaded him to give him ntiochus, because he
would make him king, and recover to him the kingdom of his father. alchus at
the first opposed him in this attempt, because he could not believe him but
when rypho lay hard at him for a long time, he over-persuaded him to comply
with rypho's intentions and entreaties. nd this was the state rypho was now
in.
. ut onathan the high priest, being desirous to get clear of those that
were in the citadel of erusalem, and of the ewish deserters, and wicked men,
as well as of those in all the garrisons in the country, sent presents and
ambassadors to emetrius, and entreated him to take away his soldiers out of
the strong holds of udea. emetrius made answer, that after the war, which he
was now deeply engaged in, was over, he would not only grant him that, but
greater things than that also and he desired he would send him some
assistance, and informed him that his army had deserted him. o onathan
chose out three thousand of his soldiers, and sent them to emetrius.
. ow the people of ntioch hated emetrius, both on account of what
mischief he had himself done them, and because they were his enemies also on

account of his father emetrius, who had greatly abused them so they watched
some opportunity which they might lay hold on to fall upon him. nd when
they were informed of the assistance that was coming to emetrius from
onathan, and considered at the same time that he would raise a numerous
army, unless they prevented him, and seized upon him, they took their weapons
immediately, and encompassed his palace in the way of a siege, and seizing
upon all the ways of getting out, they sought to subdue their king. nd when he
saw that the people of ntioch were become his bitter enemies and that they
were thus in arms, he took the mercenary soldiers which he had with them, and
those ews who were sent by onathan, and assaulted the ntiochians but he
was overpowered by them, for they were many ten thousands, and was beaten.
ut when the ews saw that the ntiochians were superior, they went up to the
top of the palace, and shot at them from thence and because they were so
remote from them by their height, that they suffered nothing on their side, but
did great eecution on the others, as fighting from such an elevation, they drove
them out of the adjoining houses, and immediately set them on fire, whereupon
the flame spread itself over the whole city, and burnt it all down. his happened
by reason of the closeness of the houses, and because they were generally built
of wood. o the ntiochians, when they were not able to help themselves, nor
to stop the fire, were put to flight. nd as the ews leaped from the top of one
house to the top of another, and pursued them after that manner, it thence
happened that the pursuit was so very surprising. ut when the king saw that
the ntiochians were busy in saving their children and their wives, and so did
not fight any longer, he fell upon them in the narrow passages, and fought
them, and slew a great many of them, till at last they were forced to throw
down their arms, and to deliver themselves up to emetrius. o he forgave
them this their insolent behavior, and put an end to the sedition and when he
had given rewards to the ews out of the rich spoils he had gotten, and had
returned them thanks, as the cause of his victory, he sent them away to
erusalem to onathan, with an ample testimony of the assistance they had
afforded him. et did he prove an ill man to onathan afterward, and broke the
promises he had made and he threatened that he would make war upon him,
unless he would pay all that tribute which the ewish nation owed to the first
kings of yria]. nd this he had done, if rypho had not hindered him, and
diverted his preparations against onathan to a concern for his own
preservation for he now returned out of rabia into yria, with the child
ntiochus, for he was yet in age but a youth, and put the diadem on his head
and as the whole forces that had left emetrius, because they had no pay, came
to his assistance, he made war upon emetrius, and joining battle with him,
overcame him in the fight, and took from him both his elephants and the city
ntioch.

. emetrius, upon this defeat, retired into ilicia but the child ntiochus
sent ambassadors and an epistle to onathan, and made him his friend and
confederate, and confirmed to him the high priesthood, and yielded up to him
the four prefectures which had been added to udea. oreover, he sent him
vessels and cups of gold, and a purple garment, and gave him leave to use
them. e also presented him with a golden button, and styled him one of his
principal friends, and appointed his brother imon to be the general over the
forces, from the adder of yre unto gypt. o onathan was so pleased with
these grants made him by ntiochus, that he sent ambassadors to him and to
rypho, and professed himself to be their friend and confederate, and said he
would join with him in a war against emetrius, informing him that he had
made no proper returns for the kindness he had done him for that when he had
received many marks of kindness from him, when he stood in great need of
them, he, for such good turns, had requited him with further injuries.
. o ntiochus gave onathan leave to raise himself a numerous army out
of yria and hoenicia and to make war against emetrius's generals
whereupon he went in haste to the several cities which received him splendidly
indeed, but put no forces into his hands. nd when he was come from thence to
skelon, the inhabitants of skelon came and brought him presents, and met
him in a splendid manner. e ehorted them, and every one of the cities of
oelesyria, to forsake emetrius, and to join with ntiochus and, in assisting
him, to endeavor to punish emetrius for what offenses he had been guilty of
against themselves and told them there were many reasons for that their
procedure, if they had a mind so to do. nd when he had persuaded those cities
to promise their assistance to ntiochus, he came to aza, in order to induce
them also to be friends to ntiochus but he found the inhabitants of aza much
more alienated from him than he epected, for they had shut their gates against
him and although they had deserted emetrius, they had not resolved to join
themselves to ntiochus. his provoked onathan to besiege them, and to
harass their country for as he set a part of his army round about aza itself, so
with the rest he overran their land, and spoiled it, and burnt what was in it.
hen the inhabitants of aza saw themselves in this state of affliction, and that
no assistance came to them from emetrius, that what distressed them was at
hand, but what should profit them was still at a great distance, and it was
uncertain whether it would come at all or not, they thought it would he prudent
conduct to leave off any longer continuance with them, and to cultivate
friendship with the other so they sent to onathan, and professed they would be
his friends, and afford him assistance for such is the temper of men, that before
they have had the trial of great afflictions, they do not understand what is for
their advantage but when they find themselves under such afflictions, they then
change their minds, and what it had been better for them to have done before

they had been at all damaged, they choose to do, but not till after they have
suffered such damages. owever, he made a league of friendship with them,
and took from them hostages for their performance of it, and sent these
hostages to erusalem, while he went himself over all the country, as far as
amascus.
. ut when he heard that the generals of emetrius's forces were come to
the city adesh with a numerous army, (the place lies between the land of the
yrians and alilee,) for they supposed they should hereby draw him out of
yria, in order to preserve alilee, and that he would not overlook the
alileans, who were his own people, when war was made upon them, he went
to meet them, having left imon in udea, who raised as great an army as he
was able out of the country, and then sat down before ethsura, and besieged it,
that being the strongest place in all udea and a garrison of emetrius's kept it,
as we have already related. ut as imon was raising banks, and bringing his
engines of war against ethsura, and was very earnest about the siege of it, the
garrison was afraid lest the place should be taken of imon by force, and they
put to the sword so they sent to imon, and desired the security of his oath,
that they should come to no harm from him, and that they would leave the
place, and go away to emetrius. ccordingly he gave them his oath, and
ejected them out of the city, and he put therein a garrison of his own.
. ut onathan removed out of alilee, and from the waters which are
called ennesar, for there he was before encamped, and came into the plain that
is called sor, without knowing that the enemy was there. hen therefore
emetrius's men knew a day beforehand that onathan was coming against
them, they laid an ambush in the mountain, who were to assault him on the
sudden, while they themselves met him with an army in the plain which army,
when onathan saw ready to engage him, he also got ready his own soldiers for
the battle as well as he was able but those that were laid in ambush by
emetrius's generals being behind them, the ews were afraid lest they should
be caught in the midst between two bodies, and perish so they ran away in
haste, and indeed all the rest left onathan but a few there were, in number
about fifty, who staid with him, and with them attathias, the son of bsalom,
and udas, the son of hapseus, who were commanders of the whole army.
hese marched boldly, and like men desperate, against the enemy, and so
pushed them, that by their courage they daunted them, and with their weapons
in their hands they put them to flight. nd when those soldiers of onathan that
had retired saw the enemy giving way, they got together after their flight, and
pursued them with great violence and this did they as far as adesh, where the
camp of the enemy lay.
. onathan having thus gotten a glorious victory, and slain two thousand of
the enemy, returned to erusalem. o when he saw that all his affairs prospered

according to his mind, by the providence of od, he sent ambassadors to the
omans, being desirous of renewing that friendship which their nation had with
them formerly. e enjoined the same ambassadors, that, as they came back,
they should go to the partans, and put them in mind of their friendship and
kindred. o when the ambassadors came to ome, they went into their senate,
and said what they were commanded by onathan the high priest to say, how he
had sent them to confirm their friendship. he senate then confirmed what had
been formerly decreed concerning their friendship with the ews, and gave
them letters to carry to all the kings of sia and urope, and to the governors of
the cities, that they might safely conduct them to their own country.
ccordingly, as they returned, they came to parta, and delivered the epistle
which they had received of onathan to them a copy of which here follows
"onathan the high priest of the ewish nation, and the senate, and body of the
people of the ews, to the ephori, and senate, and people of the acedemonians,
send greeting. f you be well, and both your public and private affairs be
agreeable to your mind, it is according to our wishes. e are well also. hen in
former times an epistle was brought to nias, who was then our high priest,
from reus, who at that time was your king, by emoteles, concerning the
kindred that was between us and you, a copy of which is here subjoined, we
both joyfully received the epistle, and were well pleased with emoteles and
reus, although we did not need such a demonstration, because we were
satisfied about it from the sacred writings,    yet did not we think fit first to
begin the claim of this relation to you, lest we should seem too early in taking
to ourselves the glory which is now given us by you. t is a long time since this
relation of ours to you hath been renewed and when we, upon holy and festival
days, offer sacrifices to od, we pray to him for your preservation and victory.
s to ourselves, although we have had many wars that have compassed us
around, by reason of the covetousness of our neighbors, yet did not we
determine to be troublesome either to you, or to others that were related to us
but since we have now overcome our enemies, and have occasion to send
umenius the son of ntiochus, and ntipater the son of ason, who are both
honorable men belonging to our senate, to the omans, we gave them this
epistle to you also, that they might renew that friendship which is between us.
ou will therefore do well yourselves to write to us, and send us an account of
what you stand in need of from us, since we are in all things disposed to act
according to your desires." o the acedemonians received the ambassadors
kindly, and made a decree for friendship and mutual assistance, and sent it to
them.
. t this time there were three sects among the ews, who had different
opinions concerning human actions the one was called the sect of the
harisees, another the sect of the adducees, and the other the sect of the

ssens. ow for the harisees,    they say that some actions, but not all, are the
work of fate, and some of them are in our own power, and that they are liable to
fate, but are not caused by fate. ut the sect of the ssens affirm, that fate
governs all things, and that nothing befalls men but what is according to its
determination. nd for the adducees, they take away fate, and say there is no
such thing, and that the events of human affairs are not at its disposal but they
suppose that all our actions are in our own power, so that we are ourselves the
causes of what is good, and receive what is evil from our own folly. owever, 
have given a more eact account of these opinions in the second book of the
ewish ar.
. ut now the generals of emetrius being willing to recover the defeat
they had had, gathered a greater army together than they had before, and came
against onathan but as soon as he was informed of their coming, he went
suddenly to meet them, to the country of amoth, for he resolved to give them
no opportunity of coming into udea so he pitched his camp at fifty furlongs'
distance from the enemy, and sent out spies to take a view of their camp, and
after what manner they were encamped. hen his spies had given him full
information, and had seized upon some of them by night, who told him the
enemy would soon attack him, he, thus apprised beforehand, provided for his
security, and placed watchmen beyond his camp, and kept all his forces armed
all night and he gave them a charge to be of good courage, and to have their
minds prepared to fight in the night time, if they should be obliged so to do, lest
their enemy's designs should seem concealed from them. ut when emetrius's
commanders were informed that onathan knew what they intended, their
counsels were disordered, and it alarmed them to find that the enemy had
discovered those their intentions nor did they epect to overcome them any
other way, now they had failed in the snares they had laid for them for should
they hazard an open battle, they did not think they should be a match for
onathan's army, so they resolved to fly and having lighted many fires, that
when the enemy saw them they might suppose they were there still, they
retired. hen onathan came to give them battle in the morning in their camp,
and found it deserted, and understood they were fled, he pursued them yet he
could not overtake them, for they had already passed over the river leutherus,
and were out of danger. o when onathan was returned thence, he went into
rabia, and fought against the abateans, and drove away a great deal of their
prey, and took many] captives, and came to amascus, and there sold off what
he had taken. bout the same time it was that imon his brother went over all
udea and alestine, as far as skelon, and fortified the strong holds and when
he had made them very strong, both in the edifices erected, and in the garrisons
placed in them, he came to oppa and when he had taken it, he brought a great
garrison into it, for he heard that the people of oppa were disposed to deliver

up the city to emetrius's generals.
. hen imon and onathan had finished these affairs, they returned to
erusalem, where onathan gathered all the people together, and took counsel to
restore the walls of erusalem, and to rebuild the wall that encompassed the
temple, which had been thrown down, and to make the places adjoining
stronger by very high towers and besides that, to build another wall in the
midst of the city, in order to eclude the market-place from the garrison, which
was in the citadel, and by that means to hinder them from any plenty of
provisions and moreover, to make the fortresses that were in the country much
stronger and more defensible than they were before. nd when these things
were approved of by the multitude, as rightly proposed, onathan himself took
care of the building that belonged to the city, and sent imon away to make the
fortresses in the country more secure than formerly. ut emetrius passed over
uphrates], and came into esopotamia, as desirous to retain that country still,
as well as abylon and when he should have obtained the dominion of the
upper provinces, to lay a foundation for recovering his entire kingdom for
those reeks and acedonians who dwelt there frequently sent ambassadors to
him, and promised, that if he would come to them, they would deliver
themselves up to him, and assist him in fighting against rsaces,    the king of
the arthians. o he was elevated with these hopes, and came hastily to them,
as having resolved, that if he had once overthrown the arthians, and gotten an
army of his own, he would make war against rypho, and eject him out of
yria and the people of that country received him with great alacrity. o he
raised forces, with which he fought against rsaces, and lost all his army, and
was himself taken alive, as we have elsewhere related.
  
        
        
      ,
  
. ow when rypho knew what had befallen emetrius, he was no longer
firm to ntiochus, but contrived by subtlety to kill him, and then take
possession of his kingdom but the fear that he was in of onathan was an
obstacle to this his design, for onathan was a friend to ntiochus, for which
cause he resolved first to take onathan out of the way, and then to set about his
design relating to ntiochus but he judging it best to take him off by deceit and
treachery, came from ntioch to ethshan, which by the reeks is called

cythopolis, at which place onathan met him with forty thousand chosen men,
for he thought that he came to fight him but when he perceived that onathan
was ready to fight, he attempted to gain him by presents and kind treatment,
and gave order to his captains to obey him, and by these means was desirous to
give assurance of his good will, and to take away all suspicions out of his mind,
that so he might make him careless and inconsiderate, and might take him when
he was unguarded. e also advised him to dismiss his army, because there was
no occasion for bringing it with him when there was no war, but all was in
peace. owever, he desired him to retain a few about him, and go with him to
tolemais, for that he would deliver the city up to him, and would bring all the
fortresses that were in the country under his dominion and he told him that he
came with those very designs.
. et did not onathan suspect any thing at all by this his management, but
believed that rypho gave him this advice out of kindness, and with a sincere
design. ccordingly, he dismissed his army, and retained no more than three
thousand of them with him, and left two thousand in alilee and he himself,
with one thousand, came with rypho to tolemais. ut when the people of
tolemais had shut their gates, as it had been commanded by rypho to do, he
took onathan alive, and slew all that were with him. e also sent soldiers
against those two thousand that were left in alilee, in order to destroy them
but those men having heard the report of what had happened to onathan, they
prevented the eecution and before those that were sent by rypho came, they
covered themselves with their armor, and went away out of the country. ow
when those that were sent against them saw that they were ready to fight for
their lives, they gave them no disturbance, but returned back to rypho.
. ut when the people of erusalem heard that onathan was taken, and
that the soldiers who were with him were destroyed, they deplored his sad fate
and there was earnest inquiry made about him by every body, and a great and
just fear fell upon them, and made them sad, lest, now they were deprived of
the courage and conduct of onathan, the nations about them should bear them
ill-will and as they were before quiet on account of onathan they should now
rise up against them, and by making war with them, should force them into the
utmost dangers. nd indeed what they suspected really befell them for when
those nations heard of the death of onathan, they began to make war with the
ews as now destitute of a governor and rypho himself got an army together,
and had intention to go up to udea, and make war against its inhabitants. ut
when imon saw that the people of erusalem were terrified at the
circumstances they were in, he desired to make a speech to them, and thereby
to render them more resolute in opposing rypho when he should come against
them. e then called the people together into the temple, and thence began thus
to encourage them " my countrymen, you are not ignorant that our father,

myself, and my brethren, have ventured to hazard our lives, and that willingly,
for the recovery of your liberty since  have therefore such plenty of eamples
before me, and we of our family have determined with ourselves to die for our
laws, and our ivine worship, there shall no terror be so great as to banish this
resolution from our souls, nor to introduce in its place a love of life, and a
contempt of glory. o you therefore follow me with alacrity whithersoever 
shall lead you, as not destitute of such a captain as is willing to suffer, and to do
the greatest things for you for neither am  better than my brethren that 
should be sparing of my own life, nor so far worse than they as to avoid and
refuse what they thought the most honorable of all things,— mean, to undergo
death for your laws, and for that worship of od which is peculiar to you  will
therefore give such proper demonstrations as will show that  am their own
brother and  am so bold as to epect that  shall avenge their blood upon our
enemies, and deliver you all with your wives and children from the injuries
they intend against you, and, with od's assistance, to preserve your temple
from destruction by them for  see that these nations have you in contempt, as
being without a governor, and that they thence are encouraged to make war
against you."
. y this speech of imon he inspired the multitude with courage and as
they had been before dispirited through fear, they were now raised to a good
hope of better things, insomuch that the whole multitude of the people cried out
all at once that imon should be their leader and that instead of udas and
onathan his brethren, he should have the government over them and they
promised that they would readily obey him in whatsoever he should command
them. o he got together immediately all his own soldiers that were fit for war,
and made haste in rebuilding the walls of the city, and strengthening them by
very high and strong towers, and sent a friend of his, one onathan, the son of
bsalom, to oppa, and gave him order to eject the inhabitants out of the city,
for he was afraid lest they should deliver up the city to rypho but he himself
staid to secure erusalem.
. ut rypho removed from tolemais with a great army, and came into
udea, and brought onathan with him in bonds. imon also met him with his
army at the city dida, which is upon a hill, and beneath it lie the plains of
udea. nd when rypho knew that imon was by the ews made their
governor, he sent to him, and would have imposed upon him by deceit and
treachery, and desired, if he would have his brother onathan released, that he
would send him a hundred talents of silver, and two of onathan's sons as
hostages, that when he shall be released, he may not make udea revolt from
the king for that at present he was kept in bonds on account of the money he
had borrowed of the king, and now owed it to him. ut imon was aware of the
craft of rypho and although he knew that if he gave him the money he should

lose it, and that rypho would not set his brother free and withal should deliver
the sons of onathan to the enemy, yet because he was afraid that he should
have a calumny raised against him among the multitude as the cause of his
brother's death, if he neither gave the money, nor sent onathan's sons, he
gathered his army together, and told them what offers rypho had made and
added this, that the offers were ensnaring and treacherous, and yet that it was
more eligible to send the money and onathan's sons, than to be liable to the
imputation of not complying with rypho's offers, and thereby refusing to save
his brother. ccordingly, imon sent the sons of onathan and the money but
when rypho had received them, he did not keep his promise, nor set onathan
free, but took his army, and went about all the country, and resolved to go
afterward to erusalem by the way of dumea, while imon went over against
him with his army, and all along pitched his own camp over against his.
. ut when those that were in the citadel had sent to rypho, and besought
him to make haste and come to them, and to send them provisions, he prepared
his cavalry as though he would be at erusalem that very night but so great a
quantity of snow fell in the night, that it covered the roads, and made them so
deep, that there was no passing, especially for the cavalry. his hindered him
from coming to erusalem whereupon rypho removed thence, and came into
oelesyria, and falling vehemently upon the land of ilead, he slew onathan
there and when he had given order for his burial, he returned himself to
ntioch. owever, imon sent some to the city asca to bring away his
brother's bones, and buried them in their own city odin and all the people
made great lamentation over him. imon also erected a very large monument
for his father and his brethren, of white and polished stone, and raised it a great
height, and so as to be seen a long way off, and made cloisters about it, and set
up pillars, which were of one stone apiece a work it was wonderful to see.
oreover, he built seven pyramids also for his parents and his brethren, one for
each of them, which were made very surprising, both for their largeness and
beauty, and which have been preserved to this day and we know that it was
imon who bestowed so much zeal about the burial of onathan, and the
building of these monuments for his relations. ow onathan died when he had
been high priest four years    and had been also the governor of his nation. nd
these were the circumstances that concerned his death.
. ut imon, who was made high priest by the multitude, on the very first
year of his high priesthood set his people free from their slavery under the
acedonians, and permitted them to pay tribute to them no longer which
liberty and freedom from tribute they obtained after a hundred and seventy
years    of the kingdom of the ssyrians, which was after eleucus, who was
called icator, got the dominion over yria. ow the affection of the multitude
towards imon was so great, that in their contracts one with another, and in

their public records, they wrote, "in the first year of imon the benefactor and
ethnarch of the ews" for under him they were very happy, and overcame the
enemies that were round about them for imon overthrew the city azara, and
oppa, and amnia. e also took the citadel of erusalem by siege, and cast it
down to the ground, that it might not be any more a place of refuge to their
enemies when they took it, to do them a mischief, as it had been till now. nd
when he had done this, he thought it their best way, and most for their
advantage, to level the very mountain itself upon which the citadel happened to
stand, that so the temple might be higher than it. nd indeed, when he had
called the multitude to an assembly, he persuaded them to have it so
demolished, and this by putting them in mind what miseries they had suffered
by its garrison and the ewish deserters, and what miseries they might hereafter
suffer in case any foreigner should obtain the kingdom, and put a garrison into
that citadel. his speech induced the multitude to a compliance, because he
ehorted them to do nothing but what was for their own good so they all set
themselves to the work, and leveled the mountain, and in that work spent both
day and night without any intermission, which cost them three whole years
before it was removed, and brought to an entire level with the plain of the rest
of the city. fter which the temple was the highest of all the buildings, now the
citadel, as well as the mountain whereon it stood, were demolished. nd these
actions were thus performed under imon.

.      ow a little while after emetrius had been carried into captivity, rypho
his governor destroyed ntiochus,    the son of leander, who was also called
he od,    and this when he had reigned four years, though he gave it out that
he died under the hands of the surgeons. e then sent his friends, and those that
were most intimate with him, to the soldiers, and promised that he would give
them a great deal of money if they would make him king. e intimated to them
that emetrius was made a captive by the arthians and that emetrius's
brother ntiochus, if he came to be king, would do them a great deal of
mischief, in way of revenge for their revolting from his brother. o the soldiers,
in epectation of the wealth they should get by bestowing the kingdom on

rypho, made him their ruler. owever, when rypho had gained the
management of affairs, he demonstrated his disposition to be wicked for while
he was a private person, he cultivated familiarity with the multitude, and
pretended to great moderation, and so drew them on artfully to whatsoever he
pleased but when he had once taken the kingdom, he laid aside any further
dissimulation, and was the true rypho which behavior made his enemies
superior to him for the soldiery hated him, and revolted from him to leopatra,
the wife of emetrius, who was then shut up in eleucia with her children. ut
as ntiochus, the brother of emetrius who was called oter, was not admitted
by any of the cities on account of rypho, leopatra sent to him, and invited
him to marry her, and to take the kingdom. he reasons why she made this
invitation were these that her friends persuaded her to it, and that she was
afraid for herself, in case some of the people of eleucia should deliver up the
city to rypho.
. s ntiochus was now come to eleucia, and his forces increased every
day, he marched to fight rypho and having beaten him in the battle, he ejected
him out of the pper yria into hoenicia, and pursued him thither, and
besieged him in ora which was a fortress hard to be taken, whither he had
fled. e also sent ambassadors to imon the ewish high priest, about a league
of friendship and mutual assistance who readily accepted of the invitation, and
sent to ntiochus great sums of money and provisions for those that besieged
ora, and thereby supplied them very plentifully, so that for a little while he
was looked upon as one of his most intimate friends but still rypho fled from
ora to pamia, where he was taken during the siege, and put to death, when
he had reigned three years.
. owever, ntiochus forgot the kind assistance that imon had afforded
him in his necessity, by reason of his covetous and wicked disposition, and
committed an army of soldiers to his friend endebeus, and sent him at once to
ravage udea, and to seize imon. hen imon heard of ntiochus's breaking
his league with him, although he were now in years, yet, provoked with the
unjust treatment he had met with from ntiochus, and taking a resolution
brisker than his age could well bear, he went like a young man to act as general
of his army. e also sent his sons before among the most hardy of his soldiers,
and he himself marched on with his army another way, and laid many of his
men in ambushes in the narrow valleys between the mountains nor did he fail
of success in any one of his attempts, but was too hard for his enemies in every
one of them. o he led the rest of his life in peace, and did also himself make a
league with the omans.
. ow he was the ruler of the ews in all eight years but at a feast came to
his end. t was caused by the treachery of his son-in-law tolemy, who caught
also his wife, and two of his sons, and kept them in bonds. e also sent some to

kill ohn the third son, whose name was yrcanus but the young man
perceiving them coming, he avoided the danger he was in from them,    and
made haste into the city erusalem], as relying on the good-will of the
multitude, because of the benefits they had received from his father, and
because of the hatred the same multitude bare to tolemy so that when
tolemy was endeavoring to enter the city by another gate, they drove him
away, as having already admitted yrcanus.

. o tolemy retired to one of the fortresses that was above ericho, which
was called agon. ut yrcanus having taken the high priesthood that had
been his father's before, and in the first place propitiated od by sacrifices, he
then made an epedition against tolemy and when he made his attacks upon
the place, in other points he was too hard for him, but was rendered weaker
than he, by the commiseration he had for his mother and brethren, and by that
only for tolemy brought them upon the wall, and tormented them in the sight
of all, and threatened that he would throw them down headlong, unless
yrcanus would leave off the siege. nd as he thought that so far as he relaed
as to the siege and taking of the place, so much favor did he show to those that
were dearest to him by preventing their misery, his zeal about it was cooled.
owever, his mother spread out her hands, and begged of him that he would
not grow remiss on her account, but indulge his indignation so much the more,
and that he would do his utmost to take the place quickly, in order to get their
enemy under his power, and then to avenge upon him what he had done to
those that were dearest to himself for that death would be to her sweet, though
with torment, if that enemy of theirs might but be brought to punishment for his
wicked dealings to them. ow when his mother said so, he resolved to take the
fortress immediately but when he saw her beaten, and torn to pieces, his
courage failed him, and he could not but sympathize with what his mother
suffered, and was thereby overcome. nd as the siege was drawn out into
length by this means, that year on which the ews used to rest came on for the
ews observe this rest every seventh year, as they do every seventh day so that
tolemy being for this cause released from the war,    he slew the brethren of
yrcanus, and his mother and when he had so done, he fled to eno, who was

called otylas, who was then the tyrant of the city hiladelphia.
. ut ntiochus, being very uneasy at the miseries that imon had brought
upon him, he invaded udea in the fourth years' of his reign, and the first year
of the principality of yrcanus, in the hundred and sity-second lympiad. 
 nd when he had burnt the country, he shut up yrcanus in the city, which he
encompassed round with seven encampments but did just nothing at the first,
because of the strength of the walls, and because of the valor of the besieged,
although they were once in want of water, which yet they were delivered from
by a large shower of rain, which fell at the setting of the leiades.    owever,
about the north part of the wall, where it happened the city was upon a level
with the outward ground, the king raised a hundred towers of three stories high,
and placed bodies of soldiers upon them and as he made his attacks every day,
he cut a double ditch, deep and broad, and confined the inhabitants within it as
within a wall but the besieged contrived to make frequent sallies out and if the
enemy were not any where upon their guard, they fell upon them, and did them
a great deal of mischief and if they perceived them, they then retired into the
city with ease. ut because yrcanus discerned the inconvenience of so great a
number of men in the city, while the provisions were the sooner spent by them,
and yet, as is natural to suppose, those great numbers did nothing, he separated
the useless part, and ecluded them out of the city, and retained that part only
which were in the flower of their age, and fit for war. owever, ntiochus
would not let those that were ecluded go away, who therefore wandering
about between the walls, and consuming away by famine, died miserably but
when the feast of tabernacles was at hand, those that were within commiserated
their condition, and received them in again. nd when yrcanus sent to
ntiochus, and desired there might be a truce for seven days, because of the
festival, be gave way to this piety towards od, and made that truce
accordingly. nd besides that, he sent in a magnificent sacrifice, bulls with
their horns gilded,    with all sorts of sweet spices, and with cups of gold and
silver. o those that were at the gates received the sacrifices from those that
brought them, and led them to the temple, ntiochus the meanwhile feasting his
army, which was a quite different conduct from ntiochus piphanes, who,
when he had taken the city, offered swine upon the altar, and sprinkled the
temple with the broth of their flesh, in order to violate the laws of the ews, and
the religion they derived from their forefathers for which reason our nation
made war with him, and would never be reconciled to him but for this
ntiochus, all men called him ntiochus the ious, for the great zeal he had
about religion.
. ccordingly, yrcanus took this moderation of his kindly and when he
understood how religious he was towards the eity, he sent an embassage to
him, and desired that he would restore the settlements they received from their

forefathers. o he rejected the counsel of those that would have him utterly
destroy the nation    by reason of their way of living, which was to others
unsociable, and did not regard what they said. ut being persuaded that all they
did was out of a religious mind, he answered the ambassadors, that if the
besieged would deliver up their arms, and pay tribute for oppa, and the other
cities which bordered upon udea, and admit a garrison of his, on these terms
he would make war against them no longer. ut the ews, although they were
content with the other conditions, did not agree to admit the garrison, because
they could not associate with other people, nor converse with them yet were
they willing, instead of the admission of the garrison, to give him hostages, and
five hundred talents of silver of which they paid down three hundred, and sent
the hostages immediately, which king ntiochus accepted. ne of those
hostages was yrcanus's brother. ut still he broke down the fortifications that
encompassed the city. nd upon these conditions ntiochus broke up the siege,
and departed.
. ut yrcanus opened the sepulchre of avid, who ecelled all other kings
in riches, and took out of it three thousand talents. e was also the first of the
ews that, relying on this wealth, maintained foreign troops. here was also a
league of friendship and mutual assistance made between them upon which
yrcanus admitted him into the city, and furnished him with whatsoever his
army wanted in great plenty, and with great generosity, and marched along with
him when he made an epedition against the arthians of which icolaus of
amascus is a witness for us who in his history writes thus "hen ntiochus
had erected a trophy at the river ycus, upon his conquest of ndates, the
general of the arthians, he staid there two days. t was the desire of yrcanus
the ew, because it was such a festival derived to them from their forefathers,
whereon the law of the ews did not allow them to travel." nd truly he did not
speak falsely in saying so for that festival, which we call entecost, did then
fall out to be the net day to the abbath. or is it lawful for us to journey,
either on the abbath day, or on a festival day.    ut when ntiochus joined
battle with rsaces, the king of arthia, he lost a great part of his army, and was
himself slain and his brother emetrius succeeded in the kingdom of yria, by
the permission of rsaces, who freed him from his captivity at the same time
that ntiochus attacked arthia, as we have formerly related elsewhere.

. ut when yrcanus heard of the death of ntiochus, he presently made an
epedition against the cities of yria, hoping to find them destitute of fighting
men, and of such as were able to defend them. owever, it was not till the sith
month that he took edaba, and that not without the greatest distress of his
army. fter this he took amega, and the neighboring places and besides these,
hechem and erizzim, and the nation of the utheans, who dwelt at the
temple which resembled that temple which was at erusalem, and which
leander permitted anballat, the general of his army, to build for the sake of
anasseh, who was son-in-law to adua the high priest, as we have formerly
related which temple was now deserted two hundred years after it was built.
yrcanus took also ora and arissa, cities of dumea, and subdued all the
dumeans and permitted them to stay in that country, if they would circumcise
their genitals, and make use of the laws of the ews and they were so desirous
of living in the country of their forefathers, that they submitted to the use of
circumcision,    and of the rest of the ewish ways of living at which time
therefore this befell them, that they were hereafter no other than ews.
. ut yrcanus the high priest was desirous to renew that league of
friendship they had with the omans. ccordingly, he sent an embassage to
them and when the senate had received their epistle, they made a league of
friendship with them, after the manner following "anius, the son of arcus,
the praetor, gathered the senate together on the eighth day before the des of
ebruary, in the senate-house, when ucius anlius, the son of ucius, of the
entine tribe, and aius empronius, the son of aius, of the alernian tribe,
were present. he occasion was, that the ambassadors sent by the people of the
ews,    imon, the son of ositheus, and pollonius, the son of leander, and
iodorus, the son of ason, who were good and virtuous men, had somewhat to
propose about that league of friendship and mutual assistance which subsisted
between them and the omans, and about other public affairs, who desired that
oppa, and the havens, and azara, and the springs of ordan], and the several
other cities and countries of theirs, which ntiochus had taken from them in the
war, contrary to the decree of the senate, might be restored to them and that it
might not be lawful for the king's troops to pass through their country, and the
countries of those that are subject to them and that what attempts ntiochus
had made during that war, without the decree of the senate, might be made
void and that they would send ambassadors, who should take care that
restitution be made them of what ntiochus had taken from them, and that they
should make an estimate of the country that had been laid waste in the war and
that they would grant them letters of protection to the kings and free people, in
order to their quiet return home. t was therefore decreed, as to these points, to
renew their league of friendship and mutual assistance with these good men,

and who were sent by a good and a friendly people." ut as to the letters
desired, their answer was, that the senate would consult about that matter when
their own affairs would give them leave and that they would endeavor, for the
time to come, that no like injury should be done to them and that their praetor
anius should give them money out of the public treasury to bear their
epenses home. nd thus did anius dismiss the ewish ambassadors, and gave
them money out of the public treasury and gave the decree of the senate to
those that were to conduct them, and to take care that they should return home
in safety.
. nd thus stood the affairs of yrcanus the high priest. ut as for king
emetrius, who had a mind to make war against yrcanus, there was no
opportunity nor room for it, while both the yrians and the soldiers bare ill-will
to him, because he was an ill man. ut when they had sent ambassadors to
tolemy, who was called hyscon, that he would send them one of the family at
eleucus, in order to take the kingdom, and he had sent them leander, who
was called ebina, with an army, and there had been a battle between them,
emetrius was beaten in the fight, and fled to leopatra his wife, to tolemais
but his wife would not receive him. e went thence to yre, and was there
caught and when he had suffered much from his enemies before his death, he
was slain by them. o leander took the kingdom, and made a league with
yrcanus, who yet, when he afterward fought with ntiochus the son of
emetrius, who was called rypus, he was also beaten in the fight, and slain.
