. hen ntiochus had taken the kingdom, he was afraid to make war against
udea, because he heard that his brother by the same mother, who was also
called ntiochus, was raising an army against him out of yzicum so he staid
in his own land, and resolved to prepare himself for the attack he epected from
his brother, who was called yzicenus, because he had been brought up in that
city. e was the son of ntiochus that was called oter, who died in arthia. e
was the brother of emetrius, the father of rypus for it had so happened, that
one and the same leopatra was married to two who were brethren, as we have
related elsewhere. ut ntiochus yzicenus coming into yria, continued many

years at war with his brother. ow yrcanus lived all this while in peace for
after the death of ntiochus, he revolted from the acedonians,    nor did he
any longer pay them the least regard, either as their subject or their friend but
his affairs were in a very improving and flourishing condition in the times of
leander ebina, and especially under these brethren, for the war which they
had with one another gave yrcanus the opportunity of enjoying himself in
udea quietly, insomuch that he got an immense quantity of money. owever,
when ntiochus yzicenus distressed his land, he then openly showed what he
meant. nd when he saw that ntiochus was destitute of gyptian auiliaries,
and that both he and his brother were in an ill condition in the struggles they
had one with another, he despised them both.
. o he made an epedition against amaria which was a very strong city
of whose present name ebaste, and its rebuilding by erod, we shall speak at a
proper time but he made his attack against it, and besieged it with a great deal
of pains for he was greatly displeased with the amaritans for the injuries they
had done to the people of arissa, a colony of the ews, and confederate with
them, and this in compliance to the kings of yria. hen he had therefore
drawn a ditch, and built a double wall round the city, which was fourscore
furlongs long, he set his sons ntigonus and ristobulus over the siege which
brought the amaritans to that great distress by famine, that they were forced to
eat what used not to be eaten, and to call for ntiochus yzicenus to help them,
who came readily to their assistance, but was beaten by ristobulus and when
he was pursued as far as cythopolis by the two brethren, he got away. o they
returned to amaria, and shut them again within the wall, till they were forced
to send for the same ntiochus a second time to help them, who procured about
si thousand men from tolemy athyrus, which were sent them without his
mother's consent, who had then in a manner turned him out of his government.
ith these gyptians ntiochus did at first overrun and ravage the country of
yrcanus after the manner of a robber, for he durst not meet him in the face to
fight with him, as not having an army sufficient for that purpose, but only from
this supposal, that by thus harassing his land he should force yrcanus to raise
the siege of amaria but because he fell into snares, and lost many of his
soldiers therein, he went away to ripoli, and committed the prosecution of the
war against the ews to allimander and picrates.
. ut as to allimander, he attacked the enemy too rashly, and was put to
flight, and destroyed immediately and as to picrates, he was such a lover of
money, that he openly betrayed cythopolis, and other places near it, to the
ews, but was not able to make them raise the siege of amaria. nd when
yrcanus had taken that city, which was not done till after a year's siege, he
was not contented with doing that only, but he demolished it entirely, and
brought rivulets to it to drown it, for he dug such hollows as might let the water

run under it nay, he took away the very marks that there had ever been such a
city there. ow a very surprising thing is related of this high priest yrcanus,
how od came to discourse with him for they say that on the very same day on
which his sons fought with ntiochus yzicenus, he was alone in the temple, as
high priest, offering incense, and heard a voice, that his sons had just then
overcome ntiochus. nd this he openly declared before all the multitude upon
his coming out of the temple and it accordingly proved true and in this posture
were the affairs of yrcanus.
. ow it happened at this time, that not only those ews who were at
erusalem and in udea were in prosperity, but also those of them that were at
leandria, and in gypt and yprus for leopatra the queen was at variance
with her son tolemy, who was called athyrus, and appointed for her generals
helcias and nanias, the sons of that nias who built the temple in the
prefecture of eliopolis, like to that at erusalem, as we have elsewhere related.
leopatra intrusted these men with her army, and did nothing without their
advice, as trabo of appadocia attests, when he saith thus "ow the greater
part, both those that came to yprus with us, and those that were sent afterward
thither, revolted to tolemy immediately only those that were called nias's
party, being ews, continued faithful, because their countrymen helcias and
nanias were in chief favor with the queen." hese are the words of trabo.
. owever, this prosperous state of affairs moved the ews to envy
yrcanus but they that were the worst disposed to him were the harisees, 
 who were one of the sects of the ews, as we have informed you already. hese
have so great a power over the multitude, that when they say any thing against
the king, or against the high priest, they are presently believed. ow yrcanus
was a disciple of theirs, and greatly beloved by them. nd when he once invited
them to a feast, and entertained them very kindly, when he saw them in a good
humor, he began to say to them, that they knew he was desirous to be a
righteous man, and to do all things whereby he might please od, which was
the profession of the harisees also. owever, he desired, that if they observed
him offending in any point, and going out of the right way, they would call him
back and correct him. n which occasion they attested to his being entirely
virtuous with which commendation he was well pleased. ut still there was
one of his guests there, whose name was leazar,    a man of an ill temper, and
delighting in seditious practices. his man said, "ince thou desirest to know
the truth, if thou wilt be righteous in earnest, lay down the high priesthood, and
content thyself with the civil government of the people." nd when he desired
to know for what cause he ought to lay down the high priesthood, the other
replied, "e have heard it from old men, that thy mother had been a captive
under the reign of ntiochus piphanes." his story was false, and yrcanus
was provoked against him and all the harisees had a very great indignation

against him.
. ow there was one onathan, a very great friend of yrcanus's, but of the
sect of the adducees, whose notions are quite contrary to those of the
harisees. e told yrcanus that leazar had cast such a reproach upon him,
according to the common sentiments of all the harisees, and that this would be
made manifest if he would but ask them the question, what punishment they
thought this man deserved for that he might depend upon it, that the reproach
was not laid on him with their approbation, if they were for punishing him as
his crime deserved. o the harisees made answer, that he deserved stripes and
bonds, but that it did not seem right to punish reproaches with death. nd
indeed the harisees, even upon other occasions, are not apt to be severe in
punishments. t this gentle sentence, yrcanus was very angry, and thought
that this man reproached him by their approbation. t was this onathan who
chiefly irritated him, and influenced him so far, that he made him leave the
party of the harisees, and abolish the decrees they had imposed on the people,
and to punish those that observed them. rom this source arose that hatred
which he and his sons met with from the multitude but of these matters we
shall speak hereafter. hat  would now eplain is this, that the harisees have
delivered to the people a great many observances by succession from their
fathers, which are not written in the laws of oses and for that reason it is that
the adducees reject them, and say that we are to esteem those observances to
be obligatory which are in the written word, but are not to observe what are
derived from the tradition of our forefathers. nd concerning these things it is
that great disputes and differences have arisen among them, while the
adducees are able to persuade none but the rich, and have not the populace
obsequious to them, but the harisees have the multitude on their side. ut
about these two sects, and that of the ssens,  have treated accurately in the
second book of ewish affairs.
. ut when yrcanus had put an end to this sedition, he after that lived
happily, and administered the government in the best manner for thirty-one
years, and then died,    leaving behind him five sons. e was esteemed by od
worthy of three of the greatest privileges,—the government of his nation, the
dignity of the high priesthood, and prophecy for od was with him, and
enabled him to know futurities and to foretell this in particular, that, as to his
two eldest sons, he foretold that they would not long continue in the
government of public affairs whose unhappy catastrophe will be worth our
description, that we may thence learn how very much they were inferior to their
father's happiness.

. ow when their father yrcanus was dead, the eldest son ristobulus,
intending to change the government into a kingdom, for so he resolved to do,
first of all put a diadem on his head, four hundred eighty and one years and
three months after the people had been delivered from the abylonish slavery,
and were returned to their own country again. his ristobulus loved his net
brother ntigonus, and treated him as his equal but the others he held in bonds.
e also cast his mother into prison, because she disputed the government with
him for yrcanus had left her to be mistress of all. e also proceeded to that
degree of barbarity, as to kill her in prison with hunger nay, he was alienated
from his brother ntigonus by calumnies, and added him to the rest whom he
slew yet he seemed to have an affection for him, and made him above the rest
a partner with him in the kingdom. hose calumnies he at first did not give
credit to, partly because he loved him, and so did not give heed to what was
said against him, and partly because he thought the reproaches were derived
from the envy of the relaters. ut when ntigonus was once returned from the
army, and that feast was then at hand when they make tabernacles to the honor
of od], it happened that ristobulus was fallen sick, and that ntigonus went
up most splendidly adorned, and with his soldiers about him in their armor, to
the temple to celebrate the feast, and to put up many prayers for the recovery of
his brother, when some wicked persons, who had a great mind to raise a
difference between the brethren, made use of this opportunity of the pompous
appearance of ntigonus, and of the great actions which he had done, and went
to the king, and spitefully aggravated the pompous show of his at the feast, and
pretended that all these circumstances were not like those of a private person
that these actions were indications of an affectation of royal authority and that
his coming with a strong body of men must be with an intention to kill him and
that his way of reasoning was this that it was a silly thing in him, while it was
in his power to reign himself, to look upon it as a great favor that he was
honored with a lower dignity by his brother.
. ristobulus yielded to these imputations, but took care both that his
brother should not suspect him, and that he himself might not run the hazard of
his own safety so he ordered his guards to lie in a certain place that was under
ground, and dark (he himself then lying sick in the tower which was called
ntonia) and he commanded them, that in case ntigonus came in to him
unarmed, they should not touch any body, but if armed, they should kill him
yet did he send to ntigonus, and desired that he would come unarmed but the

queen, and those that joined with her in the plot against ntigonus, persuaded
the messenger to tell him the direct contrary how his brother had heard that he
had made himself a fine suit of armor for war, and desired him to come to him
in that armor, that he might see how fine it was. o ntigonus suspecting no
treachery, but depending on the good-will of his brother, came to ristobulus
armed, as he used to be, with his entire armor, in order to show it to him but
when he was come to a place which was called trato's ower, where the
passage happened to be eceeding dark, the guards slew him which death of
his demonstrates that nothing is stronger than envy and calumny, and that
nothing does more certainly divide the good-will and natural affections of men
than those passions. ut here one may take occasion to wonder at one udas,
who was of the sect of the ssens, and who never missed the truth in his
predictions for this man, when he saw ntigonus passing by the temple, cried
out to his companions and friends, who abode with him as his scholars, in order
to learn the art of foretelling things to come,    "hat it was good for him to die
now, since he had spoken falsely about ntigonus, who is still alive, and  see
him passing by, although he had foretold he should die at the place called
trato's ower that very day, while yet the place is si hundred furlongs off,
where he had foretold he should be slain and still this day is a great part of it
already past, so that he was in danger of proving a false prophet." s he was
saying this, and that in a melancholy mood, the news came that ntigonus was
slain in a place under ground, which itself was called also trato's ower, or of
the same name with that esarea which is seated at the sea. his event put the
prophet into a great disorder.
. ut ristobulus repented immediately of this slaughter of his brother on
which account his disease increased upon him, and he was disturbed in his
mind, upon the guilt of such wickedness, insomuch that his entrails were
corrupted by his intolerable pain, and he vomited blood at which time one of
the servants that attended upon him, and was carrying his blood away, did, by
ivine rovidence, as  cannot but suppose, slip down, and shed part of his
blood at the very place where there were spots of ntigonus's blood, there slain,
still remaining and when there was a cry made by the spectators, as if the
servant had on purpose shed the blood on that place, ristobulus heard it, and
inquired what the matter was and as they did not answer him, he was the more
earnest to know what it was, it being natural to men to suspect that what is thus
concealed is very bad so upon his threatening, and forcing them by terrors to
speak, they at length told him the truth whereupon he shed many tears, in that
disorder of mind which arose from his consciousness of what he had done, and
gave a deep groan, and said, " am not therefore,  perceive, to be concealed
from od, in the impious and horrid crimes  have been guilty of but a sudden
punishment is coming upon me for the shedding the blood of my relations. nd

now,  thou most impudent body of mine, how long wilt thou retain a soul that
ought to die, in order to appease the ghosts of my brother and my mother hy
dost thou not give it all up at once nd why do  deliver up my blood drop by
drop to those whom  have so wickedly murdered" n saying which last words
he died, having reigned a year. e was called a lover of the recians and had
conferred many benefits on his own country, and made war against turea, and
added a great part of it to udea, and compelled the inhabitants, if they would
continue in that country, to be circumcised, and to live according to the ewish
laws. e was naturally a man of candor, and of great modesty, as trabo bears
witness, in the name of imagenes who says thus "his man was a person of
candor, and very serviceable to the ews for he added a country to them, and
obtained a part of the nation of the tureans for them, and bound them to them
by the bond of the circumcision of their genitals."

. hen ristobulus was dead, his wife alome, who, by the reeks, was
called leandra, let his brethren out of prison, (for ristobulus had kept them
in bonds, as we have said already,) and made leander anneus king, who was
the superior in age and in moderation. his child happened to be hated by his
father as soon as he was born, and could never be permitted to come into his
father's sight till he died. he occasion of which hatred is thus reported when
yrcanus chiefly loved the two eldest of his sons, ntigonus and ristobulus,
od appeared to him in his sleep, of whom he inquired which of his sons
should be his successor. pon od's representing to him the countenance of
leander, he was grieved that he was to be the heir of all his goods, and
suffered him to be brought up in alilee.    owever, od did not deceive
yrcanus for after the death of ristobulus, he certainly took the kingdom and
one of his brethren, who affected the kingdom, he slew and the other, who
chose to live a private and quiet life, he had in esteem.
. hen leander anneus had settled the government in the manner that
he judged best, he made an epedition against tolemais and having overcome

the men in battle, he shut them up in the city, and sat round about it, and
besieged it for of the maritime cities there remained only tolemais and aza
to be conquered, besides trato's ower and ora, which were held by the
tyrant oilus. ow while ntiochus hilometor, and ntiochus who was called
yzicenus, were making war one against another, and destroying one another's
armies, the people of tolemais could have no assistance from them but when
they were distressed with this siege, oilus, who possessed trato's ower and
ora, and maintained a legion of soldiers, and, on occasion of the contest
between the kings, affected tyranny himself, came and brought some small
assistance to the people of tolemais nor indeed had the kings such a
friendship for them, as that they should hope for any advantage from them.
oth those kings were in the case of wrestlers, who finding themselves
deficient in strength, and yet being ashamed to yield, put off the fight by
laziness, and by lying still as long as they can. he only hope they had
remaining was from the kings of gypt, and from tolemy athyrus, who now
held yprus, and who came to yprus when he was driven from the
government of gypt by leopatra his mother. o the people of tolemais sent
to this tolemy athyrus, and desired him to come as a confederate, to deliver
them, now they were in such danger, out of the hands of leander. nd as the
ambassadors gave him hopes, that if he would pass over into yria, he would
have the people of aza on the side of those of tolemais as also they said,
that oilus, and besides these the idonians, and many others, would assist
them so he was elevated at this, and got his fleet ready as soon as possible.
. ut in this interval emetrius, one that was of abilities to persuade men
to do as he would have them, and a leader of the populace, made those of
tolemais change their opinions and said to them, that it was better to run the
hazard of being subject to the ews, than to admit of evident slavery by
delivering themselves up to a master and besides that, to have not only a war at
present, but to epect a much greater war from gypt for that leopatra would
not overlook an army raised by tolemy for himself out of the neighborhood,
but would come against them with a great army of her own, and this because
she was laboring to eject her son out of yprus also that as for tolemy, if he
fail of his hopes, he can still retire to yprus, but that they will be left in the
greatest danger possible. ow tolemy, although he had heard of the change
that was made in the people of tolemais, yet did he still go on with his voyage,
and came to the country called ycamine, and there set his army on shore. his
army of his, in the whole horse and foot together, were about thirty thousand,
with which he marched near to tolemais, and there pitched his camp. ut
when the people of tolemais neither received his ambassadors, nor would hear
what they had to say, he was under a very great concern.
. ut when oilus and the people of aza came to him, and desired his

assistance, because their country was laid waste by the ews, and by leander,
leander raised the siege, for fear of tolemy and when he had drawn off his
army into his own country, he used a stratagem afterwards, by privately inviting
leopatra to come against tolemy, but publicly pretending to desire a league
of friendship and mutual assistance with him and promising to give him four
hundred talents of silver, he desired that, by way of requital, he would take off
oilus the tyrant, and give his country to the ews. nd then indeed tolemy,
with pleasure, made such a league of friendship with leander, and subdued
oilus but when he afterwards heard that he had privily sent to leopatra his
mother, he broke the league with him, which yet he had confirmed with an
oath, and fell upon him, and besieged tolemais, because it would not receive
him. owever, leaving his generals, with some part of his forces, to go on with
the siege, he went himself immediately with the rest to lay udea waste and
when leander understood this to be tolemy's intention, he also got together
about fifty thousand soldiers out of his own country nay, as some writers have
said, eighty thousand.    e then took his army, and went to meet tolemy but
tolemy fell upon sochis, a city of alilee, and took it by force on the
abbath day, and there he took about ten thousand slaves, and a great deal of
other prey.
. e then tried to take epphoris, which was a city not far from that which
was destroyed, but lost many of his men yet did he then go to fight with
leander. leander met him at the river ordan, near a certain place called
aphoth, not far from the river ordan,] and pitched his camp near to the
enemy. e had however eight thousand in the first rank, which he styled
ecatontomachi, having shields of brass. hose in the first rank of tolemy's
soldiers also had shields covered with brass. ut tolemy's soldiers in other
respects were inferior to those of leander, and therefore were more fearful of
running hazards but hilostephanus, the camp-master, put great courage into
them, and ordered them to pass the river, which was between their camps. or

enemy had once gotten the river on their back, that he should the easier take
them prisoners, when they could not flee out of the battle in the beginning of

alike, and a great slaughter was made by both the armies but leander was
superior, till hilostephanus opportunely brought up the auiliaries, to help
those that were giving way but as there were no auiliaries to afford help to
that part of the ews that gave way, it fell out that they fled, and those near
them did no assist them, but fled along with them. owever, tolemy's soldiers
acted quite otherwise for they followed the ews, and killed them, till at length
those that slew them pursued after them when they had made them all run
away, and slew them so long, that their weapons of iron were blunted, and their

hands quite tired with the slaughter for the report was, that thirty thousand men
were then slain. imagenes says they were fifty thousand. s for the rest, they
were part of them taken captives, and the other part ran away to their own
country.
. fter this victory, tolemy overran all the country and when night came
on, he abode in certain villages of udea, which when he found full of women
and children, he commanded his soldiers to strangle them, and to cut them in
pieces, and then to cast them into boiling caldrons, and then to devour their
limbs as sacrifices. his commandment was given, that such as fled from the
battle, and came to them, might suppose their enemies were cannibals, and eat
men's flesh, and might on that account be still more terrified at them upon such
a sight. nd both trabo and icolaus of amascus] affirm, that they used
these people after this manner, as  have already related. tolemy also took
tolemais by force, as we have declared elsewhere.

. hen leopatra saw that her son was grown great, and laid udea waste,
without disturbance, and had gotten the city of aza under his power, she
resolved no longer to overlook what he did, when he was almost at her gates
and she concluded, that now he was so much stronger than before, he would be
very desirous of the dominion over the gyptians but she immediately
marched against him, with a fleet at sea and an army of foot on land, and made
helcias and nanias the ews generals of her whole army, while she sent the
greatest part of her riches, her grandchildren, and her testament, to the people
of os.    leopatra also ordered her son leander to sail with a great fleet to
hoenicia and when that country had revolted, she came to tolemais and
because the people of tolemais did not receive her, she besieged the city but
tolemy went out of yria, and made haste unto gypt, supposing that he
should find it destitute of an army, and soon take it, though he failed of his
hopes. t this time helcias, one of leopatra's generals, happened to die in
oelesyria, as he was in pursuit of tolemy.

. hen leopatra heard of her son's attempt, and that his gyptian
epedition did not succeed according to his epectations, she sent thither part
of her army, and drove him out of that country so when he was returned out of
gypt again, he abode during the winter at aza, in which time leopatra took
the garrison that was in tolemais by siege, as well as the city and when
leander came to her, he gave her presents, and such marks of respect as were
but proper, since under the miseries he endured by tolemy he had no other
refuge but her. ow there were some of her friends who persuaded her to seize
leander, and to overrun and take possession of the country, and not to sit still
and see such a multitude of brave ews subject to one man. ut nanias's
counsel was contrary to theirs, who said that she would do an unjust action if
she deprived a man that was her ally of that authority which belonged to him,
and this a man who is related to us "for (said he)  would not have thee
ignorant of this, that what in justice thou dost to him will make all us that are
ews to be thy enemies." his desire of nanias leopatra complied with, and
did no injury to leander, but made a league of mutual assistance with him at
cythopolis, a city of oelesyria.
. o when leander was delivered from the fear he was in of tolemy, he
presently made an epedition against oelesyria. e also took adara, after a
siege of ten months. e took also mathus, a very strong fortress belonging to
the inhabitants above ordan, where heodorus, the son of eno, had his chief
treasure, and what he esteemed most precious. his eno fell unepectedly
upon the ews, and slew ten thousand of them, and seized upon leander's
baggage. et did not this misfortune terrify leander but he made an
epedition upon the maritime parts of the country, aphia and nthedon, (the
name of which king erod afterwards changed to grippias,) and took even
that by force. ut when leander saw that tolemy was retired from aza to
yprus, and his mother leopatra was returned to gypt, he grew angry at the
people of aza, because they had invited tolemy to assist them, and besieged
their city, and ravaged their country. ut as pollodotus, the general of the
army of aza, fell upon the camp of the ews by night, with two thousand
foreign and ten thousand of his own forces, while the night lasted, those of
aza prevailed, because the enemy was made to believe that it was tolemy
who attacked them but when day was come on, and that mistake was
corrected, and the ews knew the truth of the matter, they came back again, and
fell upon those of aza, and slew of them about a thousand. ut as those of
aza stoutly resisted them, and would not yield for either their want of any
thing, nor for the great multitude that were slain, (for they would rather suffer
any hardship whatever than come under the power of their enemies,) retas,
king of the rabians, a person then very illustrious, encouraged them to go on
with alacrity, and promised them that he would come to their assistance but it

happened that before he came pollodotus was slain for his brother
ysimachus envying him for the great reputation he had gained among the
citizens, slew him, and got the army together, and delivered up the city to
leander, who, when he came in at first, lay quiet, but afterward set his army
upon the inhabitants of aza, and gave them leave to punish them so some
went one way, and some went another, and slew the inhabitants of aza yet
were not they of cowardly hearts, but opposed those that came to slay them,
and slew as many of the ews and some of them, when they saw themselves
deserted, burnt their own houses, that the enemy might get none of their spoils
nay, some of them, with their own hands, slew their children and their wives,
having no other way but this of avoiding slavery for them but the senators,
who were in all five hundred, fled to pollo's temple, (for this attack happened
to be made as they were sitting,) whom leander slew and when he had
utterly overthrown their city, he returned to erusalem, having spent a year in
that siege.
. bout this very time ntiochus, who was called rypus, died.    is death
was caused by eracleon's treachery, when he had lived forty-five years, and
had reigned twenty-nine.    is son eleucus succeeded him in the kingdom, and
made war with ntiochus, his father's brother, who was called ntiochus
yzicenus, and beat him, and took him prisoner, and slew him. ut after a
while ntiochus,    the son of yzicenus, who was called ius, came to radus,
and put the diadem on his own head, and made war with eleucus, and beat
him, and drove him out of all yria. ut when he fled out of yria, he came to
opsuestia again, and levied money upon them but the people of opsuestia
had indignation at what he did, and burnt down his palace, and slew him,
together with his friends. ut when ntiochus, the son of yzicenus, was king
of yria, ntiochus,    the brother of eleucus, made war upon him, and was
overcome, and destroyed, he and his army. fter him, his brother hilip put on
the diadem, and reigned over some part of yria but tolemy athyrus sent for
his fourth brother emetrius, who was called ucerous, from nidus, and made
him king of amascus. oth these brothers did ntiochus vehemently oppose,
but presently died for when he was come as an auiliary to aodice, queen of
the ileadites,    when she was making war against the arthians, and he was
fighting courageously, he fell, while emetrius and hilip governed yria, as
hath been elsewhere related.
. s to leander, his own people were seditious against him for at a
festival which was then celebrated, when he stood upon the altar, and was
going to sacrifice, the nation rose upon him, and pelted him with citrons,
which they then had in their hands, because] the law of the ews required that
at the feast of tabernacles every one should have branches of the palm tree and
citron tree which thing we have elsewhere related. hey also reviled him, as

derived from a captive,    and so unworthy of his dignity and of sacrificing. t
this he was in a rage, and slew of them about si thousand. e also built a
partition-wall of wood round the altar and the temple, as far as that partition
within which it was only lawful for the priests to enter and by this means he
obstructed the multitude from coming at him. e also maintained foreigners of
isidiae and ilicia for as to the yrians, he was at war with them, and so
made no use of them. e also overcame the rabians, such as the oabites and
ileadites, and made them bring tribute. oreover, he demolished mathus,
while heodorus    durst not fight with him but as he had joined battle with
bedas, king of the rabians, and fell into an ambush in the places that were
rugged and difficult to be traveled over, he was thrown down into a deep valley,
by the multitude of the camels at adara, a village of ilead, and hardly
escaped with his life. rom thence he fled to erusalem, where, besides his
other ill success, the nation insulted him, and he fought against them for si
years, and slew no fewer than fifty thousand of them. nd when he desired that
they would desist from their ill-will to him, they hated him so much the more,
on account of what had already happened and when he had asked them what
he ought to do, they all cried out, that he ought to kill himself. hey also sent to
emetrius ucerus, and desired him to make a league of mutual defence with
them.

. o emetrius came with an army, and took those that invited him, and
pitched his camp near the city hechem upon which leander, with his si
thousand two hundred mercenaries, and about twenty thousand ews, who were
of his party, went against emetrius, who had three thousand horsemen, and
forty thousand footmen. ow there were great endeavors used on both sides,—
emetrius trying to bring off the mercenaries that were with leander,
because they were reeks, and leander trying to bring off the ews that were
with emetrius. owever, when neither of them could persuade them so to do,
they came to a battle, and emetrius was the conqueror in which all
leander's mercenaries were killed, when they had given demonstration of
their fidelity and courage.  great number of emetrius's soldiers were slain

also.
. ow as leander fled to the mountains, si thousand of the ews
hereupon came together from emetrius] to him out of pity at the change of
his fortune upon which emetrius was afraid, and retired out of the country
after which the ews fought against leander, and being beaten, were slain in
great numbers in the several battles which they had and when he had shut up
the most powerful of them in the city ethome, he besieged them therein and
when he had taken the city, and gotten the men into his power, he brought them
to erusalem, and did one of the most barbarous actions in the world to them
for as he was feasting with his concubines, in the sight of all the city, he
ordered about eight hundred of them to be crucified and while they were
living, he ordered the throats of their children and wives to be cut before their
eyes. his was indeed by way of revenge for the injuries they had done him
which punishment yet was of an inhuman nature, though we suppose that he
had been never so much distressed, as indeed he had been, by his wars with
them, for he had by their means come to the last degree of hazard, both of his
life and of his kingdom, while they were not satisfied by themselves only to
fight against him, but introduced foreigners also for the same purpose nay, at
length they reduced him to that degree of necessity, that he was forced to
deliver back to the king of rabia the land of oab and ilead, which he had
subdued, and the places that were in them, that they might not join with them in
the war against him, as they had done ten thousand other things that tended to
affront and reproach him. owever, this barbarity seems to have been without
any necessity, on which account he bare the name of a hracian among the
ews    whereupon the soldiers that had fought against him, being about eight
thousand in number, ran away by night, and continued fugitives all the time that
leander lived who being now freed from any further disturbance from them,
reigned the rest of his time in the utmost tranquillity.
. ut when emetrius was departed out of udea, he went to erea, and
besieged his brother hilip, having with him ten thousand footmen, and a
thousand horsemen. owever trato, the tyrant of erea, the confederate of
hilip, called in izon, the ruler of the rabian tribes, and ithridates ina,
the ruler of the arthians, who coming with a great number of forces, and
besieging emetrius in his encampment, into which they had driven them with
their arrows, they compelled those that were with him by thirst to deliver up
themselves. o they took a great many spoils out of that country, and emetrius
himself, whom they sent to ithridates, who was then king of arthia but as to
those whom they took captives of the people of ntioch, they restored them to
the ntiochians without any reward. ow ithridates, the king of arthia, had
emetrius in great honor, till emetrius ended his life by sickness. o hilip,
presently after the fight was over, came to ntioch, and took it, and reigned

over yria.

. fter this, ntiochus, who was called ionysius,    and was hilip's brother,
aspired to the dominion, and came to amascus, and got the power into his
hands, and there he reigned but as he was making war against the rabians, his
brother hilip heard of it, and came to amascus, where ilesius, who had
been left governor of the citadel, and the amascenes themselves, delivered up
the city to him yet because hilip was become ungrateful to him, and had
bestowed upon him nothing of that in hopes whereof he had received him into
the city, but had a mind to have it believed that it was rather delivered up out of
fear than by the kindness of ilesius, and because he had not rewarded him as
he ought to have done, he became suspected by him, and so he was obliged to
leave amascus again for ilesius caught him marching out into the
ippodrome, and shut him up in it, and kept amascus for ntiochus
ucerus], who hearing how hilip's affairs stood, came back out of rabia. e
also came immediately, and made an epedition against udea, with eight
thousand armed footmen, and eight hundred horsemen. o leander, out of
fear of his coming, dug a deep ditch, beginning at habarzaba, which is now
called ntipatris, to the sea of oppa, on which part only his army could be
brought against him. e also raised a wall, and erected wooden towers, and
intermediate redoubts, for one hundred and fifty furlongs in length, and there
epected the coming of ntiochus but he soon burnt them all, and made his
army pass by that way into rabia. he rabian king retas] at first retreated,
but afterward appeared on the sudden with ten thousand horsemen. ntiochus
gave them the meeting, and fought desperately and indeed when he had gotten
the victory, and was bringing some auiliaries to that part of his army that was
in distress, he was slain. hen ntiochus was fallen, his army fled to the
village ana, where the greatest part of them perished by famine.
. fter him    retas reigned over oelesyria, being called to the
government by those that held amascus, by reason of the hatred they bare to
tolemy enneus. e also made thence an epedition against udea, and beat
leander in battle, near a place called dida yet did he, upon certain

conditions agreed on between them, retire out of udea.
. ut leander marched again to the city ios, and took it and then made
an epedition against ssa, where was the best part of eno's treasures, and
there he encompassed the place with three walls and when he had taken the
city by fighting, he marched to olar and elucia and when he had taken these
cities, he, besides them, took that valley which is called he alley of
ntiochus, as also the fortress of amala. e also accused emetrius, who was
governor of those places, of many crimes, and turned him out and after he had
spent three years in this war, he returned to his own country, when the ews
joyfully received him upon this his good success.
. ow at this time the ews were in possession of the following cities that
had belonged to the yrians, and dumeans, and hoenicians t the sea-side,
trato's ower, pollonia, oppa, amnia, shdod, aza, nthedon, aphia,
and hinocolura in the middle of the country, near to dumea, dora, and
arissa near the country of amaria, ount armel, and ount abor,
cythopolis, and adara of the country of aulonites, eleucia and abala in
the country of oab, eshbon, and edaba, emba, and ronas, elithon,
ara, the valley of the ilices, and ella which last they utterly destroyed,
because its inhabitants would not bear to change their religious rites for those
peculiar to the ews.    he ews also possessed others of the principal cities of
yria, which had been destroyed.
. fter this, king leander, although he fell into a distemper by hard
drinking, and had a quartan ague, which held him three years, yet would not
leave off going out with his army, till he was quite spent with the labors he had
undergone, and died in the bounds of agaba, a fortress beyond ordan. ut
when his queen saw that he was ready to die, and had no longer any hopes of
surviving, she came to him weeping and lamenting, and bewailed herself and
her sons on the desolate condition they should be left in and said to him, "o
whom dost thou thus leave me and my children, who are destitute of all other
supports, and this when thou knowest how much ill-will thy nation bears thee"
ut he gave her the following advice that she need but follow what he would
suggest to her, in order to retain the kingdom securely, with her children that
she should conceal his death from the soldiers till she should have taken that
place after this she should go in triumph, as upon a victory, to erusalem, and
put some of her authority into the hands of the harisees for that they would
commend her for the honor she had done them, and would reconcile the nation
to her for he told her they had great authority among the ews, both to do hurt
to such as they hated, and to bring advantages to those to whom they were
friendly disposed for that they are then believed best of all by the multitude
when they speak any severe thing against others, though it be only out of envy
at them. nd he said that it was by their means that he had incurred the

displeasure of the nation, whom indeed he had injured. "o thou, therefore,"
said he, "when thou art come to erusalem, send for the leading men among
them, and show them my body, and with great appearance of sincerity, give
them leave to use it as they themselves please, whether they will dishonor the
dead body by refusing it burial, as having severely suffered by my means, or
whether in their anger they will offer any other injury to that body. romise
them also that thou wilt do nothing without them in the affairs of the kingdom.
f thou dost but say this to them,  shall have the honor of a more glorious
funeral from them than thou couldst have made for me and when it is in their
power to abuse my dead body, they will do it no injury at all, and thou wilt rule
in safety."    o when he had given his wife this advice, he died, after he had
reigned twenty-seven years, and lived fifty years within one.

. o leandra, when she had taken the fortress, acted as her husband had
suggested to her, and spake to the harisees, and put all things into their power,
both as to the dead body, and as to the affairs of the kingdom, and thereby
pacified their anger against leander, and made them bear goodwill and
friendship to him who then came among the multitude, and made speeches to
them, and laid before them the actions of leander, and told them that they
had lost a righteous king and by the commendation they gave him, they
brought them to grieve, and to be in heaviness for him, so that he had a funeral
more splendid than had any of the kings before him. leander left behind him
two sons, yrcanus and ristobulus, but committed the kingdom to leandra.
ow, as to these two sons, yrcanus was indeed unable to manage public
affairs, and delighted rather in a quiet life but the younger, ristobulus, was an
active and a bold man and for this woman herself, leandra, she was loved by
the multitude, because she seemed displeased at the offenses her husband had
been guilty of.
. o she made yrcanus high priest, because he was the elder, but much
more because he cared not to meddle with politics, and permitted the harisees
to do every thing to whom also she ordered the multitude to be obedient. he
also restored again those practices which the harisees had introduced,
according to the traditions of their forefathers, and which her father-in-law,
yrcanus, had abrogated. o she had indeed the name of the regent, but the

harisees had the authority for it was they who restored such as had been
banished, and set such as were prisoners at liberty, and, to say all at once, they
differed in nothing from lords. owever, the queen also took care of the affairs
of the kingdom, and got together a great body of mercenary soldiers, and
increased her own army to such a degree, that she became terrible to the
neighboring tyrants, and took hostages of them and the country was entirely at
peace, ecepting the harisees for they disturbed the queen, and desired that
she would kill those who persuaded leander to slay the eight hundred men
after which they cut the throat of one of them, iogenes and after him they did
the same to several, one after another, till the men that were the most potent
came into the palace, and ristobulus with them, for he seemed to be
displeased at what was done and it appeared openly, that if he had an
opportunity, he would not permit his mother to go on so. hese put the queen in
mind what great dangers they had gone through, and great things they had
done, whereby they had demonstrated the firmness of their fidelity to their

. e have related the affairs of queen leandra, and her death, in the
foregoing book and will now speak of what followed, and was connected with
those histories declaring, before we proceed, that we have nothing so much at
heart as this, that we may omit no facts, either through ignorance or laziness 
 for we are upon the history and eplication of such things as the greatest part
are unacquainted withal, because of their distance from our times and we aim
to do it with a proper beauty of style, so far as that is derived from proper
words harmonically disposed, and from such ornaments of speech also as may
contribute to the pleasure of our readers, that they may entertain the knowledge
of what we write with some agreeable satisfaction and pleasure. ut the

principal scope that authors ought to aim at above all the rest, is to speak
accurately, and to speak truly, for the satisfaction of those that are otherwise
unacquainted with such transactions, and obliged to believe what these writers
inform them of.
. yrcanus then began his high priesthood on the third year of the hundred
and seventy-seventh olympiad, when uintus ortensius and uintus etellus,
who was called etellus of rete, were consuls at ome when presently
ristobulus began to make war against him and as it came to a battle with
yrcanus at ericho, many of his soldiers deserted him, and went over to his
brother upon which yrcanus fled into the citadel, where ristobulus's wife
and children were imprisoned by their mother, as we have said already, and
attacked and overcame those his adversaries that had fled thither, and lay within
the walls of the temple. o when he had sent a message to his brother about
agreeing the matters between them, he laid aside his enmity to him on these
conditions, that ristobulus should be king, that he should live without
intermeddling with public affairs, and quietly enjoy the estate he had acquired.
hen they had agreed upon these terms in the temple, and had confirmed the
agreement with oaths, and the giving one another their right hands, and
embracing one another in the sight of the whole multitude, they departed the
one, ristobulus, to the palace and yrcanus, as a private man, to the former
house of ristobulus.
. ut there was a certain friend of yrcanus, an dumean, called ntipater,
who was very rich, and in his nature an active and a seditious man who was at
enmity with ristobulus, and had differences with him on account of his good-
will to yrcanus. t is true that icolaus of amascus says, that ntipater was
of the stock of the principal ews who came out of abylon into udea but that
assertion of his was to gratify erod, who was his son, and who, by certain
revolutions of fortune, came afterward to be king of the ews, whose history we
shall give you in its proper place hereafter. owever, this ntipater was at first
called ntipas,    and that was his father's name also of whom they relate this
that king leander and his wife made him general of all dumea, and that he
made a league of friendship with those rabians, and azites, and scalonites,
that were of his own party, and had, by many and large presents, made them his
fast friends. ut now this younger ntipater was suspicious of the power of
ristobulus, and was afraid of some mischief he might do him, because of his
hatred to him so he stirred up the most powerful of the ews, and talked against
him to them privately and said that it was unjust to overlook the conduct of
ristobulus, who had gotten the government unrighteously, and ejected his
brother out of it, who was the elder, and ought to retain what belonged to him
by prerogative of his birth. nd the same speeches he perpetually made to
yrcanus and told him that his own life would be in danger, unless he guarded

himself, and got quit of ristobulus for he said that the friends of ristobulus
omitted no opportunity of advising him to kill him, as being then, and not
before, sure to retain his principality. yrcanus gave no credit to these words of
his, as being of a gentle disposition, and one that did not easily admit of
calumnies against other men. his temper of his not disposing him to meddle
with public affairs, and want of spirit, occasioned him to appear to spectators to
be degenerous and unmanly while. ristobulus was of a contrary temper, an
active man, and one of a great and generous soul.
. ince therefore ntipater saw that yrcanus did not attend to what he
said, he never ceased, day by day, to charge reigned crimes upon ristobulus,

he said that it was for his advantage to fly away to retas. ow rabia is a

etra, where the palace of retas was and as he was a very familiar friend of

. n the meantime ompey sent caurus into yria, while he was himself in

.  little afterward ompey came to amascus, and marched over oelesyria

. hen ompey had heard the causes of these two, and had condemned

. t this behavior ompey was angry and taking with him that army

. ow there was a sedition of the men that were within the city, who did

our law gives us leave then to defend ourselves against those that begin to fight
with us and assault us, yet does it not permit us to meddle with our enemies
while they do any thing else.
. hich thing when the omans understood, on those days which we call
abbaths they threw nothing at the ews, nor came to any pitched battle with

when aius ntonius and arcus ullius icero were consuls, and the enemy

could not those that offered the sacrifices be compelled to run away, neither by

altars, than to omit any thing that their laws required of them    and that this is
not a mere brag, or an encomium to manifest a degree of our piety that was
false, but is the real truth,  appeal to those that have written of the acts of
ompey and, among them, to trabo and icolaus of amascus] and besides
these two, itus ivius, the writer of the oman istory, who will bear witness
to this thing.
