authors of that war and bestowed proper rewards on austus, and those others
that mounted the wall with such alacrity and he made erusalem tributary to
the omans, and took away those cities of oelesyria which the inhabitants of
udea had subdued, and put them under the government of the oman
president, and confined the whole nation, which had elevated itself so high
before, within its own bounds. oreover, he rebuilt adara, which had been
demolished a little before,    to gratify emetrius of adara, who was his
freedman, and restored the rest of the cities, ippos, and cythopolis, and
ella, and ios, and amaria, as also arissa, and shdod, and amnia, and
rethusa, to their own inhabitants these were in the inland parts. esides those
that had been demolished, and also of the maritime cities, aza, and oppa, and
ora, and trato's ower which last erod rebuilt after a glorious manner, and
adorned with havens and temples, and changed its name to aesarea. ll these
ompey left in a state of freedom, and joined them to the province of yria.
. ow the occasions of this misery which came upon erusalem were
yrcanus and ristobulus, by raising a sedition one against the other for now
we lost our liberty, and became subject to the omans, and were deprived of
that country which we had gained by our arms from the yrians, and were
compelled to restore it to the yrians. oreover, the omans eacted of us, in a
little time, above ten thousand talents and the royal authority, which was a
dignity formerly bestowed on those that were high priests, by the right of their
family, became the property of private men. ut of these matters we shall treat
in their proper places. ow ompey committed oelesyria, as far as the river
uphrates and gypt, to caurus, with two oman legions, and then went away
to ilicia, and made haste to ome. e also carried bound along with him
ristobulus and his children for he had two daughters, and as many sons the
one of which ran away, but the younger, ntigonius, was carried to ome,
together with his sisters.

,   
 ,    
. caurus made now an epedition against etrea, in rabia, and set on fire all
the places round about it, because of the great difficulty of access to it. nd as
his army was pinched by famine, ntipater furnished him with corn out of
udea, and with whatever else he wanted, and this at the command of yrcanus.
nd when he was sent to retas, as an ambassador by caurus, because he had
lived with him formerly, he persuaded retas to give caurus a sum of money,
to prevent the burning of his country, and undertook to be his surety for three
hundred talents. o caurus, upon these terms, ceased to make war any longer
which was done as much at caurus's desire, as at the desire of retas.
. ome time after this, when leander, the son of ristobulus, made an
incursion into udea, abinius came from ome into yria, as commander of
the oman forces. e did many considerable actions and particularly made
war with leander, since yrcanus was not yet able to oppose his power, but
was already attempting to rebuild the wall of erusalem, which ompey had
overthrown, although the omans which were there restrained him from that
his design. owever, leander went over all the country round about, and
armed many of the ews, and suddenly got together ten thousand armed
footmen, and fifteen hundred horsemen, and fortified leandrium, a fortress
near to oreae, and acherus, near the mountains of rabia. abinius
therefore came upon him, having sent arcus ntonius, with other
commanders, before. hese armed such omans as followed them and,
together with them, such ews as were subject to them, whose leaders were
itholaus and alichus and they took with them also their friends that were
with ntipater, and met leander, while abinius himself followed with his
legion. ereupon leander retired to the neighborhood of erusalem, where
they fell upon one another, and it came to a pitched battle, in which the omans
slew of their enemies about three thousand, and took a like number alive.
. t which time abinius came to leandrium, and invited those that
were in it to deliver it up on certain conditions, and promised that then their
former offenses should be forgiven. ut as a great number of the enemy had
pitched their camp before the fortress, whom the omans attacked, arcus
ntonius fought bravely, and slew a great number, and seemed to come off
with the greatest honor. o abinius left part of his army there, in order to take
the place, and he himself went into other parts of udea, and gave order to
rebuild all the cities that he met with that had been demolished at which time
were rebuilt amaria, shdod, cythopolis, nthedon, aphia, and ora
arissa also, and aza, and not a few others besides. nd as the men acted

according to abinius's command, it came to pass, that at this time these cities
were securely inhabited, which had been desolate for a long time.
. hen abinius had done thus in the country, he returned to
leandrium and when he urged on the siege of the place, leander sent an
embassage to him, desiring that he would pardon his former offenses he also
delivered up the fortresses, yrcania and acherus, and at last leandrium
itself which fortresses abinius demolished. ut when leander's mother,
who was of the side of the omans, as having her husband and other children at
ome, came to him, he granted her whatsoever she asked and when he had
settled matters with her, he brought yrcanus to erusalem, and committed the
care of the temple to him. nd when he had ordained five councils, he
distributed the nation into the same number of parts. o these councils
governed the people the first was at erusalem, the second at adara, the third
at mathus, the fourth at ericho, and the fifth at epphoris in alilee. o the
ews were now freed from monarchic authority, and were governed by an
aristocracy. 
  
        
 ,         
  ,      ,
      
. ow ristobulus ran away from ome to udea, and set about the rebuilding
of leandrium, which had been newly demolished. ereupon abinius sent
soldiers against him, and for their commanders isenna, and ntonius, and
ervilius, in order to hinder him from getting possession of the country, and to
take him again. nd indeed many of the ews ran to ristobulus, on account of
his former glory, as also because they should be glad of an innovation. ow
there was one itholaus, a lieutenant at erusalem, who deserted to him with a
thousand men, although a great number of those that came to him were
unarmed and when ristobulus had resolved to go to acherus, he dismissed
those people, because they were unarmed for they could not be useful to him
in what actions he was going about but he took with him eight thousand that
were armed, and marched on and as the omans fell upon them severely, the
ews fought valiantly, but were beaten in the battle and when they had fought
with alacrity, but were overborne by the enemy, they were put to flight of
whom were slain about five thousand, and the rest being dispersed, tried, as
well as they were able, to save themselves. owever, ristobulus had with him

still above a thousand, and with them he fled to acherus, and fortified the
place and though he had had ill success, he still had good hope of his affairs
but when he had struggled against the siege for two days' time, and had
received many wounds, he was brought as a captive to abinius, with his son
ntigonius, who also fled with him from ome. nd this was the fortune of
ristobulus, who was sent back again to ome, and was there retained in
bonds, having been both king and high priest for three years and si months
and was indeed an eminent person, and one of a great soul. owever, the senate
let his children go, upon abinius's writing to them that he had promised their
mother so much when she delivered up the fortresses to him and accordingly
they then returned into udea.
. ow when abinius was making an epedition against the arthians, and
had already passed over uphrates, he changed his mind, and resolved to return
into gypt, in order to restore tolemy to his kingdom.    his hath also been
related elsewhere. owever, ntipater supplied his army, which he sent against
rchelaus, with corn, and weapons, and money. e also made those ews who
were above elusium his friends and confederates, and had been the guardians
of the passes that led into gypt. ut when he came back out of gypt, he
found yria in disorder, with seditions and troubles for leander, the son of
ristobulus, having seized on the government a second time by force, made
many of the ews revolt to him and so he marched over the country with a
great army, and slew all the omans he could light upon, and proceeded to
besiege the mountain called erizzim, whither they had retreated.
. ut when abinius found yria in such a state, he sent ntipater, who
was a prudent man, to those that were seditious, to try whether he could cure
them of their madness, and persuade them to return to a better mind and when
he came to them, he brought many of them to a sound mind, and induced them
to do what they ought to do but he could not restrain leander, for he had an
army of thirty thousand ews, and met abinius, and joining battle with him,
was beaten, and lost ten thousand of his men about ount abor.
. o abinius settled the affairs which belonged to the city erusalem, as
was agreeable to ntipater's inclination, and went against the abateans, and
overcame them in battle. e also sent away in a friendly manner ithridates
and rsanes, who were arthian deserters, and came to him, though the report
went abroad that they had run away from him. nd when abinius had
performed great and glorious actions, in his management of the affairs of war,
he returned to ome, and delivered the government to rassus. ow icolaus
of amascus, and trabo of appadocia, both describe the epeditions of
ompey and abinius against the ews, while neither of them say anything new
which is not in the other.

,    
     ,  ,
  .     ,   
   ,      
. ow rassus, as he was going upon his epedition against the arthians,
came into udea, and carried off the money that was in the temple, which
ompey had left, being two thousand talents, and was disposed to spoil it of all
the gold belonging to it, which was eight thousand talents. e also took a beam,
which was made of solid beaten gold, of the weight of three hundred minae,
each of which weighed two pounds and a half. t was the priest who was
guardian of the sacred treasures, and whose name was leazar, that gave him
this beam, not out of a wicked design, for he was a good and a righteous man
but being intrusted with the custody of the veils belonging to the temple, which
were of admirable beauty, and of very costly workmanship, and hung down
from this beam, when lie saw that rassus was busy in gathering money, and
was in fear for the entire ornaments of the temple, he gave him this beam of
gold as a ransom for the whole, but this not till he had given his oath that he
would remove nothing else out of the temple, but be satisfied with this only,
which he should give him, being worth many ten thousand shekels]. ow this
beam was contained in a wooden beam that was hollow, but was known to no
others but leazar alone knew it yet did rassus take away this beam, upon
the condition of touching nothing else that belonged to the temple, and then
brake his oath, and carried away all the gold that was in the temple.
. nd let no one wonder that there was so much wealth in our temple,
since all the ews throughout the habitable earth, and those that worshipped
od, nay, even those of sia and urope, sent their contributions to it, and this
from very ancient times. or is the largeness of these sums without its
attestation nor is that greatness owing to our vanity, as raising it without
ground to so great a height but there are many witnesses to it, and particularly
trabo of appadocia, who says thus "ithridates sent to os, and took the
money which queen leopatra had deposited there, as also eight hundred
talents belonging to the ews." ow we have no public money but only what
appertains to od and it is evident that the sian ews removed this money out
of fear of ithridates for it is not probable that those of udea, who had a
strong city and temple, should send their money to os nor is it likely that the
ews who are inhabitants of leandria should do so neither, since they were ill
no fear of ithridates. nd trabo himself bears witness to the same thing in
another place, that at the same time that ylla passed over into reece, in order

to fight against ithridates, he sent ucullus to put an end to a sedition that our
nation, of whom the habitable earth is full, had raised in yrene where he
speaks thus "here were four classes of men among those of yrene that of
citizens, that of husbandmen, the third of strangers, and the fourth of ews.
ow these ews are already gotten into all cities and it is hard to find a place in
the habitable earth that hath not admitted this tribe of men, and is not possessed
by them and it hath come to pass that gypt and yrene, as having the same
governors, and a great number of other nations, imitate their way of living, and
maintain great bodies of these ews in a peculiar manner, and grow up to
greater prosperity with them, and make use of the same laws with that nation
also. ccordingly, the ews have places assigned them in gypt, wherein they
inhabit, besides what is peculiarly allotted to this nation at leandria, which is
a large part of that city. here is also an ethnarch allowed them, who governs
the nation, and distributes justice to them, and takes care of their contracts, and
of the laws to them belonging, as if he were the ruler of a free republic. n
gypt, therefore, this nation is powerful, because the ews were originally
gyptians, and because the land wherein they inhabit, since they went thence,
is near to gypt. hey also removed into yrene, because that this land
adjoined to the government of gypt, as well as does udea, or rather was
formerly under the same government." nd this is what trabo says.
. o when rassus had settled all things as he himself pleased, he marched
into arthia, where both he himself and all his army perished, as hath been
related elsewhere. ut assius, as he fled from ome to yria, took possession
of it, and was an impediment to the arthians, who by reason of their victory
over rassus made incursions upon it. nd as he came back to yre, he went up
into udea also, and fell upon aricheae, and presently took it, and carried about
thirty thousand ews captives and slew itholaus, who succeeded ristobulus
in his seditious practices, and that by the persuasion of ntipater, who proved
to have great interest in him, and was at that time in great repute with the
dumeans also out of which nation he married a wife, who was the daughter of
one of their eminent men, and her name was ypros,    by whom he had four
sons, hasael, and erod, who was afterwards made king, and oseph, and
heroras and a daughter, named alome. his ntipater cultivated also a
friendship and mutual kindness with other potentates, but especially with the
king of rabia, to whom he committed his children, while he fought against
ristobulus. o assius removed his camp, and marched to uphrates, to meet
those that were coming to attack him, as hath been related by others.
. ut some time afterwards, aesar, when he had taken ome, and after
ompey and the senate were fled beyond the onian ea, freed ristobulus from
his bonds, and resolved to send him into yria, and delivered two legions to
him, that he might set matters right, as being a potent man in that country. ut

ristobulus had no enjoyment of what he hoped for from the power that was
given him by aesar for those of ompey's party prevented it, and destroyed
him by poison and those of aesar's party buried him. is dead body also lay,
for a good while, embalmed in honey, till ntony afterward sent it to udea, and
caused him to be buried in the royal sepulchre. ut cipio, upon ompey's
sending to him to slay leander, the son of ristobulus, because the young
man was accused of what offenses he had been guilty of at first against the
omans, cut off his head and thus did he die at ntioch. ut tolemy, the son
of enneus, who was the ruler of halcis, under ount ibanus, took his
brethren to him, and sent his son hilippion to skelon to ristobulus's wife,
and desired her to send back with him her son ntigonus, and her daughters
the one of which, whose name was leandra, hilippion fell in love with, and
married her, though afterward his father tolemy slew him, and married
leandra, and continued to take care of her brethren.
  
        
       ,
        
      
. ow after ompey was dead, and after that victory aesar had gained over
him, ntipater, who managed the ewish affairs, became very useful to aesar
when he made war against gypt, and that by the order of yrcanus for when
ithridates of ergamus was bringing his auiliaries, and was not able to
continue his march through elusium, but obliged to stay at skelon, ntipater
came to him, conducting three thousand of the ews, armed men. e had also
taken care the principal men of the rabians should come to his assistance and
on his account it was that all the yrians assisted him also, as not willing to
appear behindhand in their alacrity for aesar—viz., amblicus the ruler, and
tolemy his son, and holomy the son of ohemus, who dwelt at ount
ibanus, and almost all the cities. o ithridates marched out of yria, and
came to elusium and when its inhabitants would not admit him, he besieged
the city. ow ntipater signalised himself here, and was the first who plucked
down a part of the wall, and so opened a way to the rest, whereby they might
enter the city, and by this means elusium was taken. ut it happened that the
gyptian ews, who dwelt in the country called nion, would not let ntipater
and ithridates, with their soldiers, pass to aesar but ntipater persuaded
them to come over with their party, because he was of the same people with

them, and that chiefly by showing them the epistles of yrcanus the high priest,
wherein he ehorted them to cultivate friendship with aesar, and to supply his
army with money, and all sorts of provisions which they wanted and
accordingly, when they saw ntipater and the high priest of the same
sentiments, they did as they were desired. nd when the ews about emphis
heard that these ews were come over to aesar, they also invited ithridates
to come to them so he came and received them also into his army.
. nd when ithridates had gone over all elta, as the place is called, he
came to a pitched battle with the enemy, near the place called the ewish amp.
ow ithridates had the right wing, and ntipater the left and when it came to
a fight, that wing where ithridates was gave way, and was likely to suffer
etremely, unless ntipater had come running to him with his own soldiers
along the shore, when he had already beaten the enemy that opposed him so he
delivered ithridates, and put those gyptians who had been too hard for him
to flight. e also took their camp, and continued in the pursuit of them. e also
recalled ithridates, who had been worsted, and was retired a great way off of
whose soldiers eight hundred fell, but of ntipater's fifty. o ithridates sent
an account of this battle to aesar, and openly declared that ntipater was the
author of this victory, and of his own preservation, insomuch that aesar
commended ntipater then, and made use of him all the rest of that war in the
most hazardous undertakings he happened also to be wounded in one of those
engagements.
. owever, when aesar, after some time, had finished that war, and was
sailed away for yria, he honored ntipater greatly, and confirmed yrcanus in
the high priesthood and bestowed on ntipater the privilege of a citizen of
ome, and a freedom from taes every where and it is reported by many, that
yrcanus went along with ntipater in this epedition, and came himself into
gypt. nd trabo of appadocia bears witness to this, when he says thus, in
the name of sinius "fter ithridates had invaded gypt, and with him
yrcanus the high priest of the ews." ay, the same trabo says thus again, in
another place, in the name of ypsicrates, that "ithridates at first went out
alone but that ntipater, who had the care of the ewish affairs, was called by
him to skelon, and that he had gotten ready three thousand soldiers to go
along with him, and encouraged other governors of the country to go along
with him also and that yrcanus the high priest was also present in this
epedition." his is what trabo says.
. ut ntigonus, the son of ristobulus, came at this time to aesar, and
lamented his father's fate and complained, that it was by ntipater's means that
ristobulus was taken off by poison, and his brother was beheaded by cipio,
and desired that he would take pity of him who had been ejected out of that
principality which was due to him. e also accused yrcanus and ntipater as

governing the nation by violence, and offering injuries to himself. ntipater
was present, and made his defence as to the accusations that were laid against
him. e demonstrated that ntigonus and his party were given to innovation,
and were seditious persons. e also put aesar in mind what difficult services
he had undergone when he assisted him in his wars, and discoursed about what
he was a witness of himself. e added, that ristobulus was justly carried away
to ome, as one that was an enemy to the omans, and could never be brought
to be a friend to them, and that his brother had no more than he deserved from
cipio, as being seized in committing robberies and that this punishment was
not inflicted on him in a way of violence or injustice by him that did it.
. hen ntipater had made this speech, aesar appointed yrcanus to be
high priest, and gave ntipater what principality he himself should choose,
leaving the determination to himself so he made him procurator of udea. e
also gave yrcanus leave to raise up the walls of his own city, upon his asking
that favor of him, for they had been demolished by ompey. nd this grant he
sent to the consuls to ome, to be engraven in the capitol. he decree of the
senate was this that follows    "ucius alerius, the son of ucius the praetor,
referred this to the senate, upon the des of ecember, in the temple of
oncord. here were present at the writing of this decree ucius oponius, the
son of ucius of the olline tribe, and apirius of the uirine tribe, concerning
the affairs which leander, the son of ason, and umenius, the son of
ntiochus, and leander, the son of ositheus, ambassadors of the ews, good
and worthy men, proposed, who came to renew that league of goodwill and
friendship with the omans which was in being before. hey also brought a
shield of gold, as a mark of confederacy, valued at fifty thousand pieces of
gold and desired that letters might be given them, directed both to the free
cities and to the kings, that their country and their havens might be at peace,
and that no one among them might receive any injury. t therefore pleased the
senate] to make a league of friendship and good-will with them, and to bestow
on them whatsoever they stood in need of, and to accept of the shield which
was brought by them. his was done in the ninth year of yrcanus the high
priest and ethnarch, in the month anemus." yrcanus also received honors
from the people of thens, as having been useful to them on many occasions.
nd when they wrote to him, they sent him this decree, as it here follows
"nder the prutaneia and priesthood of ionysius, the son of sculapius, on the
fifth day of the latter part of the month anemus, this decree of the thenians
was given to their commanders, when gathocles was archon, and ucles, the
son of enander of limusia, was the scribe. n the month unychion, on the
eleventh day of the prutaneia, a council of the presidents was held in the
theatre. orotheus the high priest, and the fellow presidents with him, put it to
the vote of the people. ionysius, the son of ionysius, gave the sentence.

ince yrcanus, the son of leander, the high priest and ethnarch of the ews,
continues to bear good-will to our people in general, and to every one of our
citizens in particular, and treats them with all sorts of kindness and when any
of the thenians come to him, either as ambassadors, or on any occasion of
their own, he receives them in an obliging manner, and sees that they are
conducted back in safety, of which we have had several former testimonies it is
now also decreed, at the report of heodosius, the son of heodorus, and upon
his putting the people in mind of the virtue of this man, and that his purpose is
to do us all the good that is in his power, to honor him with a crown of gold, the
usual reward according to the law, and to erect his statue in brass in the temple
of emus and of the races and that this present of a crown shall be
proclaimed publicly in the theatre, in the ionysian shows, while the new
tragedies are acting and in the anathenean, and leusinian, and ymnical
shows also and that the commanders shall take care, while he continues in his
friendship, and preserves his good-will to us, to return all possible honor and
favor to the man for his affection and generosity that by this treatment it may
appear how our people receive the good kindly, and repay them a suitable
reward and he may be induced to proceed in his affection towards us, by the
honors we have already paid him. hat ambassadors be also chosen out of all
the thenians, who shall carry this decree to him, and desire him to accept of
the honors we do him, and to endeavor always to be doing some good to our
city." nd this shall suffice us to have spoken as to the honors that were paid by
the omans and the people of thens to yrcanus.
  
         ,
       ,  ,
  '   ,   

. ow when aesar had settled the affairs of yria, he sailed away. nd as
soon as ntipater had conducted aesar out of yria, he returned to udea. e
then immediately raised up the wall which had been thrown down by ompey
and, by coming thither, he pacified that tumult which had been in the country,
and this by both threatening and advising them to be quiet for that if they
would be of yrcanus's side, they would live happily, and lead their lives
without disturbance, and in the enjoyment of their own possessions but if they
were addicted to the hopes of what might come by innovation, and aimed to get
wealth thereby, they should have him a severe master instead of a gentle

governor, and yrcanus a tyrant instead of a king, and the omans, together
with aesar, their bitter enemies instead of rulers, for that they would never
bear him to be set aside whom they had appointed to govern. nd when
ntipater had said this to them, he himself settled the affairs of this country.
. nd seeing that yrcanus was of a slow and slothful temper, he made
hasaelus, his eldest son, governor of erusalem, and of the places that were
about it, but committed alilee to erod, his net son, who was then a very
young man, for he was but fifteen years of age    but that youth of his was no
impediment to him but as he was a youth of great mind, he presently met with
an opportunity of signalizing his courage for finding that there was one
ezekias, a captain of a band of robbers, who overran the neighboring parts of
yria with a great troop of them, he seized him and slew him, as well as a great
number of the other robbers that were with him for which action he was
greatly beloved by the yrians for when they were very desirous to have their
country freed from this nest of robbers, he purged it of them. o they sung
songs in his commendation in their villages and cities, as having procured them
peace, and the secure enjoyment of their possessions and on this account it was
that he became known to etus aesar, who was a relation of the great aesar,
and was now president of yria. ow hasaelus, erod's brother, was moved
with emulation at his actions, and envied the fame be had thereby gotten, and
became ambitious not to be behindhand with him in deserving it. o he made
the inhabitants of erusalem bear him the greatest good-will while he held the
city himself, but did neither manage its affairs improperly, nor abuse his
authority therein. his conduct procured from the nation to ntipater such
respect as is due to kings, and such honors as he might partake of if he were an
absolute lord of the country. et did not this splendor of his, as frequently
happens, in the least diminish in him that kindness and fidelity which he owed
to yrcanus.
. ut now the principal men among the ews, when they saw ntipater and
his sons to grow so much in the good-will the nation bare to them, and in the
revenues which they received out of udea, and out of yrcanus's own wealth,
they became ill-disposed to him for indeed ntipater had contracted a
friendship with the oman emperors and when he had prevailed with
yrcanus to send them money, he took it to himself, and purloined the present
intended, and sent it as if it were his own, and not yrcanus's gift to them.
yrcanus heard of this his management, but took no care about it nay, he
rather was very glad of it. ut the chief men of the ews were therefore in fear,
because they saw that erod was a violent and bold man, and very desirous of
acting tyrannically so they came to yrcanus, and now accused ntipater
openly, and said to him, "ow long wilt thou be quiet under such actions as are
now done r dost thou not see that ntipater and his sons have already seized

upon the government, and that it is only the name of a king which is given
thee ut do not thou suffer these things to be hidden from thee, nor do thou
think to escape danger by being so careless of thyself and of thy kingdom for
ntipater and his sons are not now stewards of thine affairs do not thou
deceive thyself with such a notion they are evidently absolute lords for erod,
ntipater's son, hath slain ezekiah and those that were with him, and hath
thereby transgressed our law, which hath forbidden to slay any man, even

be a magistrate or private person, hindereth them from so doing, he shall be
liable to a fine, to be applied to the uses of the city."
. he decree of the ardians. "his decree was made by the senate and
people, upon the representation of the praetors whereas those ews who are
fellow citizens, and live with us in this city, have ever had great benefits heaped
upon them by the people, and have come now into the senate, and desired of
the people, that upon the restitution of their law and their liberty, by the senate
and people of ome, they may assemble together, according to their ancient
legal custom, and that we will not bring any suit against them about it and that
a place may be given them where they may have their congregations, with their
wives and children, and may offer, as did their forefathers, their prayers and
sacrifices to od. ow the senate and people have decreed to permit them to
assemble together on the days formerly appointed, and to act according to their
own laws and that such a place be set apart for them by the praetors, for the
building and inhabiting the same, as they shall esteem fit for that purpose and
that those that take care of the provision for the city, shall take care that such
sorts of food as they esteem fit for their eating may be imported into the city."
. he decree of the phesians. "hen enophilus was prytanis, on the
first day of the month rtemisius, this decree was made by the people icanor,
the son of uphemus, pronounced it, upon the representation of the praetors.
ince the ews that dwell in this city have petitioned arcus ulius ompeius,
the son of rutus, the proconsul, that they might be allowed to observe their
abbaths, and to act in all things according to the customs of their forefathers,
without impediment from any body, the praetor hath granted their petition.
ccordingly, it was decreed by the senate and people, that in this affair that
concerned the omans, no one of them should be hindered from keeping the
abbath day, nor be fined for so doing, but that they may be allowed to do all
things according to their own laws."
. ow there are many such decrees of the senate and imperators of the
omans,    and those different from these before us, which have been made in
favor of yrcanus, and of our nation as also, there have been more decrees of
the cities, and rescripts of the praetors, to such epistles as concerned our rights

and privileges and certainly such as are not ill-disposed to what we write may
believe that they are all to this purpose, and that by the specimens which we
have inserted for since we have produced evident marks that may still be seen
of the friendship we have had with the omans, and demonstrated that those
marks are engraven upon columns and tables of brass in the capitol, that ae
still in being, and preserved to this day, we have omitted to set them all down,
as needless and disagreeable for  cannot suppose any one so perverse as not to
believe the friendship we have had with the omans, while they have
demonstrated the same by such a great number of their decrees relating to us
nor will they doubt of our fidelity as to the rest of those decrees, since we have
shown the same in those we have produced, nd thus have we sufficiently
eplained that friendship and confederacy we at those times had with the
omans.

. ow it so fell out, that about this very time the affairs of yria were in great
disorder, and this on the occasion following ecilius assus, one of ompey's
party, laid a treacherous design against etus aesar, and slew him, and then
took his army, and got the management of public affairs into his own hand so
there arose a great war about pamia, while aesar's generals came against him
with an army of horsemen and footmen to these ntipater also sent succors,
and his sons with them, as calling to mind the kindnesses they had received
from aesar, and on that account he thought it but just to require punishment
for him, and to take vengeance on the man that had murdered him. nd as the
war was drawn out into a great length, arcus came from ome to take
etus's government upon him. ut aesar was slain by assius and rutus in
the senate-house, after he had retained the government three years and si
months. his fact however, is related elsewhere.
. s the war that arose upon the death of aesar was now begun, and the
principal men were all gone, some one way, and some another, to raise armies,
assius came from ome into yria, in order to receive the army that lay in
the] camp at pamia and having raised the siege, he brought over both assus
and arcus to his party. e then went over the cities, and got together weapons

and soldiers, and laid great taes upon those cities and he chiefly oppressed
udea, and eacted of it seven hundred talents but ntipater, when he saw the
state to be in so great consternation and disorder, he divided the collection of
that sum, and appointed his two sons to gather it and so that part of it was to be
eacted by alichus, who was ill-disposed to him, and part by others. nd
because erod did eact what is required of him from alilee before others, he
was in the greatest favor with assius for he thought it a part of prudence to
cultivate a friendship with the omans, and to gain their goodwill at the
epense of others whereas the curators of the other cities, with their citizens,
were sold for slaves and assius reduced four cities into a state of slavery, the
two most potent of which were ophna and mmaus and, besides these, ydia
and hamna. ay, assius was so very angry at alichus, that he had killed
him, (for he assaulted him,) had not yrcanus, by the means of ntipater, sent
him a hundred talents of his own, and thereby pacified his anger against him.
. ut after assius was gone out of udea, alichus laid snares for
ntipater, as thinking that his death would be the preservation of yrcanus's
government but his design was not unknown to ntipater, which when he
perceived, he retired beyond ordan, and got together an army, partly of rabs,
and partly of his own countrymen. owever, alichus, being one of great
cunning, denied that he had laid any snares for him, and made his defence with
an oath, both to himself and his sons and said that while hasaelus had a
garrison in erusalem, and erod had the weapons of war in his custody, he
could never have a thought of any such thing. o ntipater, perceiving the
distress that alichus was in, was reconciled to him, and made an agreement
with him this was when arcus was president of yria who yet perceiving
that this alichus was making a disturbance in udea, proceeded so far that he
had almost killed him but still, at the intercession of ntipater, he saved him.
. owever, ntipater little thought that by saving alichus he had saved
his own murderer for now assius and arcus had got together an army, and
intrusted the entire care of it with erod, and made him general of the forces of
oelesyria, and gave him a fleet of ships, and an army of horsemen and
footmen and promised him, that after the war was over they would make him
king of udea for a war was already begun between ntony and the younger
aesar but as alichus was most afraid of ntipater, he took him out of the
way and by the offer of money, persuaded the butler of yrcanus, with whom
they were both to feast, to kill him by poison. his being done, and he having
armed men with him, settled the affairs of the city. ut when ntipater's sons,
erod and hasaelus, were acquainted with this conspiracy against their father,
and had indignation at it, alichus denied all, and utterly renounced any
knowledge of the murder. nd thus died ntipater, a man that had distinguished
himself for piety and justice, and love to his country. nd whereas one of his

sons, erod, resolved immediately to revenge their father's death, and was
coming upon alichus with an army for that purpose, the elder of his sons,
hasaelus, thought it best rather to get this man into their hands by policy, lest
they should appear to begin a civil war in the country so he accepted of
alichus's defence for himself, and pretended to believe him that he had had no
hand in the violent death of ntipater his father, but erected a fine monument
for him. erod also went to amaria and when he found them in great distress,
he revived their spirits, and composed their differences.
. owever, a little after this, erod, upon the approach of a festival, came
with his soldiers into the city whereupon alichus was affrighted, and
persuaded yrcanus not to permit him to come into the city. yrcanus
complied and, for a pretence of ecluding him, alleged, that a rout of strangers
ought not to be admitted when the multitude were purifying themselves. ut
erod had little regard to the messengers that were sent to him, and entered the
city in the night time, and affrighted alichus yet did he remit nothing of his
former dissimulation, but wept for ntipater, and bewailed him as a friend of
his with a loud voice but erod and his friends thought it proper not openly to
contradict alichus's hypocrisy, but to give him tokens of mutual friendship, in
order to prevent his suspicion of them.
. owever, erod sent to assius, and informed him of the murder of his
father who knowing what sort of man alichus was as to his morals, sent him
back word that he should revenge his father's death and also sent privately to
the commanders of his army at yre, with orders to assist erod in the
eecution of a very just design of his. ow when assius had taken aodicea,
they all went together to him, and carried him garlands and money and erod
thought that alichus might be punished while he was there but he was
somewhat apprehensive of the thing, and designed to make some great attempt,
and because his son was then a hostage at yre, he went to that city, and
resolved to steal him away privately, and to march thence into udea and as
assius was in haste to march against ntony, he thought to bring the country
to revolt, and to procure the government for himself. ut rovidence opposed
his counsels and erod being a shrewd man, and perceiving what his intention
was, he sent thither beforehand a servant, in appearance indeed to get a supper
ready, for he had said before that he would feast them all there, but in reality to
the commanders of the army, whom he persuaded to go out against alichus,
with their daggers. o they went out and met the man near the city, upon the
seashore, and there stabbed him. hereupon yrcanus was so astonished at
what had happened, that his speech failed him and when, after some difficulty,
he had recovered himself, he asked erod what the matter could be, and who it
was that slew alichus and when he said that it was done by the command of
assius, he commended the action for that alichus was a very wicked man,

and one that conspired against his own country. nd this was the punishment
that was inflicted on alichus for what he wickedly did to ntipater.
. ut when assius was marched out of yria, disturbances arose in udea
for eli, who was left at erusalem with an army, made a sudden attempt
against hasaelus, and the people themselves rose in arms but erod went to
abius, the prefect of amascus, and was desirous to run to his brother's
assistance, but was hindered by a distemper that seized upon him, till hasaelus
by himself had been too hard for eli, and had shut him up in the tower, and
there, on certain conditions, dismissed him. hasaelus also complained of
yrcanus, that although he had received a great many benefits from them, yet
did he support their enemies for alichus's brother had made many places to
revolt, and kept garrisons in them, and particularly asada, the strongest
fortress of them all. n the mean time, erod was recovered of his disease, and
came and took from eli all the places he bad gotten and, upon certain
conditions, dismissed him also.

. ow    tolemy, the son of enneus, brought back into udea ntigonus, the
son of ristobulus, who had already raised an army, and had, by money, made
abius to be his friend, and this because he was of kin to him. arion also gave
him assistance. e had been left by assius to tyrannise over yre for this
assius was a man that seized on yria, and then kept it under, in the way of a
tyrant. arion also marched into alilee, which lay in his neighborhood, and
took three of his fortresses, and put garrisons into them to keep them. ut when
erod came, he took all from him but the yrian garrison he dismissed in a
very civil manner nay, to some of the soldiers he made presents out of the
good-will he bare to that city. hen he had despatched these affairs, and was
gone to meet ntigonus, he joined battle with him, and beat him, and drove him
out of udea presently, when he was just come into its borders. ut when he
was come to erusalem, yrcanus and the people put garlands about his head
for he had already contracted an affinity with the family of yrcanus by having
espoused a descendant of his, and for that reason erod took the greater care of

him, as being to marry the daughter of leander, the son of ristobulus, and
the grand-daughter of yrcanus, by which wife he became the father of three
male and two female children. e had also married before this another wife,
out of a lower family of his own nation, whose name was oris, by whom he
had his eldest son ntipater.
. ow ntonius and aesar had beaten assius near hilippi, as others
have related but after the victory, aesar went into aul, taly,] and ntony
marched for sia, who, when he was arrived at ithynia, he had ambassadors
that met him from all parts. he principal men also of the ews came thither, to
accuse hasaelus and erod and they said that yrcanus had indeed the
appearance of reigning, but that these men had all the power but ntony paid
great respect to erod, who was come to him to make his defence against his
accusers, on which account his adversaries could not so much as obtain a
hearing which favor erod had gained of ntony by money. ut still, when
ntony was come to phesus, yrcanus the high priest, and our nation, sent an
embassage to him, which carried a crown of gold with them, and desired that he
would write to the governors of the provinces, to set those ews free who had
been carried captive by assius, and this without their having fought against
him, and to restore them that country, which, in the days of assius, had been
taken from them. ntony thought the ews' desires were just, and wrote
immediately to yrcanus, and to the ews. e also sent, at the same time, a
decree to the yrians the contents of which were to the same purpose.
. "arcus ntonius, imperator, to yrcanus the high priest and ethnarch of
the ews, sendeth greeting. f you be in health, it is well  am also in health,
with the army. ysimachus, the son of ausanias, and osephus the son of
enneus, and leander the son of heodorus, your ambassadors, met me at
phesus, and have renewed the embassage which they had formerly been upon
at ome, and have diligently acquitted themselves of the present embassage,
which thou and thy nation have intrusted to them, and have fully declared the
good-will thou hast for us.  am therefore satisfied, both by your actions and
your words, that you are well-disposed to us and  understand that your
conduct of life is constant and religious so  reckon upon you as our own. ut
when those that were adversaries to you, and to the oman people, abstained
neither from cities nor temples, and did not observe the agreement they had
confirmed by oath, it was not only on account of our contest with them, but on
account of all mankind in common, that we have taken vengeance on those who
have been the authors of great injustice towards men, and of great wickedness
towards the gods for the sake of which we suppose it was that the sun turned
away his light from us,    as unwilling to view the horrid crime they were guilty
of in the case of aesar. e have also overcome their conspiracies, which
threatened the gods themselves, which acedonia received, as it is a climate

peculiarly proper for impious and insolent attempts and we have overcome that
confused rout of men, half mad with spite against us, which they got together at
hilippi in acedonia, when they seized on the places that were proper for
their purpose, and, as it were, walled them round with mountains to the very
sea, and where the passage was open only through a single gate. his victory
we gained, because the gods had condemned those men for their wicked
enterprises. ow rutus, when he had fled as far as hilippi, was shut up by us,
and became a partaker of the same perdition with assius and now these have
received their punishment, we suppose that we may enjoy peace for the time to
come, and that sia may be at rest from war. e therefore make that peace
which od hath given us common to our confederates also, insomuch that the
body of sia is now recovered out of that distemper it was under by the means
of our victory. , therefore, bearing in mind both thee and your nation, shall take
care of what may be for your advantage.  have also sent epistles in writing to
the several cities, that if any persons, whether free-men or bond-men, have
been sold under the spear by aius assius or his subordinate officers, they
may be set free. nd  will that you kindly make use of the favors which  and
olabella have granted you.  also forbid the yrians to use any violence with
you and for what places of the ews they now possess,  order them to restore
them.  have withal accepted of the crown which thou sentest me."
. "arcus ntonius, imperator, to the magistrates, senate, and people of
yre, sendeth greeting. he ambassadors of yrcanus, the high priest and
ethnarch of the ews], appeared before me at phesus, and told me that you are
in possession of part of their country, which you entered upon under the
government of our adversaries. ince, therefore, we have undertaken a war for
the obtaining the government, and have taken care to do what was agreeable to
piety and justice, and have brought to punishment those that had neither any
remembrance of the kindnesses they had received, nor have kept their oaths, 
will that you be at peace with those that are our confederates as also, that what
you have taken by the means of our adversaries shall not be reckoned your
own, but be returned to those from whom you took them for none of them took
their provinces or their armies by the gift of the senate, but they seized them by
force, and bestowed them by violence upon such as became useful to them in
their unjust proceedings. ince, therefore, those men have received the
punishment due to them, we desire that our confederates may retain whatsoever
it was that they formerly possessed without disturbance, and that you restore all
the places which belong to yrcanus, the ethnarch of the ews, which you have
had, though it were but one day before aius assius began an unjustifiable
war against us, and entered into our province nor do you use any force against
him, in order to weaken him, that he may not be able to dispose of that which is
his own but if you have any contest with him about your respective rights, it

shall be lawful for you to plead your cause when we come upon the places
concerned, for we shall alike preserve the rights and hear all the causes of our
confederates."
. "arcus ntonius, imperator, to the magistrates, senate, and people of
yre, sendeth greeting.  have sent you my decree, of which  will that ye take
care that it be engraven on the public tables, in oman and reek letters, and
that it stand engraven in the most illustrious places, that it may be read by all.
arcus ntonius, imperator, one of the triumvirate over the public affairs,
made this declaration since aius assius, in this revolt he hath made, hath
pillaged that province which belonged not to him, and was held by garrisons
there encamped, while they were our confederates, and hath spoiled that nation
of the ews that was in friendship with the oman people, as in war and since
we have overcome his madness by arms, we now correct by our decrees and
judicial determinations what he hath laid waste, that those things may be
restored to our confederates. nd as for what hath been sold of the ewish
possessions, whether they be bodies or possessions, let them be released the
bodies into that state of freedom they were originally in, and the possessions to
their former owners.  also will that he who shall not comply with this decree of
mine shall be punished for his disobedience and if such a one be caught,  will
take care that the offenders suffer condign punishment."
. he same thing did ntony write to the idonians, and the ntiochians,
and the rabians. e have produced these decrees, therefore, as marks for
futurity of the truth of what we have said, that the omans had a great concern
about our nation.

. hen after this ntony came into yria, leopatra met him in ilicia, and
brought him to fall in love with her. nd there came now also a hundred of the
most potent of the ews to accuse erod and those about him, and set the men
of the greatest eloquence among them to speak. ut essala contradicted them,
on behalf of the young men, and all this in the presence of yrcanus, who was
erod's father-in-law    already. hen ntony had heard both sides at aphne,
he asked yrcanus who they were that governed the nation best e replied,

erod and his friends. ereupon ntony, by reason of the old hospitable
friendship he had made with his father ntipater], at that time when he was
with abinius, he made both erod and hasaelus tetrarchs, and committed the
public affairs of the ews to them, and wrote letters to that purpose. e also
bound fifteen of their adversaries, and was going to kill them, but that erod
obtained their pardon.
. et did not these men continue quiet when they were come back, but a
thousand of the ews came to yre to meet him there, whither the report was
that he would come. ut ntony was corrupted by the money which erod and
his brother had given him and so he gave order to the governor of the place to
punish the ewish ambassadors, who were for making innovations, and to settle
the government upon erod but erod went out hastily to them, and yrcanus
was with him, (for they stood upon the shore before the city,) and he charged
them to go their ways, because great mischief would befall them if they went
on with their accusation. ut they did not acquiesce whereupon the omans
ran upon them with their daggers, and slew some, and wounded more of them,
and the rest fled away and went home, and lay still in great consternation. nd
when the people made a clamor against erod, ntony was so provoked at it,
that he slew the prisoners.
. ow, in the second year, acorus, the king of arthia's son, and
arzapharnes, a commander of the arthians, possessed themselves of yria.
tolemy, the son of enneus, also was now dead, and ysanias his son took his
government, and made a league of friendship with ntigonus, the son of
ristobulus and in order to obtain it, made use of that commander, who had
great interest in him. ow ntigonus had promised to give the arthians a
thousand talents, and five hundred women, upon condition they would take the
government away from yrcanus, and bestow it upon him, and withal kill
erod. nd although he did not give them what he had promised, yet did the
arthians make an epedition into udea on that account, and carried ntigonus
with them. acorus went along the maritime parts, but the commander
arzapharnes through the midland. ow the yrians ecluded acorus, but the
idonians, and those of tolemais received him. owever, acorus sent a troop
of horsemen into udea, to take a view of the state of the country, and to assist
ntigonus and sent also the king's butler, of the same name with himself. o
when the ews that dwelt about ount armel came to ntigonus, and were
ready to march with him into udea, ntigonus hoped to get some part of the
country by their assistance. he place is called rymi and when some others
came and met them, the men privately fell upon erusalem and when some
more were come to them, they got together in great numbers, and came against
the king's palace, and besieged it. ut as hasaelus's and erod's party came to
the other's assistance, and a battle happened between them in the market-place,

the young men beat their enemies, and pursued them into the temple, and sent
some armed men into the adjoining houses to keep them in, who yet being
destitute of such as should support them, were burnt, and the houses with them,
by the people who rose up against them. ut erod was revenged on these
seditious adversaries of his a little afterward for this injury they had offered
him, when he fought with them, and slew a great number of them.
. ut while there were daily skirmishes, the enemy waited for the coming
of the multitude out of the country to entecost, a feast of ours so called and
when that day was come, many ten thousands of the people were gathered
together about the temple, some in armor, and some without. ow those that
came guarded both the temple and the city, ecepting what belonged to the
palace, which erod guarded with a few of his soldiers and hasaelus had the
charge of the wall, while erod, with a body of his men, sallied out upon the
enemy, who lay in the suburbs, and fought courageously, and put many ten
thousands to flight, some flying into the city, and some into the temple, and
some into the outer fortifications, for some such fortifications there were in that
place. hasaelus came also to his assistance yet was acorus, the general of the
arthians, at the desire of ntigonus, admitted into the city, with a few of his
horsemen, under pretence indeed as if he would still the sedition, but in reality
to assist ntigonus in obtaining the government. nd when hasaelus met him,
and received him kindly, acorus persuaded him to go himself as ambassador
to arzapharnes, which was done fraudulently. ccordingly, hasaelus,
suspecting no harm, complied with his proposal, while erod did not give his
consent to what was done, because of the perfidiousness of these barbarians,
but desired hasaelus rather to fight those that were come into the city.
. o both yrcanus and hasaelus went on the embassage but acorus left
with erod two hundred horsemen, and ten men, who were called the freemen,
and conducted the others on their journey and when they were in alilee, the
governors of the cities there met them in their arms. arzapharnes also received
them at the first with cheerfulness, and made them presents, though he
afterward conspired against them and hasaelus, with his horsemen, were
conducted to the sea-side. ut when they heard that ntigonus had promised to
give the arthians a thousand talents, and five hundred women, to assist him
against them, they soon had a suspicion of the barbarians. oreover, there was
one who informed them that snares were laid for them by night, while a guard
came about them secretly and they had then been seized upon, had not they
waited for the seizure of erod by the arthians that were about erusalem, lest,
upon the slaughter of yrcanus and hasaelus, he should have an intimation of
it, and escape out of their hands. nd these were the circumstances they were
now in and they saw who they were that guarded them. ome persons indeed
would have persuaded hasaelus to fly away immediately on horseback, and

not stay any longer and there was one phellius, who, above all the rest, was
earnest with him to do so for he had heard of this treachery from aramalla,
the richest of all the yrians at that time, who also promised to provide him
ships to carry him off for the sea was just by them. ut he had no mind to
desert yrcanus, nor bring his brother into danger but he went to
arzapharnes, and told him he did not act justly when he made such a
contrivance against them for that if he wanted money, he would give him more
than ntigonus and besides, that it was a horrible thing to slay those that came
to him upon the security of their oaths, and that when they had done them no
injury. ut the barbarian swore to him that there was no truth in any of his
suspicions, but that he was troubled with nothing but false proposals, and then
went away to acorus.
. ut as soon as he was gone away, some men came and bound yrcanus
and hasaelus, while hasaelus greatly reproached the arthians for their
perjury owever, that butler who was sent against erod had it in command to
get him without the walls of the city, and seize upon him but messengers had
been sent by hasaelus to inform erod of the perfidiousness of the arthians.
nd when he knew that the enemy had seized upon them, he went to acorus,
and to the most potent of the arthians, as to the lord of the rest, who, although
they knew the whole matter, dissembled with him in a deceitful way and said
that he ought to go out with them before the walls, and meet those which were
bringing him his letters, for that they were not taken by his adversaries, but
were coming to give him an account of the good success hasaelus had had.
erod did not give credit to what they said for he had heard that his brother
was seized upon by others also and the daughter of yrcanus, whose daughter
he had espoused, was his monitor also not to credit them], which made him
still more suspicious of the arthians for although other people did not give
heed to her, yet did he believe her as a woman of very great wisdom.
. ow while the arthians were in consultation what was fit to be done for
they did not think it proper to make an open attempt upon a person of his
character and while they put off the determination to the net day, erod was
under great disturbance of mind, and rather inclining to believe the reports he
heard about his brother and the arthians, than to give heed to what was said on
the other side, he determined, that when the evening came on, he would make
use of it for his flight, and not make any longer delay, as if the dangers from the
enemy were not yet certain. e therefore removed with the armed men whom
he had with him and set his wives upon the beasts, as also his mother, and
sister, and her whom he was about to marry, ariamne,] the daughter of
leander, the son of ristobulus, with her mother, the daughter of yrcanus,
and his youngest brother, and all their servants, and the rest of the multitude
that was with him, and without the enemy's privity pursued his way to dumea.

or could any enemy of his who then saw him in this case be so hardhearted,
but would have commiserated his fortune, while the women drew along their
infant children and left their own country, and their friends in prison, with tears
in their eyes, and sad lamentations, and in epectation of nothing but what was
of a melancholy nature.
. ut for erod himself, he raised his mind above the miserable state he
was in, and was of good courage in the midst of his misfortunes and as he
passed along, he bid them every one to be of good cheer, and not to give
themselves up to sorrow, because that would hinder them in their flight, which
was now the only hope of safety that they had. ccordingly, they tried to bear
with patience the calamity they were under, as he ehorted them to do yet was
he once almost going to kill himself, upon the overthrow of a waggon, and the
danger his mother was then in of being killed and this on two accounts,
because of his great concern for her, and because he was afraid lest, by this
delay, the enemy should overtake him in the pursuit but as he was drawing his
sword, and going to kill himself therewith, those that were present restrained
him, and being so many in number, were too hard for him and told him that he
ought not to desert them, and leave them a prey to their enemies, for that it was
not the part of a brave man to free himself from the distresses he was in, and to
overlook his friends that were in the same distresses also. o he was compelled
to let that horrid attempt alone, partly out of shame at what they said to him,
and partly out of regard to the great number of those that would not permit him
to do what he intended. o he encouraged his mother, and took all the care of
her the time would allow, and proceeded on the way he proposed to go with the
utmost haste, and that was to the fortress of asada. nd as he had many
skirmishes with such of the arthians as attacked him and pursued him, he was
conqueror in them all.
. or indeed was he free from the ews all along as he was in his flight
for by that time he was gotten sity furlongs out of the city, and was upon the
road, they fell upon him, and fought hand to hand with him, whom he also put
to flight, and overcame, not like one that was in distress and in necessity, but
like one that was ecellently prepared for war, and had what he wanted in great
plenty. nd in this very place where he overcame the ews it was that he some
time afterward build a most ecellent palace, and a city round about it, and
called it erodium. nd when he was come to dumea, at a place called
hressa, his brother oseph met him, and he then held a council to take advice
about all his affairs, and what was fit to be done in his circumstances, since he
had a great multitude that followed him, besides his mercenary soldiers, and the
place asada, whither he proposed to fly, was too small to contain so great a
multitude so he sent away the greater part of his company, being above nine
thousand, and bid them go, some one way, and some another, and so save

themselves in dumea, and gave them what would buy them provisions in their
journey. ut he took with him those that were the least encumbered, and were
most intimate with him, and came to the fortress, and placed there his wives
and his followers, being eight hundred in number, there being in the place a
sufficient quantity of corn and water, and other necessaries, and went directly
for etra, in rabia. ut when it was day, the arthians plundered all erusalem,
and the palace, and abstained from nothing but yrcanus's money, which was
three hundred talents.  great deal of erod's money escaped, and principally
all that the man had been so provident as to send into dumea beforehand nor
indeed did what was in the city suffice the arthians, but they went out into the
country, and plundered it, and demolished the city arissa.
. nd thus was ntigonus brought back into udea by the king of the
arthians, and received yrcanus and hasaelus for his prisoners but he was
greatly cast down because the women had escaped, whom he intended to have
given the enemy, as having promised they should have them, with the money,
for their reward but being afraid that yrcanus, who was under the guard of
the arthians, might have his kingdom restored to him by the multitude, he cut
off his ears, and thereby took care that the high priesthood should never come
to him any more, because he was maimed, while the law required that this
dignity should belong to none but such as had all their members entire.    ut
now one cannot but here admire the fortitude of hasaelus, who, perceiving that
he was to be put to death, did not think death any terrible thing at all but to die
thus by the means of his enemy, this he thought a most pitiable and
dishonorable thing and therefore, since he had not his hands at liberty, but the
bonds he was in prevented him from killing himself thereby, he dashed his head
against a great stone, and thereby took away his own life, which he thought to
be the best thing he could do in such a distress as he was in, and thereby put it
out of the power of the enemy to bring him to any death he pleased. t is also
reported, that when he had made a great wound in his head, ntigonus sent
physicians to cure it, and, by ordering them to infuse poison into the wound,
killed him. owever, hasaelus hearing, before he was quite dead, by a certain
woman, that his brother erod had escaped the enemy, underwent his death
cheerfully, since he now left behind him one who would revenge his death, and
who was able to inflict punishment on his enemies.

. s for erod, the great miseries he was in did not discourage him, but made
him sharp in discovering surprising undertakings for he went to alchus, king
of rabia, whom he had formerly been very kind to, in order to receive
somewhat by way of requital, now he was in more than ordinary want of it, and
desired he would let him have some money, either by way of loan, or as his free
gift, on account of the many benefits he had received from him for not
knowing what was become of his brother, he was in haste to redeem him out of
the hand of his enemies, as willing to give three hundred talents for the price of
his redemption. e also took with him the son of hasaelus, who was a child of
but seven years of age, for this very reason, that he might be a hostage for the
repayment of the money. ut there came messengers from alchus to meet
him, by whom he was desired to be gone, for that the arthians had laid a
charge upon him not to entertain erod. his was only a pretence which he
made use of, that he might not be obliged to repay him what he owed him and
this he was further induced to by the principal men among the rabians, that
they might cheat him of what sums they had received from his father]
ntipater, and which he had committed to their fidelity. e made answer, that
he did not intend to be troublesome to them by his coming thither, but that he
desired only to discourse with them about certain affairs that were to him of the
greatest importance.
. ereupon he resolved to go away, and did go very prudently the road to
gypt and then it was that he lodged in a certain temple for he had left a great
many of his followers there. n the net day he came to hinocolura, and there
it was that he heard what was befallen his brother. hough alchus soon
repented of what he had done, and came running after erod but with no
manner of success, for he was gotten a very great way off, and made haste into
the road to elusium and when the stationary ships that lay there hindered him
from sailing to leandria, he went to their captains, by whose assistance, and
that out of much reverence of and great regard to him, he was conducted into
the city leandria], and was retained there by leopatra yet was she not able
to prevail with him to stay there, because he was making haste to ome, even
though the weather was stormy, and he was informed that the affairs of taly
were very tumultuous, and in great disorder.
. o he set sail from thence to amphylia, and falling into a violent storm,
he had much ado to escape to hodes, with the loss of the ship's burden and
there it was that two of his friends, appinas and tolemeus, met with him and
as he found that city very much damaged in the war against assius, though he
were in necessity himself, he neglected not to do it a kindness, but did what he
could to recover it to its former state. e also built there a three-decked ship,

and set sail thence, with his friends, for taly, and came to the port of
rundusium and when he was come from thence to ome, he first related to
ntony what had befallen him in udea, and how hasaelus his brother was
seized on by the arthians, and put to death by them, and how yrcanus was
detained captive by them, and how they had made ntigonus king, who had
promised them a sum of money, no less than a thousand talents, with five
hundred women, who were to be of the principal families, and of the ewish
stock and that he had carried off the women by night and that, by undergoing
a great many hardships, he had escaped the hands of his enemies as also, that
his own relations were in danger of being besieged and taken, and that he had
sailed through a storm, and contemned all these terrible dangers of it, in order
to come, as soon as possible, to him, who was his hope and only succor at this
time.
. his account made ntony commiserate the change that had happened in
erod's condition    and reasoning with himself that this was a common case
among those that are placed in such great dignities, and that they are liable to
the mutations that come from fortune, he was very ready to give him the
assistance he desired, and this because he called to mind the friendship he had
had with ntipater because erod offered him money to make him king, as he
had formerly given it him to make him tetrarch, and chiefly because of his
hatred to ntigonus for he took him to be a seditious person, and an enemy to
the omans. aesar was also the forwarder to raise erod's dignity, and to give
him his assistance in what he desired, on account of the toils of war which he
had himself undergone with ntipater his father in gypt, and of the hospitality
he had treated him withal, and the kindness he had always showed him, as also
to gratify ntony, who was very zealous for erod. o a senate was
convocated and essala first, and then tratinus, introduced erod into it, and
enlarged upon the benefits they had received from his father, and put them in
mind of the good-will he had borne to the omans. t the same time, they
accused ntigonus, and declared him an enemy, not only because of his former
opposition to them, but that he had now overlooked the omans, and taken the
government from the arthians. pon this the senate was irritated and ntony
informed them further, that it was for their advantage in the arthian war that
erod should be king. his seemed good to all the senators and so they made a
decree accordingly.
. nd this was the principal instance of ntony's affection for erod, that
he not only procured him a kingdom which he did not epect, (for he did not
come with an intention to ask the kingdom for himself, which he did not
suppose the omans would grant him, who used to bestow it on some of the
royal family, but intended to desire it for his wife's brother, who was grandson
by his father to ristobulus, and to yrcanus by his mother,) but that he

procured it for him so suddenly, that he obtained what he did not epect, and
departed out of taly in so few days as seven in all. his young man the
grandson] erod afterward took care to have slain, as we shall show in its
proper place. ut when the senate was dissolved, ntony and aesar went out
of the senate house with erod between them, and with the consuls and other
magistrates before them, in order to offer sacrifices, and to lay up their decrees
in the capitol. ntony also feasted erod the first day of his reign. nd thus did
this man receive the kingdom, having obtained it on the hundred and eighty-
fourth olympiad, when aius omitius alvinus was consul the second time,
and aius sinius ollio the first time].
. ll this while ntigonus besieged those that were in asada, who had
plenty of all other necessaries, but were only in want of water,    insomuch that
on this occasion oseph, erod's brother, was contriving to run away from it,
with two hundred of his dependents, to the rabians for he had heard that
alchus repented of the offenses he had been guilty of with regard to erod
but od, by sending rain in the night time, prevented his going away, for their
cisterns were thereby filled, and he was under no necessity of running away on
that account but they were now of good courage, and the more so, because the
sending that plenty of water which they had been in want of seemed a mark of
ivine rovidence so they made a sally, and fought hand to hand with
ntigonus's soldiers, (with some openly, with some privately,) and destroyed a
great number of them. t the same time entidius, the general of the omans,
was sent out of yria, to drive the arthians out of it, and marched after them
into udea, in pretence indeed to succor oseph but in reality the whole affair
was no more than a stratagem, in order to get money of ntigonus so they
pitched their camp very near to erusalem, and stripped ntigonus of a great
deal of money, and then he retired himself with the greater part of the army
but, that the wickedness he had been guilty of might be found out, he left ilo
there, with a certain part of his soldiers, with whom also ntigonus cultivated
an acquaintance, that he might cause him no disturbance, and was still in hopes
that the arthians would come again and defend him.
