. y this time erod had sailed out of taly to tolemais, and had gotten
together no small army, both of strangers and of his own countrymen, and

marched through alilee against ntigonus. ilo also, and entidius, came and
assisted him, being persuaded by ellius, who was sent by ntony to assist in
bringing back erod. ow for entidius, he was employed in composing the
disturbances that had been made in the cities by the means of the arthians and
for ilo, he was in udea indeed, but corrupted by ntigonus. owever, as
erod went along his army increased every day, and all alilee, with some
small eception, joined him but as he was to those that were in asada, (for he
was obliged to endeavor to save those that were in that fortress now they were
besieged, because they were his relations,) oppa was a hindrance to him, for it
was necessary for him to take that place first, it being a city at variance with
him, that no strong hold might be left in his enemies' hands behind him when
he should go to erusalem. nd when ilo made this a pretence for rising up
from erusalem, and was thereupon pursued by the ews, erod fell upon them
with a small body of men, and both put the ews to flight and saved ilo, when
he was very poorly able to defend himself but when erod had taken oppa, he
made haste to set free those of his family that were in asada. ow of the
people of the country, some joined him because of the friendship they had had
with his father, and some because of the splendid appearance he made, and
others by way of requital for the benefits they had received from both of them
but the greatest number came to him in hopes of getting somewhat from him
afterward, if he were once firmly settled in the kingdom.
. erod had now a strong army and as he marched on, ntigonus laid
snares and ambushes in the passes and places most proper for them but in truth
he thereby did little or no damage to the enemy. o erod received those of his
family out of asada, and the fortress essa, and then went on for erusalem.
he soldiery also that was with ilo accompanied him all along, as did many of
the citizens, being afraid of his power and as soon as he had pitched his camp
on the west side of the city, the soldiers that were set to guard that part shot
their arrows and threw their darts at him and when some sallied out in a crowd,
and came to fight hand to hand with the first ranks of erod's army, he gave
orders that they should, in the first place, make proclamation about the wall,
that he came for the good of the people, and for the preservation of the city, and
not to bear any old grudge at even his most open enemies, but ready to forget
the offenses which his greatest adversaries had done him. ut ntigonus, by
way of reply to what erod had caused to be proclaimed, and this before the
omans, and before ilo also, said that they would not do justly, if they gave
the kingdom to erod, who was no more than a private man, and an dumean,
i.e. a half ew,    whereas they ought to bestow it on one of the royal family, as
their custom was for that in case they at present bear an ill-will to him, and had
resolved to deprive him of the kingdom, as having received it from the
arthians, yet were there many others of his family that might by their law take

it, and these such as had no way offended the omans and being of the
sacerdotal family, it would be an unworthy thing to put them by. ow while
they said thus one to another, and fell to reproaching one another on both sides,
ntigonus permitted his own men that were upon the wall to defend
themselves, who using their bows, and showing great alacrity against their
enemies, easily drove them away from the towers.
. nd now it was that ilo discovered that he had taken bribes for he set a
good number of his soldiers to complain aloud of the want of provisions they
were in, and to require money to buy them food and that it was fit to let them
go into places proper for winter quarters, since the places near the city were a
desert, by reason that ntigonus's soldiers had carried all away so he set the
army upon removing, and endeavored to march away but erod pressed ilo
not to depart, and ehorted ilo's captains and soldiers not to desert him, when
aesar, and ntony, and the senate had sent him thither, for that he would
provide them plenty of all the things they wanted, and easily procure them a
great abundance of what they required after which entreaty, he immediately
went out into the country, and left not the least pretence to ilo for his
departure for he brought an unepected quantity of provisions, and sent to
those friends of his who inhabited about amaria to bring down corn, and wine,
and oil, and cattle, and all other provisions, to ericho, that those might be no
want of a supply for the soldiers for the time to come. ntigonus was sensible
of this, and sent presently over the country such as might restrain and lie in
ambush for those that went out for provisions. o these men obeyed the orders
of ntigonus, and got together a great number of armed men about ericho, and
sat upon the mountains, and watched those that brought the provisions.
owever, erod was not idle in the mean time, for he took ten bands of
soldiers, of whom five were of the omans, and five of the ews, with some
mercenaries among them, and with some few horsemen, and came to ericho
and as they found the city deserted, but that five hundred of them had settled
themselves on the tops of the hills, with their wives and children, those he took
and sent away but the omans fell upon the city, and plundered it, and found
the houses full of all sorts of good things. o the king left a garrison at ericho,
and came back again, and sent the oman army to take their winter quarters in
the countries that were come over to him, udea, and alilee, and amaria. nd
so much did ntigonus gain of ilo for the bribes he gave him, that part of the
army should be quartered at ydda, in order to please ntony. o the omans
laid their weapons aside, and lived in plenty of all things.
. ut erod was not pleased with lying still, but sent out his brother
oseph against dumea with two thousand armed footmen, and four hundred
horsemen, while he himself came to amaria, and left his mother and his other
relations there, for they were already gone out of asada, and went into

alilee, to take certain places which were held by the garrisons of ntigonus
and he passed on to epphoris, as od sent a snow, while ntigonus's garrisons
withdrew themselves, and had great plenty of provisions. e also went thence,
and resolved to destroy those robbers that dwelt in the caves, and did much
mischief in the country so he sent a troop of horsemen, and three companies of
armed footmen, against them. hey were very near to a village called rbela
and on the fortieth day after, he came himself with his whole army and as the
enemy sallied out boldly upon him, the left wing of his army gave way but he
appearing with a body of men, put those to flight who were already conquerors,
and recalled his men that ran away. e also pressed upon his enemies, and
pursued them as far as the river ordan, though they ran away by different
roads. o he brought over to him all alilee, ecepting those that dwelt in the
caves, and distributed money to every one of his soldiers, giving them a
hundred and fifty drachmae apiece, and much more to their captains, and sent
them into winter quarters at which time ilo came to him, and his commanders
with him, because ntigonus would not give them provisions any longer, for he
supplied them for no more than one month nay, he had sent to all the country
about, and ordered them to carry off the provisions that were there, and retire to
the mountains, that the omans might have no provisions to live upon, and so
might perish by famine. ut erod committed the care of that matter to
heroras, his youngest brother, and ordered him to repair leandrium also.
ccordingly, he quickly made the soldiers abound with great plenty of
provisions, and rebuilt leandrium, which had been before desolate.
. bout this time it was that ntony continued some time at thens, and
that entidius, who was now in yria, sent for ilo, and commanded him to
assist erod, in the first place, to finish the present war, and then to send for
their confederates for the war they were themselves engaged in but as for
erod, he went in haste against the robbers that were in the caves, and sent ilo
away to entidius, while he marched against them. hese caves were in
mountains that were eceeding abrupt, and in their middle were no other than
precipices, with certain entrances into the caves, and those caves were
encompassed with sharp rocks, and in these did the robbers lie concealed, with
all their families about them but the king caused certain chests to be made, in
order to destroy them, and to be hung down, bound about with iron chains, by
an engine, from the top of the mountain, it being not possible to get up to them,
by reason of the sharp ascent of the mountains, nor to creep down to them from
above. ow these chests were filled with armed men, who had long hooks in
their hands, by which they might pull out such as resisted them, and then
tumble them down, and kill them by so doing but the letting the chests down
proved to be a matter of great danger, because of the vast depth they were to be
let down, although they had their provisions in the chests themselves. ut when

the chests were let down, and not one of those in the mouths of the caves durst
come near them, but lay still out of fear, some of the armed men girt on their
armor, and by both their hands took hold of the chain by which the chests were
let down, and went into the mouths of the caves, because they fretted that such
delay was made by the robbers not daring to come out of the caves and when
they were at any of those mouths, they first killed many of those that were in
the mouths with their darts, and afterwards pulled those to them that resisted
them with their hooks, and tumbled them down the precipices, and afterwards
went into the caves, and killed many more, and then went into their chests
again, and lay still there but, upon this, terror seized the rest, when they heard
the lamentations that were made, and they despaired of escaping. owever,
when the night came on, that put an end to the whole work and as the king
proclaimed pardon by a herald to such as delivered themselves up to him, many
accepted of the offer. he same method of assault was made use of the net
day and they went further, and got out in baskets to fight them, and fought
them at their doors, and sent fire among them, and set their caves on fire, for
there was a great deal of combustible matter within them. ow there was one
old man who was caught within one of these caves, with seven children and a
wife these prayed him to give them leave to go out, and yield themselves up to
the enemy but he stood at the cave's mouth, and always slew that child of his
who went out, till he had destroyed them every one, and after that he slew his
wife, and cast their dead bodies down the precipice, and himself after them, and
so underwent death rather than slavery but before he did this, he greatly
reproached erod with the meanness of his family, although he was then king.
erod also saw what he was doing, and stretched out his hand, and offered him
all manner of security for his life by which means all these caves were at
length subdued entirely.
. nd when the king had set tolemy over these parts of the country as his
general, he went to amaria, with si hundred horsemen, and three thousand
armed footmen, as intending to fight ntigonus. ut still this command of the
army did not succeed well with tolemy, but those that had been troublesome to
alilee before attacked him, and slew him and when they had done this, they
fled among the lakes and places almost inaccessible laying waste and
plundering whatsoever they could come at in those places. ut erod soon
returned, and punished them for what they had done for some of these rebels
he slew, and others of them, who had fled to the strong holds he besieged, and
both slew them, and demolished their strong holds. nd when he had thus put
an end to their rebellion, he laid a fine upon the cities of a hundred talents.
. n the mean time, acorus was fallen in a battle, and the arthians were
defeated, when entidius sent acheras to the assistance of erod, with two
legions, and a thousand horsemen, while ntony encouraged him to make

haste. ut acheras, at the instigation of ntigonus, without the approbation of
erod, as being corrupted by money, went about to take a view of his affairs
but ntigonus suspecting this intention of his coming, did not admit him into
the city, but kept him at a distance, with throwing stones at him, and plainly
showed what he himself meant. ut when acheras was sensible that erod
had given him good advice, and that he had made a mistake himself in not
hearkening to that advice, he retired to the city mmaus and what ews he met
with he slew them, whether they were enemies or friends, out of the rage he
was in at what hardships he had undergone. he king was provoked at this
conduct of his, and went to amaria, and resolved to go to ntony about these
affairs, and to inform him that he stood in no need of such helpers, who did him
more mischief than they did his enemies and that he was able of himself to
beat ntigonus. ut acheras followed him, and desired that he would not go
to ntony or if he was resolved to go, that he would join his brother oseph
with them, and let them fight against ntigonus. o he was reconciled to
acheras, upon his earnest entreaties. ccordingly, he left oseph there with
his army, but charged him to run no hazards, nor to quarrel with acheras.
. ut for his own part, he made haste to ntony (who was then at the siege
of amosata, a place upon uphrates) with his troops, both horsemen and
footmen, to be auiliaries to him. nd when he came to ntioch, and met there
a great number of men gotten together that were very desirous to go to ntony,
but durst not venture to go, out of fear, because the barbarians fell upon men on
the road, and slew many, so he encouraged them, and became their conductor
upon the road. ow when they were within two days' march of amosata, the
barbarians had laid an ambush there to disturb those that came to ntony, and
where the woods made the passes narrow, as they led to the plains, there they
laid not a few of their horsemen, who were to lie still until those passengers
were gone by into the wide place. ow as soon as the first ranks were gone by,
(for erod brought on the rear,) those that lay in ambush, who were about five
hundred, fell upon them on the sudden, and when they had put the foremost to
flight, the king came riding hard, with the forces that were about him, and
immediately drove back the enemy by which means he made the minds of his
own men courageous, and imboldened them to go on, insomuch that those who
ran away before now returned back, and the barbarians were slain on all sides.
he king also went on killing them, and recovered all the baggage, among
which were a great number of beasts for burden, and of slaves, and proceeded
on in his march and whereas there were a great number of those in the woods
that attacked them, and were near the passage that led into the plain, he made a
sally upon these also with a strong body of men, and put them to flight, and
slew many of them, and thereby rendered the way safe for those that came
after and these called erod their savior and protector.

. nd when he was near to amosata, ntony sent out his army in all their
proper habiliments to meet him, in order to pay erod this respect, and because
of the assistance he had given him for he had heard what attacks the barbarians
had made upon him in udea]. e also was very glad to see him there, as
having been made acquainted with the great actions he had performed upon the
road. o he entertained him very kindly, and could not but admire his courage.
ntony also embraced him as soon as he saw him, and saluted him after a most
affectionate manner, and gave him the upper hand, as having himself lately
made him a king and in a little time ntiochus delivered up the fortress, and on
that account this war was at an end then ntony committed the rest to ossius,
and gave him orders to assist erod, and went himself to gypt. ccordingly,
ossius sent two legions before into udea to the assistance of erod, and he
followed himself with the body of the army.
. ow oseph was already slain in udea, in the manner following he
forgot what charge his brother erod had given him when he went to ntony
and when he had pitched his camp among the mountains, for acheras had lent
him five regiments, with these he went hastily to ericho, in order to reap the

to keep him against his triumph but when he heard that the nation grew
seditious, and that, out of their hatred to erod, they continued to bear good-
will to ntigonus, he resolved to behead him at ntioch, for otherwise the ews
could no way be brought to be quiet. nd trabo of appadocia attests to what
 have said, when he thus speaks "ntony ordered ntigonus the ew to be
brought to ntioch, and there to be beheaded. nd this ntony seems to me to
have been the very first man who beheaded a king, as supposing he could no
other way bend the minds of the ews so as to receive erod, whom he had
made king in his stead for by no torments could they he forced to call him
king, so great a fondness they had for their former king so he thought that this
dishonorable death would diminish the value they had for ntigonus's memory,
and at the same time would diminish the hatred they bare to erod." hus far
trabo.

. ow after erod was in possession of the kingdom, yrcanus the high
priest, who was then a captive among the arthians, came to him again, and
was set free from his captivity, in the manner following arzapharnes and
acorus, the generals of the arthians, took yrcanus, who was first made high
priest and afterward king, and erod's brother, hasaelus captives, and were
them away into arthia. hasaelus indeed could not bear the reproach of being
in bonds and thinking that death with glory was better than any life
whatsoever, he became his own eecutioner, as  have formerly related.
. ut when yrcanus was brought into arthia the king hraates treated
him after a very gentle manner, as having already learned of what an illustrious
family he was on which account he set him free from his bonds, and gave him
a habitation at abylon,    where there were ews in great numbers. hese ews
honored yrcanus as their high priest and king, as did all the ewish nation that
dwelt as far as uphrates which respect was very much to his satisfaction. ut
when he was informed that erod had received the kingdom, new hopes came
upon him, as having been himself still of a kind disposition towards him, and
epecting that erod would bear in mind what favor be had received from him
and when he was upon his trial, and when he was in danger that a capital
sentence would be pronounced against him, he delivered him from that danger,
and from all punishment. ccordingly, he talked of that matter with the ews

that came often to him with great affection but they endeavored to retain him
among them, and desired that he would stay with them, putting him in mind of
the kind offices and honors they did him, and that those honors they paid him
were not at all inferior to what they could pay to either their high priests or their
kings and what was a greater motive to determine him, they said, was this, that
he could not have those dignities in udea] because of that maim in his body,
which had been inflicted on him by ntigonus and that kings do not use to
requite men for those kindnesses which they received when they were private
persons, the height of their fortune making usually no small changes in them.
. ow although they suggested these arguments to him for his own
advantage, yet did yrcanus still desire to depart. erod also wrote to him, and
persuaded him to desire of hraates, and the ews that were there, that they
should not grudge him the royal authority, which he should have jointly with
himself, for that now was the proper time for himself to make him amends for
the favors he had received from him, as having been brought up by him, and
saved by him also, as well as for yrcanus to receive it. nd as he wrote thus to
yrcanus, so did he send also aramallas, his ambassador, to hraates, and
many presents with him, and desired him in the most obliging way that he
would be no hindrance to his gratitude towards his benefactor. ut this zeal of
erod's did not flow from that principle, but because he had been made
governor of that country without having any just claim to it, he was afraid, and
that upon reasons good enough, of a change in his condition, and so made what
haste he could to get yrcanus into his power, or indeed to put him quite out of
the way which last thing he compassed afterward.
. ccordingly, when yrcanus came, full of assurance, by the permission
of the king of arthia, and at the epense of the ews, who supplied him with
money, erod received him with all possible respect, and gave him the upper
place at public meetings, and set him above all the rest at feasts, and thereby
deceived him. e called him his father, and endeavored, by all the ways
possible, that he might have no suspicion of any treacherous design against
him. e also did other things, in order to secure his government, which yet
occasioned a sedition in his own family for being cautious how he made any
illustrious person the high priest of od,    he sent for an obscure priest out of
abylon, whose name was nanelus, and bestowed the high priesthood upon
him.
. owever, leandra, the daughter of yrcanus, and wife of leander,
the son of ristobulus the king, who had also brought leander two] children,
could not bear this indignity. ow this son was one of the greatest comeliness,
and was called ristobulus and the daughter, ariamne, was married to erod,
and eminent for her beauty also. his leandra was much disturbed, and took
this indignity offered to her son eceeding ill, that while be was alive, any one

else should be sent for to have the dignity of the high priesthood conferred
upon him. ccordingly, she wrote to leopatra (a musician assisting her in
taking care to have her letters carried) to desire her intercession with ntony, in
order to gain the high priesthood for her son.
. ut as ntony was slow in granting this request, his friend ellius    came
into udea upon some affairs and when he saw ristobulus, he stood in
admiration at the tallness and handsomeness of the child, and no less at
ariamne, the king's wife, and was open in his commendations of leandra,
as the mother of most beautiful children. nd when she came to discourse with
him, he persuaded her to get pictures drawn of them both, and to send them to
ntony, for that when he saw them, he would deny her nothing that she should
ask. ccordingly, leandra was elevated with these words of his, and sent the
pictures to ntony. ellius also talked etravagantly, and said that these
children seemed not derived from men, but from some god or other. is design
in doing so was to entice ntony into lewd pleasures with them, who was
ashamed to send for the damsel, as being the wife of erod, and avoided it,
because of the reproaches he should have from leopatra on that account but
he sent, in the most decent manner he could, for the young man but added this
withal, unless he thought it hard upon him so to do. hen this letter was
brought to erod, he did not think it safe for him to send one so handsome as
was ristobulus, in the prime of his life, for he was siteen years of age, and of
so noble a family, and particularly not to ntony, the principal man among the
omans, and one that would abuse him in his amours, and besides, one that
openly indulged himself in such pleasures as his power allowed him without
control. e therefore wrote back to him, that if this boy should only go out of
the country, all would be in a state of war and uproar, because the ews were in
hopes of a change in the government, and to have another king over them.
. hen erod had thus ecused himself to ntony, he resolved that he
would not entirely permit the child or leandra to be treated dishonorably but
his wife ariamne lay vehemently at him to restore the high priesthood to her
brother and he judged it was for his advantage so to do, because if he once had
that dignity, he could not go out of the country. o he called his friends
together, and told them that leandra privately conspired against his royal
authority, and endeavored, by the means of leopatra, so to bring it about, that
he might be deprived of the government, and that by ntony's means this youth
might have the management of public affairs in his stead and that this
procedure of hers was unjust, since she would at the same time deprive her
daughter of the dignity she now had, and would bring disturbances upon the
kingdom, for which he had taken a great deal of pains, and had gotten it with
etraordinary hazards that yet, while he well remembered her wicked
practices, he would not leave off doing what was right himself, but would even

now give the youth the high priesthood and that he formerly set up nanelus,
because ristobulus was then so very young a child. ow when he had said
this, not at random, but as he thought with the best discretion he had, in order to
deceive the women, and those friends whom he had taken to consult withal,
leandra, out of the great joy she had at this unepected promise, and out of
fear from the suspicions she lay under, fell a weeping and made the following
apology for herself and said, that as to the high] priesthood, she was very
much concerned for the disgrace her son was under, and so did her utmost
endeavors to procure it for him but that as to the kingdom, she had made no
attempts, and that if it were offered her for her son], she would not accept it
and that now she would be satisfied with her son's dignity, while he himself
held the civil government, and she had thereby the security that arose from his
peculiar ability in governing to all the remainder of her family that she was
now overcome by his benefits, and thankfully accepted of this honor showed by
him to her son, and that she would hereafter be entirely obedient. nd she
desired him to ecuse her, if the nobility of her family, and that freedom of
acting which she thought that allowed her, had made her act too precipitately
and imprudently in this matter. o when they had spoken thus to one another,
they came to an agreement, and all suspicions, so far as appeared, were
vanished away.

. o king erod immediately took the high priesthood away from nanelus,
who, as we said before, was not of this country, but one of those ews that had
been carried captive beyond uphrates for there were not a few ten thousands
of this people that had been carried captives, and dwelt about abylonia,
whence nanelus came. e was one of the stock of the high priests,    and had
been of old a particular friend of erod and when he was first made king, he
conferred that dignity upon him, and now put him out of it again, in order to
quiet the troubles in his family, though what he did was plainly unlawful, for at
no other time of old] was any one that had once been in that dignity deprived
of it. t was ntiochus piphanes who first brake that law, and deprived esus,
and made his brother nias high priest in his stead. ristobulus was the second
that did so, and took that dignity from his brother yrcanus] and this erod

was the third, who took that high office away from nanelus], and gave it to
this young man, ristobulus, in his stead.
. nd now erod seemed to have healed the divisions in his family yet
was he not without suspicion, as is frequently the case, of people seeming to be
reconciled to one another, but thought that, as leandra had already made
attempts tending to innovations, so did he fear that she would go on therein, if
she found a fit opportunity for so doing so he gave a command that she should
dwell in the palace, and meddle with no public affairs. er guards also were so
careful, that nothing she did in private life every day was concealed. ll these
hardships put her out of patience, by little and little, and she began to hate
erod for as she had the pride of a woman to the utmost degree, she had great
indignation at this suspicious guard that was about her, as desirous rather to
undergo any thing that could befall her, than to be deprived of her liberty of
speech, and, under the notion of an honorary guard, to live in a state of slavery
and terror. he therefore sent to leopatra, and made a long complaint of the
circumstances she was in, and entreated her to do her utmost for her assistance.
leopatra hereupon advised her to take her son with her, and come away
immediately to her into gypt. his advice pleased her and she had this
contrivance for getting away she got two coffins made, as if they were to carry
away two dead bodies and put herself into one, and her son into the other and
gave orders to such of her servants as knew of her intentions to carry them
away in the night time. ow their road was to be thence to the sea-side and
there was a ship ready to carry them into gypt. ow esop, one of her
servants, happened to fall upon abion, one of her friends, and spake of this
matter to him, as thinking he had known of it before. hen abion knew this,
(who had formerly been an enemy of erod, and been esteemed one of those
that laid snares for and gave the poison to his father] ntipater,) he epected
that this discovery would change erod's hatred into kindness so he told the
king of this private stratagem of leandra whereupon be suffered her to
proceed to the eecution of her project, and caught her in the very fact but still
he passed by her offense and though he had a great mind to do it, he durst not
inflict any thing that was severe upon her, for he knew that leopatra would not
bear that he should have her accused, on account of her hatred to him but made
a show as if it were rather the generosity of his soul, and his great moderation,
that made him forgive them. owever, he fully proposed to himself to put this
young man out of the way, by one means or other but he thought he might in
probability be better concealed in doing it, if he did it not presently, nor
immediately after what had lately happened.
. nd now, upon the approach of the feast of tabernacles, which is a
festival very much observed among us, he let those days pass over, and both he
and the rest of the people were therein very merry yet did the envy which at

this time arose in him cause him to make haste to do what lie was about, and
provoke him to it for when this youth ristobulus, who was now in the
seventeenth year of his age, went up to the altar, according to the law, to offer
the sacrifices, and this with the ornaments of his high priesthood, and when he
performed the sacred offices,    he seemed to be eceedingly comely, and taller
than men usually were at that age, and to ehibit in his countenance a great deal
of that high family he was sprung from,—a warm zeal and affection towards
him appeared among the people, and the memory of the actions of his
grandfather ristobulus was fresh in their minds and their affections got so far
the mastery of them, that they could not forbear to show their inclinations to
him. hey at once rejoiced and were confounded, and mingled with good
wishes their joyful acclamations which they made to him, till the good-will of
the multitude was made too evident and they more rashly proclaimed the
happiness they had received from his family than was fit under a monarchy to
have done. pon all this, erod resolved to complete what he had intended
against the young man. hen therefore the festival was over, and he was
feasting at ericho    with leandra, who entertained them there, he was then
very pleasant with the young man, and drew him into a lonely place, and at the
same time played with him in a juvenile and ludicrous manner. ow the nature
of that place was hotter than ordinary so they went out in a body, and of a
sudden, and in a vein of madness and as they stood by the fish-ponds, of which
there were large ones about the house, they went to cool themselves by
bathing], because it was in the midst of a hot day. t first they were only
spectators of erod's servants and acquaintance as they were swimming but
after a while, the young man, at the instigation of erod, went into the water
among them, while such of erod's acquaintance, as he had appointed to do it,
dipped him as he was swimming, and plunged him under water, in the dark of
the evening, as if it had been done in sport only nor did they desist till he was
entirely suffocated. nd thus was ristobulus murdered, having lived no more
in all than eighteen years,    and kept the high priesthood one year only which
high priesthood nanelus now recovered again.
. hen this sad accident was told the women, their joy was soon changed
to lamentation, at the sight of the dead body that lay before them, and their
sorrow was immoderate. he city also of erusalem], upon the spreading of
this news, were in very great grief, every family looking on this calamity as if it
had not belonged to another, but that one of themselves was slain. ut
leandra was more deeply affected, upon her knowledge that he had been
destroyed on purpose]. er sorrow was greater than that of others, by her
knowing how the murder was committed but she was under the necessity of
bearing up under it, out of her prospect of a greater mischief that might
otherwise follow and she oftentimes came to an inclination to kill herself with

her own hand, but still she restrained herself, in hopes she might live long
enough to revenge the unjust murder thus privately committed nay, she further
resolved to endeavor to live longer, and to give no occasion to think she
suspected that her son was slain on purpose, and supposed that she might
thereby be in a capacity of revenging it at a proper opportunity. hus did she
restrain herself, that she might not be noted for entertaining any such suspicion.
owever, erod endeavored that none abroad should believe that the child's
death was caused by any design of his and for this purpose he did not only use
the ordinary signs of sorrow, but fell into tears also, and ehibited a real
confusion of soul and perhaps his affections were overcome on this occasion,
when he saw the child's countenance so young and so beautiful, although his
death was supposed to tend to his own security. o far at least this grief served
as to make some apology for him and as for his funeral, that he took care
should be very magnificent, by making great preparation for a sepulchre to lay
his body in, and providing a great quantity of spices, and burying many
ornaments together with him, till the very women, who were in such deep
sorrow, were astonished at it, and received in this way some consolation.
. owever, no such things could overcome leandra's grief but the
remembrance of this miserable case made her sorrow, both deep and obstinate.
ccordingly, she wrote an account of this treacherous scene to leopatra, and
how her son was murdered but leopatra, as she had formerly been desirous to
give her what satisfaction she could, and commiserating leandra's
misfortunes, made the case her own, and would not let ntony be quiet, but
ecited him to punish the child's murder for that it was an unworthy thing that
erod, who had been by him made king of a kingdom that no way belonged to
him, should be guilty of such horrid crimes against those that were of the royal
blood in reality. ntony was persuaded by these arguments and when he came
to aodicea, he sent and commanded erod to come and make his defence, as
to what he had done to ristobulus, for that such a treacherous design was not
well done, if he had any hand in it. erod was now in fear, both of the
accusation, and of leopatra's ill-will to him, which was such that she was ever
endeavoring to make ntony hate him. e therefore determined to obey his
summons, for he had no possible way to avoid it. o he left his uncle oseph
procurator for his government, and for the public affairs, and gave him a private
charge, that if ntony should kill him, he also should kill ariamne
immediately for that he had a tender affection for this his wife, and was afraid
of the injury that should be offered him, if, after his death, she, for her beauty,
should be engaged to some other man but his intimation was nothing but this at
the bottom, that ntony had fallen in love with her, when he had formerly
heard somewhat of her beauty. o when erod had given oseph this charge,
and had indeed no sure hopes of escaping with his life, he went away to

ntony.
. ut as oseph was administering the public affairs of the kingdom, and
for that reason was very frequently with ariamne, both because his business
required it, and because of the respects he ought to pay to the queen, he
frequently let himself into discourses about erod's kindness, and great
affection towards her and when the women, especially leandra, used to turn
his discourses into feminine raillery, oseph was so over-desirous to
demonstrate the kings inclinations, that he proceeded so far as to mention the
charge he had received, and thence drew his demonstration, that erod was not
able to live without her and that if he should come to any ill end, he could not
endure a separation from her, even after he was dead. hus spake oseph. ut
the women, as was natural, did not take this to be an instance of erod's strong
affection for them, but of his severe usage of them, that they could not escape
destruction, nor a tyrannical death, even when he was dead himself. nd this
saying of oseph] was a foundation for the women's severe suspicions about
him afterwards.
. t this time a report went about the city of erusalem among erod's
enemies, that ntony had tortured erod, and put him to death. his report, as
is natural, disturbed those that were about the palace, but chiefly the women
upon which leandra endeavored to persuade oseph to go out of the palace,
and fly away with them to the ensigns of the oman legion, which then lay
encamped about the city, as a guard to the kingdom, under the command of
ulius for that by this means, if any disturbance should happen about the
palace, they should be in greater security, as having the omans favorable to
them and that besides, they hoped to obtain the highest authority, if ntony did
but once see ariamne, by whose means they should recover the kingdom, and
want nothing which was reasonable for them to hope for, because of their royal
etraction.
. ut as they were in the midst of these deliberations, letters were brought
from erod about all his affairs, and proved contrary to the report, and of what
they before epected for when he was come to ntony, he soon recovered his
interest with him, by the presents he made him, which he had brought with him
from erusalem and he soon induced him, upon discoursing with him, to leave
off his indignation at him, so that leopatra's persuasions had less force than
the arguments and presents he brought to regain his friendship for ntony said
that it was not good to require an account of a king, as to the affairs of his
government, for at this rate he could be no king at all, but that those who had
given him that authority ought to permit him to make use of it. e also said the
same things to leopatra, that it would be best for her not busily to meddle with
the acts of the king's government. erod wrote an account of these things, and
enlarged upon the other honors which he had received from ntony how he sat

by him at his hearing causes, and took his diet with him every day, and that he
enjoyed those favors from him, notwithstanding the reproaches that leopatra
so severely laid against him, who having a great desire of his country, and
earnestly entreating ntony that the kingdom might be given to her, labored
with her utmost diligence to have him out of the way but that he still found
ntony just to him, and had no longer any apprehensions of hard treatment
from him and that he was soon upon his return, with a firmer additional
assurance of his favor to him, in his reigning and managing public affairs and
that there was no longer any hope for leopatra's covetous temper, since
ntony had given her oelesyria instead of what she had desired by which
means he had at once pacified her, and got clear of the entreaties which she
made him to have udea bestowed upon her.
. hen these letters were brought, the women left off their attempt for
flying to the omans, which they thought of while erod was supposed to be
dead yet was not that purpose of theirs a secret but when the king had
conducted ntony on his way against the arthians, he returned to udea, when
both his sister alome and his mother informed him of leandra's intentions.
alome also added somewhat further against oseph, though it was no more
than a calumny, that he had often had criminal conversation with ariamne.
he reason of her saying so was this, that she for a long time bare her ill-will
for when they had differences with one another, ariamne took great freedoms,
and reproached the rest for the meanness of their birth. ut erod, whose
affection to ariamne was always very warm, was presently disturbed at this,
and could not bear the torments of jealousy, but was still restrained from doing
any rash thing to her by the love he had for her yet did his vehement affection
and jealousy together make him ask ariamne by herself about this matter of
oseph but she denied it upon her oath, and said all that an innocent woman
could possibly say in her own defence so that by little and little the king was
prevailed upon to drop the suspicion, and left off his anger at her and being
overcome with his passion for his wife, he made an apology to her for having
seemed to believe what he had heard about her, and returned her a great many
acknowledgments of her modest behavior, and professed the etraordinary
affection and kindness he had for her, till at last, as is usual between lovers,
they both fell into tears, and embraced one another with a most tender affection.
ut as the king gave more and more assurances of his belief of her fidelity, and
endeavored to draw her to a like confidence in him, ariamne said, "et was
not that command thou gavest, that if any harm came to thee from ntony, ,
who had been no occasion of it, should perish with thee, a sign of thy love to
me" hen these words were fallen from her, the king was shocked at them,
and presently let her go out of his arms, and cried out, and tore his hair with his
own hands, and said, that now he had an evident demonstration that oseph had

had criminal conversation with his wife for that he would never have uttered
what he had told him alone by himself, unless there had been such a great
familiarity and firm confidence between them. nd while he was in this passion
he had liked to have killed his wife but being still overborne by his love to her,
he restrained this his passion, though not without a lasting grief and
disquietness of mind. owever, he gave order to slay oseph, without
permitting him to come into his sight and as for leandra, he bound her, and
kept her in custody, as the cause of all this mischief.

. ow at this time the affairs of yria were in confusion by leopatra's
constant persuasions to ntony to make an attempt upon every body's
dominions for she persuaded him to take those dominions away from their
several princes, and bestow them upon her and she had a mighty influence
upon him, by reason of his being enslaved to her by his affections. he was also
by nature very covetous, and stuck at no wickedness. he had already poisoned
her brother, because she knew that he was to be king of gypt, and this when
he was but fifteen years old and she got her sister rsinoe to be slain, by the
means of ntony, when she was a supplicant at iana's temple at phesus for
if there were but any hopes of getting money, she would violate both temples
and sepulchres. or was there any holy place that was esteemed the most
inviolable, from which she would not fetch the ornaments it had in it nor any
place so profane, but was to suffer the most flagitious treatment possible from
her, if it could but contribute somewhat to the covetous humor of this wicked
creature yet did not all this suffice so etravagant a woman, who was a slave to
her lusts, but she still imagined that she wanted every thing she could think of,
and did her utmost to gain it for which reason she hurried ntony on
perpetually to deprive others of their dominions, and give them to her. nd as
she went over yria with him, she contrived to get it into her possession so he
slew ysanias, the son of tolemy, accusing him of his bringing the arthians
upon those countries. he also petitioned ntony to give her udea and rabia
and, in order thereto, desired him to take these countries away from their
present governors. s for ntony, he was so entirely overcome by this woman,
that one would not think her conversation only could do it, but that he was

some way or other bewitched to do whatsoever she would have him yet did the
grossest parts of her injustice make him so ashamed, that he would not always
hearken to her to do those flagrant enormities she would have persuaded him
to. hat therefore he might not totally deny her, nor, by doing every thing
which she enjoined him, appear openly to be an ill man, he took some parts of
each of those countries away from their former governors, and gave them to
her. hus he gave her the cities that were within the river leutherus, as far as
gypt, ecepting yre and idon, which he knew to have been free cities from
their ancestors, although she pressed him very often to bestow those on her
also.
. hen leopatra had obtained thus much, and had accompanied ntony
in his epedition to rmenia as far as uphrates, she returned back, and came
to pamia and amascus, and passed on to udea, where erod met her, and
farmed of her her parts of rabia, and those revenues that came to her from the
region about ericho. his country bears that balsam, which is the most
precious drug that is there, and grows there alone. he place bears also palm
trees, both many in number, and those ecellent in their kind. hen she was
there, and was very often with erod, she endeavored to have criminal
conversation with the king nor did she affect secrecy in the indulgence of such
sort of pleasures and perhaps she had in some measure a passion of love to
him or rather, what is most probable, she laid a treacherous snare for him, by
aiming to obtain such adulterous conversation from him however, upon the
whole, she seemed overcome with love to him. ow erod had a great while
borne no good-will to leopatra, as knowing that she was a woman irksome to
all and at that time he thought her particularly worthy of his hatred, if this
attempt proceeded out of lust he had also thought of preventing her intrigues,
by putting her to death, if such were her endeavors. owever, he refused to
comply with her proposals, and called a counsel of his friends to consult with
them whether he should not kill her, now he had her in his power for that he
should thereby deliver all those from a multitude of evils to whom she was
already become irksome, and was epected to be still so for the time to come
and that this very thing would be much for the advantage of ntony himself,
since she would certainly not be faithful to him, in case any such season or
necessity should come upon him as that he should stand in need of her fidelity.
ut when he thought to follow this advice, his friends would not let him and

. ut ntony subdued rmenia, and sent rtabazes, the son of igranes, in

. ut then, as to the tributes which erod was to pay leopatra for that

rabia, whose tribute erod had undertaken to pay her, for some time indeed

. ereupon erod held himself ready to go against the king of rabia,
because of his ingratitude to him, and because, after all, he would do nothing
that was just to him, although erod made the oman war an occasion of
delaying his own for the battle at ctium was now epected, which fell into

the hundred eighty and seventh olympiad, where aesar and ntony were to
fight for the supreme power of the world but erod having enjoyed a country

taes, and raised great armies therewith, got together a body of men, and

and from leopatra, how perfidious he was for this was what leopatra
desired, who thought it for her own advantage that these two kings should do
one another as great mischief as possible. pon this message from ntony,
erod returned back, but kept his army with him, in order to invade rabia

marched to iospolis, whither the rabians came also to meet them, for they

whereupon a consternation fell upon the rabians for when they perceived that

ews unepectedly, when they were fatigued, and thought they had already

camp was taken so that the rabians had unepectedly a most glorious
success, having gained that victory which of themselves they were no way
likely to have gained, and slaying a great part of the enemy's army whence

many parts of rabia, and distress them by sudden incursions, while he
encamped among the mountains, and avoided by any means to come to a

. t this time it was that the fight happened at ctium, between ctavius
aesar and ntony, in the seventh year of the reign of erod,    and then it was
also that there was an earthquake in udea, such a one as had not happened at
any other time, and which earthquake brought a great destruction upon the
cattle in that country. bout ten thousand men also perished by the fall of
houses but the army, which lodged in the field, received no damage by this sad
accident. hen the rabians were informed of this, and when those that hated
the ews, and pleased themselves with aggravating the reports, told them of it,
they raised their spirits, as if their enemy's country was quite overthrown, and
the men were utterly destroyed, and thought there now remained nothing that

to them after all this had happened, to make peace with them, and slew them,
and came with great alacrity against their army but the ews durst not
withstand them, and were so cast down by the calamities they were under, that
they took no care of their affairs, but gave up themselves to despair for they
had no hope that they should be upon a level again with them in battles, nor
obtain any assistance elsewhere, while their affairs at home were in such great
distress also. hen matters were in this condition, the king persuaded the
commanders by his words, and tried to raise their spirits, which were quite
sunk and first he endeavored to encourage and embolden some of the better
sort beforehand, and then ventured to make a speech to the multitude, which he

had before avoided to do, lest he should find them uneasy thereat, because of

multitude, in the manner following—
. "ou are not unacquainted, my fellow soldiers, that we have had, not
long since, many accidents that have put a stop to what we are about, and it is
probable that even those that are most distinguished above others for their
courage can hardly keep up their spirits in such circumstances but since we
cannot avoid fighting, and nothing that hath happened is of such a nature but it
may by ourselves be recovered into a good state, and this by one brave action
only well performed,  have proposed to myself both to give you some
encouragement, and, at the same time, some information both which parts of
my design will tend to this point that you may still continue in your own
proper fortitude.  will then, in the first place, demonstrate to you that this war
is a just one on our side, and that on this account it is a war of necessity, and
occasioned by the injustice of our adversaries for if you be once satisfied of
this, it will be a real cause of alacrity to you after which  will further
demonstrate, that the misfortunes we are under are of no great consequence,
and that we have the greatest reason to hope for victory.  shall begin with the
first, and appeal to yourselves as witnesses to what  shall say. ou are not
ignorant certainly of the wickedness of the rabians, which is to that degree as
to appear incredible to all other men, and to include somewhat that shows the
grossest barbarity and ignorance of od. he chief things wherein they have
affronted us have arisen from covetousness and envy and they have attacked us
in an insidious manner, and on the sudden. nd what occasion is there for me to
mention many instances of such their procedure hen they were in danger of
losing their own government of themselves, and of being slaves to leopatra,
what others were they that freed them from that fear for it was the friendship 
had with ntony, and the kind disposition he was in towards us, that hath been
the occasion that even these rabians have not been utterly undone, ntony
being unwilling to undertake any thing which might be suspected by us of
unkindness but when he had a mind to bestow some parts of each of our
dominions on leopatra,  also managed that matter so, that by giving him
presents of my own,  might obtain a security to both nations, while  undertook
myself to answer for the money, and gave him two hundred talents, and became
surety for those two hundred more which were imposed upon the land that was
subject to this tribute and this they have defrauded us of, although it was not
reasonable that ews should pay tribute to any man living, or allow part of their
land to be taable but although that was to be, yet ought we not to pay tribute
for these rabians, whom we have ourselves preserved nor is it fit that they,
who have professed (and that with great integrity and sense of our kindness)
that it is by our means that they keep their principality, should injure us, and

deprive us of what is our due, and this while we have been still not their
enemies, but their friends. nd whereas observation of covenants takes place
among the bitterest enemies, but among friends is absolutely necessary, this is
not observed among these men, who think gain to be the best of all things, let it
be by any means whatsoever, and that injustice is no harm, if they may but get
money by it is it therefore a question with you, whether the unjust are to be
punished or not when od himself hath declared his mind that so it ought to
be, and hath commanded that we ever should hate injuries and injustice, which
is not only just, but necessary, in wars between several nations for these
rabians have done what both the reeks and barbarians own to be an instance
of the grossest wickedness, with regard to our ambassadors, which they have
beheaded, while the reeks declare that such ambassadors are sacred and
inviolable.    nd for ourselves, we have learned from od the most ecellent of
our doctrines, and the most holy part of our law, by angels or ambassadors for
this name brings od to the knowledge of mankind, and is sufficient to
reconcile enemies one to another. hat wickedness then can be greater than the
slaughter of ambassadors, who come to treat about doing what is right nd
when such have been their actions, how is it possible they can either live
securely in common life, or be successful in war n my opinion, this is
impossible but perhaps some will say, that what is holy, and what is righteous,
is indeed on our side, but that the rabians are either more courageous or more
numerous than we are. ow, as to this, in the first place, it is not fit for us to
say so, for with whom is what is righteous, with them is od himself now
where od is, there is both multitude and courage. ut to eamine our own
circumstances a little, we were conquerors in the first battle and when we
fought again, they were not able to oppose us, but ran away, and could not
endure our attacks or our courage but when we had conquered them, then came
thenion, and made war against us without declaring it and pray, is this an
instance of their manhood or is it not a second instance of their wickedness
and treachery hy are we therefore of less courage, on account of that which
ought to inspire us with stronger hopes and why are we terrified at these, who,
when they fight upon the level, are continually beaten, and when they seem to
be conquerors, they gain it by wickedness and if we suppose that any one
should deem them to be men of real courage, will not he be ecited by that very
consideration to do his utmost against them for true valor is not shown by
fighting against weak persons, but in being able to overcome the most hardy.
ut then if the distresses we are ourselves under, and the miseries that have
come by the earthquake, hath affrighted any one, let him consider, in the first
place, that this very thing will deceive the rabians, by their supposal that what
hath befallen us is greater than it really is. oreover, it is not right that the
same thing that emboldens them should discourage us for these men, you see,

do not derive their alacrity from any advantageous virtue of their own, but from
their hope, as to us, that we are quite cast down by our misfortunes but when
we boldly march against them, we shall soon pull down their insolent conceit of
themselves, and shall gain this by attacking them, that they will not be so
insolent when we come to the battle for our distresses are not so great, nor is
what hath happened all indication of the anger of od against us, as some
imagine for such things are accidental, and adversities that come in the usual
course of things and if we allow that this was done by the will of od, we must
allow that it is now over by his will also, and that he is satisfied with what hath
already happened for had he been willing to afflict us still more thereby, he had
not changed his mind so soon. nd as for the war we are engaged in, he hath
himself demonstrated that he is willing it should go on, and that he knows it to
be a just war for while some of the people in the country have perished, all you
who were in arms have suffered nothing, but are all preserved alive whereby
od makes it plain to us, that if you had universally, with your children and
wives, been in the army, it had come to pass that you had not undergone any
thing that would have much hurt you. onsider these things, and, what is more
than all the rest, that you have od at all times for your rotector and
prosecute these men with a just bravery, who, in point of friendship, are unjust,
in their battles perfidious, towards ambassadors impious, and always inferior to
you in valor."
. hen the ews heard this speech, they were much raised in their minds,
and more disposed to fight than before. o erod, when he had offered the
sacrifices appointed by the law,    made haste, and took them, and led them
against the rabians and in order to that passed over ordan, and pitched his
camp near to that of the enemy. e also thought fit to seize upon a certain
castle that lay in the midst of them, as hoping it would be for his advantage, and
would the sooner produce a battle and that if there were occasion for delay, he
should by it have his camp fortified and as the rabians had the same
intentions upon that place, a contest arose about it at first they were but
skirmishes, after which there came more soldiers, and it proved a sort of fight,
and some fell on both sides, till those of the rabian side were beaten and
retreated. his was no small encouragement to the ews immediately and when
erod observed that the enemy's army was disposed to any thing rather than to

when they were forced out of their trenches, they went out in disorder, and had

because they were more in number than the ews, and because they were in
such a disposition of war that they were under a necessity of coming on boldly
so they came to a terrible battle, while not a few fell on each side. owever, at

length the rabians fled and so great a slaughter was made upon their being
routed, that they were not only killed by their enemies, but became the authors

want of necessaries, and especially of water. he ews pursued them, but could
not get in with them, but sat round about the bulwark, and watched any
assistance that would get in to them, and prevented any there, that had a mind
to it, from running away.
. hen the rabians were in these circumstances, they sent ambassadors
to erod, in the first place, to propose terms of accommodation, and after that
to offer him, so pressing was their thirst upon them, to undergo whatsoever he
pleased, if he would free them from their present distress but he would admit

whatever, being very desirous to revenge those unjust actions which they had
been guilty of towards his nation. o they were necessitated by other motives,
and particularly by their thirst, to come out, and deliver themselves up to him,
to be carried away captives and in five days' time the number of four thousand
were taken prisoners, while all the rest resolved to make a sally upon their
enemies, and to fight it out with them, choosing rather, if so it must be, to die
therein, than to perish gradually and ingloriously. hen they had taken this
resolution, they came out of their trenches, but could no way sustain the fight,

at the first onset there fell about seven thousand of them, after which stroke
they let all the courage they had put on before fall, and stood amazed at erod's
warlike spirit under his own calamities so for the future they yielded, and made
him ruler of their nation whereupon he was greatly elevated at so seasonable a
success, and returned home, taking great authority upon him, on account of so
bold and glorious an epedition as he had made.

. erod's other affairs were now very prosperous, and he was not to be easily

assaulted on any side. et did there come upon him a danger that would hazard
his entire dominions, after ntony had been beaten at the battle of ctium by
aesar ctavian] for at that time both erod's enemies and friends despaired
of his affairs, for it was not probable that he would remain without punishment,
who had showed so much friendship for ntony. o it happened that his friends
despaired, and had no hopes of his escape but for his enemies, they all
outwardly appeared to be troubled at his case, but were privately very glad of it,
as hoping to obtain a change for the better. s for erod himself he saw that
there was no one of royal dignity left but yrcanus, and therefore he thought it
would be for his advantage not to suffer him to be an obstacle in his way any
longer for that in case he himself survived, and escaped the danger he was in,
he thought it the safest way to put it out of the power of such a man to make
any attempt against him, at such junctures of affairs, as was more worthy of the
kingdom than himself and in case he should be slain by aesar, his envy
prompted him to desire to slay him that would otherwise be king after him.
. hile erod had these things in his mind, there was a certain occasion
afforded him for yrcanus was of so mild a temper, both then and at other
times, that he desired not to meddle with public affairs, nor to concern himself
with innovations, but left all to fortune, and contented himself with what that
afforded him but leandra his daughter] was a lover of strife, and was
eceeding desirous of a change of the government, and spake to her father not
to bear for ever erod's injurious treatment of their family, but to anticipate
their future hopes, as he safely might and desired him to write about these
matters to alchus, who was then governor of rabia, to receive them, and to
secure them from erod], for that if they went away, and erod's affairs
proved to be as it was likely they would be, by reason of aesar's enmity to
him, they should then be the only persons that could take the government and
this, both on account of the royal family they were of, and on account of the
good disposition of the multitude to them. hile she used these persuasions,
yrcanus put off her suit but as she showed that she was a woman, and a
contentious woman too, and would not desist either night or day, but would
always be speaking to him about these matters, and about erod's treacherous
designs, she at last prevailed with him to intrust ositheus, one of his friends,
with a letter, wherein his resolution was declared and he desired the rabian
governor to send to him some horsemen, who should receive him, and conduct
him to the lake sphaltites, which is from the bounds of erusalem three
hundred furlongs and he did therefore trust ositheus with this letter, because
he was a careful attendant on him, and on leandra, and had no small
occasions to bear ill-will to erod for he was a kinsman of one oseph, whom
he had slain, and a brother of those that were formerly slain at yre by ntony
yet could not these motives induce ositheus to serve yrcanus in this affair

for, preferring the hopes he had from the present king to those he had from him,
he gave erod the letter. o he took his kindness in good part, and bid him
besides do what he had already done, that is, go on in serving him, by rolling
up the epistle and sealing it again, and delivering it to alchus, and then to
bring back his letter in answer to it for it would be much better if he could
know alchus's intentions also. nd when ositheus was very ready to serve
him in this point also, the rabian governor returned back for answer, that he
would receive yrcanus, and all that should come with him, and even all the
ews that were of his party that he would, moreover, send forces sufficient to
secure them in their journey and that he should be in no want of any thing he
should desire. ow as soon as erod had received this letter, he immediately
sent for yrcanus, and questioned him about the league he had made with
alchus and when he denied it, he showed his letter to the anhedrim, and put
the man to death immediately.
. nd this account we give the reader, as it is contained in the
commentaries of king erod but other historians do not agree with them, for
they suppose that erod did not find, but rather made this an occasion for thus
putting him to death, and that by treacherously laying a snare for him for thus
do they write that erod and he were once at a treat, and that erod had given
no occasion to suspect that he was displeased at him], but put this question to
yrcanus, whether he had received any letters from alchus and when he
answered that he had received letters, but those of salutation only and when he
asked further, whether he had not received any presents from him and when he
had replied that he had received no more than four horses to ride on, which
alchus had sent him they pretended that erod charged these upon him as
the crimes of bribery and treason, and gave order that he should be led away
and slain. nd in order to demonstrate that he had been guilty of no offense,
when he was thus brought to his end, they alleged how mild his temper had
been, and that even in his youth he had never given any demonstration of
boldness or rashness, and that the case was the same when he came to be king,
but that he even then committed the management of the greatest part of public
affairs to ntipater and that he was now above fourscore years old, and knew
that erod's government was in a secure state. e also came over uphrates,
and left those who greatly honored him beyond that river, though he were to be
entirely under erod's government and that it was a most incredible thing that
he should enterprise any thing by way of innovation, and not at all agreeable to
his temper, but that this was a plot of erod's contrivance.
. nd this was the fate of yrcanus and thus did he end his life, after he
had endured various and manifold turns of fortune in his lifetime. or he was
made high priest of the ewish nation in the beginning of his mother
leandra's reign, who held the government nine years and when, after his

mother's death, he took the kingdom himself, and held it three months, he lost
it, by the means of his brother ristobulus. e was then restored by ompey,
and received all sorts of honor from him, and enjoyed them forty years but
when he was again deprived by ntigonus, and was maimed in his body, he
was made a captive by the arthians, and thence returned home again after
some time, on account of the hopes that erod had given him none of which
came to pass according to his epectation, but he still conflicted with many
misfortunes through the whole course of his life and, what was the heaviest
calamity of all, as we have related already, he came to an end which was
undeserved by him. is character appeared to be that of a man of a mild and
moderate disposition, and suffered the administration of affairs to be generally
done by others under him. e was averse to much meddling with the public,
nor had shrewdness enough to govern a kingdom. nd both ntipater and
erod came to their greatness by reason of his mildness and at last he met with
such an end from them as was not agreeable either to justice or piety.
. ow erod, as soon as he had put yrcanus out of the way, made haste
to aesar and because he could not have any hopes of kindness from him, on
account of the friendship he had for ntony, he had a suspicion of leandra,
lest she should take this opportunity to bring the multitude to a revolt, and
introduce a sedition into the affairs of the kingdom so he committed the care of
every thing to his brother heroras, and placed his mother ypros, and his
sister alome], and the whole family at asada, and gave him a charge, that if
he should hear any sad news about him, he should take care of the government.
ut as to ariamne his wife, because of the misunderstanding between her and
his sister, and his sister's mother, which made it impossible for them to live
together, he placed her at leandrium, with leandra her mother, and left his
treasurer oseph and ohemus of turea to take care of that fortress. hese two
had been very faithful to him from the beginning, and were now left as a guard
to the women. hey also had it in charge, that if they should hear any mischief
had befallen him, they should kill them both, and, as far as they were able, to
preserve the kingdom for his sons, and for his brother heroras.
. hen he had given them this charge, he made haste to hodes, to meet
aesar and when he had sailed to that city, he took off his diadem, but remitted
nothing else of his usual dignity. nd when, upon his meeting him, he desired
that he would let him speak to him, he therein ehibited a much more noble
specimen of a great soul for he did not betake himself to supplications, as men
usually do upon such occasions, nor offered him any petition, as if he were an
offender but, after an undaunted manner, gave an account of what he had done
for he spake thus to aesar that he had the greatest friendship for ntony, and
did every thing he could that he might attain the government that he was not
indeed in the army with him, because the rabians had diverted him but that

he had sent him both money and corn, which was but too little in comparison of
what he ought to have done for him "for if a man owns himself to be another's
friend, and knows him to be a benefactor, he is obliged to hazard every thing, to
use every faculty of his soul, every member of his body, and all the wealth he
hath, for him, in which  confess  have been too deficient. owever,  am
conscious to myself, that so far  have done right, that  have not deserted him
upon his defeat at ctium nor upon the evident change of his fortune have 
transferred my hopes from him to another, but have preserved myself, though
not as a valuable fellow soldier, yet certainly as a faithful counselor, to ntony,
when  demonstrated to him that the only way that he had to save himself, and
not to lose all his authority, was to slay leopatra for when she was once dead,
there would be room for him to retain his authority, and rather to bring thee to
make a composition with him, than to continue at enmity any longer. one of
which advises would he attend to, but preferred his own rash resolution before
them, which have happened unprofitably for him, but profitably for thee. ow,
therefore, in case thou determinest about me, and my alacrity in serving
ntony, according to thy anger at him,  own there is no room for me to deny
what  have done, nor will  be ashamed to own, and that publicly too, that 
had a great kindness for him. ut if thou wilt put him out of the case, and only
eamine how  behave myself to my benefactors in general, and what sort of
friend  am, thou wilt find by eperience that we shall do and be the same to
thyself, for it is but changing the names, and the firmness of friendship that we
shall bear to thee will not be disapproved by thee."
. y this speech, and by his behavior, which showed aesar the frankness
of his mind, he greatly gained upon him, who was himself of a generous and
magnificent temper, insomuch that those very actions, which were the
foundation of the accusation against him, procured him aesar's good-will.
ccordingly, he restored him his diadem again and encouraged him to ehibit
himself as great a friend to himself as he had been to ntony, and then had him
in great esteem. oreover, he added this, that uintus idius had written to
him that erod had very readily assisted him in the affair of the gladiators. o
when he had obtained such a kind reception, and had, beyond all his hopes,
procured his crown to be more entirely and firmly settled upon him than ever
by aesar's donation, as well as by that decree of the omans, which aesar
took care to procure for his greater security, he conducted aesar on his way to
gypt, and made presents, even beyond his ability, to both him and his friends,
and in general behaved himself with great magnanimity. e also desired that
aesar would not put to death one leander, who had been a companion of
ntony but aesar had sworn to put him to death, and so he could not obtain
that his petition. nd now he returned to udea again with greater honor and
assurance than ever, and affrighted those that had epectations to the contrary,

as still acquiring from his very dangers greater splendor than before, by the
favor of od to him. o he prepared for the reception of aesar, as he was
going out of yria to invade gypt and when he came, he entertained him at
tolemais with all royal magnificence. e also bestowed presents on the army,
and brought them provisions in abundance. e also proved to be one of
aesar's most cordial friends, and put the army in array, and rode along with
aesar, and had a hundred and fifty men, well appointed in all respects, after a
rich and sumptuous manner, for the better reception of him and his friends. e
also provided them with what they should want, as they passed over the dry

soldiers stood in the greatest need of and besides, he presented aesar with
eight hundred talents, and procured to himself the good-will of them all,
because he was assisting to them in a much greater and more splendid degree
than the kingdom he had obtained could afford by which means he more and
more demonstrated to aesar the firmness of his friendship, and his readiness
to assist him and what was of the greatest advantage to him was this, that his
liberality came at a seasonable time also. nd when they returned again out of
gypt, his assistances were no way inferior to the good offices he had formerly
done them.
