. s king erod was very zealous in the administration of his entire
government, and desirous to put a stop to particular acts of injustice which were
done by criminals about the city and country, he made a law, no way like our
original laws, and which he enacted of himself, to epose house-breakers to be
ejected out of his kingdom which punishment was not only grievous to be
borne by the offenders, but contained in it a dissolution of the customs of our

forefathers for this slavery to foreigners, and such as did not live after the
manner of ews, and this necessity that they were under to do whatsoever such
men should command, was an offense against our religious settlement, rather
than a punishment to such as were found to have offended, such a punishment
being avoided in our original laws for those laws ordain, that the thief shall
restore fourfold and that if he have not so much, he shall be sold indeed, but
not to foreigners, nor so that he be under perpetual slavery, for he must have
been released after si years. ut this law, thus enacted, in order to introduce a
severe and illegal punishment, seemed to be a piece of insolence of erod,
when he did not act as a king, but as a tyrant, and thus contemptuously, and
without any regard to his subjects, did he venture to introduce such a
punishment. ow this penalty, thus brought into practice, was like erod's
other actions, and became a part of his accusation, and an occasion of the
hatred he lay under.
. ow at this time it was that he sailed to taly, as very desirous to meet
with aesar, and to see his sons who lived at ome and aesar was not only
very obliging to him in other respects, but delivered him his sons again, that he
might take them home with him, as having already completed themselves in the
sciences but as soon as the young men were come from taly, the multitude
were very desirous to see them, and they became conspicuous among them all,
as adorned with great blessings of fortune, and having the countenances of
persons of royal dignity. o they soon appeared to be the objects of envy to
alome, the king's sister, and to such as had raised calumnies against
ariamne for they were suspicious, that when these came to the government,
they should be punished for the wickedness they had been guilty of against
their mother so they made this very fear of theirs a motive to raise calumnies
against them also. hey gave it out that they were not pleased with their father's
company, because he had put their mother to death, as if it were not agreeable
to piety to appear to converse with their mother's murderer. ow, by carrying
these stories that had indeed a true foundation in the fact], but were only built
on probabilities as to the present accusation, they were able to do them
mischief, and to make erod take away that kindness from his sons which he
had before borne to them for they did not say these things to him openly, but
scattered abroad such words, among the rest of the multitude from which
words, when carried to erod, he was induced at last] to hate them, and which
natural affection itself, even in length of time, was not able to overcome yet
was the king at that time in a condition to prefer the natural affection of a father
before all the suspicions and calumnies his sons lay under. o he respected
them as he ought to do, and married them to wives, now they were of an age
suitable thereto. o ristobulus he gave for a wife ernice, alome's daughter
and to leander, laphyra, the daughter of rchelaus, king of appadocia.

,  
   
. hen erod had despatched these affairs, and he understood that arcus
grippa had sailed again out of taly into sia, he made haste to him, and
besought him to come to him into his kingdom, and to partake of what he might
justly epect from one that had been his guest, and was his friend. his request
he greatly pressed, and to it grippa agreed, and came into udea whereupon
erod omitted nothing that might please him. e entertained him in his new-
built cities, and showed him the edifices he had built, and provided all sorts of
the best and most costly dainties for him and his friends, and that at ebaste and
esarea, about that port that he had built, and at the fortresses which he had
erected at great epenses, leandrium, and erodium, and yrcania. e also
conducted him to the city erusalem, where all the people met him in their
festival garments, and received him with acclamations. grippa also offered a
hecatomb of sacrifices to od and feasted the people, without omitting any of
the greatest dainties that could be gotten. e also took so much pleasure there,
that he abode many days with them, and would willingly have staid longer, but
that the season of the year made him make haste away for as winter was
coming on, he thought it not safe to go to sea later, and yet he was of necessity
to return again to onia.
. o grippa went away, when erod had bestowed on him, and on the
principal of those that were with him, many presents but king erod, when he
had passed the winter in his own dominions, made haste to get to him again in
the spring, when he knew he designed to go to a campaign at the osphorus. o
when he had sailed by hodes and by os, he touched at esbos, as thinking he
should have overtaken grippa there but he was taken short here by a north
wind, which hindered his ship from going to the shore so he continued many
days at hius, and there he kindly treated a great many that came to him, and
obliged them by giving them royal gifts. nd when he saw that the portico of
the city was fallen down, which as it was overthrown in the ithridatic war,
and was very large and fine building, so was it not so easy to rebuild that as it
was the rest, yet did he furnish a sum not only large enough for that purpose,
but what was more than sufficient to finish the building and ordered them not
to overlook that portico, but to rebuild it quickly, that so the city might recover
its proper ornaments. nd when the high winds were laid, he sailed to
itylene, and thence to yzantium and when he heard that grippa was sailed
beyond the yanean rocks, he made all the haste possible to overtake him, and

came up with him about ynope, in ontus. e was seen sailing by the ship-
men most unepectedly, but appeared to their great joy and many friendly
salutations there were between them, insomuch that grippa thought he had
received the greatest marks of the king's kindness and humanity towards him
possible, since the king had come so long a voyage, and at a very proper
season, for his assistance, and had left the government of his own dominions,
and thought it more worth his while to come to him. ccordingly, erod was
all in all to grippa, in the management of the war, and a great assistant in civil
affairs, and in giving him counsel as to particular matters. e was also a
pleasant companion for him when he relaed himself, and a joint partaker with
him in all things ill troubles because of his kindness, and in prosperity because
of the respect grippa had for him. ow as soon as those affairs of ontus were
finished, for whose sake grippa was sent thither, they did not think fit to
return by sea, but passed through aphlagonia and appadocia they then
traveled thence over great hrygia, and came to phesus, and then they sailed
from phesus to amos. nd indeed the king bestowed a great many benefits
on every city that he came to, according as they stood in need of them for as
for those that wanted either money or kind treatment, he was not wanting to
them but he supplied the former himself out of his own epenses he also
became an intercessor with grippa for all such as sought after his favor, and
he brought things so about, that the petitioners failed in none of their suits to
him, grippa being himself of a good disposition, and of great generosity, and
ready to grant all such requests as might be advantageous to the petitioners,
provided they were not to the detriment of others. he inclination of the king
was of great weight also, and still ecited grippa, who was himself ready to
do good for he made a reconciliation between the people of lium, at whom he
was angry, and paid what money the people of hius owed aesar's
procurators, and discharged them of their tributes and helped all others,
according as their several necessities required.
. ut now, when grippa and erod were in onia, a great multitude of
ews, who dwelt in their cities, came to them, and laying hold of the
opportunity and the liberty now given them, laid before them the injuries which
they suffered, while they were not permitted to use their own laws, but were
compelled to prosecute their law-suits, by the ill usage of the judges, upon their
holy days, and were deprived of the money they used to lay up at erusalem,
and were forced into the army, and upon such other offices as obliged them to
spend their sacred money from which burdens they always used to be freed by
the omans, who had still permitted them to live according to their own laws.
hen this clamor was made, the king desired of grippa that he would hear
their cause, and assigned icolaus, one of his friends, to plead for those their
privileges. ccordingly, when grippa had called the principal of the omans,

and such of the kings and rulers as were there, to be his assessors, icolaus
stood up, and pleaded for the ews, as follows "t is of necessity incumbent on
such as are in distress to have recourse to those that have it in their power to
free them from those injuries they lie under and for those that now are
complainants, they approach you with great assurance for as they have
formerly often obtained your favor, so far as they have even wished to have it,
they now only entreat that you, who have been the donors, will take care that
those favors you have already granted them may not be taken away from them.
e have received these favors from you, who alone have power to grant them,
but have them taken from us by such as are no greater than ourselves, and by
such as we know are as much subjects as we are and certainly, if we have been
vouchsafed great favors, it is to our commendation who have obtained them, as
having been found deserving of such great favors and if those favors be but
small ones, it would be barbarous for the donors not to confirm them to us. nd
for those that are the hindrance of the ews, and use them reproachfully, it is
evident that they affront both the receivers, while they will not allow those to
be worthy men to whom their ecellent rulers themselves have borne their
testimony, and the donors, while they desire those favors already granted may
be abrogated. ow if any one should ask these entiles themselves, which of
the two things they would choose to part with, their lives, or the customs of
their forefathers, their solemnities, their sacrifices, their festivals, which they
celebrated in honor of those they suppose to be gods  know very well that
they would choose to suffer any thing whatsoever rather than a dissolution of
any of the customs of their forefathers for a great many of them have rather
chosen to go to war on that account, as very solicitous not to transgress in those
matters. nd indeed we take an estimate of that happiness which all mankind
do now enjoy by your means from this very thing, that we are allowed every
one to worship as our own institutions require, and yet to live in peace] and
although they would not be thus treated themselves, yet do they endeavor to
compel others to comply with them, as if it were not as great an instance of
impiety profanely to dissolve the religious solemnities of any others, as to be
negligent in the observation of their own towards their gods. nd let us now
consider the one of these practices. s there any people, or city, or community
of men, to whom your government and the oman power does not appear to be
the greatest blessing s there any one that can desire to make void the favors
they have granted o one is certainly so mad for there are no men but such as
have been partakers of their favors, both public and private and indeed those
that take away what you have granted, can have no assurance but every one of
their own grants made them by you may be taken from them also which grants
of yours can yet never be sufficiently valued for if they consider the old
governments under kings, together with your present government, besides the

great number of benefits which this government hath bestowed on them, in
order to their happiness, this is instead of all the rest, that they appear to be no
longer in a state of slavery, but of freedom. ow the privileges we desire, even
when we are in the best circumstances, are not such as deserve to be envied, for
we are indeed in a prosperous state by your means, but this is only in common
with others and it is no more than this which we desire, to preserve our religion
without any prohibition which as it appears not in itself a privilege to be
envied us, so it is for the advantage of those that grant it to us for if the
ivinity delights in being honored, it must delight in those that permit them to
be honored. nd there are none of our customs which are inhuman, but all
tending to piety, and devoted to the preservation of justice nor do we conceal
those injunctions of ours by which we govern our lives, they being memorials
of piety, and of a friendly conversation among men. nd the seventh day we set
apart from labor it is dedicated to the learning of our customs and laws,    we
thinking it proper to reflect on them, as well as on any good] thing else, in
order to our avoiding of sin. f any one therefore eamine into our observances,
he will find they are good in themselves, and that they are ancient also, though
some think otherwise, insomuch that those who have received them cannot
easily be brought to depart from them, out of that honor they pay to the length
of time they have religiously enjoyed them and observed them. ow our
adversaries take these our privileges away in the way of injustice they
violently seize upon that money of ours which is owed to od, and called
sacred money, and this openly, after a sacrilegious manner and they impose
tributes upon us, and bring us before tribunals on holy days, and then require
other like debts of us, not because the contracts require it, and for their own
advantage, but because they would put an affront on our religion, of which they
are conscious as well as we, and have indulged themselves in an unjust, and to
them involuntary, hatred for your government over all is one, tending to the
establishing of benevolence, and abolishing of ill-will among such as are
disposed to it. his is therefore what we implore from thee, most ecellent
grippa, that we may not be ill-treated that we may not be abused that we
may not be hindered from making use of our own customs, nor be despoiled of
our goods, nor be forced by these men to do what we ourselves force nobody to
do for these privileges of ours are not only according to justice, but have
formerly been granted us by you. nd we are able to read to you many decrees
of the senate, and the tables that contain them, which are still etant in the
capitol, concerning these things, which it is evident were granted after you had
eperience of our fidelity towards you, which ought to be valued, though no
such fidelity had been for you have hitherto preserved what people were in
possession of, not to us only, but almost to all men, and have added greater
advantages than they could have hoped for, and thereby your government is

become a great advantage to them. nd if any one were able to enumerate the
prosperity you have conferred on every nation, which they possess by your
means, he could never put an end to his discourse but that we may demonstrate
that we are not unworthy of all those advantages we have obtained, it will be
sufficient for us, to say nothing of other things, but to speak freely of this king
who now governs us, and is now one of thy assessors and indeed in what
instance of good-will, as to your house, hath he been deficient hat mark of
fidelity to it hath he omitted hat token of honor hath he not devised hat
occasion for his assistance of you hath he not regarded at the very first hat
hindereth therefore, but that your kindnesses may be as numerous as his so
great benefits to you have been t may also perhaps be fit not here to pass over
in silence the valor of his father ntipater, who, when aesar made an
epedition into gypt, assisted him with two thousand armed men, and proved
inferior to none, neither in the battles on land, nor in the management of the
navy and what need  say any thing of how great weight those soldiers were at
that juncture or how many and how great presents they were vouchsafed by
aesar nd truly  ought before now to have mentioned the epistles which
aesar wrote to the senate and how ntipater had honors, and the freedom of
the city of ome, bestowed upon him for these are demonstrations both that
we have received these favors by our own deserts, and do on that account
petition thee for thy confirmation of them, from whom we had reason to hope
for them, though they had not been given us before, both out of regard to our
king's disposition towards you, and your disposition towards him. nd further,
we have been informed by those ews that were there with what kindness thou
camest into our country, and how thou offeredst the most perfect sacrifices to
od, and honoredst him with remarkable vows, and how thou gavest the people
a feast, and acceptedst of their own hospitable presents to thee. e ought to
esteem all these kind entertainments made both by our nation and to our city, to
a man who is the ruler and manager of so much of the public affairs, as
indications of that friendship which thou hast returned to the ewish nation, and
which hath been procured them by the family of erod. o we put thee in mind
of these things in the presence of the king, now sitting by thee, and make our
request for no more but this, that what you have given us yourselves you will
not see taken away by others from us."
. hen icolaus had made this speech, there was no opposition made to it
by the reeks, for this was not an inquiry made, as in a court of justice, but an
intercession to prevent violence to be offered to the ews any longer nor did
the reeks make any defence of themselves, or deny what it was supposed they
had done. heir pretence was no more than this, that while the ews inhabited
in their country, they were entirely unjust to them in not joining in their
worship] but they demonstrated their generosity in this, that though they

worshipped according to their institutions, they did nothing that ought to grieve
them. o when grippa perceived that they had been oppressed by violence, he
made this answer that, on account of erod's good-will and friendship, he was
ready to grant the ews whatsoever they should ask him, and that their requests
seemed to him in themselves just and that if they requested any thing further,
he should not scruple to grant it them, provided they were no way to the
detriment of the oman government but that while their request was no more
than this, that what privileges they had already given them might not be
abrogated, he confirmed this to them, that they might continue in the
observation of their own customs, without any one offering them the least
injury. nd when he had said thus, he dissolved the assembly upon which
erod stood up and saluted him, and gave him thanks for the kind disposition
he showed to them. grippa also took this in a very obliging manner, and
saluted him again, and embraced him in his arms after which he went away
from esbos but the king determined to sail from amos to his own country
and when he had taken his leave of grippa, he pursued his voyage, and landed
at esarea in a few days' time, as having favorable winds from whence he
went to erusalem, and there gathered all the people together to an assembly,
not a few being there out of the country also. o he came to them, and gave
them a particular account of all his journey, and of the affairs of all the ews in
sia, how by his means they would live without injurious treatment for the
time to come. e also told them of the entire good fortune he had met with and
how he had administered the government, and had not neglected any thing
which was for their advantage and as he was very joyful, he now remitted to
them the fourth part of their taes for the last year. ccordingly, they were so
pleased with his favor and speech to them, that they went their ways with great
gladness, and wished the king all manner of happiness.

. ut now the affairs in erod's family were in more and more disorder, and
became more severe upon him, by the hatred of alome to the young men
leander and ristobulus], which descended as it were by inheritance from
their mother ariamne] and as she had fully succeeded against their mother,
so she proceeded to that degree of madness and insolence, as to endeavor that
none of her posterity might be left alive, who might have it in their power to

revenge her death. he young men had also somewhat of a bold and uneasy
disposition towards their father occasioned by the remembrance of what their
mother had unjustly suffered, and by their own affectation of dominion. he old
grudge was also renewed and they cast reproaches on alome and heroras,
who requited the young men with malicious designs, and actually laid
treacherous snares for them. ow as for this hatred, it was equal on both sides,
but the manner of eerting that hatred was different for as for the young men,
they were rash, reproaching and affronting the others openly, and were
ineperienced enough to think it the most generous to declare their minds in
that undaunted manner but the others did not take that method, but made use of
calumnies after a subtle and a spiteful manner, still provoking the young men,
and imagining that their boldness might in time turn to the offering violence to
their father for inasmuch as they were not ashamed of the pretended crimes of
their mother, nor thought she suffered justly, these supposed that might at
length eceed all bounds, and induce them to think they ought to be avenged on
their father, though it were by despatching him with their own hands. t length
it came to this, that the whole city was full of their discourses, and, as is usual
in such contests, the unskilfulness of the young men was pitied but the
contrivance of alome was too hard for them, and what imputations she laid
upon them came to be believed, by means of their own conduct for they who
were so deeply affected with the death of their mother, that while they said both
she and themselves were in a miserable case, they vehemently complained of
her pitiable end, which indeed was truly such, and said that they were
themselves in a pitiable case also, because they were forced to live with those
that had been her murderers, and to be partakers with them.
. hese disorders increased greatly, and the king's absence abroad had
afforded a fit opportunity for that increase but as soon as erod was returned,
and had made the forementioned speech to the multitude, heroras and alome
let fall words immediately as if he were in great danger, and as if the young
men openly threatened that they would not spare him any longer, but revenge
their mother's death upon him. hey also added another circumstance, that their
hopes were fied on rchelaus, the king of appadocia, that they should be
able by his means to come to aesar, and accuse their father. pon hearing
such things, erod was immediately disturbed and indeed was the more
astonished, because the same things were related to him by some others also.
e then called to mind his former calamity, and considered that the disorders in
his family had hindered him from enjoying any comfort from those that were
dearest to him or from his wife whom he loved so well and suspecting that his
future troubles would soon be heavier and greater than those that were past, he
was in great confusion of mind for ivine rovidence had in reality conferred
upon him a great many outward advantages for his happiness, even beyond his

hopes but the troubles he had at home were such as he never epected to have
met with, and rendered him unfortunate nay, both sorts came upon him to such
a degree as no one could imagine, and made it a doubtful question, whether,
upon the comparison of both, he ought to have echanged so great a success of
outward good things for so great misfortunes at home, or whether he ought not
to have chosen to avoid the calamities relating to his family, though he had, for
a compensation, never been possessed of the admired grandeur of a kingdom.
. s he was thus disturbed and afflicted, in order to depress these young
men, he brought to court another of his sons, that was born to him when he was
a private man his name was ntipater yet did he not then indulge him as he
did afterwards, when he was quite overcome by him, and let him do every thing
as he pleased, but rather with a design of depressing the insolence of the sons of
ariamne, and managing this elevation of his so, that it might be for a warning
to them for this bold behavior of theirs he thought] would not be so great, if
they were once persuaded that the succession to the kingdom did not appertain
to them alone, or must of necessity come to them. o he introduced ntipater as
their antagonist, and imagined that he made a good provision for discouraging
their pride, and that after this was done to the young men, there might be a
proper season for epecting these to be of a better disposition but the event
proved otherwise than he intended, for the young men thought he did them a
very great injury and as ntipater was a shrewd man, when he had once
obtained this degree of freedom, and began to epect greater things than he had
before hoped for, he had but one single design in his head, and that was to
distress his brethren, and not at all to yield to them the pre-eminence, but to
keep close to his father, who was already alienated from them by the calumnies
he had heard about them, and ready to be wrought upon in any way his zeal
against them should advise him to pursue, that he might be continually more
and more severe against them. ccordingly, all the reports that were spread
abroad came from him, while he avoided himself the suspicion as if those
discoveries proceeded from him but he rather chose to make use of those
persons for his assistants that were unsuspected, and such as might be believed
to speak truth by reason of the good-will they bore to the king and indeed there
were already not a few who cultivated a friendship with ntipater, in hopes of
gaining somewhat by him, and these were the men who most of all persuaded
erod, because they appeared to speak thus out of their good-will to him and
with these joint accusations, which from various foundations supported one
another's veracity, the young men themselves afforded further occasions to
ntipater also for they were observed to shed tears often, on account of the
injury that was offered them, and had their mother in their mouths and among
their friends they ventured to reproach their father, as not acting justly by them
all which things were with an evil intention reserved in memory by ntipater

against a proper opportunity and when they were told to erod, with
aggravations, increased the disorder so much, that it brought a great tumult into
the family for while the king was very angry at imputations that were laid upon
the sons of ariamne, and was desirous to humble them, he still increased the
honor that he had bestowed on ntipater, and was at last so overcome by his
persuasions, that he brought his mother to court also. e also wrote frequently
to aesar in favor of him, and more earnestly recommended him to his care
particularly. nd when grippa was returning to ome, after he had finished
his ten years' government in sia,    erod sailed from udea and when he met
with him, he had none with him but ntipater, whom he delivered to grippa,
that he might take him along with him, together with many presents, that so he
might become aesar's friend, insomuch that things already looked as if he had
all his father's favor, and that the young men were already entirely rejected
from any hopes of the kingdom.

. nd now what happened during ntipater's absence augmented the honor to
which he had been promoted, and his apparent eminence above his brethren
for he had made a great figure in ome, because erod had sent
recommendations of him to all his friends there only he was grieved that he
was not at home, nor had proper opportunities of perpetually calumniating his
brethren and his chief fear was, lest his father should alter his mind, and
entertain a more favorable opinion of the sons of ariamne and as he had this
in his mind, he did not desist from his purpose, but continually sent from ome
any such stories as he hoped might grieve and irritate his father against his
brethren, under pretence indeed of a deep concern for his preservation, but in
truth such as his malicious mind dictated, in order to purchase a greater hope of
the succession, which yet was already great in itself and thus he did till he had
ecited such a degree of anger in erod, that he was already become very ill-
disposed towards the young men but still while he delayed to eercise so
violent a disgust against them, and that he might not either be too remiss or too
rash, and so offend, he thought it best to sail to ome, and there accuse his sons
before aesar, and not indulge himself in any such crime as might be heinous
enough to be suspected of impiety. ut as he was going up to ome, it

happened that he made such haste as to meet with aesar at the city quilei    so
when he came to the speech of aesar, he asked for a time for hearing this great
cause, wherein he thought himself very miserable, and presented his sons there,
and accused them of their mad actions, and of their attempts against him that
they were enemies to him and by all the means they were able, did their
endeavors to show their hatred to their own father, and would take away his
life, and so obtain his kingdom, after the most barbarous manner that he had
power from aesar to dispose of it, not by necessity, but by choice, to him who
shall eercise the greatest piety towards him while these my sons are not so
desirous of ruling, as they are, upon a disappointment thereof, to epose their
own life, if so be they may but deprive their father of his life so wild and
polluted is their mind by time become, out of their hatred to him that whereas
he had a long time borne this his misfortune, he was now compelled to lay it
before aesar, and to pollute his ears with such language, while he himself
wants to know what severity they have ever suffered from him, or what
hardships he hath ever laid upon them to make them complain of him and how
they can think it just that he should not be lord of that kingdom which he in a
long time, and with great danger, had gained, and not allow him to keep it and
dispose of it to him who should deserve best and this, with other advantages,
he proposes as a reward for the piety of such a one as will hereafter imitate the
care he hath taken of it, and that such a one may gain so great a requital as that
is and that it is an impious thing for them to pretend to meddle with it
beforehand for he who hath ever the kingdom in his view, at the same time
reckons upon procuring the death of his father, because otherwise he cannot
come at the government that as for himself, he had hitherto given them all that
he was able, and what was agreeable to such as are subject to the royal
authority, and the sons of a king what ornaments they wanted, with servants
and delicate fare, and had married them into the most illustrious families, the
one ristobulus] to his sister's daughter, but leander to the daughter of king
rchelaus and, what was the greatest favor of all, when their crimes were so
very bad, and he had authority to punish them, yet had he not made use of it
against them, but had brought them before aesar, their common benefactor,
and had not used the severity which, either as a father who had been impiously
abused, or as a king who had been assaulted treacherously, he might have done,
but made them stand upon a level with him in judgment that, however, it was
necessary that all this should not be passed over without punishment, nor

. hese were the accusations which erod laid with great vehemency

against his sons before aesar. ow the young men, both while he was

. ut when they saw there was a kind disposition arisen both in him and in

ath either of us lamented our mother yes but not because she is dead, but

. hen leander had thus spoken, aesar, who did not before believe so

. ow some there are who stand amazed at the diversity of erod's nature
and purposes for when we have respect to his magnificence, and the benefits

nature vastly beneficent but when any one looks upon the punishments he

contradiction with itself but  am myself of another opinion, and imagine that
the occasion of both these sort of actions was one and the same for being a

be magnificent, wherever there appeared any hopes of a future memorial, or of
reputation at present and as his epenses were beyond his abilities, he was
necessitated to be harsh to his subjects for the persons on whom he epended

because he was conscious that he was hated by those under him, for the injuries
he did them, he thought it not an easy thing to amend his offenses, for that it
was inconvenient for his revenue he therefore strove on the other side to make
their ill-will an occasion of his gains. s to his own court, therefore, if any one
was not very obsequious to him in his language, and would not confess himself
to be his slave, or but seemed to think of any innovation in his government, he
was not able to contain himself, but prosecuted his very kindred and friends,

assertion about that passion of his, we have the greatest evidence, by what he

had no relation to him.

.  have been obliged to set down these decrees, because the present
history of our own acts will go generally among the reeks and  have hereby
demonstrated to them that we have formerly been in great esteem, and have not
been prohibited by those governors we were under from keeping any of the
laws of our forefathers nay, that we have been supported by them, while we

every city almost we meet with them different from one another but natural
justice is most agreeable to the advantage of all men equally, both reeks and
barbarians, to which our laws have the greatest regard, and thereby render us, if
we abide in them after a pure manner, benevolent and friendly to all men on
which account we have reason to epect the like return from others, and to

. s for erod, he had spent vast sums about the cities, both without and
within his own kingdom and as he had before heard that yrcanus, who had
been king before him, had opened avid's sepulchre, and taken out of it three
thousand talents of silver, and that there was a much greater number left
behind, and indeed enough to suffice all his wants, he had a great while an
intention to make the attempt and at this time he opened that sepulchre by
night, and went into it, and endeavored that it should not be at all known in the
city, but took only his most faithful friends with him. s for any money, he

found none, as yrcanus had done, but that furniture of gold, and those
precious goods that were laid up there all which he took away. owever, he
had a great desire to make a more diligent search, and to go farther in, even as
far as the very bodies of avid and olomon where two of his guards were
slain, by a flame that burst out upon those that went in, as the report was. o he
was terribly affrighted, and went out, and built a propitiatory monument of that
fright he had been in and this of white stone, at the mouth of the sepulchre, and
that at great epense also. nd even icolaus    his historiographer makes
mention of this monument built by erod, though he does not mention his

many other things he treats of in the same manner in his book for he wrote in
erod's lifetime, and under his reign, and so as to please him, and as a servant
to him, touching upon nothing but what tended to his glory, and openly
ecusing many of his notorious crimes, and very diligently concealing them.
nd as he was desirous to put handsome colors on the death of ariamne and
her sons, which were barbarous actions in the king, he tells falsehoods about
the incontinence of ariamne, and the treacherous designs of his sons upon
him and thus he proceeded in his whole work, making a pompous encomium
upon what just actions he had done, but earnestly apologizing for his unjust
ones. ndeed, a man, as  said, may have a great deal to say by way of ecuse
for icolaus for he did not so properly write this as a history for others, as
somewhat that might be subservient to the king himself. s for ourselves, who
come of a family nearly allied to the samonean kings, and on that account
have an honorable place, which is the priesthood, we think it indecent to say
any thing that is false about them, and accordingly we have described their
actions after an unblemished and upright manner. nd although we reverence
many of erod's posterity, who still reign, yet do we pay a greater regard to
truth than to them, and this though it sometimes happens that we incur their
displeasure by so doing.
. nd indeed erod's troubles in his family seemed to be augmented by

and their hatred towards one another was like that where each one strove to

erod also recommended tolemy, who was a great director of the affairs of his

nd for the women, laphyra, leander's wife, the daughter of rchelaus,

. ow, besides this second contention that had fallen among them, neither

because he had bestowed many favors upon him, and had advanced him to that

unhappy on that account. o upon heroras's unworthy refusal, he gave the

were over, he was such a slave to his affections, that he no longer performed
any thing he had promised, but continued still with his former wife. his
occasioned erod to grieve openly, and made him angry, while the king
dropped one word or other against heroras perpetually and many made the
king's anger an opportunity for raising calumnies against him. or had the king
any longer a single quiet day or hour, but occasions of one fresh quarrel or

another arose among his relations, and those that were dearest to him for
alome was of a harsh temper, and ill-natured to ariamne's sons nor would
she suffer her own daughter, who was the wife of ristobulus, one of those
young men, to bear a good-will to her husband, but persuaded her to tell her if
he said any thing to her in private, and when any misunderstandings happened,
as is common, she raised a great many suspicions out of it by which means she
learned all their concerns, and made the damsel ill-natured to the young man.
nd in order to gratify her mother, she often said that the young men used to
mention ariamne when they were by themselves and that they hated their
father, and were continually threatening, that if they had once got the kingdom,
they would make erod's sons by his other wives country schoolmasters, for
that the present education which was given them, and their diligence in
learning, fitted them for such an employment. nd as for the women, whenever
they saw them adorned with their mother's clothes, they threatened, that instead
of their present gaudy apparel, they should be clothed in sackcloth, and
confined so closely that they should not see the light of the sun. hese stories
were presently carried by alome to the king, who was troubled to hear them,
and endeavored to make up matters but these suspicions afflicted him, and
becoming more and more uneasy, he believed every body against every body.
owever, upon his rebuking his sons, and hearing the defence they made for
themselves, he was easier for a while, though a little afterwards much worse
accidents came upon him.
. or heroras came to leander, the husband of laphyra, who was the
daughter of rchelaus, as we have already told you, and said that he had heard
from alome that erod has enamored on laphyra, and that his passion for her
was incurable. hen leander heard that, he was all on fire, from his youth
and jealousy and he interpreted the instances of erod's obliging behavior to
her, which were very frequent, for the worse, which came from those
suspicions he had on account of that word which fell from heroras nor could
he conceal his grief at the thing, but informed him what word heroras had
said. pon which erod was in a greater disorder than ever and not bearing
such a false calumny, which was to his shame, was much disturbed at it and
often did he lament the wickedness of his domestics, and how good he had
been to them, and how ill requitals they had made him. o he sent for heroras,
and reproached him, and said, "hou vilest of all men! art thou come to that
unmeasurable and etravagant degree of ingratitude, as not only to suppose
such things of me, but to speak of them  now indeed perceive what thy
intentions are. t is not thy only aim to reproach me, when thou usest such
words to my son, but thereby to persuade him to plot against me, and get me
destroyed by poison. nd who is there, if he had not a good genius at his elbow,
as hath my son, but would not bear such a suspicion of his father, but would

revenge himself upon him ost thou suppose that thou hast only dropped a
word for him to think of, and not rather hast put a sword into his hand to slay
his father nd what dost thou mean, when thou really hatest both him and his
brother, to pretend kindness to them, only in order to raise a reproach against
me, and talk of such things as no one but such an impious wretch as thou art
could either devise in their mind, or declare in their words egone, thou art
such a plague to thy benefactor and thy brother, and may that evil conscience of
thine go along with thee while  still overcome my relations by kindness, and
am so far from avenging myself of them, as they deserve, that  bestow greater
benefits upon them than they are worthy of."
. hus did the king speak. hereupon heroras, who was caught in the
very act of his villainy, said that "it was alome who was the framer of this
plot, and that the words came from her." ut as soon as she heard that, for she
was at hand, she cried out, like one that would be believed, that no such thing
ever came out of her mouth that they all earnestly endeavored to make the king
hate her, and to make her away, because of the good-will she bore to erod,
and because she was always foreseeing the dangers that were coming upon
him, and that at present there were more plots against him than usual for while
she was the only person who persuaded her brother to put away the wife he
now had, and to take the king's daughter, it was no wonder if she were hated by
him. s she said this, and often tore her hair, and often beat her breast, her
countenance made her denial to be believed but the perverseness of her
manners declared at the same time her dissimulation in these proceedings but
heroras was caught between them, and had nothing plausible to offer in his
own defence, while he confessed that he had said what was charged upon him,
but was not believed when he said he had heard it from alome so the
confusion among them was increased, and their quarrelsome words one to
another. t last the king, out of his hatred to his brother and sister, sent them
both away and when he had commended the moderation of his son, and that he
had himself told him of the report, he went in the evening to refresh himself.
fter such a contest as this had fallen out among them, alome's reputation
suffered greatly, since she was supposed to have first raised the calumny and
the king's wives were grieved at her, as knowing she was a very ill-natured
woman, and would sometimes be a friend, and sometimes an enemy, at
different seasons so they perpetually said one thing or another against her and
somewhat that now fell out made them the bolder in speaking against her.
. here was one bodas, king of rabia, an inactive and slothful man in
his nature but ylleus managed most of his affairs for him. e was a shrewd
man, although he was but young, and was handsome withal. his ylleus, upon
some occasion coming to erod, and supping with him, saw alome, and set
his heart upon her and understanding that she was a widow, he discoursed with

her. ow because alome was at this time less in favor with her brother, she
looked upon ylleus with some passion, and was very earnest to be married to
him and on the days following there appeared many, and those very great,
indications of their agreement together. ow the women carried this news to
the king, and laughed at the indecency of it whereupon erod inquired about it
further of heroras, and desired him to observe them at supper, how their
behavior was one toward another who told him, that by the signals which came
from their heads and their eyes, they both were evidently in love. fter this,
ylleus the rabian being suspected, went away, but came again in two or three
months afterwards, as it were on that very design, and spake to erod about it,
and desired that alome might be given him to wife for that his affinity might
not be disadvantageous to his affairs, by a union with rabia, the government
of which country was already in effect under his power, and more evidently
would be his hereafter. ccordingly, when erod discoursed with his sister
about it, and asked her whether she were disposed to this match, she
immediately agreed to it. ut when ylleus was desired to come over to the
ewish religion, and then he should marry her, and that it was impossible to do
it on any other terms, he could not bear that proposal, and went his way for he
said, that if he should do so, he should be stoned by the rabs. hen did
heroras reproach alome for her incontinency, as did the women much more
and said that ylleus had debauched her. s for that damsel which the king had
betrothed to his brother heroras, but he had not taken her, as  have before
related, because he was enamored on his former wife, alome desired of erod
she might be given to her son by ostobarus which match he was very willing
to, but was dissuaded from it by heroras, who pleaded that this young man
would not be kind to her, since his father had been slain by him, and that it was
more just that his son, who was to be his successor in the tetrarchy, should have
her. o he begged his pardon, and persuaded him to do so. ccordingly the
damsel, upon this change of her espousals, was disposed of to this young man,
the son of heroras, the king giving for her portion a hundred talents.

. ut still the affairs of erod's family were no better, but perpetually more
troublesome. ow this accident happened, which arose from no decent
occasion, but proceeded so far as to bring great difficulties upon him. here

were certain eunuchs which the king had, and on account of their beauty was
very fond of them and the care of bringing him drink was intrusted to one of
them of bringing him his supper, to another and of putting him to bed, to the
third, who also managed the principal affairs of the government and there was
one told the king that these eunuchs were corrupted by leander the king's son
with great sums of money. nd when they were asked whether leander had
had criminal conversation with them, they confessed it, but said they knew of
no further mischief of his against his father but when they were more severely
tortured, and were in the utmost etremity, and the tormentors, out of
compliance with ntipater, stretched the rack to the very utmost, they said that
leander bare great ill-will and innate hatred to his father and that he told
them that erod despaired to live much longer and that, in order to cover his
great age, he colored his hair black, and endeavored to conceal what would
discover how old he was but that if he would apply himself to him, when he
should attain the kingdom, which, in spite of his father, could come to no one
else, he should quickly have the first place in that kingdom under him, for that
he was now ready to take the kingdom, not only as his birth-right, but by the
preparations he had made for obtaining it, because a great many of the rulers,
and a great many of his friends, were of his side, and those no ill men neither,
ready both to do and to suffer whatsoever should come on that account.
. hen erod heard this confession, he was all over anger and fear, some
parts seeming to him reproachful, and some made him suspicious of dangers
that attended him, insomuch that on both accounts he was provoked, and
bitterly afraid lest some more heavy plot was laid against him than he should be
then able to escape from whereupon he did not now make an open search, but
sent about spies to watch such as he suspected, for he was now overrun with
suspicion and hatred against all about him and indulging abundance of those
suspicions, in order to his preservation, he continued to suspect those that were
guiltless nor did he set any bounds to himself, but supposing that those who
staid with him had the most power to hurt him, they were to him very frightful
and for those that did not use to come to him, it seemed enough to name them
to make them suspected], and he thought himself safer when they were
destroyed. nd at last his domestics were come to that pass, that being no way
secure of escaping themselves, they fell to accusing one another, and imagining
that he who first accused another was most likely to save himself yet when any
had overthrown others, they were hated and they were thought to suffer justly
who unjustly accused others, and they only thereby prevented their own
accusation nay, they now eecuted their own private enmities by this means,
and when they were caught, they were punished in the same way. hus these
men contrived to make use of this opportunity as an instrument and a snare
against their enemies yet when they tried it, were themselves caught also in the

same snare which they laid for others and the king soon repented of what he
had done, because he had no clear evidence of the guilt of those whom he had
slain and yet what was still more severe in him, he did not make use of his
repentance, in order to leave off doing the like again, but in order to inflict the
same punishment upon their accusers.
. nd in this state of disorder were the affairs of the palace and he had
already told many of his friends directly that they ought not to appear before
him, her come into the palace and the reason of this injunction was, that when
they were there], he had less freedom of acting, or a greater restraint on himself
on their account for at this time it was that he epelled ndromachus and
emellus, men who had of old been his friends, and been very useful to him in
the affairs of his kingdom, and been of advantage to his family, by their
embassages and counsels and had been tutors to his sons, and had in a manner
the first degree of freedom with him. e epelled ndromachus, because his
son emetrius was a companion to leander and emellus, because he knew
that he wished him well, which arose from his having been with him in his
youth, when he was at school, and absent at ome. hese he epelled out of his
palace, and was willing enough to have done worse by them but that he might
not seem to take such liberty against men of so great reputation, he contented
himself with depriving them of their dignity, and of their power to hinder his
wicked proceedings.
. ow it was ntipater who was the cause of all this who when he knew
what a mad and licentious way of acting his father was in, and had been a great
while one of his counselors, he hurried him on, and then thought he should
bring him to do somewhat to purpose, when every one that could oppose him
was taken away. hen therefore ndromachus and his friends were driven
away, and had no discourse nor freedom with the king any longer, the king, in
the first place, eamined by torture all whom he thought to be faithful to
leander, whether they knew of any of his attempts against him but these
died without having any thing to say to that matter, which made the king more
zealous after discoveries], when he could not find out what evil proceedings he
suspected them of. s for ntipater, he was very sagacious to raise a calumny
against those that were really innocent, as if their denial was only their
constancy and fidelity to leander], and thereupon provoked erod to
discover by the torture of great numbers what attempts were still concealed.
ow there was a certain person among the many that were tortured, who said
that he knew that the young man had often said, that when he was commended
as a tall man in his body, and a skilful marksman, and that in his other
commendable eercises he eceeded all men, these qualifications given him by
nature, though good in themselves, were not advantageous to him, because his
father was grieved at them, and envied him for them and that when he walked

along with his father, he endeavored to depress and shorten himself, that he
might not appear too tall and that when he shot at any thing as he was hunting,
when his father was by, he missed his mark on purpose, for he knew how
ambitious his father was of being superior in such eercises. o when the man
was tormented about this saying, and had ease given his body after it, he added,
that he had his brother ristobulus for his assistance, and contrived to lie in
wait for their father, as they were hunting, and kill him and when they had
done so to fly to ome, and desire to have the kingdom given them. here were
also letters of the young man found, written to his brother, wherein he
complained that his father did not act justly in giving ntipater a country,
whose yearly] revenues amounted to two hundred talents. pon these
confessions erod presently thought he had somewhat to depend on, in his own
opinion, as to his suspicion about his sons so he took up leander and bound
him yet did he still continue to be uneasy, and was not quite satisfied of the
truth of what he had heard and when he came to recollect himself, he found
that they had only made juvenile complaints and contentions, and that it was an
incredible thing, that when his son should have slain him, he should openly go
to ome to beg the kingdom] so he was desirous to have some surer mark of
his son's wickedness, and was very solicitous about it, that he might not appear
to have condemned him to be put in prison too rashly so he tortured the
principal of leander's friends, and put not a few of them to death, without
getting any of the things out of them which he suspected. nd while erod was
very busy about this matter, and the palace was full of terror and trouble, one of
the younger sort, when he was in the utmost agony, confessed that leander
had sent to his friends at ome, and desired that he might be quickly invited
thither by aesar, and that he could discover a plot against him that
ithridates, the king of arthia, was joined in friendship with his father against
the omans, and that he had a poisonous potion ready prepared at skelon.
. o these accusations erod gave credit, and enjoyed hereby, in his
miserable case, some sort of consolation, in ecuse of his rashness, as flattering
himself with finding things in so bad a condition but as for the poisonous
potion, which he labored to find, he could find none. s for leander, he was
very desirous to aggravate the vast misfortunes he was under, so he pretended
not to deny the accusations, but punished the rashness of his father with a
greater crime of his own and perhaps he was willing to make his father
ashamed of his easy belief of such calumnies he aimed especially, if he could
gain belief to his story, to plague him and his whole kingdom for he wrote four
letters, and sent them to him, that he did not need to torture any more persons,
for he had plotted against him and that he had for his partners heroras and the
most faithful of his friends and that alome came in to him by night, and that
she lay with him whether he would or not and that all men were come to be of

one mind, to make away with him as soon as they could, and so get clear of the
continual fear they were in from him. mong these were accused tolemy and
apinnius, who were the most faithful friends to the king. nd what more can
be said, but that those who before were the most intimate friends, were become
wild beasts to one another, as if a certain madness had fallen upon them, while
there was no room for defence or refutation, in order to the discovery of the
truth, but all were at random doomed to destruction so that some lamented
those that were in prison, some those that were put to death, and others
lamented that they were in epectation of the same miseries and a melancholy
solitude rendered the kingdom deformed, and quite the reverse to that happy
state it was formerly in. erod's own life also was entirely disturbed and
because he could trust nobody, he was sorely punished by the epectation of
further misery for he often fancied in his imagination that his son had fallen
upon him, or stood by him with a sword in his hand and thus was his mind
night and day intent upon this thing, and revolved it over and over, no
otherwise than if he were under a distraction. nd this was the sad condition
erod was now in.
. ut when rchelaus, king of appadocia, heard of the state that erod
was in, and being in great distress about his daughter, and the young man her
husband], and grieving with erod, as with a man that was his friend, on
account of so great a disturbance as he was under, he came to erusalem] on
purpose to compose their differences and when he found erod in such a
temper, he thought it wholly unseasonable to reprove him, or to pretend that he
had done any thing rashly, for that he should thereby naturally bring him to
dispute the point with him, and by still more and more apologizing for himself
to be the more irritated he went, therefore, another way to work, in order to
correct the former misfortunes, and appeared angry at the young man, and said
that erod had been so very mild a man, that he had not acted a rash part at all.
e also said he would dissolve his daughter's marriage with leander, nor
could in justice spare his own daughter, if she were conscious of any thing, and
did not inform erod of it. hen rchelaus appeared to be of this temper, and
otherwise than erod epected or imagined, and, for the main, took erod's
part, and was angry on his account, the king abated of his harshness, and took
occasion from his appearing to have acted justly hitherto, to come by degrees to
put on the affection of a father, and was on both sides to be pitied for when
some persons refuted the calumnies that were laid on the young man, he was
thrown into a passion but when rchelaus joined in the accusation, he was
dissolved into tears and sorrow after an affectionate manner. ccordingly, he
desired that he would not dissolve his son's marriage, and became not so angry
as before for his offenses. o when rchelaus had brought him to a more
moderate temper, he transferred the calumnies upon his friends and said it

must be owing to them that so young a man, and one unacquainted with malice,
was corrupted and he supposed that there was more reason to suspect the
brother than the soft. pon which erod was very much displeased at heroras,
who indeed now had no one that could make a reconciliation between him and
his brother. o when he saw that rchelaus had the greatest power with erod,
he betook himself to him in the habit of a mourner, and like one that had all the
signs upon him of an undone man. pon this rchelaus did not overlook the
intercession he made to him, nor yet did he undertake to change the king's
disposition towards him immediately and he said that it was better for him to
come himself to the king, and confess himself the occasion of all that this
would make the king's anger not to be etravagant towards him, and that then
he would be present to assist him. hen he had persuaded him to this, he
gained his point with both of them and the calumnies raised against the young
man were, beyond all epectation, wiped off. nd rchelaus, as soon as he had
made the reconciliation, went then away to appadocia, having proved at this
juncture of time the most acceptable person to erod in the world on which
account he gave him the richest presents, as tokens of his respects to him and
being on other occasions magnanimous, he esteemed him one of his dearest
friends. e also made an agreement with him that he would go to ome,
because he had written to aesar about these affairs so they went together as
far as ntioch, and there erod made a reconciliation between rchelaus and
itus, the president of yria, who had been greatly at variance, and so returned
back to udea.

. hen erod had been at ome, and was come back again, a war arose
between him and the rabians, on the occasion following the inhabitants of
rachonitis, after aesar had taken the country away from enodorus, and
added it to erod, had not now power to rob, but were forced to plough the
land, and to live quietly, which was a thing they did not like and when they did
take that pains, the ground did not produce much fruit for them. owever, at
the first the king would not permit them to rob, and so they abstained from that
unjust way of living upon their neighbors, which procured erod a great
reputation for his care. ut when he was sailing to ome, it was at that time

when he went to accuse his son leander, and to commit ntipater to aesar's
protection, the rachonites spread a report as if he were dead, and revolted
from his dominion, and betook themselves again to their accustomed way of
robbing their neighbors at which time the king's commanders subdued them
during his absence but about forty of the principal robbers, being terrified by
those that had been taken, left the country, and retired into rabia, ylleus
entertaining them, after he had missed of marrying alome, and gave them a
place of strength, in which they dwelt. o they overran not only udea, but all
oelesyria also, and carried off the prey, while ylleus afforded them places of
protection and quietness during their wicked practices. ut when erod came
back from ome, he perceived that his dominions had greatly suffered by them
and since he could not reach the robbers themselves, because of the secure
retreat they had in that country, and which the rabian government afforded
them, and yet being very uneasy at the injuries they had done him, he went all
over rachonitis, and slew their relations whereupon these robbers were more
angry than before, it being a law among them to be avenged on the murderers
of their relations by all possible means so they continued to tear and rend every
thing under erod's dominion with impunity. hen did he discourse about these
robberies to aturninus and olumnius, and required that they should be
punished upon which occasion they still the more confirmed themselves in
their robberies, and became more numerous, and made very great disturbances,
laying waste the countries and villages that belonged to erod's kingdom, and
killing those men whom they caught, till these unjust proceedings came to be
like a real war, for the robbers were now become about a thousand—at which
erod was sore displeased, and required the robbers, as well as the money
which he had lent bodas, by ylleus, which was sity talents, and since the
time of payment was now past, he desired to have it paid him but ylleus, who
had laid bodas aside, and managed all by himself, denied that the robbers
were in rabia, and put off the payment of the money about which there was a
hearing before aturninus and olumnius, who were then the presidents of
yria.    t last he, by their means, agreed, that within thirty days' time erod
should be paid his money, and that each of them should deliver up the other's
subjects reciprocally. ow, as to erod, there was not one of the other's
subjects found in his kingdom, either as doing any injustice, or on any other
account, but it was proved that the rabians had the robbers amongst them.
. hen this day appointed for payment of the money was past, without
ylleus's performing any part of his agreement, and he was gone to ome,
erod demanded the payment of the money, and that the robbers that were in
rabia should be delivered up and, by the permission of aturninus and
olumnius, eecuted the judgment himself upon those that were refractory. e
took an army that he had, and let it into rabia, and in three days' time marched

seven mansiones and when he came to the garrison wherein the robbers were,
he made an assault upon them, and took them all, and demolished the place,
which was called aepta, but did no harm to any others. ut as the rabians
came to their assistance, under aceb their captain, there ensued a battle,
wherein a few of erod's soldiers, and aceb, the captain of the rabians, and
about twenty of his soldiers, fell, while the rest betook themselves to flight. o
when he had brought these to punishment, he placed three thousand dumeans
in rachonitis, and thereby restrained the robbers that were there. e also sent
an account to the captains that were about hoenicia, and demonstrated that he
had done nothing but what he ought to do, in punishing the refractory rabians,
which, upon an eact inquiry, they found to be no more than what was true.
. owever, messengers were hasted away to ylleus to ome, and
informed him what had been done, and, as is usual, aggravated every thing.
ow ylleus had already insinuated himself into the knowledge of aesar, and
was then about the palace and as soon as he heard of these things, he changed
his habit into black, and went in, and told aesar that rabia was afflicted with
war, and that all his kingdom was in great confusion, upon erod's laying it
waste with his army and he said, with tears in his eyes, that two thousand five
hundred of the principal men among the rabians had been destroyed, and that
their captain acebus, his familiar friend and kinsman, was slain and that the
riches that were at aepta were carried off and that bodas was despised,
whose infirm state of body rendered him unfit for war on which account
neither he, nor the rabian army, were present. hen ylleus said so, and
added invidiously, that he would not himself have come out of the country,
unless he had believed that aesar would have provided that they should all
have peace one with another, and that, had he been there, he would have taken
care that the war should not have been to erod's advantage aesar was
provoked when this was said, and asked no more than this one question, both of
erod's friends that were there, and of his own friends, who were come from
yria, hether erod had led an army thither nd when they were forced to
confess so much, aesar, without staying to hear for what reason he did it, and
how it was done, grew very angry, and wrote to erod sharply. he sum of his
epistle was this, that whereas of old he had used him as his friend, he should
now use him as his subject. ylleus also wrote an account of this to the
rabians, who were so elevated with it, that they neither delivered up the
robbers that had fled to them, nor paid the money that was due they retained
those pastures also which they had hired, and kept them without paying their
rent, and all this because the king of the ews was now in a low condition, by
reason of aesar's anger at him. hose of rachonitis also made use of this
opportunity, and rose up against the dumean garrison, and followed the same
way of robbing with the rabians, who had pillaged their country, and were

more rigid in their unjust proceedings, not only in order to get by it, but by way
of revenge also.
. ow erod was forced to bear all this, that confidence of his being quite
gone with which aesar's favor used to inspire him for aesar would not admit
so much as an embassage from him to make an apology for him and when they
came again, he sent them away without success. o he was cast into sadness
and fear and ylleus's circumstances grieved him eceedingly, who was now
believed by aesar, and was present at ome, nay, sometimes aspiring higher.
ow it came to pass that bodas was dead and eneas, whose name was
retas,    took the government, for ylleus endeavored by calumnies to get him
turned out of his principality, that he might himself take it with which design
he gave much money to the courtiers, and promised much money to aesar,
who indeed was angry that retas had not sent to him first before he took the
kingdom yet did eneas send an epistle and presents to aesar, and a golden
crown, of the weight of many talents. ow that epistle accused ylleus as
having been a wicked servant, and having killed bodas by poison and that
while he was alive, he had governed him as he pleased and had also debauched
the wives of the rabians and had borrowed money, in order to obtain the
dominion for himself yet did not aesar give heed to these accusations, but
sent his ambassadors back, without receiving any of his presents. ut in the
meantime the affairs of udea and rabia became worse and worse, partly
because of the anarchy they were under, and partly because, as bad as they
were, nobody had power to govern them for of the two kings, the one was not
yet confirmed in his kingdom, and so had not authority sufficient to restrain the

. o aesar was now reconciled to erod, and wrote thus to him that he was

.    nd now perhaps it may not seem unreasonable to some, that such an
inveterate hatred might increase so much on both sides], as to proceed further,
and overcome nature but it may justly deserve consideration, whether it be to
be laid to the charge of the young men, that they gave such an occasion to their
father's anger, and led him to do what he did, and by going on long in the same
way put things past remedy, and brought him to use them so unmercifully or
whether it be to be laid to the father's charge, that he was so hard-hearted, and
so very tender in the desire of government, and of other things that would tend

whatsoever he would have done himself might continue immovable or, indeed,
whether fortune have not greater power than all prudent reasonings whence we
are persuaded that human actions are thereby determined beforehand by an
inevitable necessity, and we call her ate, because there is nothing which is not
done by her wherefore  suppose it will be sufficient to compare this notion

with that other, which attribute somewhat to ourselves, and renders men not
unaccountable for the different conducts of their lives, which notion is no other
than the philosophical determination of our ancient law. ccordingly, of the two
other causes of this sad event, any body may lay the blame on the young men,
who acted by youthful vanity, and pride of their royal birth, that they should

suspecting, and intemperate in speaking of it, and on both accounts easily

bodies, and the great darlings of other men, and no way deficient in their
conduct, whether it were in hunting, or in warlike eercises, or in speaking
upon occasional topics of discourse for in all these they were skilful, and
especially leander, who was the eldest for certainly it had been sufficient,
even though he had condemned them, to have kept them alive in bonds, or to
let them live at a distance from his dominions in banishment, while he was
surrounded by the oman forces, which were a strong security to him, whose
help would prevent his suffering any thing by a sudden onset, or by open force
but for him to kill them on the sudden, in order to gratify a passion that
governed him, was a demonstration of insufferable impiety. e also was guilty
of so great a crime in his older age nor will the delays that he made, and the
length of time in which the thing was done, plead at all for his ecuse for when
a man is on a sudden amazed, and in commotion of mind, and then commits a
wicked action, although this be a heavy crime, yet is it a thing that frequently
happens but to do it upon deliberation, and after frequent attempts, and as
frequent puttings-off, to undertake it at last, and accomplish it, was the action
of a murderous mind, and such as was not easily moved from that which is evil.
nd this temper he showed in what he did afterward, when he did not spare
those that seemed to be the best beloved of his friends that were left, wherein,
though the justice of the punishment caused those that perished to be the less
pitied, yet was the barbarity of the man here equal, in that he did not abstain
from their slaughter also. ut of those persons we shall have occasion to
discourse more hereafter.

. hen ntipater had thus taken off his brethren, and had brought his father
into the highest degree of impiety, till he was haunted with furies for what he
had done, his hopes did not succeed to his mind, as to the rest of his life for
although he was delivered from the fear of his brethren being his rivals as to the
government, yet did he find it a very hard thing, and almost impracticable, to
come at the kingdom, because the hatred of the nation against him on that
account was become very great and besides this very disagreeable
circumstance, the affair of the soldiery grieved him still more, who were
alienated from him, from which yet these kings derived all the safety which
they had, whenever they found the nation desirous of innovation and all this
danger was drawn upon him by his destruction of his brethren. owever, he
governed the nation jointly with his father, being indeed no other than a king
already and he was for that very reason trusted, and the more firmly depended
on, for the which he ought himself to have been put to death, as appearing to
have betrayed his brethren out of his concern for the preservation of erod, and
not rather out of his ill-will to them, and, before them, to his father himself and
this was the accursed state he was in. ow all ntipater's contrivances tended
to make his way to take off erod, that he might have nobody to accuse him in
the vile practices he was devising and that erod might have no refuge, nor
any to afford him their assistance, since they must thereby have ntipater for
their open enemy insomuch that the very plots he had laid against his brethren
were occasioned by the hatred he bore his father. ut at this time he was more
than ever set upon the eecution of his attempts against erod, because if he
were once dead, the government would now be firmly secured to him but if he
were suffered to live any longer, he should be in danger, upon a discovery of
that wickedness of which he had been the contriver, and his father would of

necessity then become his enemy. nd on this account it was that he became
very bountiful to his father's friends, and bestowed great sums on several of
them, in order to surprise men with his good deeds, and take off their hatred
against him. nd he sent great presents to his friends at ome particularly, to
gain their good-will and above all to aturninus, the president of yria. e also
hoped to gain the favor of aturninus's brother with the large presents he
bestowed on him as also he used the same art to alome] the king's sister, who
had married one of erod's chief friends. nd when he counterfeited friendship
to those with whom he conversed, he was very subtle in gaining their belief,
and very cunning to hide his hatred against any that he really did hate. ut he
could not impose upon his aunt, who understood him of a long time, and was a
woman not easily to be deluded, especially while she had already used all
possible caution in preventing his pernicious designs. lthough ntipater's
uncle by the mother's side was married to her daughter, and this by his own
connivance and management, while she had before been married to
ristobulus, and while alome's other daughter by that husband was married to
the son of alleas yet that marriage was no obstacle to her, who knew how
wicked he was, in her discovering his designs, as her former kindred to him
could not prevent her hatred of him. ow erod had compelled alome, while
she was in love with ylleus the rabian, and had taken a fondness for him, to
marry leas which match was by her submitted to at the instance of ulia,
who persuaded alome not to refuse it, lest she should herself be their open
enemy, since erod had sworn that he would never be friends with alome, if
she would not accept of leas for her husband so she submitted to ulia as
being aesar's wife and besides that, she advised her to nothing but what was
very much for her own advantage. t this time also it was that erod sent back
king rchelaus's daughter, who had been leander's wife, to her father,
returning the portion he had with her out of his own estate, that there might be
no dispute between them about it.
. ow erod brought up his sons' children with great care for leander
had two sons by laphyra and ristobulus had three sons by ernice, alome's
daughter, and two daughters and as his friends were once with him, he
presented the children before them and deploring the hard fortune of his own
sons, he prayed that no such ill fortune would befall these who were their
children, but that they might improve in virtue, and obtain what they justly
deserved, and might make him amends for his care of their education. e also
caused them to be betrothed against they should come to the proper age of
marriage the elder of leander's sons to heroras's daughter, and ntipater's
daughter to ristobulus's eldest son. e also allotted one of ristobulus's
daughters to ntipater's son, and ristobulus's other daughter to erod, a son of
his own, who was born to him by the high priest's daughter for it is the ancient

practice among us to have many wives at the same time. ow the king made
these espousals for the children, out of commiseration of them now they were
fatherless, as endeavoring to render ntipater kind to them by these
intermarriages. ut ntipater did not fail to bear the same temper of mind to his
brothers' children which he had borne to his brothers themselves and his

supposal, that they would become greater than ever his brothers had been
while rchelaus, a king, would support his daughter's sons, and heroras, a
tetrarch, would accept of one of the daughters as a wife to his son. hat
provoked him also was this, that all the multitude would so commiserate these
fatherless children, and so hate him for making them fatherless], that all would

entreaty and the determination now was, that ntipater himself should marry
ristobulus's daughter, and ntipater's son should marry heroras's daughter.
o the espousals for the marriages were changed after this manner, even
without the king's real approbation.
. ow erod    the king had at this time nine wives one of them ntipater's
mother, and another the high priest's daughter, by whom he had a son of his
own name. e had also one who was his brother's daughter, and another his
sister's daughter which two had no children. ne of his wives also was of the
amaritan nation, whose sons were ntipas and rchelaus, and whose daughter
was lympias which daughter was afterward married to oseph, the king's
brother's son but rchelaus and ntipas were brought up with a certain private

also was one of his wives, which bare him his son hasaelus. nd besides

oana and alome. s for his elder daughters by the same mother with

his brother's son. nd this was the posterity of erod.
