. nd now it was that erod, being desirous of securing himself on the side of
the rachonites, resolved to build a village as large as a city for the ews, in the
middle of that country, which might make his own country difficult to be
assaulted, and whence he might be at hand to make sallies upon them, and do
them a mischief. ccordingly, when he understood that there was a man that
was a ew come out of abylon, with five hundred horsemen, all of whom
could shoot their arrows as they rode on horse-back, and, with a hundred of his
relations, had passed over uphrates, and now abode at ntioch by aphne of
yria, where aturninus, who was then president, had given them a place for
habitation, called alatha, he sent for this man, with the multitude that followed
him, and promised to give him land in the toparchy called atanea, which
country is bounded with rachonitis, as desirous to make that his habitation a
guard to himself. e also engaged to let him hold the country free from tribute,
and that they should dwell entirely without paying such customs as used to be
paid, and gave it him ta-free.
. he abylonian was reduced by these offers to come hither so he took
possession of the land, and built in it fortresses and a village, and named it
athyra. hereby this man became a safeguard to the inhabitants against the
rachonites, and preserved those ews who came out of abylon, to offer their
sacrifices at erusalem, from being hurt by the rachonite robbers so that a
great number came to him from all those parts where the ancient ewish laws
were observed, and the country became full of people, by reason of their
universal freedom from taes. his continued during the life of erod but
when hilip, who was tetrarch] after him, took the government, he made them
pay some small taes, and that for a little while only and grippa the reat,
and his son of the same name, although they harassed them greatly, yet would
they not take their liberty away. rom whom, when the omans have now
taken the government into their own hands, they still gave them the privilege of
their freedom, but oppress them entirely with the imposition of taes. f which
matter  shall treat more accurately in the progress of this history. 
 . t length amaris the abylonian, to whom erod had given that
country for a possession, died, having lived virtuously, and left children of a
good character behind him one of whom was acim, who was famous for his
valor, and taught his abylonians how to ride their horses and a troop of them

age, he left a son, whose name was hilip, one of great strength in his hands,
and in other respects also more eminent for his valor than any of his
contemporaries on which account there was a confidence and firm friendship
between him and king grippa. e had also an army which he maintained as
great as that of a king, which he eercised and led wheresoever lie had occasion

to march.
. hen the affairs of erod were in the condition  have described, all the
public affairs depended upon ntipater and his power was such, that he could
do good turns to as many as he pleased, and this by his father's concession, in
hopes of his good-will and fidelity to him and this till he ventured to use his
power still further, because his wicked designs were concealed from his father,
and he made him believe every thing he said. e was also formidable to all, not
so much on account of the power and authority he had, as for the shrewdness of
his vile attempts beforehand but he who principally cultivated a friendship
with him was heroras, who received the like marks of his friendship while
ntipater had cunningly encompassed him about by a company of women,
whom he placed as guards about him for heroras was greatly enslaved to his
wife, and to her mother, and to her sister and this notwithstanding the hatred he
bare them for the indignities they had offered to his virgin daughters. et did he
bear them and nothing was to he done without the women, who had got this
man into their circle, and continued still to assist each other in all things,
insomuch that ntipater was entirely addicted to them, both by himself and by
his mother for these four women    said all one and the same thing but the
opinions of heroras and ntipater were different in some points of no
consequence. ut the king's sister alome] was their antagonist, who for a
good while had looked about all their affairs, and was apprised that this their
friendship was made in order to do erod some mischief, and was disposed to
inform the king of it. nd since these people knew that their friendship was
very disagreeable to erod, as tending to do him a mischief, they contrived that
their meetings should not be discovered so they pretended to hate one another,
and to abuse one another when time served, and especially when erod was
present, or when any one was there that would tell him but still their intimacy
was firmer than ever, when they were private. nd this was the course they
took. ut they could not conceal from alome neither their first contrivance,
when they set about these their intentions, nor when they had made some
progress in them but she searched out every thing and, aggravating the
relations to her brother, declared to him, as well their secret assemblies and
compotations, as their counsels taken in a clandestine manner, which if they
were not in order to destroy him, they might well enough have been open and
public. ut to appearance they are at variance, and speak about one another as
if they intended one another a mischief, but agree so well together when they
are out of the sight of the multitude for when they are alone by themselves,
they act in concert, and profess that they will never leave off their friendship,
but will fight against those from whom they conceal their designs. nd thus did
she search out these things, and get a perfect knowledge of them, and then told
her brother of them, who understood also of himself a great deal of what she

said, but still durst not depend upon it, because of the suspicions he had of his
sister's calumnies. or there was a certain sect of men that were ews, who
valued themselves highly upon the eact skill they had in the law of their
fathers, and made men believe they were highly favored by od, by whom this
set of women were inveigled. hese are those that are called the sect of the
harisees, who were in a capacity of greatly opposing kings.  cunning sect
they were, and soon elevated to a pitch of open fighting and doing mischief.
ccordingly, when all the people of the ews gave assurance of their good-will
to aesar, and to the king's government, these very men did not swear, being
above si thousand and when the king imposed a fine upon them, heroras's
wife paid their fine for them. n order to requite which kindness of hers, since
they were believed to have the foreknowledge of things to come by ivine
inspiration, they foretold how od had decreed that erod's government should
cease, and his posterity should be deprived of it but that the kingdom should
come to her and heroras, and to their children. hese predictions were not
concealed from alome, but were told the king as also how they had perverted
some persons about the palace itself so the king slew such of the harisees as
were principally accused, and agoas the eunuch, and one arus, who
eceeded all men of that time in comeliness, and one that was his catamite. e
slew also all those of his own family who had consented to what the harisees
foretold and for agoas, he had been puffed up by them, as though he should
be named the father and the benefactor of him who, by the prediction, was
foretold to be their appointed king for that this king would have all things in
his power, and would enable agoas to marry, and to have children of his own
body begotten.
  
       
          

. hen erod had punished those harisees who had been convicted of the
foregoing crimes, he gathered an assembly together of his friends, and accused
heroras's wife and ascribing the abuses of the virgins to the impudence of that
woman, brought an accusation against her for the dishonor she had brought
upon them that she had studiously introduced a quarrel between him and his
brother, and, by her ill temper, had brought them into a state of war, both by her
words and actions that the fines which he had laid had not been paid, and the
offenders had escaped punishment by her means and that nothing which had of

late been done had been done without her "for which reason heroras would
do well, if he would of his own accord, and by his own command, and not at
my entreaty, or as following my opinion, put this his wife away, as one that will
still be the occasion of war between thee and me. nd now, heroras, if thou
valuest thy relation to me, put this wife of thine away for by this means thou
wilt continue to be a brother to me, and wilt abide in thy love to me." hen said
heroras, (although he was pressed hard by the former words,) that as he would
not do so unjust a thing as to renounce his brotherly relation to him, so would

erod put off his anger against heroras on these accounts, although he himself

conversation with heroras's wife, and that they were brought together by
ntipater's mother.
. ut ntipater had now a suspicion of his father, and was afraid that the

were the things of which ylleus was accused, and that on the occasion

them to ome.
. t this time erod commanded heroras, that since he was so obstinate
in his affection for his wife, he should retire into his own tetrarchy which he

a solemn mourning for him. his death of heroras] became the origin of
ntipater's misfortunes, although he were already sailed for ome, od now
being about to punish him for the murder of his brethren,  will eplain the

. s soon as heroras was dead, and his funeral was over, two of heroras's
freedmen, who were much esteemed by him, came to erod, and entreated him
not to leave the murder of his brother without avenging it, but to eamine into
such an unreasonable and unhappy death. hen he was moved with these
words, for they seemed to him to be true, they said that heroras supped with
his wife the day before he fell sick, and that a certain potion was brought him in
such a sort of food as he was not used to eat but that when he had eaten, he
died of it that this potion was brought out of rabia by a woman, under
pretence indeed as a love potion, for that was its name, but in reality to kill
heroras for that the rabian women are skilful in making such poisons and

the woman to whom they ascribe this was confessedly a most intimate friend of
one of ylleus's mistresses and that both the mother and the sister of heroras's
wife had been at the places where she lived, and had persuaded her to sell them

supper. ereupon the king was provoked, and put the women slaves to the

king's son, and by that means came to heroras's wife, her husband having

. ow erod, upon ntipater's writing to him, that having done all that he

. ow uintilius arus was at this time at erusalem, being sent to succeed

. n the net day arus and the king sat together in judgment, and both

. hen erod had thus spoken, he fell a weeping, and was not able to say

always conversant with him, and acquainted with whatsoever he did, and with

instanced in the honors that had been done him, which yet had not been done,
had he not deserved them by his virtuous concern about him for that he had
made provision for every thing that was fit to be foreseen beforehand, as to
giving him his wisest advice and whenever there was occasion for the labor of
his own hands, he had not grudged any such pains for him. nd that it was
almost impossible that he, who had delivered his father from so many

had done at ome, aesar was a witness thereto, who yet was no more to be

imposed upon than od himself of whose opinions his letters sent hither are

oreover he showed the weakness of the evidence obtained by torture, which

. ereupon there was a change observed in the assembly, while they

while he never could gain any advantage thereby, but still fell from one

with that thoughtless behavior of his former sons, who were but young, and
were besides corrupted by wicked counselors, who were the occasion of their

although he had not only had great benefits bestowed on him by his father,

confessed,) the informer as to what wicked actions they had done, and the
searcher out of the evidence against them, and the author of the punishment
they underwent upon their detection. or do we say this as accusing thee for

father, and mightest thereby get thee power enough to do mischief with the
greatest impunity which design thy actions indeed demonstrate. t is true, thou
tookest thy brethren off, because thou didst convict theft of their wicked

designs but thou didst not yield up to justice those who were their partners and
thereby didst make it evident to all men that thou madest a covenant with them
against thy father, when thou chosest to be the accuser of thy brethren, as
desirous to gain to thyself alone this advantage of laying plots to kill thy father,
and so to enjoy double pleasure, which is truly worthy of thy evil disposition,
which thou has openly showed against thy brethren on which account thou
didst rejoice, as having done a most famous eploit, nor was that behavior
unworthy of thee. ut if thy intention were otherwise, thou art worse than they
while thou didst contrive to hide thy treachery against thy father, thou didst

thyself fallen upon the like crime, but as successors of his dominions, and more
worthy of that succession than thyself. hou wouldst kill thy father after thy
brethren, lest thy lies raised against them might be detected and lest thou
shouldst suffer what punishment thou hadst deserved, thou hadst a mind to
eact that punishment of thy unhappy father, and didst devise such a sort of
uncommon parricide as the world never yet saw. or thou who art his son didst
not only lay a treacherous design against thy father, and didst it while he loved

what was future by thy father's determination, and the security of a written
testament but, for certain, thou didst not measure these things according to thy

and was desirous to take the part that remained away from thy too indulgent
father, and soughtest to destroy him with thy deeds, whom thou in words
pretendedst to preserve. or wast thou content to be wicked thyself, but thou
filledst thy mother's head with thy devices, and raised disturbances among thy

those already eamined thereby have made them tell lies that those that have

perpetration and he who does not punish it is injurious to nature itself."
. icolaus added further what belonged to ntipater's mother, and

there were a great number who for a long time beforehand had seen all this, and

. o when arus, upon asking ntipater what he had to say for himself,

the contents of this letter but the king made inquiry about the other letter also,

was this, that she had a mind to be married to ylleus. o thou therefore tear
this letter in pieces, that  may not come into danger of my life." ow cme
had written to ntipater himself, and informed him, that, in compliance with

. ereupon erod was so amazed at the prodigious wickedness of

. ow erod's ambassadors made haste to ome but sent, as instructed
beforehand, what answers they were to make to the questions put to them. hey
also carried the epistles with them. ut erod now fell into a distemper, and
made his will, and bequeathed his kingdom to ntipas], his youngest son and

had raised against them. e also bequeathed a thousand talents to aesar, and
five hundred to ulia, aesar's wife, to aesar's children, and friends and freed-
men. e also distributed among his sons and their sons his money, his
revenues, and his lands. e also made alome his sister very rich, because she
had continued faithful to him in all his circumstances, and was never so rash as
to do him any harm and as he despaired of recovering, for he was about the
seventieth year of his age, he grew fierce, and indulged the bitterest anger upon
all occasions the cause whereof was this, that he thought himself despised, and
that the nation was pleased with his misfortunes besides which, he resented a
sedition which some of the lower sort of men ecited against him, the occasion
of which was as follows.
. here was one udas, the son of aripheus, and atthias, the son of
argalothus, two of the most eloquent men among the ews, and the most
celebrated interpreters of the ewish laws, and men well beloved by the people,
because of their education of their youth for all those that were studious of
virtue frequented their lectures every day. hese men, when they found that the
king's distemper was incurable, ecited the young men that they would pull
down all those works which the king had erected contrary to the law of their
fathers, and thereby obtain the rewards which the law will confer on them for
such actions of piety for that it was truly on account of erod's rashness in
making such things as the law had forbidden, that his other misfortunes, and
this distemper also, which was so unusual among mankind, and with which he
was now afflicted, came upon him for erod had caused such things to be
made which were contrary to the law, of which he was accused by udas and
atthias for the king had erected over the great gate of the temple a large
golden eagle, of great value, and had dedicated it to the temple. ow the law
forbids those that propose to live according to it, to erect images,    or
representations of any living creature. o these wise men persuaded their
scholars] to pull down the golden eagle alleging, that although they should
incur any danger, which might bring them to their deaths, the virtue of the
action now proposed to them would appear much more advantageous to them

than the pleasures of life since they would die for the preservation and
observation of the law of their fathers since they would also acquire an
everlasting fame and commendation since they would be both commended by
the present generation, and leave an eample of life that would never be
forgotten to posterity since that common calamity of dying cannot be avoided
by our living so as to escape any such dangers that therefore it is a right thing
for those who are in love with a virtuous conduct, to wait for that fatal hour by
such behavior as may carry them out of the world with praise and honor and
that this will alleviate death to a great degree, thus to come at it by the
performance of brave actions, which bring us into danger of it and at the same
time to leave that reputation behind them to their children, and to all their
relations, whether they be men or women, which will be of great advantage to
them afterward.
. nd with such discourses as this did these men ecite the young men to
this action and a report being come to them that the king was dead, this was an
addition to the wise men's persuasions so, in the very middle of the day, they
got upon the place, they pulled down the eagle, and cut it into pieces with aes,
while a great number of the people were in the temple. nd now the king's
captain, upon hearing what the undertaking was, and supposing it was a thing
of a higher nature than it proved to be, came up thither, having a great band of
soldiers with him, such as was sufficient to put a stop to the multitude of those
who pulled down what was dedicated to od so he fell upon them
unepectedly, and as they were upon this bold attempt, in a foolish presumption
rather than a cautious circumspection, as is usual with the multitude, and while
they were in disorder, and incautious of what was for their advantage so he
caught no fewer than forty of the young men, who had the courage to stay
behind when the rest ran away, together with the authors of this bold attempt,
udas and atthias, who thought it an ignominious thing to retire upon his
approach, and led them to the king. nd when they were come to the king, and
he asked them if they had been so bold as to pull down what he had dedicated

oses had suggested to him, and were taught him by od, and which he wrote

ccordingly we will undergo death, and all sorts of punishments which thou
canst inflict upon us, with pleasure, since we are conscious to ourselves that we

od as that was that he had also adorned it with very valuable donations, on

. ut the people, on account of erod's barbarous temper, and for fear he
should be so cruel and to inflict punishment on them, said what was done was

he occasion was this this atthias the high priest, on the night before that
day when the fast was to be celebrated, seemed, in a dream,    to have
conversation with his wife and because he could not officiate himself on that

office. ut erod deprived this atthias of the high priesthood, and burnt the

that very night there was an eclipse of the moon. 
 . ut now erod's distemper greatly increased upon him after a severe
manner, and this by od's judgment upon him for his sins for a fire glowed in
him slowly, which did not so much appear to the touch outwardly, as it
augmented his pains inwardly for it brought upon him a vehement appetite to
eating, which he could not avoid to supply with one sort of food or other. is
entrails were also e-ulcerated, and the chief violence of his pain lay on his
colon an aqueous and transparent liquor also had settled itself about his feet,
and a like matter afflicted him at the bottom of his belly. ay, further, his privy-

member was putrified, and produced worms and when he sat upright, he had a
difficulty of breathing, which was very loathsome, on account of the stench of
his breath, and the quickness of its returns he had also convulsions in all parts
of his body, which increased his strength to an insufferable degree. t was said
by those who pretended to divine, and who were endued with wisdom to
foretell such things, that od inflicted this punishment on the king on account

and did not refuse to follow what they prescribed for his assistance, and went
beyond the river ordan, and bathed himself in the warm baths that were at
allirrhoe, which, besides their other general virtues, were also fit to drink
which water runs into the lake called sphaltitis. nd when the physicians once

having no longer the least hopes of recovering, he gave order that every soldier
should be paid fifty drachmae and he also gave a great deal to their
commanders, and to his friends, and came again to ericho, where he grew so
choleric, that it brought him to do all things like a madman and though he were

be called to him. ccordingly, they were a great number that came, because the
whole nation was called, and all men heard of this call, and death was the

now the king was in a wild rage against them all, the innocent as well as those

her husband leas, and spake thus to them " shall die in a little time, so great
are my pains which death ought to be cheerfully borne, and to be welcomed by
all men but what principally troubles me is this, that  shall die without being
lamented, and without such mourning as men usually epect at a king's death.
or that he was not unacquainted with the temper of the ews, that his death
would be a thing very desirable, and eceedingly acceptable to them, because

donations he had dedicated to od that it therefore was their business to resolve

they do not refuse him their consent in what he desires, he shall have a great
mourning at his funeral, and such as never had any king before him for then
the whole nation would mourn from their very soul, which otherwise would be
done in sport and mockery only. e desired therefore, that as soon as they see
he hath given up the ghost, they shall place soldiers round the hippodrome,
while they do not know that he is dead and that they shall not declare his death

to the multitude till this is done, but that they shall give orders to have those

cause that he shall not miss to rejoice on a double account that as he is dying,
they will make him secure that his will shall be eecuted in what he charges
them to do and that he shall have the honor of a memorable mourning at his
funeral. o he deplored his condition, with tears in his eyes, and obtested them

od, and begged of them that they would not hinder him of this honorable
mourning at his funeral. o they promised him not to transgress his commands.
. ow any one may easily discover the temper of this man's mind, which
not only took pleasure in doing what he had done formerly against his relations,
out of the love of life, but by those commands of his which savored of no
humanity since he took care, when he was departing out of this life, that the
whole nation should be put into mourning, and indeed made desolate of their
dearest kindred, when he gave order that one out of every family should be
slain, although they had done nothing that was unjust, or that was against him,
nor were they accused of any other crimes while it is usual for those who have
any regard to virtue to lay aside their hatred at such a time, even with respect to
those they justly esteemed their enemies.
  
         
         

. s he was giving these commands to his relations, there came letters from
his ambassadors, who had been sent to ome unto aesar, which, when they
were read, their purport was this that cme was slain by aesar, out of his
indignation at what hand she had in ntipater's wicked practices and that as to
ntipater himself, aesar left it to erod to act as became a father and a king,
and either to banish him, or to take away his life, which he pleased. hen
erod heard this, he was some-what better, out of the pleasure he had from the
contents of the letters, and was elevated at the death of cme, and at the power
that was given him over his son but as his pains were become very great, he
was now ready to faint for want of somewhat to eat so he called for an apple
and a knife for it was his custom formerly to pare the apple himself, and soon
afterwards to cut it, and eat it. hen he had got the knife, he looked about, and
had a mind to stab himself with it and he had done it, had not his first cousin,
chiabus, prevented him, and held his hand, and cried out loudly. hereupon a

woeful lamentation echoed through the palace, and a great tumult was made, as
if the king were dead. pon which ntipater, who verily believed his father
was deceased, grew bold in his discourse, as hoping to be immediately and
entirely released from his bonds, and to take the kingdom into his hands

in that case promised him great things, both now and hereafter, as if that were
the only thing now in question. ut the jailer did not only refuse to do what
ntipater would have him, but informed the king of his intentions, and how
many solicitations he had had from him of that nature]. ereupon erod, who
had formerly no affection nor good-will towards his son to restrain him, when

guards, and commanded them to kill ntipater without any further delay, and to
do it presently, and to bury him in an ignoble manner at yrcania.
  
  ' ,  ,  
. nd now erod altered his testament upon the alteration of his mind for he
appointed ntipas, to whom he had before left the kingdom, to be tetrarch of
alilee and erea, and granted the kingdom to rchelaus. e also gave
aulonitis, and rachonitis, and aneas to hilip, who was his son, but own
brother to rchelaus,    by the name of a tetrarchy and bequeathed amnia, and
shdod, and hasaelis to alome his sister, with five hundred thousand
drachmae] of silver that was coined. e also made provision for all the rest of
his kindred, by giving them sums of money and annual revenues, and so left
them all in a wealthy condition. e bequeathed also to aesar ten millions of
drachmae] of coined money, besides both vessels of gold and silver, and
garments eceeding costly, to ulia, aesar's wife and to certain others, five
millions. hen he had done these things, he died, the fifth day after he had
caused ntipater to be slain having reigned, since he had procured ntigonus 
 to be slain, thirty-four years but since he had been declared king by the
omans, thirty-seven.— man he was of great barbarity towards all men
equally, and a slave to his passion but above the consideration of what was
right yet was he favored by fortune as much as any man ever was, for from a
private man he became a king and though he were encompassed with ten
thousand dangers, he got clear of them all, and continued his life till a very old
age. ut then, as to the affairs of his family and children, in which indeed,
according to his own opinion, he was also very fortunate, because he was able

to conquer his enemies, yet, in my opinion, he was herein very unfortunate.
. ut then alome and leas, before the king's death was made known,
dismissed those that were shut up in the hippodrome, and told them that the
king ordered them to go away to their own lands, and take care of their own
affairs, which was esteemed by the nation a great benefit. nd now the king's
death was made public, when alome and leas gathered the soldiery together
in the amphitheatre at ericho and the first thing they did was, they read
erod's letter, written to the soldiery, thanking them for their fidelity and good-
will to him, and ehorting them to afford his son rchelaus, whom he had
appointed for their king, like fidelity and good-will. fter which tolemy, who
had the king's seal intrusted to him, read the king's testament, which was to be
of force no otherwise than as it should stand when aesar had inspected it so
there was presently an acclamation made to rchelaus, as king and the soldiers
came by bands, and their commanders with them, and promised the same good-
will to him, and readiness to serve him, which they had ehibited to erod and
they prayed od to be assistant to him.
. fter this was over, they prepared for his funeral, it being rchelaus's
care that the procession to his father's sepulchre should be very sumptuous.
ccordingly, he brought out all his ornaments to adorn the pomp of the funeral.
he body was carried upon a golden bier, embroidered with very precious
stones of great variety, and it was covered over with purple, as well as the body
itself he had a diadem upon his head, and above it a crown of gold he also had
a sceptre in his right hand. bout the bier were his sons and his numerous
relations net to these was the soldiery, distinguished according to their several
countries and denominations and they were put into the following order first

and net the band of alatians, every one in their habiliments of war and
behind these marched the whole army in the same manner as they used to go

centurions these were followed by five hundred of his domestics carrying
spices. o they went eight furlongs, to erodium for there by his own
command he was to be buried. nd thus did erod end his life.
. ow rchelaus paid him so much respect, as to continue his mourning
till the seventh day for so many days are appointed for it by the law of our
fathers. nd when he had given a treat to the multitude, and left off his
mourning, he went up into the temple he had also acclamations and praises
given him, which way soever he went, every one striving with the rest who
should appear to use the loudest acclamations. o he ascended a high elevation
made for him, and took his seat, in a throne made of gold, and spake kindly to
the multitude, and declared with what joy he received their acclamations, and
the marks of the good-will they showed to him and returned them thanks that

they did not remember the injuries his father had done them to his
disadvantage and promised them he would endeavor not to be behindhand with

he should abstain at present from the name of king, and that he should have the
honor of that dignity, if aesar should confirm and settle that testament which
his father had made and that it was on this account, that when the army would
have put the diadem on him at ericho, he would not accept of that honor,
which is usually so much desired, because it was not yet evident that he who
was to be principally concerned in bestowing it would give it him although, by

they were concerned, to prove in every respect better than his father.
hereupon the multitude, as it is usual with them, supposed that the first days
of those that enter upon such governments declare the intentions of those that
accept them and so by how much rchelaus spake the more gently and civilly
to them, by so much did they more highly commend him, and made application
to him for the grant of what they desired. ome made a clamor that he would
ease them of some of their annual payments but others desired him to release
those that were put into prison by erod, who were many, and had been put
there at several times others of them required that he would take away those
taes which had been severely laid upon what was publicly sold and bought. o
rchelaus contradicted them in nothing, since he pretended to do all things so
as to get the good-will of the multitude to him, as looking upon that good-will
to be a great step towards his preservation of the government. ereupon he
went and offered sacrifice to od, and then betook himself to feast with his
friends.

. t this time also it was that some of the ews got together out of a desire of
innovation. hey lamented atthias, and those that were slain with him by
erod, who had not any respect paid them by a funeral mourning, out of the
fear men were in of that man they were those who had been condemned for
pulling down the golden eagle. he people made a great clamor and
lamentation hereupon, and cast out some reproaches against the king also, as if
that tended to alleviate the miseries of the deceased. he people assembled
together, and desired of rchelaus, that, in way of revenge on their account, he

would inflict punishment on those who had been honored by erod and that, in

made, and would choose one more agreeable to the law, and of greater purity, to
officiate as high priest. his was granted by rchelaus, although he was
mightily offended at their importunity, because he proposed to himself to go to
ome immediately to look after aesar's determination about him. owever, he
sent the general of his forces to use persuasions, and to tell them that the death
which was inflicted on their friends was according to the law and to represent

. o when the king had suggested these things, and instructed his general
in what he was to say, be sent him away to the people but they made a clamor,
and would not give him leave to speak, and put him in danger of his life, and as
many more as were desirous to venture upon saying openly any thing which

courses, because they had more concern to have all their own wills performed

that, while erod was alive, they should lose those that were most dear to them,
and that when he was dead, they could not get the actors to be punished. o
they went on with their designs after a violent manner, and thought all to be
lawful and right which tended to please them, and being unskilful in foreseeing
what dangers they incurred and when they had suspicion of such a thing, yet
did the present pleasure they took in the punishment of those they deemed their
enemies overweigh all such considerations and although rchelaus sent many
to speak to them, yet they treated them not as messengers sent by him, but as
persons that came of their own accord to mitigate their anger, and would not let
one of them speak. he sedition also was made by such as were in a great
passion and it was evident that they were proceeding further in seditious
practices, by the multitude running so fast upon them.
. ow, upon the approach of that feast of unleavened bread, which the law
of their fathers had appointed for the ews at this time, which feast is called the
assover,    and is a memorial of their deliverance out of gypt, when they offer
sacrifices with great alacrity and when they are required to slay more sacrifices
in number than at any other festival and when an innumerable multitude came
thither out of the country, nay, from beyond its limits also, in order to worship
od, the seditious lamented udas and atthias, those teachers of the laws, and

kept together in the temple, and had plenty of food, because these seditious
persons were not ashamed to beg it. nd as rchelaus was afraid lest some

should bring them to him. ut those that were seditious on account of those

encourage the people in their designs so they made an assault upon the
soldiers, and came up to them, and stoned the greatest part of them, although
some of them ran away wounded, and their captain among them and when they

ow rchelaus thought there was no way to preserve the entire government but
by cutting off those who made this attempt upon it so he sent out the whole

without the temple from assisting those that were within the temple, and to kill
such as ran away from the footmen when they thought themselves out of

neighboring mountains. hen did rchelaus order proclamation to be made to

the festival, out of fear of somewhat worse which would follow, although they

belonging both to his own family and to the public. here went out also with

steward for yrian affairs, as he was making haste into udea to preserve

came at that time, and restrained him from meddling with them, for he was

regard to arus, did neither seize upon any of the castles that were among the
ews, nor did he seal up the treasures in them, but permitted rchelaus to have
them, until aesar should declare his resolution about them so that, upon this

ome, and arus was removed to ntioch, abinus went to erusalem, and

for all those that had the charge of erod's effects, and declared publicly that he

should require them to give an account of what they had and he disposed of the
castles in the manner he pleased but those who kept them did not neglect what

them all for aesar.
. t the same time also did ntipas, another of erod's sons, sail to ome,
in order to gain the government being buoyed up by alome with promises that
he should take that government and that he was a much honester and fitter man
than rchelaus for that authority, since erod had, in his former testament,
deemed him the worthiest to be made king, which ought to be esteemed more
valid than his latter testament. ntipas also brought with him his mother, and
tolemy the brother of icolaus, one that had been erod's most honored
friend, and was now zealous for ntipas but it was renaeus the orator, and one
who, on account of his reputation for sagacity, was intrusted with the affairs of
the kingdom, who most of all encouraged him to attempt to gain the kingdom
by whose means it was, that when some advised him to yield to rchelaus, as
to his elder brother, and who had been declared king by their father's last will,
he would not submit so to do. nd when he was come to ome, all his relations
revolted to him not out of their good-will to him, but out of their hatred to
rchelaus though indeed they were most of all desirous of gaining their
liberty, and to be put under a oman governor but if there were too great an
opposition made to that, they thought ntipas preferable to rchelaus, and so
joined with him, in order to procure the kingdom for him. abinus also, by
letters, accused rchelaus to aesar.
. ow when rchelaus had sent in his papers to aesar, wherein he
pleaded his right to the kingdom, and his father's testament, with the accounts
of erod's money, and with tolemy, who brought erod's seal, he so epected
the event but when aesar had read these papers, and arus's and abinus's
letters, with the accounts of the money, and what were the annual incomes of
the kingdom, and understood that ntipas had also sent letters to lay claim to
the kingdom, he summoned his friends together, to know their opinions, and
with them aius, the son of grippa, and of ulia his daughter, whom he had
adopted, and took him, and made him sit first of all, and desired such as pleased
to speak their minds about the affairs now before them. ow ntipater,
alome's son, a very subtle orator, and a bitter enemy to rchelaus, spake first
to this purpose that it was ridiculous in rchelaus to plead now to have the
kingdom given him, since he had, in reality, taken already the power over it to
himself, before aesar had granted it to him and appealed to those bold actions
of his, in destroying so many at the ewish festival and if the men had acted
unjustly, it was but fit the punishing of them should have been reserved to those
that were out of the country, but had the power to punish them, and not been

eecuted by a man that, if he pretended to be a king, he did an injury to aesar,
by usurping that authority before it was determined for him by aesar but if he
owned himself to be a private person, his case was much worse, since he who
was putting in for the kingdom could by no means epect to have that power
granted him, of which he had already deprived aesar by taking it to himself].
e also touched sharply upon him, and appealed to his changing the
commanders in the army, and his sitting in the royal throne beforehand, and his
determination of law-suits all done as if he were no other than a king. e
appealed also to his concessions to those that petitioned him on a public
account, and indeed doing such things, than which he could devise no greater if
he had been already settled in the kingdom by aesar. e also ascribed to him
the releasing of the prisoners that were in the hippodrome, and many other
things, that either had been certainly done by him, or were believed to be done,
and easily might be believed to have been done, because they were of such a
nature as to be usually done by young men, and by such as, out of a desire of
ruling, seize upon the government too soon. e also charged him with his
neglect of the funeral mourning for his father, and with having merry meetings
the very night in which he died and that it was thence the multitude took the
handle of raising a tumult and if rchelaus could thus requite his dead father,
who had bestowed such benefits upon him, and bequeathed such great things to
him, by pretending to shed tears for him in the day time, like an actor on the
stage, but every night making mirth for having gotten the government, he
would appear to be the same rchelaus with regard to aesar, if he granted him
the kingdom, which he hath been to his father since he had then dancing and
singing, as though an enemy of his were fallen, and not as though a man were
carried to his funeral, that was so nearly related, and had been so great a
benefactor to him. ut he said that the greatest crime of all was this, that he
came now before aesar to obtain the government by his grant, while he had
before acted in all things as he could have acted if aesar himself, who ruled
all, had fied him firmly in the government. nd what he most aggravated in
his pleading was the slaughter of those about the temple, and the impiety of it,
as done at the festival and how they were slain like sacrifices themselves, some
of whom were foreigners, and others of their own country, till the temple was
full of dead bodies and all this was done, not by an alien, but by one who
pretended to the lawful title of a king, that he might complete the wicked
tyranny which his nature prompted him to, and which is hated by all men. n
which account his father never so much as dreamed of making him his
successor in the kingdom, when he was of a sound mind, because he knew his
disposition and in his former and more authentic testament, he appointed his
antagonist ntipas to succeed but that rchelaus was called by his father to
that dignity when he was in a dying condition, both of body and mind while

ntipas was called when he was ripest in his judgment, and of such strength of
body as made him capable of managing his own affairs and if his father had
the like notion of him formerly that he hath now showed, yet hath he given a
sufficient specimen what a king he is likely to be, when he hath in effect]
deprived aesar of that power of disposing of the kingdom, which he justly
hath, and hath not abstained from making a terrible slaughter of his fellow
citizens in the temple, while lie was but a private person.
. o when ntipater had made this speech, and had confirmed what he had
said by producing many witnesses from among rchelaus's own relations, he
made an end of his pleading. pon which icolaus arose up to plead for
rchelaus, and said, "hat what had been done at the temple was rather to be
attributed to the mind of those that had been killed, than to the authority of
rchelaus for that those who were the authors of such things are not only
wicked in the injuries they do of themselves, but in forcing sober persons to
avenge themselves upon them. ow it is evident that what these did in way of
opposition was done under pretence, indeed, against rchelaus, but in reality
against aesar himself, for they, after an injurious manner, attacked and slew

 . ow yrenius, a oman senator, and one who had gone through other

without any dispute about it. et was there one udas, a aulonite,    of a city
whose name was amala, who, taking with him adduc,    a harisee, became
zealous to draw them to a revolt, who both said that this taation was no better
than an introduction to slavery, and ehorted the nation to assert their liberty as
if they could procure them happiness and security for what they possessed, and
an assured enjoyment of a still greater good, which was that of the honor and
glory they would thereby acquire for magnanimity. hey also said that od
would not otherwise be assisting to them, than upon their joining with one
another in such councils as might be successful, and for their own advantage
and this especially, if they would set about great eploits, and not grow weary

in eecuting the same so men received what they said with pleasure, and this
bold attempt proceeded to a great height. ll sorts of misfortunes also sprang
from these men, and the nation was infected with this doctrine to an incredible
degree one violent war came upon us after another, and we lost our friends
which used to alleviate our pains there were also very great robberies and
murder of our principal men. his was done in pretence indeed for the public
welfare, but in reality for the hopes of gain to themselves whence arose
seditions, and from them murders of men, which sometimes fell on those of
their own people, (by the madness of these men towards one another, while
their desire was that none of the adverse party might be left,) and sometimes on
their enemies a famine also coming upon us, reduced us to the last degree of
despair, as did also the taking and demolishing of cities nay, the sedition at last
increased so high, that the very temple of od was burnt down by their
enemies' fire. uch were the consequences of this, that the customs of our
fathers were altered, and such a change was made, as added a mighty weight
toward bringing all to destruction, which these men occasioned by their thus
conspiring together for udas and adduc,    who ecited a fourth philosophic
sect among us, and had a great many followers therein, filled our civil
government with tumults at present, and laid the foundations of our future
miseries, by this system of philosophy, which we were before unacquainted
withal, concerning which  will discourse a little, and this the rather because the
infection which spread thence among the younger sort, who were zealous for it,
brought the public to destruction.
. he ews had for a great while had three sects of philosophy peculiar to
themselves the sect of the ssens, and the sect of the adducees, and the third
sort of opinions was that of those called harisees of which sects, although 
have already spoken in the second book of the ewish ar, yet will  a little
touch upon them now.
. ow, for the harisees, they live meanly, and despise delicacies in diet
and they follow the conduct of reason and what that prescribes to them as good
for them they do and they think they ought earnestly to strive to observe
reason's dictates for practice. hey also pay a respect to such as are in years
nor are they so bold as to contradict them in any thing which they have
introduced and when they determine that all things are done by fate, they do
not take away the freedom from men of acting as they think fit since their
notion is, that it hath pleased od to make a temperament, whereby what he
wills is done, but so that the will of man can act virtuously or viciously. hey
also believe that souls have an immortal rigor in them, and that under the earth
there will be rewards or punishments, according as they have lived virtuously
or viciously in this life and the latter are to be detained in an everlasting prison,
but that the former shall have power to revive and live again on account of

which doctrines they are able greatly to persuade the body of the people and
whatsoever they do about ivine worship, prayers, and sacrifices, they perform
them according to their direction insomuch that the cities give great
attestations to them on account of their entire virtuous conduct, both in the
actions of their lives and their discourses also.
. ut the doctrine of the adducees is this that souls die with the bodies
nor do they regard the observation of any thing besides what the law enjoins
them for they think it an instance of virtue to dispute with those teachers of
philosophy whom they frequent but this doctrine is received but by a few, yet
by those still of the greatest dignity. ut they are able to do almost nothing of
themselves for when they become magistrates, as they are unwillingly and by
force sometimes obliged to be, they addict themselves to the notions of the
harisees, because the multitude would not otherwise bear them.
. he doctrine of the ssens is this that all things are best ascribed to od.
hey teach the immortality of souls, and esteem that the rewards of
righteousness are to be earnestly striven for and when they send what they
have dedicated to od into the temple, they do not offer sacrifices,    because
they have more pure lustrations of their own on which account they are
ecluded from the common court of the temple, but offer their sacrifices
themselves yet is their course of life better than that of other men and they
entirely addict themselves to husbandry. t also deserves our admiration, how
much they eceed all other men that addict themselves to virtue, and this in
righteousness and indeed to such a degree, that as it hath never appeared
among any other men, neither reeks nor barbarians, no, not for a little time, so
hath it endured a long while among them. his is demonstrated by that
institution of theirs, which will not suffer any thing to hinder them from having
all things in common so that a rich man enjoys no more of his own wealth than
he who hath nothing at all. here are about four thousand men that live in this
way, and neither marry wives, nor are desirous to keep servants as thinking the
latter tempts men to be unjust, and the former gives the handle to domestic
quarrels but as they live by themselves, they minister one to another. hey also
appoint certain stewards to receive the incomes of their revenues, and of the
fruits of the ground such as are good men and priests, who are to get their corn
and their food ready for them. hey none of them differ from others of the
ssens in their way of living, but do the most resemble those acae who are
called olistae    dwellers in cities].
. ut of the fourth sect of ewish philosophy, udas the alilean was the
author. hese men agree in all other things with the harisaic notions but they
have an inviolable attachment to liberty, and say that od is to be their only
uler and ord. hey also do not value dying any kinds of death, nor indeed do
they heed the deaths of their relations and friends, nor can any such fear make

them call any man lord. nd since this immovable resolution of theirs is well
known to a great many,  shall speak no further about that matter nor am 
afraid that any thing  have said of them should be disbelieved, but rather fear,
that what  have said is beneath the resolution they show when they undergo
pain. nd it was in essius lorus's time that the nation began to grow mad
with this distemper, who was our procurator, and who occasioned the ews to
go wild with it by the abuse of his authority, and to make them revolt from the
omans. nd these are the sects of ewish philosophy.

. ut now ilate, the procurator of udea, removed the army from esarea to

. ut the nation of the amaritans did not escape without tumults. he man

interposition of rovidence, how a multitude of children is of no advantage, no

. o when iberius had at this time appointed aius to be his successor,

. ut erodias, grippa's sister, who now lived as wife to that erod who

. e then called the ews together to iberias, who came many ten

. hen etronius had said this, and had dismissed rite assembly of the

. ut king grippa, who now lived at ome, was more and more in the

. his was the epistle which aius wrote to etronius but etronius did

give aius his due reward, because he had been unmercifully severe to them

for he died not long after he had written to etronius that epistle which
threatened him with death. ut as for the occasion of his death, and the nature
of the plot against him,  shall relate them in the progress of this narration. ow
that epistle which informed etronius of aius's death came first, and a little
afterward came that which commanded him to kill himself with his own hands.
hereupon he rejoiced at this coincidence as to the death of aius, and
admired od's providence, who, without the least delay, and immediately, gave
him a reward for the regard he had to the temple, and the assistance he afforded
the ews for avoiding the dangers they were in. nd by this means etronius
escaped that danger of death, which he could not foresee.

.  very sad calamity now befell the ews that were in esopotamia, and
especially those that dwelt in abylonia. nferior it was to none of the
calamities which had gone before, and came together with a great slaughter of
them, and that greater than any upon record before concerning all which  shall
speak accurately, and shall eplain the occasions whence these miseries came
upon them. here was a city of abylonia called eerda not only a ver
populous one, but one that had a good and a large territory about it, and,
besides its other advantages, full of men also. t was, besides, not easily to be
assaulted by enemies, from the river uphrates encompassing it all round, and
from the wails that were built about it. here was also the city isibis, situate
on the same current of the river. or which reason the ews, depending on the
natural strength of these places, deposited in them that half shekel which every
one, by the custom of our country, offers unto od, as well as they did other
things devoted to him for they made use of these cities as a treasury, whence,
at a proper time, they were transmitted to erusalem and many ten thousand
men undertook the carriage of those donations, out of fear of the ravages of the
arthians, to whom the abylonians were then subject. ow there were two
men, sineus and nileus, of the city eerda by birth, and brethren to one
another. hey were destitute of a father, and their mother put them to learn the
art of weaving curtains, it not being esteemed a disgrace among them for men
to be weavers of cloth. ow he that taught them that art, and was set over them,
complained that they came too late to their work, and punished them with
stripes but they took this just punishment as an affront, and carried off all the
weapons which were kept in that house, which were not a few, and went into a

certain place where was a partition of the rivers, and was a place naturally very
fit for the feeding of cattle, and for preserving such fruits as were usually laid
up against winter. he poorest sort of the young men also resorted to them,
whom they armed with the weapons they had gotten, and became their
captains and nothing hindered them from being their leaders into mischief for
as soon as they were become invincible, and had built them a citadel, they sent
to such as fed cattle, and ordered them to pay them so much tribute out of them
as might be sufficient for their maintenance, proposing also that they would be
their friends, if they would submit to them, and that they would defend them
from all their other enemies on every side, but that they would kill the cattle of
those that refused to obey them. o they hearkened to their proposals, (for they
could do nothing else,) and sent them as many sheep as were required of them
whereby their forces grew greater, and they became lords over all they pleased,
because they marched suddenly, and did them a mischief, insomuch that every
body who had to do with them chose to pay them respect and they became
formidable to such as came to assault them, till the report about them came to
the ears of the king of arthia himself.
. ut when the governor of abylonia understood this, and had a mind to
put a stop to them before they grew greater, and before greater mischiefs should
arise from them, he got together as great an army as he could, both of arthians
and abylonians, and marched against them, thinking to attack them and
destroy them before any one should carry them the news that he had got an
army together. e then encamped at a lake, and lay still but on the net day (it
was the abbath, which is among the ews a day of rest from all sorts of work)
he supposed that the enemy would not dare to fight him thereon, but that he
would take them and carry them away prisoners, without fighting. e therefore
proceeded gradually, and thought to fall upon them on the sudden. ow
sineus was sitting with the rest, and their weapons lay by them upon which
he said, "irs,  hear a neighing of horses not of such as are feeding, but such
as have men on their backs  also hear such a noise of their bridles, that  am
afraid that some enemies are coming upon us to encompass us round. owever,
let somebody go to look about, and make    report of what reality there is in the
present state of things and may what  have said prove a false alarm." nd
when he had said this, some of them went out to spy out what was the matter
and they came again immediately, and said to him, that "neither hast thou been
mistaken in telling us what our enemies were doing, nor will those enemies
permit us to be injurious to people any longer. e are caught by their intrigues
like brute beasts, and there is a large body of cavalry marching upon us, while
we are destitute of hands to defend ourselves withal, because we are restrained
from doing it by the prohibition of our law, which obliges us to rest on this
day]." ut sineus did not by any means agree with the opinion of his spy as to

what was to be done, but thought it more agreeable to the law to pluck up their
spirits in this necessity they were fallen into, and break their law by avenging
themselves, although they should die in the action, than by doing nothing to
please their enemies in submitting to be slain by them. ccordingly, he took up
his weapons, and infused courage into those that were with him to act as
courageously as himself. o they fell upon their enemies, and slew a great
many of them, because they despised them and came as to a certain victory, and
put the rest to flight.
. ut when the news of this fight came to the king of arthia, he was
surprised at the boldness of these brethren, and was desirous to see them, and
speak with them. e therefore sent the most trusty of all his guards to say thus
to them "hat king rtabanus, although he had been unjustly treated by you,
who have made an attempt against his government, yet hath he more regard to
your courageous behavior, than to the anger he bears to you, and hath sent me
to give you his right hand    and security and he permits you to come to him
safely, and without any violence upon the road and he wants to have you
address yourselves to him as friends, without meaning any guile or deceit to
you. e also promises to make you presents, and to pay you those respects
which will make an addition of his power to your courage, and thereby be of
advantage to you." et did sineus himself put off his journey thither, but sent
his brother nileus with all such presents as he could procure. o he went, and
was admitted to the king's presence and when rtabanus saw nileus coming
alone, he inquired into the reason why sineus avoided to come along with
him and when he understood that he was afraid, and staid by the lake, he took
an oath, by the gods of his country, that he would do them no harm, if they
came to him upon the assurances he gave them, and gave him his right hand. 
 his is of the greatest force there with all these barbarians, and affords a firm
security to those who converse with them for none of them will deceive you
when once they have given you their right hands, nor will any one doubt of
their fidelity, when that is once given, even though they were before suspected
of injustice. hen rtabanus had done this, he sent away nileus to persuade
his brother to come to him. ow this the king did, because he wanted to curb
his own governors of provinces by the courage of these ewish brethren, lest
they should make a league with them for they were ready for a revolt, and
were disposed to rebel, had they been sent on an epedition against them. e
was also afraid, lest when he was engaged in a war, in order to subdue those
governors of provinces that had revolted, the party of sineus, and those in
abylonia, should be augmented, and either make war upon him, when they
should hear of that revolt, or if they should be disappointed in that case, they
would not fail of doing further mischief to him.
. hen the king had these intentions, he sent away nileus, and nileus

prevailed on his brother to come to the king], when he had related to him the
king's good-will, and the oath that he had taken. ccordingly, they made haste
to go to rtabanus, who received them when they were come with pleasure,
and admired sineus's courage in the actions he had done, and this because he
was a little man to see to, and at first sight appeared contemptible also, and
such as one might deem a person of no value at all. e also said to his friends,
how, upon the comparison, he showed his soul to be in all respects superior to
his body and when, as they were drinking together, he once showed sineus to
bdagases, one of the generals of his army, and told him his name, and
described the great courage he was of in war, and bdagases had desired leave
to kill him, and thereby to inflict on him a punishment for those injuries he had
done to the arthian government, the king replied, " will never give thee leave
to kill a man who hath depended on my faith, especially not after  have sent
him my right hand, and endeavored to gain his belief by oaths made by the
gods. ut if thou be a truly warlike man, thou standest not in need of my
perjury. o thou then, and avenge the arthian government attack this man,
when he is returned back, and conquer him by the forces that are under thy
command, without my privity." ereupon the king called for sineus, and said
to him, "t is time for thee,  thou young man! to return home, and not provoke
the indignation of my generals in this place any further, lest they attempt to
murder thee, and that without my approbation.  commit to thee the country of
abylonia in trust, that it may, by thy care, be preserved free from robbers, and
from other mischiefs.  have kept my faith inviolable to thee, and that not in
trifling affairs, but in those that concerned thy safety, and do therefore deserve
thou shouldst be kind to me." hen he had said this, and given sineus some
presents, he sent him away immediately who, when he was come home, built
fortresses, and became great in a little time, and managed things with such
courage and success, as no other person, that had no higher a beginning, ever
did before him. hose arthian governors also, who were sent that way, paid
him great respect and the honor that was paid him by the abylonians seemed
to them too small, and beneath his deserts, although he were in no small dignity
and power there nay, indeed, all the affairs of esopotamia depended upon
him, and he more and more flourished in this happy condition of his for fifteen
years.
. ut as their affairs were in so flourishing a state, there sprang up a
calamity among them on the following occasion. hen once they had deviated
from that course of virtue whereby they had gained so great power, they
affronted and transgressed the laws of their forefathers, and fell under the
dominion of their lusts and pleasures.  certain arthian, who came as general
of an army into those parts, had a wife following him, who had a vast
reputation for other accomplishments, and particularly was admired above all

other women for her beauty. nileus, the brother of sineus, either heard of
that her beauty from others, or perhaps saw her himself also, and so became at
once her lover and her enemy partly because he could not hope to enjoy this
woman but by obtaining power over her as a captive, and partly because he
thought he could not conquer his inclinations for her. s soon therefore as her
husband had been declared an enemy to them, and was fallen in the battle, the
widow of the deceased was married to this her lover. owever, this woman did
not come into their house without producing great misfortunes, both to nileus
himself, and to sineus also but brought great mischiefs upon them on the
occasion following. ince she was led away captive, upon the death of her
husband, she concealed the images of those gods which were their country
gods, common to her husband and to herself now it was the custom    of that
country for all to have the idols they worship in their own houses, and to carry
them along with them when they go into a foreign land agreeable to which
custom of theirs she carried her idols with her. ow at first she performed her
worship to them privately but when she was become nileus's married wife,
she worshipped them in her accustomed manner, and with the same appointed
ceremonies which she used in her former husband's days upon which their
most esteemed friends blamed him at first, that he did not act after the manner
of the ebrews, nor perform what was agreeable to their laws, in marrying a
foreign wife, and one that transgressed the accurate appointments of their
sacrifices and religious ceremonies that he ought to consider, lest, by allowing
himself in many pleasures of the body, he might lose his principality, on
account of the beauty of a wife, and that high authority which, by od's
blessing, he had arrived at. ut when they prevailed not at all upon him, he
slew one of them for whom he had the greatest respect, because of the liberty
he took with him who, when he was dying, out of regard to the laws,
imprecated a punishment upon his murderer nileus, and upon sineus also,
and that all their companions might come to a like end from their enemies
upon the two first as the principal actors of this wickedness, and upon the rest
as those that would not assist him when he suffered in the defence of their laws.
ow these latter were sorely grieved, yet did they tolerate these doings,
because they remembered that they had arrived at their present happy state by
no other means than their fortitude. ut when they also heard of the worship of
those gods whom the arthians adore, they thought the injury that nileus
offered to their laws was to be borne no longer and a greater number of them
came to sineus, and loudly complained of nileus, and told him that it had
been well that he had of himself seen what was advantageous to them but that
however it was now high time to correct what had been done amiss, before the
crime that had been committed proved the ruin of himself and all the rest of
them. hey added, that the marriage of this woman was made without their

consent, and without a regard to their old laws and that the worship which this
woman paid to her gods] was a reproach to the od whom they worshipped.
ow sineus was sensible of his brother's offense, that it had been already the
cause of great mischiefs, and would be so for the time to come yet did he
tolerate the same from the good-will he had to so near a relation, and forgiving
it to him, on account that his brother was quite overborne by his wicked
inclinations. ut as more and more still came about him every day, and the
clamors about it became greater, he at length spake to nileus about these
clamors, reproving him for his former actions, and desiring him for the future
to leave them off, and send the woman back to her relations. ut nothing was
gained by these reproofs for as the woman perceived what a tumult was made
among the people on her account, and was afraid for nileus, lest he should
come to any harm for his love to her, she infused poison into sineus's food,
and thereby took him off, and was now secure of prevailing, when her lover
was to be judge of what should be done about her.
. o nileus took the government upon himself alone, and led his army
against the villages of ithridates, who was a man of principal authority in
arthia, and had married king rtabanus's daughter he also plundered them,
and among that prey was found much money, and many slaves, as also a great
number of sheep, and many other things, which, when gained, make men's
condition happy. ow when ithridates, who was there at this time, heard that
his villages were taken, he was very much displeased to find that nileus had
first begun to injure him, and to affront him in his present dignity, when he had
not offered any injury to him beforehand and he got together the greatest body
of horsemen he was able, and those out of that number which were of an age fit
for war, and came to fight nileus and when he was arrived at a certain village
of his own, he lay still there, as intending to fight him on the day following,
because it was the abbath, the day on which the ews rest. nd when nileus
was informed of this by a yrian stranger of another village, who not only gave
him an eact account of other circumstances, but told him where ithridates
would have a feast, he took his supper at a proper time, and marched by night,
with an intent of falling upon the arthians while they were unapprised what
they should do so he fell upon them about the fourth watch of the night, and
some of them he slew while they were asleep, and others he put to flight, and
took ithridates alive, and set him naked upon an ass,    which, among the
arthians, is esteemed the greatest reproach possible. nd when he had brought
him into a wood with such a resolution, and his friends desired him to kill
ithridates, he soon told them his own mind to the contrary, and said that it
was not right to kill a man who was of one of the principal families among the
arthians, and greatly honored with matching into the royal family that so far
as they had hitherto gone was tolerable for although they had injured

ithridates, yet if they preserved his life, this benefit would be remembered by
him to the advantage of those that gave it him but that if be were once put to
death, the king would not be at rest till he had made a great slaughter of the
ews that dwelt at abylon "to whose safety we ought to have a regard, both
on account of our relation to them, and because if any misfortune befall us, we
have no other place to retire to, since he hath gotten the flower of their youth
under him." y this thought, and this speech of his made in council, he
persuaded them to act accordingly so ithridates was let go. ut when he was
got away, his wife reproached him, that although he was son-in-law to the king,
he neglected to avenge himself on those that had injured him, while he took no
care about it, but was contented to have been made a captive by the ews, and
to have escaped them and she bid him either to go back like a man of courage,
or else she sware by the gods of their royal family that she would certainly
dissolve her marriage with him. pon which, partly because he could not bear
the daily trouble of her taunts, and partly because he was afraid of her
insolence, lest she should in earnest dissolve their marriage, he unwillingly, and
against his inclinations, got together again as great an army as he could, and
marched along with them, as himself thinking it a thing not to be borne any
longer, that he, a arthian, should owe his preservation to the ews, when they
had been too hard for him in the war.
. ut as soon as nileus understood that ithridates was marching with a
great army against him, he thought it too ignominious a thing to tarry about the
lakes, and not to take the first opportunity of meeting his enemies, and he
hoped to have the same success, and to beat their enemies as they did before as
also he ventured boldly upon the like attempts. ccordingly, he led out his
army, and a great many more joined themselves to that army, in order to betake
themselves to plunder the people, and in order to terrify the enemy again by
their numbers. ut when they had marched ninety furlongs, while the road had
been through dry and sandy] places, and about the midst of the day, they were
become very thirsty and ithridates appeared, and fell upon them, as they
were in distress for want of water, on which account, and on account of the
time of the day, they were not able to bear their weapons. o nileus and his
men were put to an ignominious rout, while men in despair were to attack those
that were fresh and in good plight so a great slaughter was made, and many ten
thousand men fell. ow nileus, and all that stood firm about him, ran away as
fast as they were able into a wood, and afforded ithridates the pleasure of
having gained a great victory over them. ut there now came in to nileus a
conflu of bad men, who regarded their own lives very little, if they might but
gain some present ease, insomuch that they, by thus coming to him,
compensated the multitude of those that perished in the fight. et were not
these men like to those that fell, because they were rash, and uneercised in

war however, with these he came upon the villages of the abylonians, and a
mighty devastation of all things was made there by the injuries that nileus did
them. o the abylonians, and those that had already been in the war, sent to
eerda to the ews there, and demanded nileus. ut although they did not
agree to their demands, (for if they had been willing to deliver him up, it was
not in their power so to do,) yet did they desire to make peace with them. o
which the other replied, that they also wanted to settle conditions of peace with
them, and sent men together with the abylonians, who discoursed with
nileus about them. ut the abylonians, upon taking a view of his situation,
and having learned where nileus and his men lay, fell secretly upon them as
they were drunk and fallen asleep, and slew all that they caught of them,
without any fear, and killed nileus himself also.
. he abylonians were now freed from nileus's heavy incursions, which
had been a great restraint to the effects of that hatred they bore to the ews for
they were almost always at variance, by reason of the contrariety of their laws
and which party soever grew boldest before the other, they assaulted the other
and at this time in particular it was, that upon the ruin of nileus's party, the
abylonians attacked the ews, which made those ews so vehemently to resent
the injuries they received from the abylonians, that being neither able to fight
them, nor bearing to live with them, they went to eleucia, the principal city of
those parts, which was built by eleucus icator. t was inhabited by many of
the acedonians, but by more of the recians not a few of the yrians also
dwelt there and thither did the ews fly, and lived there five years, without any
misfortunes. ut on the sith year, a pestilence came upon these at abylon,
which occasioned new removals of men's habitations out of that city and
because they came to eleucia, it happened that a still heavier calamity came
upon them on that account which  am going to relate immediately.
. ow the way of living of the people of eleucia, which were reeks and
yrians, was commonly quarrelsome, and full of discords, though the reeks
were too hard for the yrians. hen, therefore, the ews were come thither, and
dwelt among them, there arose a sedition, and the yrians were too hard for the
other, by the assistance of the ews, who are men that despise dangers, and very
ready to fight upon any occasion. ow when the reeks had the worst in this
sedition, and saw that they had but one way of recovering their former
authority, and that was, if they could prevent the agreement between the ews
and the yrians, they every one discoursed with such of the yrians as were
formerly their acquaintance, and promised they would be at peace and
friendship with them. ccordingly, they gladly agreed so to do and when this
was done by the principal men of both nations, they soon agreed to a
reconciliation and when they were so agreed, they both knew that the great
design of such their union would be their common hatred to the ews.

ccordingly, they fell upon them, and slew about fifty thousand of them nay,
the ews were all destroyed, ecepting a few who escaped, either by the
compassion which their friends or neighbors afforded them, in order to let them
fly away. hese retired to tesiphon, a recian city, and situate near to
eleucia, where the king of arthia] lives in winter every year, and where the
greatest part of his riches are reposited but the ews had here no certain
settlement, those of eleucia having little concern for the king's honor. ow the
whole nation of the ews were in fear both of the abylonians and of the
eleucians, because all the yrians that live in those places agreed with the
eleucians in the war against the ews so the most of them gathered
themselves together, and went to eerda and isibis, and obtained security
there by the strength of those cities besides which their inhabitants, who were a
great many, were all warlike men. nd this was the state of the ews at this time
in abylonia.

without being dismayed, made an ehortatory oration to them, and such a one

. "lthough it be a thing incredible,  omans! because of the great length

. ow laudius aesar, by these decrees of his which were sent to

. ow ilas, the general of the king's horse, because he had been faithful to

. ow this king was by nature very beneficent and liberal in his gifts, and

. nd thus did king grippa depart this life. ut he left behind him a son,

. bout this time it was that elena, queen of diabene, and her son zates,
changed their course of life, and embraced the ewish customs, and this on the
occasion following onobazus, the king of diabene, who had also the name
of azeus, fell in love with his sister elena, and took her to be his wife, and
begat her with child. ut as he was in bed with her one night, he laid his hand
upon his wife's belly, and fell asleep, and seemed to hear a voice, which bid

voice put him into disorder so he awaked immediately, and told the story to his
wife and when his son was born, he called him zates. e had indeed
onobazus, his elder brother, by elena also, as he had other sons by other

begotten    son zates, which was the origin of that envy which his other

prefer zates before them. ow although their father was very sensible of these

. ut when onobazus was grown old, and saw that he had but a little

ut the very day that onobazus died, queen elena sent for all the grandees,
