them " believe you are not unacquainted that my husband was desirous zates
should succeed him in the government, and thought him worthy so to do.
owever,  wait your determination for happy is he who receives a kingdom,
not from a single person only, but from the willing suffrages of a great many."
his she said, in order to try those that were invited, and to discover their
sentiments. pon the hearing of which, they first of all paid their homage to the

. ow, during the time zates abode at hara-pasini, a certain ewish
merchant, whose name was nanias, got among the women that belonged to

with the omans but he could not prevail with him. or zates so well knew
the strength and good fortune of the omans, that he took ardanes to attempt
what was impossible to be done and having besides sent his sons, five in
number, and they but young also, to learn accurately the language of our nation,
together with our learning, as well as he had sent his mother to worship at our
temple, as  have said already, was the more backward to a compliance and
restrained ardanes, telling him perpetually of the great armies and famous
actions of the omans, and thought thereby to terrify him, and desired thereby
to hinder him from that epedition. ut the arthian king was provoked at this
his behavior, and denounced war immediately against zates. et did he gain no
advantage by this war, because od cut off all his hopes therein for the

arthians perceiving ardanes's intentions, and how he had determined to make
war with the omans, slew him, and gave his kingdom to his brother otarzes.
e also, in no long time, perished by a plot made against him, and ologases,
his brother, succeeded him, who committed two of his provinces to two of his
brothers by the same father that of the edes to the elder, acorus and
rmenia to the younger, iridates.
  
        ,  
      ,  
  ,      
. ow when the king's brother, onobazus, and his other kindred, saw how
zates, by his piety to od, was become greatly esteemed by all men, they also
had a desire to leave the religion of their country, and to embrace the customs
of the ews but that act of theirs was discovered by zates's subjects.
hereupon the grandees were much displeased, and could not contain their
anger at them but had an intention, when they should find a proper
opportunity, to inflict a punishment upon them. ccordingly, they wrote to
bia, king of the rabians, and promised him great sums of money, if he would
make an epedition against their king and they further promised him, that, on
the first onset, they would desert their king, because they were desirous to
punish him, by reason of the hatred he had to their religious worship then they
obliged themselves, by oaths, to be faithful to each other, and desired that he
would make haste in this design. he king of rabia complied with their
desires, and brought a great army into the field, and marched against zates
and, in the beginning of the first onset, and before they came to a close fight,
those grandees, as if they had a panic terror upon them, all deserted zates, as
they had agreed to do, and, turning their backs upon their enemies, ran away.
et was not zates dismayed at this but when he understood that the grandees
had betrayed him, he also retired into his camp, and made inquiry into the
matter and as soon as he knew who they were that made this conspiracy with
the king of rabia, he cut off those that were found guilty and renewing the
fight on the net day, he slew the greatest part of his enemies, and forced all the
rest to betake themselves to flight. e also pursued their king, and drove him
into a fortress called rsamus, and following on the siege vigorously, he took
that fortress. nd when he had plundered it of all the prey that was in it, which
was not small, he returned to diabene yet did not he take bia alive, because,
when he found himself encompassed on every side, he slew himself.

. ut although the grandees of diabene had failed in their first attempt, as
being delivered up by od into their king's hands, yet would they not even then
be quiet, but wrote again to ologases, who was then king of arthia, and
desired that he would kill zates, and set over them some other potentate, who
should be of a arthian family for they said that they hated their own king for
abrogating the laws of their forefathers, and embracing foreign customs. hen
the king of arthia heard this, he boldly made war upon zates and as he had
no just pretence for this war, he sent to him, and demanded back those
honorable privileges which had been bestowed on him by his father, and
threatened, on his refusal, to make war upon him. pon hearing of this, zates
was under no small trouble of mind, as thinking it would be a reproach upon
him to appear to resign those privileges that had been bestowed upon him out
of cowardice yet because he knew, that though the king of arthia should
receive back those honors, yet would he not be quiet, he resolved to commit
himself to od, his rotector, in the present danger he was in of his life and as
he esteemed him to be his principal assistant, he intrusted his children and his
wives to a very strong fortress, and laid up his corn in his citadels, and set the
hay and the grass on fire. nd when he had thus put things in order, as well as
he could, he awaited the coming of the enemy. nd when the king of arthia
was come, with a great army of footmen and horsemen, which he did sooner
than was epected, (for he marched in great haste,) and had cast up a bank at
the river that parted diabene from edia,—zates also pitched his camp not
far off, having with him si thousand horsemen. ut there came a messenger to
zates, sent by the king of arthia, who told him how large his dominions were,
as reaching from the river uphrates to actria, and enumerated that king's
subjects he also threatened him that he should be punished, as a person
ungrateful to his lords and said that the od whom he worshipped could not
deliver him out of the king's hands. hen the messenger had delivered this his
message, zates replied that he knew the king of arthia's power was much
greater than his own but that he knew also that od was much more powerful
than all men. nd when he had returned him this answer, he betook himself to
make supplication to od, and threw himself upon the ground, and put ashes
upon his head, in testimony of his confusion, and fasted, together with his
wives and children.    hen he called upon od, and said, " ord and
overnor, if  have not in vain committed myself to thy goodness, but have
justly determined that thou only art the ord and principal of all beings, come
now to my assistance, and defend me from my enemies, not only on my own
account, but on account of their insolent behavior with regard to thy power,
while they have not feared to lift up their proud and arrogant tongue against
thee." hus did he lament and bemoan himself, with tears in his eyes
whereupon od heard his prayer. nd immediately that very night ologases

received letters, the contents of which were these, that a great band of ahae
and acae, despising him, now he was gone so long a journey from home, had
made an epedition, and laid arthia waste so that he was forced to] retire
back, without doing any thing. nd thus it was that zates escaped the
threatenings of the arthians, by the providence of od.
. t was not long ere zates died, when he had completed fifty-five years of
his life, and had ruled his kingdom twenty-four years. e left behind him
twenty-four sons and twenty-four daughters. owever, he gave order that his
brother onobazus should succeed in the government, thereby requiting him,
because, while he was himself absent after their father's death, he had faithfully
preserved the government for him. ut when elena, his mother, heard of her
son's death, she was in great heaviness, as was but natural, upon her loss of
such a most dutiful son yet was it a comfort to her that she heard the
succession came to her eldest son. ccordingly, she went to him in haste and
when she was come into diabene, she did not long outlive her son zates. ut
onobazus sent her bones, as well as those of zates, his brother, to erusalem,
and gave order that they should be buried at the pyramids    which their mother
had erected they were three in number, and distant no more than three furlongs
from the city erusalem. ut for the actions of onobazus the king, which he
did during the rest of his life. we will relate them hereafter. 
  
  ,       
           
 
. ow it came to pass, while adus was procurator of udea, that a certain
magician, whose name was heudas,    persuaded a great part of the people to
take their effects with them, and follow him to the river ordan for he told
them he was a prophet, and that he would, by his own command, divide the
river, and afford them an easy passage over it and many were deluded by his
words. owever, adus did not permit them to make any advantage of his wild
attempt, but sent a troop of horsemen out against them who, falling upon them
unepectedly, slew many of them, and took many of them alive. hey also took
heudas alive, and cut off his head, and carried it to erusalem. his was what
befell the ews in the time of uspius adus's government.
. hen came iberius leander as successor to adus he was the son of
leander the alabarch of leandria, which leander was a principal person
among all his contemporaries, both for his family and wealth he was also more

eminent for his piety than this his son leander, for he did not continue in the
religion of his country. nder these procurators that great famine happened in
udea, in which queen elena bought corn in gypt at a great epense, and
distributed it to those that were in want, as  have related already. nd besides
this, the sons of udas of alilee were now slain  mean of that udas who
caused the people to revolt, when yrenius came to take an account of the
estates of the ews, as we have showed in a foregoing book. he names of those
sons were ames and imon, whom leander commanded to be crucified. ut
now erod, king of halcis, removed oseph, the son of amydus, from the
high priesthood, and made nanias, the son of ebedeus, his successor. nd
now it was that umanus came as successor to iberius leander as also that
erod, brother of grippa the great king, departed this life, in the eighth year of
the reign of laudius aesar. e left behind him three sons ristobulus, whom
he had by his first wife, with ernicianus, and yrcanus, both whom he had by
ernice his brother's daughter. ut laudius aesar bestowed his dominions on
grippa, junior.
. ow while the ewish affairs were under the administration of umanus,
there happened a great tumult at the city of erusalem, and many of the ews
perished therein. ut  shall first eplain the occasion whence it was derived.
hen that feast which is called the passover was at hand, at which time our
custom is to use unleavened bread, and a great multitude was gathered together
from all parts to that feast, umanus was afraid lest some attempt of innovation
should then be made by them so he ordered that one regiment of the army
should take their arms, and stand in the temple cloisters, to repress any attempts
of innovation, if perchance any such should begin and this was no more than
what the former procurators of udea did at such festivals. ut on the fourth
day of the feast, a certain soldier let down his breeches, and eposed his privy
members to the multitude, which put those that saw him into a furious rage, and
made them cry out that this impious action was not done to approach them, but
od himself nay, some of them reproached umanus, and pretended that the
soldier was set on by him, which, when umanus heard, he was also himself
not a little provoked at such reproaches laid upon him yet did he ehort them
to leave off such seditious attempts, and not to raise a tumult at the festival. ut
when he could not induce them to be quiet for they still went on in their
reproaches to him, he gave order that the whole army should take their entire
armor, and come to ntonia, which was a fortress, as we have said already,
which overlooked the temple but when the multitude saw the soldiers there,
they were affrighted at them, and ran away hastily but as the passages out were
but narrow, and as they thought their enemies followed them, they were
crowded together in their flight, and a great number were pressed to death in
those narrow passages nor indeed was the number fewer than twenty thousand

that perished in this tumult. o instead of a festival, they had at last a mournful
day of it and they all of them forgot their prayers and sacrifices, and betook
themselves to lamentation and weeping so great an affliction did the impudent
obsceneness of a single soldier bring upon them. 
 . ow before this their first mourning was over, another mischief befell
them also for some of those that raised the foregoing tumult, when they were
traveling along the public road, about a hundred furlongs from the city, robbed
tephanus, a servant of aesar, as he was journeying, and plundered him of all
that he had with him which things when umanus heard of, he sent soldiers
immediately, and ordered them to plunder the neighboring villages, and to
bring the most eminent persons among them in bonds to him. ow as this
devastation was making, one of the soldiers seized the laws of oses that lay in
one of those villages, and brought them out before the eyes of all present, and
tore them to pieces and this was done with reproachful language, and much
scurrility which things when the ews heard of, they ran together, and that in
great numbers, and came down to esarea, where umanus then was, and
besought him that he would avenge, not themselves, but od himself, whose
laws had been affronted for that they could not bear to live any longer, if the
laws of their forefathers must be affronted after this manner. ccordingly
umanus, out of fear lest the multitude should go into a sedition, and by the
advice of his friends also, took care that the soldier who had offered the affront
to the laws should be beheaded, and thereby put a stop to the sedition which
was ready to be kindled a second time.
  
          
        

. ow there arose a quarrel between the amaritans and the ews on the
occasion following it was the custom of the alileans, when they came to the
holy city at the festivals, to take their journeys through the country of the
amaritans    and at this time there lay, in the road they took, a village that was
called inea, which was situated in the limits of amaria and the great plain,
where certain persons thereto belonging fought with the alileans, and killed a
great many of them. ut when the principal of the alileans were informed of
what had been done, they came to umanus, and desired him to avenge the
murder of those that were killed but he was induced by the amaritans, with
money, to do nothing in the matter upon which the alileans were much

displeased, and persuaded the multitude of the ews to betake themselves to
arms, and to regain their liberty, saying that slavery was in itself a bitter thing,
but that when it was joined with direct injuries, it was perfectly intolerable. nd
when their principal men endeavored to pacify them, and promised to endeavor
to persuade umanus to avenge those that were killed, they would not hearken
to them, but took their weapons, and entreated the assistance of leazar, the son
of ineus, a robber, who had many years made his abode in the mountains,
with which assistance they plundered many villages of the amaritans. hen
umanus heard of this action of theirs, he took the band of ebaste, with four
regiments of footmen, and armed the amaritans, and marched out against the
ews, and caught them, and slew many of them, and took a great number of
them alive whereupon those that were the most eminent persons at erusalem,
and that both in regard to the respect that was paid them, and the families they
were of, as soon as they saw to what a height things were gone, put on
sackcloth, and heaped ashes upon their heads, and by all possible means
besought the seditious, and persuaded them that they would set before their
eyes the utter subversion of their country, the conflagration of their temple, and
the slavery of themselves, their wives, and children,    which would be the
consequences of what they were doing and would alter their minds, would cast
away their weapons, and for the future be quiet, and return to their own homes.
hese persuasions of theirs prevailed upon them. o the people dispersed
themselves, and the robbers went away again to their places of strength and
after this time all udea was overrun with robberies.
. ut the principal of the amaritans went to mmidius uadratus, the
president of yria, who at that time was at yre, and accused the ews of setting
their villages on fire, and plundering them and said withal, that they were not
so much displeased at what they had suffered, as they were at the contempt
thereby showed the omans while if they had received any injury, they ought
to have made them the judges of what had been done, and not presently to
make such devastation, as if they had not the omans for their governors on
which account they came to him, in order to obtain that vengeance they wanted.
his was the accusation which the amaritans brought against the ews. ut the
ews affirmed that the amaritans were the authors of this tumult and fighting,
and that, in the first place, umanus had been corrupted by their gifts, and
passed over the murder of those that were slain in silence—which allegations
when uadratus heard, he put off the hearing of the cause, and promised that he
would give sentence when he should come into udea, and should have a more
eact knowledge of the truth of that matter. o these men went away without
success. et was it not long ere uadratus came to amaria, where, upon
hearing the cause, he supposed that the amaritans were the authors of that
disturbance. ut when he was informed that certain of the ews were making

innovations, he ordered those to be crucified whom umanus had taken
captives. rom whence he came to a certain village called ydda, which was
not less than a city in largeness, and there heard the amaritan cause a second
time before his tribunal, and there learned from a certain amaritan that one of
the chief of the ews, whose name was ortus, and some other innovators with
him, four in number, persuaded the multitude to a revolt from the omans
whom uadratus ordered to be put to death but still he sent away nanias the
high priest, and nanus the commander of the temple], in bonds to ome, to
give an account of what they had done to laudius aesar. e also ordered the
principal men, both of the amaritans and of the ews, as also umanus the
procurator, and eler the tribune, to go to taly to the emperor, that he might
hear their cause, and determine their differences one with another. ut he came
again to the city of erusalem, out of his fear that the multitude of the ews
should attempt some innovations but he found the city in a peaceable state, and
celebrating one of the usual festivals of their country to od. o he believed
that they would not attempt any innovations, and left them at the celebration of
the festival, and returned to ntioch.
. ow umanus, and the principal of the amaritans, who were sent to
ome, had a day appointed them by the emperor whereon they were to have
pleaded their cause about the quarrels they had one with another. ut now
aesar's freed-men and his friends were very zealous on the behalf of umanus
and the amaritans and they had prevailed over the ews, unless grippa,
junior, who was then at ome, had seen the principal of the ews hard set, and
had earnestly entreated grippina, the emperor's wife, to persuade her husband
to hear the cause, so as was agreeable to his justice, and to condemn those to be
punished who were really the authors of this revolt from the oman
government—whereupon laudius was so well disposed beforehand, that
when he had heard the cause, and found that the amaritans had been the
ringleaders in those mischievous doings, he gave order that those who came up
to him should be slain, and that umanus should be banished. e also gave
order that eler the tribune should be carried back to erusalem, and should be
drawn through the city in the sight of all the people, and then should be slain.
  
         
, ,   
. o laudius sent eli, the brother of allas, to take care of the affairs of
udea and when he had already completed the twelfth year of his reign, he

bestowed upon grippa the tetrarchy of hilip and atanea, and added thereto
rachonites, with bila which last had been the tetrarchy of ysanias but he
took from him halcis, when he had been governor thereof four years. nd
when grippa had received these countries as the gift of aesar, he gave his
sister rusilla in marriage to zizus, king of mesa, upon his consent to be
circumcised for piphanes, the son of king ntiochus, had refused to marry
her, because, after he had promised her father formerly to come over to the
ewish religion, he would not now perform that promise. e also gave
ariamne in marriage to rchelaus, the son of elcias, to whom she had
formerly been betrothed by grippa her father from which marriage was
derived a daughter, whose name was ernice.
. ut for the marriage of rusilla with zizus, it was in no long time
afterward dissolved upon the following occasion while eli was procurator of
udea, he saw this rusilla, and fell in love with her for she did indeed eceed
all other women in beauty and he sent to her a person whose name was
imon,    one of his friends a ew he was, and by birth a ypriot, and one who
pretended to be a magician, and endeavored to persuade her to forsake her
present husband, and marry him and promised, that if she would not refuse
him, he would make her a happy woman. ccordingly she acted ill, and
because she was desirous to avoid her sister ernice's envy, for she was very ill
treated by her on account of her beauty, was prevailed upon to transgress the
laws of her forefathers, and to marry eli and when he had had a son by her,
he named him grippa. ut after what manner that young man, with his wife,
perished at the conflagration of the mountain esuvius,    in the days of itus
aesar, shall be related hereafter. 
 . ut as for ernice, she lived a widow a long while after the death of
erod king of halcis], who was both her husband and her uncle but when
the report went that she had criminal conversation with her brother, grippa,
junior] she persuaded olemo, who was king of ilicia, to be circumcised, and
to marry her, as supposing that by this means she should prove those calumnies
upon her to be false and olemo was prevailed upon, and that chiefly on
account of her riches. et did not this matrimony endure long but ernice left
olemo, and, as was said, with impure intentions. o he forsook at once this
matrimony, and the ewish religion and, at the same time, ariamne put away
rchelaus, and was married to emetrius, the principal man among the
leandrian ews, both for his family and his wealth and indeed he was then
their alabarch. o she named her son whom she had by him grippinus. ut of
all these particulars we shall hereafter treat more eactly.

,     , 
       
  .   ,  
,       
  
. ow laudius aesar died when he had reigned thirteen years, eight
months, and twenty days    and a report went about that he was poisoned by his
wife grippina. er father was ermanicus, the brother of aesar. er husband
was omitius enobarbus, one of the most illustrious persons that was in the
city of ome after whose death, and her long continuance in widowhood,
laudius took her to wife. he brought along with her a son, omitius, of the
same name with his father. e had before this slain his wife essalina, out of
jealousy, by whom he had his children ritannicus and ctavia their eldest
sister was ntonia, whom he had by elina his first wife. e also married
ctavia to ero for that was the name that aesar gave him afterward, upon
his adopting him for his son.
. ut now grippina was afraid, lest, when ritannicus should come to
man's estate, he should succeed his father in the government, and desired to
seize upon the principality beforehand for her own son ero] upon which the
report went that she thence compassed the death of laudius. ccordingly, she
sent urrhus, the general of the army, immediately, and with him the tribunes,
and such also of the freed-men as were of the greatest authority, to bring ero
away into the camp, and to salute him emperor. nd when ero had thus
obtained the government, he got ritannicus to be so poisoned, that the
multitude should not perceive it although he publicly put his own mother to
death not long afterward, making her this requital, not only for being born of
her, but for bringing it so about by her contrivances that he obtained the oman
empire. e also slew ctavia his own wife, and many other illustrious persons,
under this pretence, that they plotted against him.
. ut  omit any further discourse about these affairs for there have been a
great many who have composed the history of ero some of which have
departed from the truth of facts out of favor, as having received benefits from
him while others, out of hatred to him, and the great ill-will which they bare
him, have so impudently raved against him with their lies, that they justly
deserve to be condemned. or do  wonder at such as have told lies of ero,
since they have not in their writings preserved the truth of history as to those
facts that were earlier than his time, even when the actors could have no way
incurred their hatred, since those writers lived a long time after them. ut as to
those that have no regard to truth, they may write as they please for in that they
take delight but as to ourselves, who have made truth our direct aim, we shall

briefly touch upon what only belongs remotely to this undertaking, but shall
relate what hath happened to us ews with great accuracy, and shall not grudge
our pains in giving an account both of the calamities we have suffered, and of
the crimes we have been guilty of.  will now therefore return to the relation of
our own affairs.
. or in the first year of the reign of ero, upon the death of zizus, king
of mesa, oemus,    his brother, succeeded in his kingdom, and ristobulus,
the son of erod, king of halcis, was intrusted by ero with the government
of the lesser rmenia. aesar also bestowed on grippa a certain part of
alilee, iberias, and aricheae,    and ordered them to submit to his
jurisdiction. e gave him also ulias, a city of erea, with fourteen villages that
lay about it.
. ow as for the affairs of the ews, they grew worse and worse
continually, for the country was again filled with robbers and impostors, who
deluded the multitude. et did eli catch and put to death many of those
impostors every day, together with the robbers. e also caught leazar, the son
of ineus, who had gotten together a company of robbers and this he did by
treachery for he gave him assurance that he should suffer no harm, and thereby
persuaded him to come to him but when he came, he bound him, and sent him
to ome. eli also bore an ill-will to onathan, the high priest, because he
frequently gave him admonitions about governing the ewish affairs better than
he did, lest he should himself have complaints made of him by the multitude,
since he it was who had desired aesar to send him as procurator of udea. o
eli contrived a method whereby he might get rid of him, now he was become
so continually troublesome to him for such continual admonitions are grievous
to those who are disposed to act unjustly. herefore eli persuaded one of
onathan's most faithful friends, a citizen of erusalem, whose name was oras,
to bring the robbers upon onathan, in order to kill him and this he did by
promising to give him a great deal of money for so doing. oras complied with
the proposal, and contrived matters so, that the robbers might murder him after
the following manner certain of those robbers went up to the city, as if they
were going to worship od, while they had daggers under their garments, and
by thus mingling themselves among the multitude they slew onathan    and as
this murder was never avenged, the robbers went up with the greatest security
at the festivals after this time and having weapons concealed in like manner as
before, and mingling themselves among the multitude, they slew certain of
their own enemies, and were subservient to other men for money and slew
others, not only in remote parts of the city, but in the temple itself also for they
had the boldness to murder men there, without thinking of the impiety of which
they were guilty. nd this seems to me to have been the reason why od, out of
his hatred of these men's wickedness, rejected our city and as for the temple,

he no longer esteemed it sufficiently pure for him to inhabit therein, but
brought the omans upon us, and threw a fire upon the city to purge it and
brought upon us, our wives, and children, slavery, as desirous to make us wiser
by our calamities.
. hese works, that were done by the robbers, filled the city with all sorts
of impiety. nd now these impostors and deceivers    persuaded the multitude to
follow them into the wilderness, and pretended that they would ehibit manifest
wonders and signs, that should be performed by the providence of od. nd
many that were prevailed on by them suffered the punishments of their folly
for eli brought them back, and then punished them. oreover, there came
out of gypt    about this time to erusalem one that said he was a prophet, and
advised the multitude of the common people to go along with him to the ount
of lives, as it was called, which lay over against the city, and at the distance of
five furlongs. e said further, that he would show them from hence how, at his
command, the walls of erusalem would fall down and he promised them that
he would procure them an entrance into the city through those walls, when they
were fallen down. ow when eli was informed of these things, he ordered
his soldiers to take their weapons, and came against them with a great number
of horsemen and footmen from erusalem, and attacked the gyptian and the
people that were with him. e also slew four hundred of them, and took two
hundred alive. ut the gyptian himself escaped out of the fight, but did not
appear any more. nd again the robbers stirred up the people to make war with
the omans, and said they ought not to obey them at all and when any persons
would not comply with them, they set fire to their villages, and plundered them.
. nd now it was that a great sedition arose between the ews that
inhabited esarea, and the yrians who dwelt there also, concerning their equal
right to the privileges belonging to citizens for the ews claimed the pre-
eminence, because erod their king was the builder of esarea, and because he
was by birth a ew. ow the yrians did not deny what was alleged about
erod but they said that esarea was formerly called trato's ower, and that
then there was not one ewish inhabitant. hen the presidents of that country
heard of these disorders, they caught the authors of them on both sides, and
tormented them with stripes, and by that means put a stop to the disturbance for
a time. ut the ewish citizens depending on their wealth, and on that account
despising the yrians, reproached them again, and hoped to provoke them by
such reproaches. owever, the yrians, though they were inferior in wealth, yet
valuing themselves highly on this account, that the greatest part of the oman
soldiers that were there were either of esarea or ebaste, they also for some
time used reproachful language to the ews also and thus it was, till at length
they came to throwing stones at one another, and several were wounded, and
fell on both sides, though still the ews were the conquerors. ut when eli

saw that this quarrel was become a kind of war, he came upon them on the
sudden, and desired the ews to desist and when they refused so to do, he
armed his soldiers, and sent them out upon them, and slew many of them, and
took more of them alive, and permitted his soldiers to plunder some of the
houses of the citizens, which were full of riches. ow those ews that were
more moderate, and of principal dignity among them, were afraid of
themselves, and desired of eli that he would sound a retreat to his soldiers,
and spare them for the future, and afford them room for repentance for what
they had done and eli was prevailed upon to do so.
. bout this time king grippa gave the high priesthood to smael, who
was the son of abi. nd now arose a sedition between the high priests and the
principal men of the multitude of erusalem each of which got them a
company of the boldest sort of men, and of those that loved innovations about
them, and became leaders to them and when they struggled together, they did it
by casting reproachful words against one another, and by throwing stones also.
nd there was nobody to reprove them but these disorders were done after a
licentious manner in the city, as if it had no government over it. nd such was
the impudence    and boldness that had seized on the high priests, that they had
the hardiness to send their servants into the threshing-floors, to take away those
tithes that were due to the priests, insomuch that it so fell out that the poorest

prevail over all right and justice.
. ow when orcius estus was sent as successor to eli by ero, the
principal of the ewish inhabitants of esarea went up to ome to accuse eli
and he had certainly been brought to punishment, unless ero had yielded to

. pon estus's coming into udea, it happened that udea was afflicted

nd then it was that the sicarii, as they were called, who were robbers, grew

mingled themselves among the multitude at their festivals, when they were

. bout the same time king grippa built himself a very large dining-

the high priest, and elcias, the keeper of the sacred treasure. nd when ero
had heard what they had to say, he not only forgave    them what they had
already done, but also gave them leave to let the wall they had built stand. his
was granted them in order to gratify oppea, ero's wife, who was a religious
woman, and had requested these favors of ero, and who gave order to the ten
ambassadors to go their way home but retained elcias and smael as hostages

to oseph, who was called abi, the son of imon, formerly high priest.

. nd now aesar, upon hearing the death of estus, sent lbinus into udea,

. nd now  think it proper and agreeable to this history to give an account of

erea and driving away the son of nias the third], put acimus into the place

years, he departed this life, and permitted his wife leandra to appoint him

retained the kingdom herself nine years, and then departed this life. he like
duration and no longer] did her son yrcanus enjoy the high priesthood for

office of high priest to od. ut when he had reigned three years, and as many

. ow essius lorus, who was sent as successor to lbinus by ero, filled
udea with abundance of miseries. e was by birth of the city of lazomenae,
and brought along with him his wife leopatra, (by whose friendship with
oppea, ero's wife, he obtained this government,) who was no way different
from him in wickedness. his lorus was so wicked, and so violent in the use
of his authority, that the ews took lbinus to have been comparatively] their
benefactor so ecessive were the mischiefs that he brought upon them. or
lbinus concealed his wickedness, and was careful that it might not be
discovered to all men but essius lorus, as though he had been sent on
purpose to show his crimes to every body, made a pompous ostentation of them
to our nation, as never omitting any sort of violence, nor any unjust sort of
punishment for he was not to be moved by pity, and never was satisfied with
any degree of gain that came in his way nor had he any more regard to great
than to small acquisitions, but became a partner with the robbers themselves.
or a great many fell then into that practice without fear, as having him for
their security, and depending on him, that he would save them harmless in their

particular robberies so that there were no bounds set to the nation's miseries
but the unhappy ews, when they were not able to bear the devastations which
the robbers made among them, were all under a necessity of leaving their own
habitations, and of flying away, as hoping to dwell more easily any where else
in the world among foreigners than in their own country]. nd what need  say
any more upon this head since it was this lorus who necessitated us to take
up arms against the omans, while we thought it better to be destroyed at once,
than by little and little. ow this war began in the second year of the
government of lorus, and the twelfth year of the reign of ero. ut then what
actions we were forced to do, or what miseries we were enabled to suffer, may
be accurately known by such as will peruse those books which  have written
about the ewish war.
.  shall now, therefore, make an end here of my ntiquities after the
conclusion of which events,  began to write that account of the war and these
ntiquities contain what hath been delivered down to us from the original
creation of man, until the twelfth year of the reign of ero, as to what hath
befallen the ews, as well in gypt as in yria and in alestine, and what we
have suffered from the ssyrians and abylonians, and what afflictions the
ersians and acedonians, and after them the omans, have brought upon us
for  think  may say that  have composed this history with sufficient accuracy
in all things.  have attempted to enumerate those high priests that we have had
during the interval of two thousand years  have also carried down the
succession of our kings, and related their actions, and political administration,
without considerable] errors, as also the power of our monarchs and all
according to what is written in our sacred books for this it was that  promised
to do in the beginning of this history. nd  am so bold as to say, now  have so
completely perfected the work  proposed to myself to do, that no other person,
whether he were a ew or foreigner, had he ever so great an inclination to it,
could so accurately deliver these accounts to the reeks as is done in these
books. or those of my own nation freely acknowledge that  far eceed them
in the learning belonging to ews  have also taken a great deal of pains to
obtain the learning of the reeks, and understand the elements of the reek
language, although  have so long accustomed myself to speak our own tongue,
that  cannot pronounce reek with sufficient eactness for our nation does not
encourage those that learn the languages of many nations, and so adorn their
discourses with the smoothness of their periods because they look upon this
sort of accomplishment as common, not only to all sorts of free-men, but to as
many of the servants as please to learn them. ut they give him the testimony
of being a wise man who is fully acquainted with our laws, and is able to
interpret their meaning on which account, as there have been many who have
done their endeavors with great patience to obtain this learning, there have yet

hardly been so many as two or three that have succeeded therein, who were
immediately well rewarded for their pains.
. nd now it will not be perhaps an invidious thing, if  treat briefly of my
own family, and of the actions of my own life,    while there are still living such
as can either prove what  say to be false, or can attest that it is true with which
accounts  shall put an end to these ntiquities, which are contained in twenty
books, and sity thousand verses. nd if od    permit me,  will briefly run
over this war again, with what befell us therein to this very day, which is the
thirteenth year of the reign of aesar omitian, and the fifty-sith year of my
own life.  have also an intention to write three books concerning our ewish
opinions about od and his essence, and about our laws why, according to
them, some things are permitted us to do, and others are prohibited.
